text: This chapter introduces neural networks as pattern recognition machines: computer programs that learn patterns from examples instead of being given hand-coded rules. It contrasts traditional programming (humans write if/then rules) with neural networks (humans provide labeled examples and the network discovers rules from weights). It uses spam detection as a concrete example (word count 152, has 'free' yes, links 12 → 92% spam) and shows the internal structure as tiny units called neurons connected by numbers called weights, illustrated with a three-layer fully-connected network (inputs x1 x2 x3 → hidden h1 h2 h3 → outputs o1 o2). The weights are what the network learns during training.
summary: Defines a neural network as a pattern-learning math program built from neurons connected by learned weights, contrasted with rule-based programming.

text: A neuron is the basic building block of a neural network. It takes a few numbers in, multiplies each by a weight, sums the products, adds a bias, and applies an activation rule to produce one output number. Worked example uses flu detection from symptoms (fever 38.5, cough 1.0, fatigue 0.8 with weights 0.6, 0.3, 0.1 and bias -14.0) yielding output 9.48. The general formula: output = activation(Σ(input × weight) + bias). Every neuron in every AI does this same operation; weights are learned during training.
summary: Overview of single-neuron operation: weighted sum plus bias plus activation, with concrete flu-detection example.

text: Example: detecting flu from symptoms. Three inputs with weights: Input 1: fever = 38.5°C, weight 0.6 (very important). Input 2: cough = 1 (yes), weight 0.3 (somewhat important). Input 3: fatigue = 0.8, weight 0.1 (less important). Weights encode how important each symptom is to the flu decision.
summary: Concrete flu example showing three symptom inputs with their relative importance weights.

text: Step-by-step math for the flu neuron. Step 1: multiply each input by its weight. fever: 38.5 × 0.6 = 23.10. cough: 1.0 × 0.3 = 0.30. fatigue: 0.8 × 0.1 = 0.08. Step 2: sum them up. 23.10 + 0.30 + 0.08 = 23.48. Step 3: add a bias (a fixed number added every time). 23.48 + (-14.0) = 9.48. The bias shifts the result up or down. Step 4: apply a rule - if negative, output 0; if positive, keep it. 9.48 is positive → keep → 9.48. This rule is called an activation function. Final output: 9.48 → HIGH flu risk.
summary: Walks through the four-step neuron computation with concrete arithmetic ending in output 9.48.

text: The neuron formula: output = activation( Σ(input × weight) + bias ). text: A layer is a group of neurons that all receive the same inputs but apply different weights and biases. The layer takes a list of numbers in and produces a list of numbers out, with one output per neuron. Stacking layers lets later layers build on patterns found by earlier ones, and physically a layer is just parallel math operations on a GPU. Example: input [38.5, 1, 0.8] feeds three neurons (Flu, Cold, Allergy) that produce [9.48, 3.51, 0.26], so the network predicts Flu.
summary: Chapter overview: a layer is a parallel group of neurons sharing inputs but with their own weights, transforming a vector into a vector.

text: This chapter explains weights and biases - the two learnable parameters inside every neuron. A weight measures how important an input is (e.g., fever weight 0.6 = very important, shoe color near 0 = irrelevant). A bias is a fixed number added to shift the result regardless of input - without bias, price = sqft × weight forces price to 0 when sqft = 0, which is wrong because land has base value. The full neuron operation is: multiply each input by its weight, sum, add bias, then activate. Worked example: x1=1500, x2=3 with weights 0.5, 50 and bias 50 produces 1500×0.5 + 3×50 + 50 = 950, output $950k. The weights and bias are what the network LEARNS during training; everything else is fixed math.
summary: Chapter overview of weights as importance multipliers and biases as baseline shifts, with full multiply-sum-add-bias-activate computation.

text: This chapter explains why a neural network without an activation function is fundamentally limited. A bare neuron computes y = mx + b, a straight line. Real-world patterns like plant growth, drug response, or any peak-and-decline behavior need curves a line cannot fit. Worse, stacking many linear layers algebraically collapses to a single straight line: Layer 1 output × 3 + 1 fed into Layer 2 × 2 + 5 simplifies to input × 6 + 7. So 100 layers equals 1 layer without nonlinearity. The fix is an activation function that adds a bend, breaking linearity so depth becomes meaningful.
summary: Linear neurons can only draw straight lines and stacking them collapses to one line, so neural networks need activation functions.
queries: summary of why linear isn't enough
need for bends in neural networks
need for curves in neural networks

text: A neuron without activation does y = mx + b. Strip away the activation and a neuron computes: output = (input × weight) + bias. m is the weight (slope), b is the bias (y-intercept). y = mx + b. m (slope) = the weight. Controls how steep the line is. b (bias) = the bias. Shifts the line up or down. x (input) = the input value going in. y (output) = the output. Always a straight line. Key problem: no matter what m and b are, this is ALWAYS a straight line.
summary: A neuron without activation is just the line equation y = mx + b, which can only ever produce a straight line.

text: Why is this a problem? Real patterns are curved. A 1500 sqft house costs more than 1000 sqft - that's roughly linear. But plant growth vs temperature? A plant grows faster as temperature rises from 10°C to 25°C, then STOPS growing and DIES above 40°C. No straight line can go up, peak, and then come down. Same with medicine dosage: 100mg helps, 200mg helps more, but 1000mg is toxic. These real patterns require curves that bend and change direction. A straight line always goes in one direction. Three examples shown: study hours vs grade (linear, fits a straight line), temperature vs plant growth (parabolic peak, no straight line fits), drug dose vs effect (S-curve, no straight line fits).
summary: Real-world patterns like plant growth peaks and drug dosage curves require bends a straight line cannot model.
queries: why can't a straight line model real patterns
why are real world relationships curved
when does a straight line fail to fit data

text: The Fatal Problem: Stacking Without Activation. Maybe adding more layers helps? Layer 1: output₁ = input × 3 + 1. Layer 2: output₂ = output₁ × 2 + 5. Substitute: output₂ = (input × 3 + 1) × 2 + 5 => output₂ = input × 6 + 7. Still just y = 6x + 7. A straight line! No matter how many layers: 2 layers, 10 layers, 100 layers - without activation they all collapse into one straight line. 100 layers = 1 layer.
summary: Stacking linear layers without activation collapses algebraically into a single straight line, so depth alone gives no extra power.

text: What We Need: a BEND in the Line. We need something that adds a bend to the line. Something that breaks the linearity so layers actually DO different things when stacked. That something is called an activation function.
summary: To make stacked layers meaningful, we need something that bends the line: an activation function.

chapterTitle: Activation (ReLU) - Why Layers Need a Bend
text: This chapter introduces ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit), the activation function that fixes the linearity problem. ReLU's rule: if x is negative output 0, if positive keep x. Formally ReLU(x) = max(0, x). Each ReLU neuron creates one bend in the line. Two ReLU neurons combined create a valley curve. Scale to millions of neurons and you get millions of bends - enough to learn any pattern. Without ReLU, 100 layers collapse into one straight line. With ReLU, each neuron contributes a bend, enabling networks to model language, images, and anything else.
summary: ReLU activation adds bends to neuron output, breaking linearity so stacked layers can learn complex curves.

chapterTitle: Activation (ReLU) - Why Layers Need a Bend
text: The ReLU rule: after each neuron's math, apply one simple rule - If negative -> 0, If positive -> keep. Formally: ReLU(x) = max(0, x). Examples: ReLU(-5) = 0, ReLU(-100) = 0, ReLU(3) = 3, ReLU(950) = 950. Negative inputs are clipped to zero. Positive inputs pass through unchanged.
summary: ReLU outputs zero for negative inputs and keeps positive inputs unchanged, expressed as max(0, x).

chapterTitle: Activation (ReLU) - Why Layers Need a Bend
text: Adding two ReLU neurons together creates a valley curve - a shape no straight line can produce. Combined outputs (neuron1 + neuron2) at inputs 0-7: [5,4,3,2,2,2,3,4]. Concrete values: input=0 -> output=5; input=3 -> output=2 (1st bend); input=4 -> output=2; input=5 -> output=2 (2nd bend); input=7 -> output=4. Two bent lines summed produce a curve with two bends - a valley shape. This shows how combining ReLU neurons builds nonlinear functions.
summary: Summing two ReLU neurons produces a valley-shaped curve with two bends that no single straight line can fit.

chapterTitle: Activation (ReLU) - Why Layers Need a Bend
text: Scaling up ReLU: 2 neurons -> 2 bends -> valley/hill. 10 neurons -> 10 bends -> waves, zigzags. 1000 neurons -> 1000 bends -> any curve. Millions of neurons -> millions of bends -> language, images, anything. Without ReLU: 100 layers = 1 layer (a single straight line). With ReLU: each neuron adds a bend, and millions of bends together let the network learn ANY pattern. This is why activation functions are essential for deep learning.
summary: More ReLU neurons mean more bends, scaling from simple valleys to millions of bends that approximate any function.

text: LAYER 1: Each hidden neuron processes the inputs. Neuron h₁: Step 1 Multiply - x₁: 1500 × 0.5 = 750; x₂: 3 × 50 = 150. Step 2 Sum: 750 + 150 = 900. Step 3 Add bias: 900 + 50 = 950. Step 4 ReLU: 950 is positive → keep → 950. Result: h₁ = 950. Neuron h₂: Step 1 Multiply - x₁: 1500 × 0.1 = 150; x₂: 3 × 10 = 30. Step 2 Sum: 150 + 30 = 180. Step 3 Add bias: 180 + 5 = 185. Step 4 ReLU: 185 is positive → keep → 185. Result: h₂ = 185. Layer 1 output: [950, 185].

text: This chapter introduces the loss function. Loss is a single number measuring how wrong a prediction was, computed as (actual - predicted)². For our example with predicted 807 and actual 900, loss = 93² = 8,649. Loss depends on weights through the prediction, so training searches for weights that minimize loss. Squaring is used because it punishes big errors more, gives smooth derivatives needed for backpropagation, and creates a parabolic shape with one clear minimum.
summary: Loss is the squared error between actual and predicted values - a single number that drives weight updates during training.

text: Loss is a single number that measures how wrong our prediction was. Loss = (actual − predicted)². We square the error so all errors are positive (they don't cancel out) and so big errors get punished much more than small ones.
summary: Loss measures prediction error using the squared difference between actual and predicted values.

text: Key insight: the loss depends on the prediction. The prediction depends on weights and biases. So if we change a weight, the prediction changes, and the loss changes. There exists some perfect set of weights that makes the loss as small as possible. Our job is to find those values. The causal chain is: weights → prediction → loss. But how do we know which direction to change weights? We need derivatives.
summary: Loss depends on weights through the prediction, so finding the perfect weights minimizes loss - this is what training does.

text: Why square the error? Three reasons: (1) Big errors get punished more - error of 2 gives loss of 4, error of 10 gives loss of 100, so a 5x error becomes a 25x loss. (2) Smooth curve for derivatives - the derivative of x² is 2x, simple and works everywhere; backpropagation needs smooth derivatives to work cleanly. (3) One clear minimum - squared loss creates a parabolic bowl with one clear lowest point, so gradient descent rolls down smoothly to find it.
summary: Squaring the error punishes big mistakes more, gives smooth derivatives for backprop, and creates a parabolic bowl with one clear minimum.

text: This chapter - Learning - What Does It Mean? A fresh neural network has random weights and produces garbage predictions. Learning means adjusting those weights and biases through a 4-step loop: forward pass to predict, compute loss, backward pass to assign blame, and update weights. This loop is called Backpropagation plus Gradient Descent. Repeated millions of times, it converges the weights into a useful model - exactly like GPT-3's 175 billion parameters.
summary: Learning is iteratively adjusting random weights via the forward-loss-backward-update loop until the model converges.

text: This chapter builds intuition for derivatives in neural networks. A derivative answers: how much does loss change when we nudge a weight? Demonstrated with a concrete example (weight 0.5 → 0.6, loss 10,000 → 2,500), giving ratio ∂Loss/∂weight = -7,500 / 0.1 = -75,000. The sign tells which direction to update (negative = increase weight), the magnitude tells sensitivity. 'Partial' means changing one weight while holding others constant. The derivative is the compass that points toward minimum loss.
summary: Chapter-level overview: derivatives measure loss sensitivity to weight nudges and serve as the compass for gradient descent.

text: A derivative answers one simple question: if I nudge the input a tiny bit, how much does the output change? No Greek symbols, no limits, no complicated math. Just: small change in, how much change out? It measures how sensitive the output is to a small change in the input.

text: Concrete example with weight = 0.5, input = 1500, bias = 50. Prediction = 1500 × 0.5 + 50 = 800. Actual = 900. Loss = (900 - 800)² = 10,000. Now nudge the weight to 0.6. New Prediction = 1500 × 0.6 + 50 = 950. New Loss = (900 - 950)² = 2,500. Weight changed by +0.1, loss changed by -7,500. Increasing the weight made loss go DOWN. Good direction!

text: The derivative is this ratio: ∂Loss / ∂weight = how much loss changed / how much weight changed. Plugging in our numbers: ∂Loss/∂weight = (2,500 - 10,000) / (0.6 - 0.5) = -7,500 / 0.1 = -75,000. Negative means increasing the weight decreases loss. The magnitude (75,000) tells us it is very sensitive. The ∂ symbol (read 'partial') just means 'tiny change in.' So ∂Loss/∂weight means 'the tiny change in loss caused by a tiny change in weight.'
summary: Defines the partial derivative as the ratio of loss change to weight change, computing -75,000 for the example.

text: What the derivative tells us. Negative (-): increasing weight makes loss go DOWN, so increase the weight. Positive (+): increasing weight makes loss go UP, so decrease the weight. Large number: loss is very sensitive to this weight, it matters a lot. Small number: loss barely changes, this weight matters little.
summary: Explains how the sign of the derivative gives direction and the magnitude gives sensitivity importance.

text: The derivative is your compass. It tells us which direction to adjust each weight to reduce loss. With multiple weights, we turn one knob at a time. ∂L/∂w means 'how does loss change when we nudge only w, holding everything else constant?' That is what 'partial' means - changing one thing, leaving everything else still. Visualized as a parabolic loss curve: the tangent line at the current point has slope equal to the derivative, pointing toward the minimum.

Each fraction has a numerator and denominator separated by a horizontal line, color-coded (purple for the result, red, orange, yellow for the three backward-pass steps). chapterTitle: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule
text: Backpropagation uses the chain rule to compute how loss changes with respect to each weight and bias. Trace backwards from loss through every operation (squaring, subtraction, linear combination), compute the local derivative at each step, and multiply them all together. For our example: dLoss/dW = -200 × 1 × 1500 = -300,000 and dLoss/dB = -200 × 1 × 1 = -200. Negative gradients mean increasing the parameter decreases loss; magnitude tells sensitivity.
summary: Chapter summary: backprop applies the chain rule to compute gradients for every weight and bias by multiplying local derivatives along the chain.

chapterTitle: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule
text: Backward pass traces blame from loss back to weight through multiple intermediate steps. The full chain for our example: weight w=0.5 → compute (1500 × w + 50) → prediction 800 → subtract actual (800 - 900) → error -100 → square it (-100)² → loss 10,000. Each arrow is one operation in the computational graph. To find dLoss/dw, we must trace through ALL of them, not just one.
summary: Introduces backpropagation as tracing blame through multiple steps from loss back to a weight via the computational graph.

chapterTitle: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule
text: Chain rule: work backwards one step at a time and multiply all derivatives. Formula: ∂Loss/∂weight = ∂Loss/∂error × ∂error/∂pred × ∂pred/∂weight. Step 1: Loss = error², so dLoss/dError = 2 × error = 2 × (-100) = -200. Step 2: error = prediction - 900, so dError/dPred = 1. Step 3: prediction = 1500 × w + 50, so dPred/dW = 1500. Each fraction is one step. Multiply them all to get the final answer.
summary: Shows the chain rule formula and computes each of the three local derivatives needed for the backward pass.

chapterTitle: The Backward Pass - The Chain Rule

The prediction formula is prediction = 1500w + bias, so the derivative of prediction with respect to bias is 1 (because bias is directly added). Now we have a gradient for every learnable parameter (weight and bias) - time to update them.

text: The weight update rule: new_weight = old_weight - learning_rate × gradient. old_weight is the weight's current value before this update. gradient is how much loss changes when this weight changes (from the backward pass). learning_rate is a small number (like 0.001) that controls step size. new_weight is the updated value used going forward. We move in the OPPOSITE direction of the gradient: if the gradient says 'loss increases when you go right,' we go left. Visual shows a U-shaped loss curve with step 1, step 2, and step N rolling downhill toward the minimum.
summary: Introduces the gradient descent weight update rule and explains each term.

text: Learning rate scaling example. Gradient is -300,000. Applied directly: new_w = 0.5 - (-300,000) = 300,000.5. Insane! Learning rate (like 0.0000001) scales the step to something reasonable. Weight update: new_w = 0.5 - (0.0000001 × -300,000) = 0.5 + 0.03 = 0.53. Bias update: new_b = 50 - (0.0000001 × -200) = 50 + 0.00002 ≈ 50.00002.
summary: Worked example showing why learning rate is needed to scale huge gradients into tiny weight changes.

text: The landscape analogy. Imagine you are blindfolded on a hilly landscape. Height represents loss. You want to reach the lowest valley. Start (w=0.5): high up on the hill, loss is 10,000. Gradient tells us the slope: which direction is downhill? That is what the derivative shows. Goal (minimum loss): weights that make excellent predictions, loss near zero. Learning rate too big: you jump over the valley. Learning rate too small: takes forever. Just right: steady descent to the minimum.

text: This chapter walks through a complete backpropagation pass on the same two-layer house price network used in the forward pass chapter. The network has inputs x1=1500, x2=3, hidden values h1=950 and h2=185, output y=807, target=900, and loss=8649. Starting from dLoss/dy = 2*(807-900) = -186, the chain rule traces backward step by step: output weight gradients (dL/dw_o1 = -176700, dL/dw_o2 = -34410, dL/db_o = -186), gradient flow into hidden activations (dL/dh1 = -148.8, dL/dh2 = -37.2), ReLU pass-through (derivative=1 for positive pre-activations, 0 otherwise), and finally hidden weight gradients (dL/dw11 = -223200, dL/dw21 = -446.4, dL/db1 = -148.8, dL/dw12 = -55800, dL/dw22 = -111.6, dL/db2 = -37.2). The full chain for w11 multiplies four derivatives: -186 * 0.8 * 1 * 1500 = -223200. All 9 parameter gradients end up negative, meaning every weight should increase to push the prediction up toward 900.
summary: Full backprop trace through a two-layer network, computing gradients for all 9 parameters via the chain rule.

text: Our network got it wrong - now fix it. In the forward-pass chapter we traced this same network: Inputs x1=1500, x2=3. Hidden h1=950, h2=185. Output y=807. Target = 900. Loss = (807 - 900)^2 = 8,649. The network predicted 807 but the real price is 900. To fix it we need to figure out which weights are to blame and how to adjust each one - that is what backpropagation computes.

text: The network - now we trace backwards. Same network as the forward-pass chapter, with every weight labeled on its edge. The backward pass computes a gradient for each one. Weights: input-to-hidden w11=0.5, w12=0.1, w21=50, w22=10; hidden biases b1=50, b2=5; hidden-to-output w_o1=0.8, w_o2=0.2; output bias b_o=10. Hidden outputs h1=950, h2=185 and final y=807, with target 900 and loss 8649. The chain rule traces backward through every operation, multiplying derivatives at each step. Think of it as tracing blame: Loss, who caused you? The prediction. Prediction, who caused you? The weights and hidden neurons...
summary: Introduces the labeled network and frames backprop as the chain rule tracing blame backwards through every operation.

text: Step 1: How does loss change with y? Start at the very end. loss = (y - target)^2. Derivative: dLoss/dy = 2 * (y - target) = 2 * (807 - 900) = 2 * (-93) = -186. This is negative. It means: if we increased y by 1 unit, loss would decrease by 186. Makes sense - y=807 is too low, so pushing it up reduces the error.
summary: Computes dLoss/dy = 2*(y-target) = -186 as the starting point of backprop.
queries: derivative of squared error loss with respect to y
how does loss change with prediction
loss derivative when prediction is too low

text: Step 2: Output layer weight gradients. y = h1 * w_o1 + h2 * w_o2 + b_o. Applying the chain rule: dLoss/dw_o1 = dLoss/dy * h1 = -186 * 950 = -176,700. dLoss/dw_o2 = dLoss/dy * h2 = -186 * 185 = -34,410. dLoss/db_o = dLoss/dy * 1 = -186. Why is the gradient for w_o1 five times larger than w_o2? Because h1 = 950 is five times larger than h2 = 185. A bigger input means the weight has more influence on the output.
summary: Computes output-layer weight and bias gradients: -176700 for w_o1, -34410 for w_o2, -186 for b_o.
queries: how to compute output layer weight gradient
gradient of output weight equals dLoss/dy times hidden activation

output bias gradient is just dLoss/dy
how does h1 affect w_o1 gradient

text: Step 3: How does gradient reach the hidden layer? Hidden weights are two steps from loss, so the gradient must flow back through the output neuron first. Since y = h1 * w_o1 + ..., we have dy/dh1 = w_o1 = 0.8. So dLoss/dh1 = dLoss/dy * w_o1 = -186 * 0.8 = -148.8. Similarly dy/dh2 = w_o2 = 0.2, giving dLoss/dh2 = -186 * 0.2 = -37.2. h1 gets 4x more gradient than h2 because w_o1 (0.8) is 4x bigger than w_o2 (0.2). The weight connecting h1 to the output is like a pipe - a bigger weight is a wider pipe that carries more gradient backward.
summary: Gradient flows from output to hidden activations: dLoss/dh1 = -148.8, dLoss/dh2 = -37.2, scaled by output weights.
queries: why does h1 get more gradient than h2
pipe analogy for weights in backprop
how output weight controls backward gradient

text: Step 4: Through ReLU - the gate. Before reaching hidden weights, the gradient passes through ReLU. h1 = ReLU(z1) where z1 is the pre-activation. ReLU derivative: if z > 0, derivative = 1 (gradient passes through unchanged); if z <= 0, derivative = 0 (gradient is completely blocked - dead). For our example, z1 = 950 (positive) so dLoss/dz1 = dLoss/dh1 * 1 = -148.8. z2 = 185 (positive) so dLoss/dz2 = dLoss/dh2 * 1 = -37.2. Both neurons had positive pre-activations so ReLU let the full gradient through. If z had been negative (e.g. -50), ReLU would output 0 in the forward pass and block the gradient entirely on the backward pass - that neuron would learn nothing from this example, a 'dead neuron.'
summary: ReLU acts as a gate during backprop: derivative is 1 for positive pre-activations, 0 otherwise, which causes dead neurons.
queries: how does backprop go through ReLU
ReLU derivative is 1 when z positive 0 when negative
why ReLU blocks gradient when input negative

text: Step 5: Hidden layer weight gradients. z1 = x1*w11 + x2*w21 + b1, so dz1/dw11 = x1, dz1/dw21 = x2, dz1/db1 = 1. Using dLoss/dz1 = -148.8: dL/dw11 = -148.8 * 1500 = -223,200; dL/dw21 = -148.8 * 3 = -446.4; dL/db1 = -148.8 * 1 = -148.8. Using dLoss/dz2 = -37.2: dL/dw12 = -37.2 * 1500 = -55,800; dL/dw22 = -37.2 * 3 = -111.6; dL/db2 = -37.2 * 1 = -37.2. Notice w11 has gradient -223,200 while w21 has only -446.4 because x1=1500 is 500x larger than x2=3. The gradient for a weight is proportional to its input - bigger inputs create bigger gradients.
summary: Computes all hidden-layer weight and bias gradients; gradient scales with the corresponding input value.

all hidden weight gradients in network

text: The full chain for w11. Trace the complete chain from loss all the way back to w11 - four multiplications, the chain rule in action: dLoss/dw11 = dLoss/dy * dy/dh1 * dReLU/dz1 * dz1/dw11 = -186 * 0.8 * 1 * 1500 = -223,200. Each multiplication traces through one operation in the computational graph. That is why it is called the 'chain' rule - you chain derivatives together, link by link, from loss back to each weight.
summary: Full four-factor chain rule for w11: -186 * 0.8 * 1 * 1500 = -223200.

text: Complete gradient table - every parameter. All 9 learnable parameters and their gradients: w11 (value 0.5, gradient -223,200, increase a lot); w21 (50, -446.4, increase a bit); b1 (50, -148.8, increase a bit); w12 (0.1, -55,800, increase); w22 (10, -111.6, increase a bit); b2 (5, -37.2, increase a bit); w_o1 (0.8, -176,700, increase a lot); w_o2 (0.2, -34,410, increase); b_o (10, -186, increase a bit). Every gradient is negative - increasing any weight will decrease the loss. That makes sense: the prediction (807) was too low, so the network needs to push everything up to get closer to 900. This is backpropagation - start at the loss, trace backward through every operation, multiply derivatives at each step. Every weight gets a gradient telling it exactly how to change.
summary: Table of all 9 gradients with values and direction of change; all negative meaning every weight should increase.

chapterTitle: Gradients in Action - The Full Training Loop
text: Once backprop produces gradients for every parameter, the network updates all weights simultaneously using new_weight = old_weight - learning_rate x gradient with a tiny learning rate (0.0000001) because gradients are huge. Applied to a 9-parameter network: weights with large gradients (w11, w_o1) move most, biases barely move. After one update, prediction jumps from 807 to 854 and loss drops 75% from 8,649 to 2,116. Repeating the loop 10 times drives prediction to 899.8 and loss to 0.04. Every neural network including GPT-4 trains with this loop: forward pass, compute loss, backward pass, update weights, repeat - trillions of times for large models.
summary: Summarizes the full training loop: gradient descent applied to all parameters, single-step loss drop, convergence over iterations, and the universal four-step loop used by every neural network.

chapterTitle: Gradients in Action - The Full Training Loop
text: The complete training loop every neural network uses. Step 1 Forward Pass: feed input through network, get prediction. Step 2 Compute Loss: how wrong was the prediction? Step 3 Backward Pass: chain rule computes gradient for every weight. Step 4 Update Weights: nudge each weight opposite to its gradient. Repeat: thousands to billions of times. GPT-4 runs this loop trillions of times on billions of text examples. Each loop adjusts millions of weights by tiny amounts. After enough loops, the model can write poetry, answer questions, and write code - all from this simple loop: predict, measure error, trace blame, fix weights, repeat.
summary: Diagrams the four-step training loop (forward, loss, backward, update, repeat) used by every neural network including GPT-4.

text: This chapter explains why backpropagation through very deep networks (50, 100, 1000+ layers) is mathematically unstable. The chain rule multiplies one derivative per layer, so if each is < 1 the gradient vanishes exponentially (0.5^50 is essentially zero), and if each is > 1 it explodes (2^50 is over a quadrillion). Four innovations made deep learning work: ReLU activation (derivative = 1 for positive inputs, no shrinkage like sigmoid's 0.25 max), residual/skip connections (gradient flows through input + f(input) so it always has a path of 1), layer normalization (keeps activations in stable range), and careful weight initialization (He, Xavier). Every architectural choice in the Transformer (Add & Norm, scaled dot product) exists to keep gradients healthy through 120 layers.
summary: Why deep backprop fails - vanishing and exploding gradients - and the architectural solutions (ReLU, residuals, layer norm, init) that fixed it.

Modern networks have 50, 100, or even 1000+ layers. GPT-4 has 120 layers. For a weight in layer 1 of a 120-layer network, the chain rule has 120+ multiplications chained together: Layer 1 x Layer 2 x Layer 3 x ... x Loss.
summary: Backprop through deep networks chains hundreds of derivative multiplications together - very different from the 4-multiplication 2-layer case.

text: The Vanishing Gradient Problem. If each derivative in the chain is less than 1, the product shrinks exponentially. 2 layers: 0.5 x 0.5 = 0.25. 5 layers: 0.5^5 = 0.031. 10 layers: 0.5^10 = 0.001. 50 layers: 0.5^50 = 0.000000000000001. At 50 layers the gradient is essentially zero. The early layers never learn because the gradient signal has completely vanished by the time it reaches them. It is like whispering a message through 50 people - by the end, nothing is left.
summary: Vanishing gradients: when each layer's derivative is below 1, multiplying them across deep networks makes the gradient effectively zero.
queries: what is vanishing gradient with example numbers
how does gradient vanish in deep networks
what happens to gradient at 50 layers
how small does the gradient get in deep nets

text: The Exploding Gradient Problem. If each derivative is greater than 1, the product grows exponentially. 2 layers: 2 x 2 = 4. 5 layers: 2^5 = 32. 10 layers: 2^10 = 1,024. 50 layers: 2^50 = 1,125,899,906,842,624. At 50 layers the gradient is astronomically large. Weight updates become wild jumps that overshoot the minimum and the network diverges - the loss goes to infinity instead of zero. Training crashes.
summary: Exploding gradients: when each layer's derivative exceeds 1, multiplied across a deep network they balloon to astronomical values causing training to diverge.
queries: how do gradients explode in deep networks
why does loss go to infinity
gradient blow up in deep networks
why network diverges instead of converges

text: Solutions That Made Deep Learning Work. (1) ReLU Activation instead of sigmoid: sigmoid's derivative is at most 0.25 - guaranteed to vanish. ReLU's derivative is exactly 1 for positive inputs - no shrinkage. (2) Residual Connections (Skip Connections): instead of output = f(input), use output = input + f(input). The gradient for input is always 1 - it flows straight through the skip connection regardless of depth. The Transformer uses this everywhere. (3) Layer Normalization: keep activations in a stable range at every layer. Prevents tiny and huge values from accumulating. (4) Careful Weight Initialization: start weights in a range that keeps gradients close to 1. Methods like He initialization and Xavier initialization solve this mathematically.
summary: Four breakthroughs that solved vanishing/exploding gradients: ReLU, residual/skip connections, layer normalization, and principled weight initialization (He, Xavier).

text: Why This Matters for Everything Ahead. Every architecture choice in the Transformer - residual connections, layer normalization, scaled dot-product attention - exists partly to solve these gradient problems. When you see Add & Norm in the Transformer diagram, you will know exactly why it is there: to keep gradients healthy so backprop works through 120 layers. Backpropagation is the single most important algorithm in deep learning. Every model - GPT, DALL-E, AlphaGo - learned through this exact process: forward pass, compute loss, chain rule backward, update weights, repeat.
summary: Transformer design (Add & Norm, scaled attention) exists specifically to keep gradients healthy through deep stacks; backprop powers every modern model.

chapterTitle: The Dot Product - How Vectors Compare
text: This chapter introduces the dot product: the operation that compares two vectors by multiplying matching positions and summing the products. For vectors A=[2,3] and B=[4,1], the dot product is 2×4 + 3×1 = 11. The result reveals similarity: same direction gives a large positive number, opposite direction gives a large negative number, and unrelated vectors give numbers near zero. The dot product is the core operation inside every neuron - a neuron computing input·weights is performing exactly this calculation. It is the most important operation in all of AI.
summary: Dot product compares two vectors by multiplying matching positions and summing; it powers every neuron and is the most fundamental operation in AI.

chapterTitle: The Dot Product - How Vectors Compare
text: The Recipe: Multiply Matching Positions, Then Add Up. Given A = [2, 3] and B = [4, 1]. Step 1: 2 × 4 = 8 and 3 × 1 = 3. Step 2: 8 + 3 = 11. Therefore A · B = 11. Multiply matching positions, sum the products.

chapterTitle: The Dot Product - How Vectors Compare
text: What Does It Mean? [2,3] · [4,6] = 26 means same direction (similar - large value). [2,3] · [4,1] = 11 means somewhat similar. [2,3] · [0,0] = 0 means zero vector. [2,3] · [-2,-3] = -13 means opposite direction (negative value). Note on cosine similarity: the dot product is affected by magnitude. When you only care about direction, normalize to length 1 first - that is cosine similarity.
summary: The dot product reveals direction similarity: large positive for aligned vectors, zero for unrelated, large negative for opposite; cosine similarity normalizes for magnitude.

text: Matrices are 2D grids of numbers. A matrix transforms a vector into a new vector by taking a dot product between each row of the matrix and the input vector. With weights [[0.6, 0.3, 0.1], [0.1, 0.5, 0.2], [0.0, 0.2, 0.7]] and input [38.5, 1.0, 0.8], the three neuron outputs are 23.48, 4.51, and 0.76 - the pre-activation scores of a neural network layer. A neural network layer's weights ARE a matrix, and the forward pass IS matrix multiplication.
summary: Chapter-level recap: matrices are 2D grids; matrix-vector multiplication is row-wise dot products; a neural network layer is exactly this operation.

text: A neural network layer is matrix multiplication. One neuron computes a dot product of inputs with its weight vector. A layer with multiple neurons stacks each neuron's weights as a row of a matrix, so the layer's output is the input vector multiplied by the weight matrix. Many dot products at once equals matrix multiplication. A full neural network stacks such layers, chaining matrix multiplications with activation functions between them.
summary: Layer in a neural network equals matrix multiplication because each neuron is one dot product and stacking neurons stacks weight rows into a matrix.

text: This chapter covers activation functions - the nonlinear pieces placed between linear matrix-multiply layers. A layer alone computes output = W·input, which is linear. Stacking linear layers collapses into one linear function (W2·W1·x), so networks need nonlinearity to learn anything interesting. ReLU(x) = max(0,x) is the dominant choice: outputs 0 for negative inputs and x for positive. It creates folds and corners that let networks model nonlinear patterns. Other activations include Sigmoid σ(x)=1/(1+e^-x) squashing to [0,1], Tanh squashing to [-1,1], and Softmax normalizing a vector to probabilities summing to 1. ReLU dominates modern networks because it is simple and works well.
summary: Chapter overview of activation functions: ReLU plus alternatives, why nonlinearity matters between linear layers.

text: Beyond ReLU: the full menu. A layer is matrix multiplication: output = W · input. But layers alone are linear. To compute anything interesting, we need nonlinearity. That is where activation functions come in.

text: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit): ReLU(x) = max(0, x). If x is negative, output 0. If x is positive, output x. That is it. Examples: ReLU(-2.5) = max(0, -2.5) = 0. ReLU(1.3) = max(0, 1.3) = 1.3.
summary: ReLU formula max(0,x): zeros out negatives, passes positives unchanged.
queries: what does ReLU do to negative input

text: Why ReLU? It creates the bend. Multiple linear layers stacked together are still just one big linear function. ReLU breaks that. It creates folds and corners. That is how networks learn nonlinear patterns. Without activation: Layer1(x) = W1·x, Layer2(y) = W2·y. Combined: (W2·W1)·x → still linear! With ReLU: Layer1(x) = ReLU(W1·x), Layer2(y) = ReLU(W2·y). Combined: ReLU(W2·ReLU(W1·x)) → nonlinear!
summary: Without activation, stacked layers collapse into one linear map; ReLU inserts bends so deep stacks become genuinely nonlinear.

text: Other activation functions. Sigmoid: σ(x) = 1 / (1 + e^-x) - squashes output to [0, 1]. Tanh: tanh(x) = (e^x - e^-x) / (e^x + e^-x) - squashes to [-1, 1]. Softmax: normalizes a vector to probabilities summing to 1. ReLU dominates modern networks because it is simple and works well.
summary: Sigmoid maps to [0,1], Tanh to [-1,1], Softmax to a probability distribution; ReLU still dominates modern networks for simplicity.

text: This chapter explains what 'deep' means in deep learning: stacking many layers (50+ vs 1-2 in shallow nets). Each layer learns progressively more abstract features - early layers detect edges and colors, middle layers detect parts like eyes and ears, later layers detect whole objects like cat or dog. Depth matters for three reasons: composability (layers reuse and combine earlier features), efficiency (deep networks solve problems with fewer parameters than shallow ones), and expressiveness (some functions cannot be learned by shallow networks at all). The chapter also distinguishes training mode (weights update via backprop, dropout active) from inference mode (weights frozen, dropout off, no gradients).
summary: Overview of network depth: stacking layers builds hierarchical features, with depth providing composability, efficiency and expressiveness; also contrasts training vs inference modes.

text: This chapter Dropout: Training data is split into a training set the model learns from and a validation set held out as honest test. Training loss falls steadily; validation loss falls then rises - the rising gap is overfitting. Dropout fixes overfitting by randomly zeroing each neuron with probability p during training, so every forward pass uses a different random subnetwork. This forces redundancy - no neuron can be a lone specialist - producing distributed, robust representations. Math: apply a Bernoulli(1-p) mask, multiply element-wise, then scale survivors by 1/(1-p) (inverted dropout) so expected magnitude matches inference. At inference, dropout is off and all neurons run with original weights. Transformers place dropout after attention and after FFN with p=0.1.
summary: Dropout regularizes by randomly zeroing neurons during training and rescaling survivors, breaking co-adaptation while staying off at inference.

text: Before training, data is split into two groups. Training data: the model sees these examples, makes predictions, adjusts weights. Validation data: hidden during training, never seen by the model - the honest test. Typical split: 80% training (e.g., 800 examples), 20% validation (e.g., 200 examples), 1,000 total. Training loss = error measured on data the model trains on; always goes down with more training. Validation loss = error measured on data the model has NEVER seen; the real measure of learning. Training loss tells you how well the model memorizes. Validation loss tells you how well it generalizes to new, unseen data. The gap between them is everything.
summary: Training loss measures fit on data the model sees; validation loss measures generalization on held-out data, and the gap reveals overfitting.

The gap widens dramatically. Training: 0.02 - memorized everything perfectly. Validation: 2.80 - fails completely on new data. Like a student who memorizes every answer in the textbook but cannot solve a new problem on the exam.

text: The solution: during training, randomly set some neurons to zero. Every forward pass uses a different random subset of the network. In a 6-neuron layer with dropout rate p = 0.5, each neuron has a 50% chance of being zeroed out on each forward pass. Active neurons pass values through; dropped neurons output 0. Every training step sees a different random subset. Dropout rate p = 0.5 means drop 50%; p = 0.1 means drop 10%.
summary: Dropout randomly zeroes out neurons during training so each forward pass sees a different random subnetwork.
queries: what is dropout in neural networks
what is the dropout rate p
what does p=0.5 mean in dropout

text: Why dropout works: forced redundancy. No single neuron can become a 'lone specialist' that the network depends on entirely. Every neuron must be useful even when its neighbors are missing. Without dropout: neuron 3 memorizes a specific pattern; if neuron 3 fails, the network breaks. Knowledge is fragile and concentrated. With dropout: all neurons learn overlapping representations; any 3 of 6 can carry the signal. Knowledge is distributed and robust. Analogy: a team of 6 people where any 3 might be absent on any given day - everyone must learn everyone else's job. The team becomes resilient with no single point of failure.
summary: Dropout works by forcing redundancy so no single neuron becomes critical, distributing knowledge across overlapping representations.
queries: why don't neurons specialize too much
no single point of failure neural network

text: The actual math of dropout. Step 1: input vector, e.g., [0.8, 0.3, 0.5, 0.2, 0.7, 0.4]. Step 2: random Bernoulli mask with p = 0.5, e.g., [1, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]. Step 3: multiply element-wise to get [0.8, 0, 0.5, 0, 0.7, 0]. Step 4: scale by 1/(1 - p) = 1/0.5 = 2 to get [1.6, 0, 1.0, 0, 1.4, 0]. Why scale? Without scaling, the expected output is only 0.5 × original (half the neurons are zero). Scaling by 1/(1 - p) restores the expected value to match the original, so the network sees the same magnitude at training and inference. This is called inverted dropout.
summary: Inverted dropout multiplies activations by a random 0/1 Bernoulli mask then scales surviving values by 1/(1-p) to preserve expected magnitude.
queries: why is scaling needed in dropout

text: At inference, dropout is completely turned off. When the model is deployed and making predictions, all neurons are active, no mask is applied, no scaling is needed. All 6 of 6 neurons remain ACTIVE. The scaling during training (multiplying by 1/(1 - p)) already compensated for the missing neurons. So at inference, using all neurons with their original weights produces the correct expected output. This is called 'inverted dropout.'
summary: At inference, dropout is disabled and all neurons stay active because training-time scaling already compensated for the dropped units.
queries: why disable dropout at test time

text: Where dropout lives in a transformer block. Vertical flow: Input → Multi-Head Attention → DROPOUT (p = 0.1) → Add & Norm → Feed-Forward Network → DROPOUT (p = 0.1) → Add & Norm. Dropout is applied at two key locations inside each transformer block: after attention and after FFN, each with p = 0.1. Typical dropout rate in transformers: p = 0.1 (drop only 10%). This is much lower than the 0.5 used in earlier networks because transformers are already heavily regularized by their architecture.
summary: In a Transformer block, dropout with p=0.1 is applied after attention and after the FFN, just before each Add & Norm.
queries: why low dropout rate in transformers

text: Momentum formula diagram: m_t = β₁ · m_(t-1) + (1 - β₁) · g_t. Annotations label m as 'momentum', β₁ shown as 0.9, and g_t as 'gradient'. Shows the exponential moving average of gradients with β₁=0.9 weighting history at 90% and the new gradient at 10%.
summary: Diagram showing the momentum formula with annotations for the 0.9 history weight and current gradient.

text: Adaptive learning rate diagram: v_t = β₂ · v_(t-1) + (1 - β₂) · g_t². Annotations label v as 'velocity', β₂ shown as 0.999, and g_t² as 'squared grad'. Tracks average squared gradient magnitude per parameter so dividing by √v scales each parameter's step adaptively.
summary: Diagram showing the second-moment (velocity) formula with annotations for β₂=0.999 and squared gradient.

text: Bias correction formulas diagram: m-hat_t = m_t / (1 - β₁^t) and v-hat_t = v_t / (1 - β₂^t), labeled 'corrected momentum' and 'corrected velocity'. Compensates for zero-initialized running averages so early-step estimates are scaled up correctly.
summary: Diagram showing the m-hat and v-hat bias-correction formulas with their corrected-momentum and corrected-velocity labels.
queries: 1 minus beta to the t denominator visual

text: Full Adam update rule diagram: θ_(t+1) = θ_t - α · m-hat_t / (√v-hat_t + ε), with labels 'new weight', 'old weight', and 'learning rate'. Combines momentum direction, adaptive √v scaling, bias correction, and the epsilon stabilizer into a single per-parameter update.
summary: Diagram of the complete Adam update equation with labeled new weight, old weight, and learning rate components.
queries: new weight old weight learning rate diagram
m-hat over sqrt v-hat plus epsilon
Adam combine momentum and velocity diagram

text: AdamW update rule diagram: θ_(t+1) = θ_t - α · (m-hat_t / (√v-hat_t + ε) + λ · θ_t). Labels separate 'Adam step (unchanged)' from 'decay (separate)'. Shows decoupled weight decay applied directly to θ instead of being mixed into the gradient.
summary: Diagram of the AdamW update with decoupled weight decay shown as a separate λ·θ term added to the Adam step.
queries: how AdamW formula differs from Adam

text: This chapter builds Adam (and its variant AdamW) from scratch as the real optimizer used in modern deep learning. Plain SGD applies one learning rate to all parameters, but real networks have parameters with gradient magnitudes that differ by orders of magnitude (e.g., 170,000x). Adam fixes this with three pieces: (1) Momentum m = β₁·m + (1-β₁)·g (β₁=0.9) smooths noisy gradients via an exponential moving average. (2) Adaptive learning rates via v = β₂·v + (1-β₂)·g² (β₂=0.999) where dividing by √v gives each parameter its own effective step size. (3) Bias correction m-hat = m/(1-β₁ᵗ), v-hat = v/(1-β₂ᵗ) compensates for zero-initialized averages in early steps. Full update: θ = θ - α · m-hat / (√v-hat + ε), with α=0.001 and ε=1e-8. AdamW decouples weight decay (+ λ·θ applied outside the Adam step) for clean, uniform regularization. AdamW with β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e-8, λ in [0.01, 0.1] is the default optimizer for GPT, BERT, LLaMA, Gemini, and Claude.
summary: Chapter-level summary of how Adam combines momentum, adaptive rates, and bias correction, and how AdamW improves it with decoupled weight decay.
queries: why is AdamW the default optimizer
optimizer used by GPT BERT LLaMA
what does Adam stand for in deep learning

text: Plain SGD uses one learning rate for every parameter, but neural networks have millions of parameters with very different gradient scales. Parameter A (an embedding weight) sees huge gradients because many tokens flow through it: step = 0.001 x -50.0 = 0.05, which can overshoot the minimum. Parameter B (a deep layer bias) gets tiny gradients because the signal dilutes through many layers: step = 0.001 x -0.0003 = 0.0000003, barely any learning. Parameter A's step is 170,000x bigger than B's. Lowering lr to fix A starves B; raising it to fix B explodes A. SGD has no way to treat each parameter differently. Adam fixes this with two ideas: momentum (smoothing noisy gradients) and adaptive rates (per-parameter effective learning rate).
summary: Explains why a single SGD learning rate fails when parameters have wildly different gradient scales, motivating Adam.

text: Idea 1 - Momentum. Instead of using only the current gradient, keep a running average m (the first moment). Formula: m_t = β₁ · m_(t-1) + (1 - β₁) · g_t. Default β₁ = 0.9 means 90% of momentum comes from history, 10% from the current gradient. Think of a heavy ball rolling downhill - it does not change direction instantly. Concrete walkthrough across 4 gradient steps: t=1 g=+0.8 -> 0.9 x 0 + 0.1 x 0.8 = +0.080. t=2 g=+0.6 -> 0.9 x 0.08 + 0.1 x 0.6 = +0.132. t=3 g=-0.9 -> 0.9 x 0.132 + 0.1 x (-0.9) = +0.029. t=4 g=+0.7 -> 0.9 x 0.029 + 0.1 x 0.7 = +0.096. At step 3 the gradient flips to -0.9 but momentum barely moves, then recovers by step 4. Exponential moving average: gradient from 1 step ago weighted 0.9, 2 steps ago 0.81, 10 steps ago 0.35 - recent gradients matter most, old ones fade exponentially.
summary: Defines Adam's momentum term as an exponential moving average of gradients with β₁=0.9 and walks through 4 numerical steps.
queries: m_t equals beta1 m_(t-1) plus 1 minus beta1 g_t
what does m track in Adam
how does momentum react to gradient sign flip
weights of past gradients in EMA
Adam first moment vs second moment

text: Idea 2 - Adaptive Learning Rates. Track squared gradient magnitude per parameter, called v (the second moment). Formula: v_t = β₂ · v_(t-1) + (1 - β₂) · g_t². Default β₂ = 0.999. In the final update we divide by √v. Parameter A: typical gradient 50, v tracks 50² = 2500, divide by √2500 = 50, effective rate lr / 50 - big gradients get tamed automatically. Parameter B: typical gradient 0.01, v tracks 0.01² = 0.0001, divide by √0.0001 = 0.01, effective rate lr / 0.01 - small gradients get boosted automatically. Parameter A's effective learning rate is 5,000x smaller than Parameter B's. The same global learning rate α works for both - √v handles per-parameter scaling.
summary: Introduces Adam's second moment v as the per-parameter squared-gradient EMA that creates adaptive learning rates by dividing by √v.
queries: v_t equals beta2 v_(t-1) plus 1 minus beta2 g squared

text: Bias Correction - Fixing the Cold Start. Both m and v are initialized to zero, so early running averages are biased toward zero. At step 1 with β₁=0.9, m₁ = 0.9 x 0 + 0.1 x g₁ = 0.1 x g₁ - only 10% of the true gradient, our estimate is 10x too small. Correction formulas: m-hat_t = m_t / (1 - β₁^t), v-hat_t = v_t / (1 - β₂^t). How much it changes: at t=1, 1 - β₁¹ = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1, m-hat = m₁ / 0.1 = 10x boost - massive correction because m only captured 10% of the true value. At t=1000, 1 - 0.9¹⁰⁰⁰ ≈ 1 - 0 = 1.0, m-hat = m / 1.0 = no change - correction vanishes. Same logic for v-hat with β₂=0.999: at t=1 correction is 1,000x; by t=5000 it is gone. Bias correction is only significant during the first few hundred steps.
summary: Explains why m and v need bias correction in early steps and shows the dividing-by-(1-β^t) formula collapses to no-op late in training.

text: The Full Adam Update Rule: θ_(t+1) = θ_t - α · m-hat_t / (√v-hat_t + ε). Symbols: θ is the parameter value (single weight or bias); α is learning rate (typically 0.001); m-hat is bias-corrected momentum - which direction to go; v-hat is bias-corrected velocity - how much to scale the step; ε is a tiny constant 1e-8 to prevent division by zero. Full numerical walkthrough at t=1 with θ=0.500, g₁=0.4, α=0.001: Step 1 m₁ = 0.9 x 0 + 0.1 x 0.4 = 0.04. Step 2 v₁ = 0.999 x 0 + 0.001 x 0.4² = 0.001 x 0.16 = 0.00016. Step 3 m-hat = 0.04 / (1 - 0.9) = 0.04 / 0.1 = 0.4. Step 4 v-hat = 0.00016 / (1 - 0.999) = 0.00016 / 0.001 = 0.16. Step 5 update = 0.001 x 0.4 / (√0.16 + 1e-8) = 0.001 x 0.4 / 0.4 = 0.001. θ₂ = 0.500 - 0.001 = 0.499. At t=1 with bias correction, the effective step is almost exactly α regardless of gradient magnitude - the step size is bounded near α while direction comes from momentum.
summary: Combines momentum, velocity, and bias correction into the full Adam update formula and walks through every arithmetic step for one parameter.
queries: theta t+1 equals theta t minus alpha times m-hat over sqrt v-hat

text: AdamW - Weight Decay Done Right. Weight decay is regularization that shrinks all weights slightly each step. Original Adam mixed weight decay into the gradient (L2 regularization), but Adam divides by √v, which includes the decay term. Parameters with large v barely feel decay; parameters with small v feel too much - decay is not applied equally. AdamW formula: θ_(t+1) = θ_t - α · (m-hat_t / (√v-hat_t + ε) + λ · θ_t). L2 in Adam: decay added to gradient then divided by √v - parameters with large v feel almost no decay - strength varies per parameter, unintended. AdamW: decay applied directly to θ, outside Adam - every parameter gets the same decay rate λ - clean separation, uniform as intended. GPT, BERT, LLaMA, Gemini, Claude all use AdamW with β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e-8, λ between 0.01 and 0.1. The 2017 paper by Loshchilov and Hutter showed decoupled weight decay improves generalization. Complete recipe: 1) compute gradient g; 2) m = β₁·m + (1-β₁)·g; 3) v = β₂·v + (1-β₂)·g²; 4) m-hat = m/(1-β₁ᵗ), v-hat = v/(1-β₂ᵗ); 5) θ = θ - α·(m-hat/(√v-hat+ε) + λ·θ).
summary: Contrasts L2-in-Adam against AdamW's decoupled weight decay and gives the canonical hyperparameters and 5-step recipe used by modern transformers.

Points show ramp from 0 to 0.001 by step 2000, then cosine decay through 0.000927 (step 3000), 0.000691 (step 5000), 0.0005 (step 6000), 0.000146 (step 8000), down to 0 at step 10000. Color: green. text: Learning Rate Warmup & Decay: a constant learning rate fails because optimal LR changes during training. Solution: schedule the LR. First, warmup linearly from 0 to a peak value over ~2000 steps so noisy initial gradients don't destabilize training. Then apply cosine decay using lr_min + 0.5*(lr_max-lr_min)*(1+cos(pi*step/total)) to smoothly reduce LR to the minimum. GPT-3 used this exact pattern: 2000-step warmup to peak 6e-4, cosine decay to 6e-5 (10% of peak) over 300B tokens. Cosine beats linear because it spends more time at useful intermediate rates.
summary: LR scheduling combines linear warmup and cosine decay; modern LLMs like GPT-3 use this to stabilize training and converge well.
queries: what is learning rate warmup and decay
how to set learning rate over time

text: Why a constant learning rate fails: no single learning rate works well for the entire training process. Too high and you diverge. Too low and you never converge. Concrete examples: lr = 0.01 causes loss to explode to NaN after a few steps. lr = 0.0001 makes training crawl, still not converged after 100K steps. lr = 0.001 works OK but still not optimal for the whole run. The best learning rate changes over the course of training. Early on you need small steps. In the middle, larger steps. Near the end, small steps again.
summary: Constant learning rate fails because optimal LR changes during training - early needs small, middle needs larger, end needs small.

does optimal learning rate change during training
why need a learning rate schedule
best lr for early vs late training
what lr to use for whole training

text: Warmup - start tiny, ramp up. At step 1, weights are random garbage. Gradients are noisy and unreliable. A large learning rate plus bad gradients equals immediate divergence. Linear warmup over 2000 steps: step 0 has lr = 0 (no updates yet), step 1000 has lr = 0.0005 (halfway to peak), step 2000 has lr = 0.001 (peak reached). Small LR lets the model find its footing before taking bigger steps. The gradients stabilize as the weights move toward a reasonable region of the loss landscape.
summary: Warmup linearly ramps learning rate from zero to peak over the first ~2000 steps so noisy initial gradients don't blow up training.

text: Cosine decay - smooth descent. After warmup, smoothly decrease the LR following a cosine curve. The formula: lr = lr_min + 0.5 x (lr_max - lr_min) x (1 + cos(pi x step / total)). Warmup phase steps 0-2000: linear ramp from 0 to peak. Decay phase steps 2000+: cosine curve gently reduces to minimum. Combined warmup + cosine decay schedule peaks at 0.001 around step 2000 then decays smoothly to zero by step 10000.
summary: After warmup peak, cosine decay smoothly reduces LR following lr_min + 0.5*(lr_max-lr_min)*(1+cos(pi*step/total)).

text: Real-world schedule: GPT-3. Actual schedule used to train GPT-3 (175 billion parameters): Warmup over 375M tokens (~2,000 steps). Peak LR = 6 x 10^-4 (0.0006). Decay over 300B tokens using cosine schedule. Min LR = 6 x 10^-5 (10% of peak). Why cosine and not linear decay? Cosine spends more time at useful learning rates (the middle range) and approaches zero very gradually. Linear decay spends equal time at every LR, wasting steps at near-zero rates where no meaningful learning occurs.
summary: GPT-3 used 2000-step warmup to peak LR 6e-4, then cosine decay to 6e-5 over 300B tokens; cosine beats linear by spending more time at useful rates.
queries: what learning rate did GPT-3 use
why cosine instead of linear decay

text: Diagram - All Weights = 0: The Symmetry Problem. Shows an input box with [1.0, 0.5] connected to three neurons n1, n2, n3, each with w=[0,0]. All three neurons output 0.0. Arrows lead from all three identical outputs to the label 'All identical!'. Bottom caption: 'Same weights, same outputs, same gradients, stuck forever'. The diagram visualizes why zero initialization makes every neuron in a layer compute the exact same value, leaving the network unable to differentiate neurons during training.
summary: Diagram illustrating the symmetry problem - three neurons with all-zero weights produce identical outputs and the network is stuck forever.
queries: n1 n2 n3 same output diagram

text: Weight Initialization - How Random? Weights cannot start at zero (symmetry problem - all neurons identical), too small (signal vanishes through layers), or too large (signal explodes to NaN). The solution is careful scaled randomness. Xavier/Glorot uses std = sqrt(2 / (fan_in + fan_out)) to preserve variance across layers. He initialization multiplies by sqrt(2) to compensate for ReLU killing half the activations: std = sqrt(2 / fan_in). Modern networks use He for ReLU; transformers often scale by 1/sqrt(depth) for residual connections. Good initialization keeps signal variance near 1.0 through many layers, preventing both vanishing and exploding signals.
summary: Chapter overview: weight initialization must avoid zero (symmetry), too-small (vanishing), and too-large (exploding) - Xavier and He scale randomness mathematically to keep variance stable.
queries: how should I initialize neural network weights
what is the best weight initialization

text: Why not start weights at zero? If all weights start at zero, every neuron in a layer computes the exact same output. All gradients are identical. No neuron ever becomes different from any other. The network is stuck forever. This is the symmetry problem: every neuron is a clone of every other. With input [1.0, 0.5] and w=[0,0] for neurons n1, n2, n3 - all outputs equal 0.0, all identical, same gradients. No matter how long you train, they will never differentiate.
summary: Initializing all weights to zero causes the symmetry problem where every neuron stays identical and the network cannot learn.
queries: why can't I initialize weights to zero
what is the symmetry problem in neural networks
what happens if all weights start at 0
why must weights be random at start
why do networks need random initialization
what breaks symmetry in neural nets

text: Too small initialization: if weights start with very small random values (std = 0.001), numbers shrink toward zero at every layer. Each layer multiplies the previous output by tiny weights, so values get exponentially smaller. Input [1.0, 0.5] with magnitude 1.0. After Layer 1: [0.002, -0.001] magnitude 0.002. After Layer 3: [~0.000004, ~0.000002]. After Layer 5: [~0, ~0]. By layer 5-10, every value is effectively zero. Gradients also vanish - the network cannot learn. The network is dead.
summary: Initializing weights too small causes signals and gradients to shrink exponentially through layers, making the network unable to learn.
queries: what happens if weights are too small
why do tiny weights kill networks
how small is too small for weight init
why does signal disappear in deep nets

text: Too large initialization: if weights start with large random values (std = 5.0), numbers grow uncontrollably at every layer. Input [1.0, 0.5]. After Layer 1: [8.5, -12.3]. After Layer 3: [~450, ~-820]. After Layer 5: [~45000, ~-89000]. The values overflow, activations saturate, gradients explode, and training produces NaN within a few steps. The network is unstable from the very first forward pass.
summary: Initializing weights too large causes activations to grow exponentially, producing NaN and unstable training within a few steps.
queries: what happens if weights are too large
why do large weights cause instability
how large is too large for init

text: Xavier/Glorot initialization: scale random weights so the variance of the output matches the variance of the input. The key: divide by the square root of the number of inputs (fan_in). Formula: std = sqrt(2 / (fan_in + fan_out)). Example for a 512 to 512 layer: std = sqrt(2 / (512 + 512)) = sqrt(2 / 1024) = sqrt(0.00195) = 0.044. With this scale, signal variance stays approximately 1.0 through many layers: Layer 1 ~1.0, Layer 3 ~0.98, Layer 5 ~0.95, Layer 10 ~0.90. Stable signal - no vanishing, no exploding. Named after Xavier Glorot, who proved this mathematically in 2010.
summary: Xavier/Glorot initialization uses std = sqrt(2 / (fan_in + fan_out)) to keep signal variance stable across layers.
queries: std sqrt 2 over fan_in plus fan_out
how to keep signal variance constant
what is the right weight init scale

text: He initialization: designed for ReLU. ReLU sets all negative values to zero, killing roughly half the neurons' outputs. This halves the variance at each layer. To compensate, multiply Xavier's scale by sqrt(2). Formula: std = sqrt(2 / fan_in). Example for 512 inputs: std = sqrt(2 / 512) = sqrt(0.00391) = 0.0625. Compare Xavier 0.044 - He is about 42% larger to compensate for ReLU's halving. Xavier + ReLU: variance shrinks (L1=1.0, L5=0.5, L10=0.25). He + ReLU: variance stable (L1=1.0, L5=0.98, L10=0.95). Named after Kaiming He (2015). Most modern networks with ReLU use He initialization. Transformers often use a variant scaled by 1/sqrt(depth) for residual connections.
summary: He initialization uses std = sqrt(2 / fan_in) to compensate for ReLU killing half the activations, keeping variance stable in ReLU networks.
queries: modern weight init for deep networks
why does ReLU need different init

text: Tokenization converts text into numeric IDs that a neural network can consume. Three strategies exist: character-level (too granular), word-level (huge vocab and out-of-vocabulary failures), and sub-word like BPE (the sweet spot at ~50K tokens). BPE starts with characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build its vocabulary. Real GPT models tokenize 'The cat sat on the mat last week' into IDs like [464, 3797, 2906, 319, 262, 4416, 1297, 1285], where each ID indexes a 768-dim learned embedding fed into the model.
summary: Chapter-level overview of tokenization strategies, BPE training, and how token IDs feed into embeddings.

text: Computers only understand numbers. Text is useless to an AI - no matter how eloquent. The first step is to convert 'I love cats' into [0, 1, 2]. This is Tokenization: slicing text into chunks and assigning each a number from a vocabulary.

text: Three tokenization strategies exist. Character-level: 'love' -> ['l','o','v','e'] -> [108, 111, 118, 101]. Pros: works for any word, new letters rare. Cons: way too granular, sequences become huge. Word-level: 'unhappy' -> ['unhappy'] -> [5942]. Pros: human intuitive. Cons: 50K+ vocab needed, new words crash the model. Sub-word (BPE): 'unhappy' -> ['un','##happy'] -> [100, 6420]. Pros: sweet spot, ~50K tokens covers everything. Cons: need to learn splits first.
summary: Compares character, word, and sub-word tokenization strategies with tradeoffs.

text: Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) - the gold standard. BPE learns which character pairs appear together most. It starts with characters, then merges the most common pairs repeatedly. Step 0: split 'I love cats' into characters [I, _, l, o, v, e, _, c, a, t, s]. Step 1: count adjacent pairs: l+o=1, o+v=1, v+e=1, c+a=1, a+t=1, t+s=1. Step 2: merge most common pair (all tied at 1, pick l+o -> lo). Step 3: repeat merging. Recount pairs. lo+v is highest. Merge -> lov. Then lov+e -> love. Then c+a+t+s -> cats. Final tokens with IDs: I=0, space=1, love=2, space=1, cats=3. The sequence [0, 1, 2, 1, 3] is what gets fed to the model.
summary: Walks through BPE step by step on 'I love cats', merging frequent pairs into tokens with IDs.

text: Real example: GPT tokenizer. Modern tokenizers like GPT-2 use learned vocabularies of ~50,000 tokens. The sentence 'The cat sat on the mat last week' becomes: The=464, cat=3797, sat=2906, on=319, the=262, mat=4416, last=1297, week=1285. Sequence: [464, 3797, 2906, 319, 262, 4416, 1297, 1285]. Each token ID points to a learned embedding (a 768-dimensional vector in GPT-3). The model never sees text - only numbers.
summary: Real GPT tokenizer mapping a sentence to token IDs that index 768-dim learned embeddings.

text: Self-supervised learning is how GPT pretrains: it learns from raw text without labels by predicting the next token. One sentence becomes many (input, target) training pairs. The model outputs a probability distribution over the vocabulary, cross-entropy loss measures the gap (Loss = -log(P_correct)), and gradient descent adjusts weights across billions of examples. Through next-token prediction alone, the model learns grammar, facts, reasoning, and style.
summary: This chapter overview: GPT pretrains via self-supervised next-token prediction with cross-entropy loss on billions of free training examples.

text: Example: 'The cat sat on the mat'. One sentence becomes FIVE training examples. Each one says: given these words, the next word is... Input 'The' -> target 'cat' [3797]. Input 'The cat' -> target 'sat' [2906]. Input 'The cat sat' -> target 'on' [319]. Input 'The cat sat on' -> target 'the' [262]. Input 'The cat sat on the' -> target 'mat' [4416]. From a tiny 8-word sentence we get 5 (input, target) pairs. From 1 billion words we get 1 billion training examples. For FREE.

Distribution: 'the' = 0.35 (correct, 35%), 'cat' = 0.15, 'mat' = 0.12, 'on' = 0.10, all others = 0.28. The model predicted 'the' with 35% confidence. The correct answer is 'the'. Good! But not perfect - 35% is not 100%.

text: Loss: how wrong was the prediction? We use cross-entropy loss to measure the gap. For this example: Loss = -log(0.35) = 1.05. Higher probability -> lower loss. If we predicted 1.0 (100%), loss = 0. If 0.1 (10%), loss approximately 2.3. Examples: P=1.0 -> L=0.0 (perfect). P=0.5 -> L=0.69 (so-so). P=0.35 -> L=1.05 (our example). P=0.1 -> L=2.3 (bad).
summary: Introduces cross-entropy loss formula Loss = -log(P_correct) with worked numerical examples showing how loss grows as confidence drops.

text: Why self-supervised works. The model learns to predict next words by trial and error on BILLIONS of examples. Through this alone, it learns: Grammar - predicting next word requires understanding subject-verb agreement and tenses. Facts - to predict 'The capital of India is ___', it must memorize that Delhi is the answer. Reasoning - to predict the output of a math problem, it must perform the computation internally. Style transfer - after SFT the model uses this learned knowledge to follow human instructions.
summary: Explains why next-token prediction alone teaches the model grammar, facts, reasoning, and style by forcing internal understanding to make accurate predictions.

text: During training: gradient descent. After each batch of examples, the model computes loss, then uses backprop to adjust weights to reduce future loss. Iteration 1: avg loss on batch = 4.2 -> adjust weights. Iteration 2: avg loss = 3.8 -> adjust weights. Iteration 3: avg loss = 3.1 -> adjust weights. ... After 1 trillion tokens: avg loss on batch = 0.8. The model gets better at predicting. This is 'pretraining' - GPT-3 trained for 300+ billion tokens.
summary: Shows the iterative gradient descent training loop where average batch loss steadily decreases from 4.2 down to 0.8 over a trillion tokens of pretraining.

Each piece is annotated: 'Loss' on the left equals; the negative sign flips the log; log_e is natural log; P_correct (with 'correct' subscript) is the probability the model assigned to the actual next word from the training data.

text: Plotted negative log probability curve: y-axis is Loss (-log P) from 0 to ~4.6, x-axis is probability assigned to correct word from 0.0 to 1.0. The curve drops steeply near P=0 and flattens toward P=1. Annotated points: P=0.95 confident & right (loss ~0.05, green); P=0.50 coin flip (loss ~0.69, yellow); P=0.10 wrong guess (loss ~2.30, orange); P=0.01 very wrong (loss ~4.60, red). The shape demonstrates the exponential penalty for low confidence and the tiny penalty for high confidence.
summary: Diagram of the -log(P) loss curve with four annotated points spanning very wrong to confident & right.

text: Cross-entropy loss is how every LLM grades its own predictions. The formula Loss = -log_e(P_correct) takes the probability the model assigned to the actually correct next word (which comes from the training data, not the formula) and flips its log. The -log curve is steep at low probability (massive penalty for being confidently wrong) and flat near 1 (tiny penalty for being right). Cross-entropy is preferred over MSE because it punishes confident mistakes properly. During training, per-example losses are averaged across a batch to give one number that drives weight updates.
summary: Chapter recap of the cross-entropy loss formula, its shape, why it beats MSE, and how batch averaging plugs into training.
queries: why cross-entropy and where labels come from

main points about loss in language modeling

text: How do we grade the model? The model predicts probabilities for every word in the vocabulary. We need to turn that into one number that says how good the predictions are. A loss function is a single score that tells us how wrong the model was. Lower loss equals better predictions. The loss function used by every LLM is called cross-entropy loss.
summary: Introduces the need for a single loss number to grade an LLM's vocabulary-wide probability output, motivating cross-entropy loss.
queries: what is a loss function for an LLM
how do we score language model predictions
how do we measure how wrong a model is
single number to grade model output
why not just compare predicted word vs actual word
what does loss mean in deep learning
why is loss needed during training
how does training know the model is improving

text: The formula: Loss = -log_e(P_correct). In plain English: take the natural log of how confident the model was in the right answer, then flip the sign. P_correct is the probability the model assigned to the actual next word; the negative sign flips it. The shape of -log(P): on a graph of loss vs probability, the curve is steep on the left (low probability gives massive penalty) and flat on the right (high probability gives tiny penalty). Annotated points: P=0.95 (confident & right) gives loss ~0.05; P=0.50 (coin flip) gives ~0.69; P=0.10 (wrong guess) gives ~2.30; P=0.01 (very wrong) gives ~4.60. Where does 'correct' come from? Not from inside the formula - from the training data. During training, the model sees real text like 'The cat sat on the mat'. We already know the next word is 'mat' because it is right there in the sentence. The model outputs 50,000 probabilities (one per word). We reach into that list, pull out the one probability assigned to 'mat', and plug THAT into the formula. Worked example: model output {the: 2%, cat: 5%, mat: 62%, dog: 1%, ...}. Correct answer (from training data): 'mat'. So P_correct = P('mat') = 0.62. Loss = -ln(0.62) = 0.48. The 'comparison' between actual and expected is implicit: we only look at the probability the model gave to the known correct word. There is no subtraction. The entire comparison is: how confident were you in the right answer? Tied to the LLM context: model gives 'mat' P=0.95 -> Loss 0.05, almost zero, barely any weight adjustment. Model gives 'mat' P=0.50 -> Loss 0.69, weights nudged. Model gives 'mat' P=0.01 -> Loss 4.60, massive correction. The model gets punished harshly for being confidently wrong, but barely punished when already doing well.
summary: Presents Loss = -log(P_correct), shows the -log curve's shape, and explains that the correct word comes from training data with a worked 'mat' example.

text: Why -log? The intuition. Imagine you're a weather forecaster. You say '70% chance of rain'. If it rains, you did decent (70% is not 100%, but ok). If it doesn't rain, you were wrong (you said 70%). Loss measures your mistake: Loss = -log(your prediction for what actually happened). Examples: You said 'It WILL rain (P=0.95)', actual IT DID RAIN, loss = 0.05, grade A+. You said 'Maybe rain (P=0.50)', actual it rained, loss = 0.69, grade C. You said 'Probably dry (P=0.10)', actual it rained, loss = 2.30, grade F.
summary: Uses a weather forecaster analogy to give intuition for why -log of the assigned probability measures prediction error.

text: Cross-entropy formula in detail. When predicting one token from a 50K vocabulary: Loss = -log_e(P_correct), where P_correct is between 0 and 1 (it's a probability). Concrete table: P=0.99 -> L=0.01; P=0.90 -> L=0.10; P=0.50 -> L=0.69; P=0.10 -> L=2.30; P=0.01 -> L=4.60. As probability increases, loss approaches zero. As probability drops, loss explodes. This penalizes confident wrong answers.
summary: Restates the loss formula and shows a P-to-loss table illustrating how loss explodes as the assigned probability drops.
queries: what's the loss for a 50 percent prediction
bar chart of loss vs probability

text: Why cross-entropy, not MSE? MSE (Mean Squared Error) is the default loss for predicting numbers - like predicting house prices. But language models predict probabilities over a vocabulary. MSE Loss formula: (P_pred - P_true)^2. Example: P_pred=0.1, P_true=1.0 -> (0.1-1.0)^2 = 0.81. Problem: treats small and large errors equally and doesn't penalize confidence enough. Cross-Entropy Loss formula: -log(P_correct). Example: P_pred=0.1 when true=1.0 -> -log(0.1) = 2.30. It heavily punishes confident mistakes - which is what we want.
summary: Contrasts MSE with cross-entropy and shows why cross-entropy's stronger penalty for confidently wrong probabilities makes it the right loss for LLMs.
queries: why not MSE for language models
why does MSE fail for probabilities
house price loss vs token prediction loss
why log loss for softmax outputs

text: Batch loss: averaging across examples. During training, we compute loss on a BATCH of examples, then take the mean. Example 1: Predict 'sat' -> model says P=0.4 -> loss = 0.92. Example 2: Predict 'on' -> model says P=0.8 -> loss = 0.22. Example 3: Predict 'mat' -> model says P=0.1 -> loss = 2.30. Batch Loss = (0.92 + 0.22 + 2.30) / 3 = 1.15. The model updates weights to reduce this batch loss. Then the next batch is loaded and loss is recomputed.
summary: Shows that during training, per-example losses are averaged into a single batch loss that drives weight updates.

text: How the 'mat' output circle computes its score (logit). Values arriving from previous layer: [2.1, -0.8, 1.5, 0.3, -1.2, 0.9, 1.7, -0.4]. Weights for 'mat' circle: [0.6, 0.3, 0.8, 0.1, -0.5, 0.2, 0.9, 0.4]. Score for 'mat' = 4.40. Same operation runs for all 20 output circles, each with its own weights.

text: Softmax conversion from logits to probabilities. Two steps: raise e (2.718) to the power of each score, then divide each by the total. Formula: probability = e^score / sum(e^score) across all 20 words. Example computations: 'the' score=+4.2 → e^4.2 ≈ 66.69, 'mat' score=+3.8 → e^3.8 ≈ 44.70, 'a' score=+2.1 → e^2.1 ≈ 8.17, etc. Sum of all e^score = EXP_SUM. Each probability = e^score / EXP_SUM. Result: every probability positive, all sum to 100%.
summary: Converts raw logits into a probability distribution using exponentiation then normalization (softmax).

text: Key insight: the entire pipeline is just multiply and add. (1) Take numbers in: word 'on' becomes [0, 0, 0, 1, ...] or an embedding. (2) Multiply by weights, add up, apply activation - each circle does this. (3) Repeat through every layer (GPT-2 has 12 layers, GPT-4 has ~100+). (4) Last layer has one circle per vocabulary word (20 for us, 50,000 for real GPT). (5) Softmax to get probabilities. The only 'smart' part is the weights - the numbers on each connection line. Training (gradient descent) adjusts those weights trillions of times until multiply-and-add produces good predictions.
summary: Wraps up the chapter: a language model is just stacked multiply-add layers; intelligence lives entirely in the learned weights.

chapterTitle: The Output Layer - From Hidden State to Words
text: This chapter explains the output layer of an LLM: how a hidden state (768 numbers from the final transformer layer) becomes a probability distribution over 50,000 vocabulary words. It walks through what a hidden state is, what logits (raw scores) are, how the unembedding matrix multiplies the hidden state to produce one logit per vocabulary word via dot products, how softmax converts logits to probabilities (e^logit / sum of all e^logits), why a single linear layer suffices (hidden layers did the hard work), and the weight tying trick where the unembedding matrix is the embedding matrix transposed so similar words at input get similar logits at output.
summary: End-to-end walkthrough of the LLM output layer: hidden state, logits, unembedding matrix, softmax, parameter count, and weight tying.
