text: Each head gets its own W_Q, W_K, W_V. 8 heads equals 8 independent sets of weight matrices. Each set is smaller: 512x64 instead of 512x512. Heads 1 through 8 each have W_Q W_K W_V at 512x64 each. Same embedding goes into all 8 heads. Each head's different W matrices produce a different Query arrow - pointing in a different direction, asking a different question.
summary: Each of the 8 heads has its own smaller 512x64 W_Q, W_K, W_V matrices that project the same embedding into different Query directions.

text: 8 arrows, 8 questions, nothing lost. For 'sat' in 'The cat sat on the mat last week' each head's Query arrow points differently. Head 1 asks 'who did the action?' finds cat at 70%. Head 2 asks 'where?' finds mat at 65%. Head 3 asks 'when?' finds week at 55%. Head 4 asks 'what comes next?' finds on at 60%. Heads 5-8 ask about grammar, coreference, proximity, etc. Each head specializes. Together they capture everything - no information lost.

text: Now zoom out: layers. A Transformer doesn't have just one set of 8 heads. It stacks multiple layers, each with its own 8 heads. Layer 1: word proximity, basic grammar. Layer 2: syntax, clause structure. Layer 3: meaning, sentiment. ... up to Layer 96: reasoning, generation. 96 layers x 8 heads = 768 attention patterns. Each layer reads the previous layer's output. Early layers see raw words. Later layers see increasingly abstract, context-rich representations. Nobody told the model which layer should handle grammar or reasoning. It organized itself this way during training.
summary: Transformers stack many layers each with 8 heads, with early layers learning grammar and later layers learning reasoning, totaling 96x8 = 768 attention patterns.

chapterTitle: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims
text: Inside each of the 8 attention heads, the full Steps 1-5 attention algorithm runs in 64 dimensions instead of 512: compute Q, K, V via head-specific W matrices, score = Q·Kᵀ/√64, softmax, weighted sum of V. Because each head has different W_Q/W_K/W_V, each produces different attention patterns - one head learns subject-to-verb, another verb-to-location, another temporal relations - so the model captures relationships a single head would miss.
summary: This chapter shows that each of 8 heads runs the full attention computation in 64 dims and learns distinct relationship patterns through its own W matrices.

chapterTitle: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims
text:
Each head runs the FULL attention algorithm. Exactly Steps 1-5 we learned, but in 64 dims instead of 512.

For Head 1:
  Q₁ = embedding × W_Q₁  [3 words × 64 dims]
  K₁ = embedding × W_K₁  [3 words × 64 dims]
  V₁ = embedding × W_V₁  [3 words × 64 dims]

  scores₁ = Q₁ · K₁ᵀ / √64  [3×3 score matrix]
  weights₁ = softmax(scores₁)  [3×3 attention weights]
  output₁ = weights₁ × V₁  [3 words × 64 dims]

Same runs for all 8 heads. Each produces [3 words × 64 dims].
summary: Each of the 8 attention heads runs the full Step 1-5 attention algorithm independently in 64 dimensions, producing a [3 words × 64 dims] output.

chapterTitle: Inside Each Head - Full Attention in 64 Dims
text:
The KEY: each head's attention weights are different, because their W_Q, W_K, W_V are different. Here is what 'sat' sees in each head:

Head 1 - Subject to Verb: cat 70%, mat 8%, week 5%, others 17%.
Head 2 - Verb to Location: cat 10%, mat 65%, week 8%, others 17%.
Head 3 - Temporal: cat 8%, mat 7%, week 55%, others 30%.

Each head found what the single head missed. Head 3 got 'last week' at 55% - single head only gave it 5%.

text: Concatenate + W_O is the final step of multi-head attention. The 8 heads each output a separate vector slice for every word, which are concatenated into one long 512-number list. Concatenation alone leaves each head's slice isolated like sealed envelopes. W_O, a learned 512×512 weight matrix (262,144 parameters), multiplies the concatenated vector so every output dimension becomes a blend of all heads' findings. W_O works like W_Q/W_K/W_V mechanically but its job is to fuse head outputs. Its weights start random and are tuned by backpropagation. Different transformer layers learn different W_O matrices specializing in syntax, semantics, or reasoning. After W_O, the word "love" becomes a single integrated context-aware vector carrying subject, object, and sentiment together.
summary: Multi-head outputs are concatenated then mixed by the learned W_O matrix so every output dimension blends information from all heads.

text: After the 8 heads finish, each word has 8 separate results. For "love": Head 1 gave [0.3, 0.5] (figured out "I" is the one who loves), Head 2 gave [-0.2, 0.8] (figured out "cats" is what's loved), Head 3 gave [0.7, -0.1] (figured out the sentiment is positive), ... 8 results total. We stick them end-to-end (concatenate): [0.3, 0.5, -0.2, 0.8, 0.7, -0.1, ..., 0.1, 0.4]. One long list - 512 numbers. But there's a problem.
summary: Each of 8 heads produces its own output vector for a word, which are concatenated end-to-end into one 512-number list.

text: The problem - these results are like 8 sealed envelopes. 8 detectives investigated the same crime. Each wrote findings and sealed them: Head 1 "suspect is male, 30s", Head 2 "happened at the park", Head 3 "Tuesday evening", Head 4 "weapon was a knife". Tape them together (concatenation) with thick black walls between each section - they can't see each other. They're physically side by side, but each section is still sealed. H1's numbers have no idea what H2 found. H3 can't see H4. They're taped together but not talking. Boss asks: "one-line summary?" No envelope has the full picture. H1 only knows the suspect. H2 only knows the location. Nobody can combine them. We need someone to open all the envelopes, read everything, and write one combined summary.
summary: Concatenation alone leaves each head's findings sealed off from the others, so no dimension carries combined knowledge across heads.

problem with side by side head outputs

text: W_O - the person who opens all the envelopes. W_O is just another weight matrix - a grid of numbers, same as W_Q, W_K, W_V. Multiply input by grid, get output. Nothing new in how it works. But what it does is special. When you multiply the concatenated list by W_O, every number in the output is computed from all numbers in the input. Before W_O: Position 1 = 0.3 (only knows Head 1's finding). After W_O: Position 1 = blend of 0.3 + (-0.2) + 0.7 + ... all positions = knows Head 1 + Head 2 + Head 3 + ... everything. It's like the combined summary: "A male suspect in his 30s used a knife at the park on Tuesday evening." One sentence that contains information from all envelopes. After W_O, every single position in the output carries a mix of what all 8 heads found. The envelopes have been opened, read, and combined.
summary: W_O is a weight matrix that mixes the concatenated head outputs so every output dimension blends information from all 8 heads.
queries: what is W_O in multi-head attention

text: Where did W_O's numbers come from? Same exact process as all other grids: 1. Start with random numbers. 2. Model tries to predict next word, gets it wrong. 3. Error flows backward, nudges each number in W_O. 4. "If this number were 0.31 instead of 0.30, the blending would have been better → change to 0.31". 5. Repeat billions of times. 6. W_O settles into numbers that produce good blends. The training discovers the best way to combine the 8 heads' findings into one useful output. Nobody programs this - it emerges from training.
summary: W_O's weights start random and are tuned by backpropagation over billions of prediction errors until they produce useful head blends.

text: Back to "I love cats" - what W_O does for "love": After 8 heads run, "love"'s concatenated vector has: Dims 1–64: Head 1 found "I" is the subject who loves; Dims 65–128: Head 2 found "cats" is the object being loved; Dims 129–192: Head 3 found this is a positive sentiment. Without W_O: each section is isolated. Dim 1 has no idea "cats" is involved. After W_O: every dimension of "love"'s output encodes the combined meaning: "affection from 'I' directed at 'cats', with positive sentiment." One rich, integrated vector. This is what we set out to do in chapter 1 - transform "love" from an isolated word into a context-aware representation.
summary: Worked example showing how W_O fuses the dim slices from 3 heads into a single context-aware vector for the word "love".

text: W_O is LEARNED - not hardcoded. The model discovers during training which combinations of head outputs are useful. Different layers learn different W_O matrices: Layer 1's W_O might focus on combining basic syntax patterns; Layer 3's W_O might focus on combining semantic relationships; Layer 6's W_O might focus on combining abstract reasoning. Nobody programs what to combine. W_O's 512×512 = 262,144 weights are all learned through backpropagation - the same process from earlier backprop chapters.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: This chapter explains the choice of 8 attention heads as a tradeoff between head count and per-head dimensions, computes the parameter count of ~1 million per attention layer across W_Q, W_K, W_V, and W_O, shows multi-head uses the same budget as single-head but reorganized, and scales this view from the original 6-layer Transformer through GPT-2 (48 layers) to GPT-3 (96 layers, 175B params).

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: Why 8 heads, why not 4 or 16? Tradeoff: total 512 dims divided equally among heads. 16 heads = 512 / 16 = 32 dims each, more pattern types but each head weaker. 4 heads = 512 / 4 = 128 dims each, each head powerful but fewer patterns. 8 heads x 64 dims = sweet spot: enough capacity per head (64 dims) and enough perspectives (8 types).
summary: Explains 8 heads as tradeoff sweet spot between head capacity and pattern diversity.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: Parameter count per head: W_Q (512x64) + W_K (512x64) + W_V (512x64) = 3 x 32,768 = 98,304. Across 8 heads: W_Q = 32,768 x 8 = 262,144; W_K = 32,768 x 8 = 262,144; W_V = 32,768 x 8 = 262,144; W_O = 512 x 512 = 262,144. Total: 1,048,576, about 1 million parameters per attention layer.
summary: Computes total attention parameters: ~1 million per layer across W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: Surprising: same count as single-head. Single-head with 512-dim Q, K, V needs 512 x 512 per matrix = same total. Multi-head doesn't add parameters - it reorganizes them into 8 independent groups. Same budget, much better results.
summary: Multi-head attention uses same parameter budget as single-head but reorganized into independent groups.

chapterTitle: Why 8 Heads? Parameter Count & Big Picture
text: How does this scale in a full model? One attention layer = ~1 million parameters. Transformers stack multiple layers, each refining context further. Original Transformer (2017): 6 layers, ~6M attention params. GPT-2 (2019): 48 layers, ~48M attention params. GPT-3 (2020): 96 layers, 175 BILLION total params. More layers = deeper understanding. Each layer's attention asks different questions about the same words, building increasingly rich representations - same mechanism, just stacked.
summary: Shows how attention parameters scale across stacked layers from original Transformer to GPT-3.
queries: what do deeper transformer layers do

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: This chapter answers whether the output projection matrix W_O (and other weight grids) is constant. W_O changes constantly during training as gradients nudge its numbers across billions of examples, but once training finishes it is frozen to disk and never changes again. At inference every user, every sentence, and every input uses the exact same W_O. The same is true for the embedding dictionary and all W_Q, W_K, W_V matrices. What does change per input are the live computed values: Q, K, V vectors, attention scores, softmax weights, blended outputs, and the final output. The coffee-machine analogy: W_O is the fixed machine, the sentence embeddings are the beans, so same machine plus different beans yields different coffee.
summary: W_O is updated during training but frozen at inference; only the per-input Q/K/V and attention values change.

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: During training: W_O changes. Every time the model sees a new batch of sentences and makes prediction errors, every number in W_O gets nudged slightly. Over billions of examples, W_O gradually improves. It changes millions of times during training - getting better and better at blending heads. After training is done: W_O is FROZEN. Completely constant. Once training is complete, the model is saved to a file. W_O's numbers are written to disk and they NEVER change again. When you use the model (send it a sentence), W_O is loaded from disk and used as-is.
summary: W_O is nudged by gradients during training over billions of examples, then frozen to disk forever after training finishes.

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: This is true for all grids, not just W_O. CONSTANT after training (stored on disk, never changes): Embedding dictionary; W_Q₁ through W_Q₈ ← same grid for every input; W_K₁ through W_K₈ ← same grid for every input; W_V₁ through W_V₈ ← same grid for every input; W_O ← same grid for every input; All other layer weights. Different for every input (computed live, then thrown away): Q, K, V vectors ← different because the words are different; Attention scores ← different because Q, K are different; Softmax weights ← different because scores are different; Blended outputs ← different because weights and V are different; Final output ← different because everything above is different.
summary: Lists which transformer pieces are constant on disk (all weight matrices, embeddings) versus which are recomputed per input (Q, K, V, scores, softmax, outputs).

chapterTitle: Is W_O Constant? Does It Change?
text: Why do different sentences get different results if the grids are constant? Because the grids are like a coffee machine. The machine (W_O) is always the same. But the coffee beans you put in (your sentence) are different each time. Same machine + different beans = different coffee. Same W_O × 'I love cats' embeddings = one output. Same W_O × 'dogs are great' embeddings = completely different output. The grids don't need to change. They've already learned the GENERAL SKILL of 'how to blend heads' or 'how to extract queries.' That skill works on ANY input. Just like a coffee machine doesn't need to be rebuilt for each type of bean - it already knows how to grind and brew.
summary: The coffee machine analogy explains why a fixed W_O produces different outputs: the weights encode a general skill applied to different per-input embeddings.

chapterTitle: Attention Flow - Shapes at Every Step
text: This chapter traces every matrix shape through the attention pipeline using a tiny teaching example: 3 tokens ('I love cats'), d_model = 4, d_k = 2, and h = 2 heads. It walks single-head flow X to Q/K/V to scores to softmax weights to head output, then stacks heads with concat and projects through W_O. Final attention output has the same shape as input X (N x d_model).
summary: End-to-end shape tour of attention from input X through Q/K/V, scores, softmax, head outputs, concat, and W_O projection.

chapterTitle: Attention Flow - Shapes at Every Step
text: Single-head flow with 3 tokens, d_model = 4, d_k = 2. Input X is 3 x 4 (rows: I, love, cats). Copy X and multiply by three different weight matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V each 4 x 2 to get Q, K, V each 3 x 2. Compute Q dot K^T then divide by sqrt(d_k) and softmax per row to get attention weights matrix 3 x 3 (rows sum to 1). Then weights (3 x 3) dot V (3 x 2) = head output (3 x 2). Each word keeps its row through the whole pipeline. That entire pipeline is one head; the model runs it in parallel for every head, each with its own W_Q, W_K, W_V.
summary: Single head: X (3x4) projects to Q, K, V (3x2 each), produces 3x3 weight matrix via softmax, then weights times V yields 3x2 head output.

chapterTitle: Attention Flow - Shapes at Every Step
text: Multi-head: with h = 2 heads each head produces a 3 x 2 output H_1 and H_2. Stack them side-by-side (concat along column axis) to get concat(H_1, H_2) of shape 3 x 4. Multiply by W_O of shape 4 x 4 to mix information across heads. Final attention output is 3 x 4 - the same shape as input X. Shape cheat sheet: X is N x d_model = 3 x 4; W_Q, W_K, W_V per head is d_model x d_k = 4 x 2; Q, K, V per head is N x d_k = 3 x 2; Q dot K^T is N x N = 3 x 3; softmax(/sqrt(d_k)) is N x N rows-sum-to-1 = 3 x 3; weights dot V = H_i is N x d_k = 3 x 2; concat(H_1...H_h) is N x (h * d_k) = N x d_model = 3 x 4; W_O is d_model x d_model = 4 x 4; Final is N x d_model = 3 x 4. In a real Transformer the same pattern runs with N = sentence length, d_model = 512, h = 8 heads, d_k = 64. Shape rules are identical.
summary: Two head outputs concat to 3x4, then W_O (4x4) gives Final (3x4) matching input shape; full cheat sheet of every matrix shape included.

chapterTitle: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text: This chapter recaps the full attention computation in plain English as a 9-step pipeline: words become embeddings, multiplied by W_Q/W_K/W_V to make Queries, Keys, Values; Q dot K gives relevance scores; scale and softmax turn scores into percentages; percentages weight the Values into a context-aware output; steps 2-5 repeat across 8 heads with different grids; the 8 results are concatenated and multiplied by W_O for a combined summary; output is one blended vector per word ready for the next layer. The W matrices (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) are tables of numbers trained from random and then frozen, each learning a distinct role. The grids are the machine; the sentence is raw material.
summary: Plain-English recap of the entire attention pipeline as 9 steps with the role of each W matrix.

chapterTitle: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text:
The complete picture in plain English, step by step:
1. Words become numbers (embedding lookup).
2. Those numbers times three grids (W_Q, W_K, W_V give Query, Key, Value).
3. Queries dot-product with Keys (find who is relevant to whom).
4. Scores get normalized (scale plus softmax give percentages).
5. Percentages blend the Values (weighted sum gives context-aware output).
6. Steps 2-5 happen 8 times with different grids (multi-head gives 8 separate results).
7. Stick the 8 results end-to-end (concatenate, like 8 sealed envelopes taped together).
8. That list times one more grid (W_O opens all envelopes and writes a combined summary).
9. Output: one blended vector per word, ready for the next layer.
summary: Lists the 9 plain-English steps of attention from embeddings to one blended vector per word.

chapterTitle: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text:
Every grid in attention (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) is a table of numbers that started random, got nudged into useful values during training, and stays frozen forever after. All grids work the same way: multiply input by grid, get output. The only difference is what each one was trained to do:
- W_Q grids: learned to extract what a word is looking for.
- W_K grids: learned to extract what a word can be found for.
- W_V grids: learned to extract what actual info a word carries.
- W_O grid: learned to blend all heads' findings into one combined summary.
summary: Explains that all W matrices are frozen trained number grids that differ only in what they were trained to extract.

text: This chapter introduces the original 2017 Transformer's encoder-decoder design for machine translation. The Encoder reads input in parallel and builds rich contextual representations; the Decoder generates output token by token. Cross-Attention bridges them: decoder Queries attend to encoder Keys and Values. The full stack is 6 encoder layers (Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm) and 6 decoder layers (Masked Attention, Add & Norm, Cross-Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm). Three model families emerge from this design: Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART) for translation, Encoder-Only (BERT, RoBERTa) for classification, Decoder-Only (GPT, Claude) for generation.
summary: Summarizes the encoder-decoder Transformer, cross-attention, the 6+6 layer stack, and the three model-family variants.

text: The original Transformer from the 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" was built to solve machine translation. Translation needs two different operations: take an English input like "I love cats" and produce a Hindi output like "Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain". English and Hindi are different languages, so the model must fully understand the English input before it can generate the Hindi output. These are two separate tasks, which motivates splitting the architecture into two halves.
summary: Introduces the 2017 Transformer paper and why machine translation requires two distinct stages: understanding the source and generating the target.

text: Solution: two separate components. The Encoder reads the input and builds a deep understanding, outputting a compressed representation. The Decoder reads the encoder's output and generates the translation one word at a time. Encoder properties: processes input in parallel, all words attend to all words, builds context for each word, outputs rich representations of "I", "love", "cats". Decoder properties: reads encoder output, generates translation token by token, uses attention to look back at input, producing "Mujhe" then "billiyaan" then "pasand" then "hain".
summary: Splits the Transformer into Encoder (parallel understanding) and Decoder (token-by-token generation) and lists their distinct jobs.

text: Cross-Attention is the bridge between encoder and decoder. The decoder doesn't generate from nothing; it looks back at the encoder's output through a special connection called cross-attention. Flow: Encoder output (rich understanding of "I", "love", "cats") provides Keys and Values, which feed into the Cross-Attention Layer, where the decoder's Query asks the encoder "What's relevant now?". The decoder then generates "Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain". When generating a Hindi noun, the decoder's Query asks "what noun am I translating?" and the encoder's Keys/Values answer with the meaning of "love" and "cats", so the decoder picks the right Hindi word.
summary: Explains cross-attention as the mechanism where decoder Queries attend to encoder Keys/Values to ground generation in the source.

text: The full encoder-decoder stack: the 2017 Transformer stacks both halves with 6 layers each. Encoder Stack (6 layers) per layer: Attention, Add & Norm, Feed-Forward, Add & Norm. Decoder Stack (6 layers) per layer: Masked Attention, Add & Norm, Cross-Attention, Add & Norm, Feed-Forward, Add & Norm. Masked Attention means the decoder can only look at past tokens; Cross-Attention means it looks at encoder output. The encoder feeds into the decoder.
summary: Lays out the 6-layer encoder and 6-layer decoder stacks and the sublayers inside each, including masked and cross-attention in the decoder.

text: Why two halves? Different model types. The encoder-decoder design is great for translation, but other tasks need different shapes: Encoder-Decoder - Translation (T5, BART), input English, output Hindi. Encoder-Only - Classification (BERT, RoBERTa), input Sentence, output Sentiment score. Decoder-Only - Generation (GPT, Claude), input Prompt, output Continued text.
summary: Maps the three Transformer variants (encoder-decoder, encoder-only, decoder-only) to representative models and tasks.

text: This chapter explains Add & Norm, the stabilizing sublayer that appears twice inside every Transformer block - once after Multi-Head Attention and once after the FFN. The 'Add' is a residual (skip) connection that adds the original input back to the sublayer output so information and gradients always have a clean highway through deep networks. The 'Norm' is Layer Normalization: LayerNorm(x_i) = gamma · (x_i - mu) / sqrt(sigma^2 + epsilon) + beta, where mu and sigma^2 are computed from the vector itself, epsilon = 1e-5 prevents division by zero, and gamma (scale) and beta (shift) are learnable per-dimension parameters initialised to 1.0 and 0.0. Together they prevent vanishing and exploding values across deep stacks (e.g., GPT-3's 96 layers), as shown by tracing the 'cats' embedding through input, attention, Add, and Norm.
summary: High-level overview of Add & Norm: residual connection plus Layer Normalization that stabilises deep Transformers.

text: Inside one Transformer block there are 4 steps arranged top to bottom: Input Embeddings → Multi-Head Attention → Add & Norm → FFN (Feed-Forward Network) → Add & Norm → Output. Add & Norm appears twice in every block - once after Attention and once after FFN. It is the stabilizer that keeps everything in check between the heavier sublayers.
summary: Shows where Add & Norm sits inside a Transformer block: after Multi-Head Attention and again after the FFN.

text: The Problem: Values Drift in Deep Networks. GPT-3 has 96 layers. Each layer multiplies values by weights. If weights average slightly below 1.0, values shrink; if slightly above, values explode. Keeping them balanced across 96 layers is nearly impossible. Take 0.7 from the 'cats' embedding. Shrink scenario (weights avg 0.9): Start 0.7, Layer 10 = 0.24, Layer 50 = 0.004, Layer 96 = 0.00003 - value vanishes to nearly zero. Grow scenario (weights avg 1.1): Start 0.7, Layer 10 = 1.8, Layer 50 = 82, Layer 96 = 4,838 - value explodes to thousands. Both outcomes are broken: whether the value goes to 0.00003 or 4,838 it is no longer a useful embedding. Gradients during backpropagation drift the same way, so the model cannot learn either.
summary: Explains why deep networks suffer vanishing and exploding values, using GPT-3's 96 layers and a starting value of 0.7 as the running example.
queries: why do values vanish in deep networks
what happens to embeddings across 96 layers
what if weights are slightly below 1
what if weights are slightly above 1
why do deep models stop learning
drift problem in deep neural networks

text: The 'Add' - Keep the Original. The fix is simple: add the original input back to the output. The fancy name is 'residual connection' - 'residual' just means 'leftover.' You keep the leftover original so it never gets lost. Worked example for 'cats' embedding = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]: original input [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] goes through Multi-Head Attention to produce attention output [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]. The Add step computes original + attention output = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] + [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4] = [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]. Think of it as a highway: even if the attention layer learns nothing useful, the original embedding still passes through untouched. During backpropagation, error can travel backward along this highway without shrinking to zero. Without the Add: Input → Attention → Output, and if attention messes up, values are lost. With the Add: Input → Attention → Output + Input, so original values always survive.
summary: Demonstrates the residual 'Add' step by adding the original input vector back to the attention output for the 'cats' embedding.

why add input back to output
how do gradients flow through residual connections
add input to attention output formula

text: The 'Norm' - Layer Normalization. After adding, values can drift to unpredictable ranges. Layer Norm rescales them to a stable range. The Layer Normalization Formula: LayerNorm(x_i) = gamma · (x_i - mu) / sqrt(sigma^2 + epsilon) + beta. Parameters: x_i = each value in the vector (the raw input we want to normalize); mu = average of all values (centers the data around zero); sigma^2 = variance of all values (measures how spread out values are); epsilon = tiny number 1e-5 (prevents division by zero if all values are identical); gamma = learnable scale starting at 1.0 (lets the model stretch or shrink the range); beta = learnable shift starting at 0.0 (lets the model move the center up or down). Gamma and beta are learned during training - they let the model undo the normalization if needed, stretching, shrinking, or shifting the normalized values to whatever range works best for each dimension.
summary: Presents the full Layer Normalization formula with each symbol (mu, sigma squared, epsilon, gamma, beta) explained.

text: Computing Layer Norm step by step on [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] (the result from the Add step). Step 1: Compute the mean mu = (-0.2 + 0.2 + 0.9 + 1.0) / 4 = 0.475. Step 2: Subtract the mean from each value (x_i - mu): -0.2 - 0.475 = -0.675; 0.2 - 0.475 = -0.275; 0.9 - 0.475 = 0.425; 1.0 - 0.475 = 0.525. Centered: [-0.675, -0.275, 0.425, 0.525]. Step 3: Compute variance = mean of squared deviations = (0.675^2 + 0.275^2 + 0.425^2 + 0.525^2) / 4 = 0.2469. Then sqrt(0.2469 + 0.00001) = 0.4969 (epsilon = 1e-5 prevents division by zero if all values were identical). Divide: -0.675 / 0.4969 = -1.36; -0.275 / 0.4969 = -0.55; 0.425 / 0.4969 = 0.86; 0.525 / 0.4969 = 1.06. After this step: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] - centered at 0 with unit variance. Step 4: Scale by gamma and shift by beta (per-dimension learnable parameters). With initial gamma=1.0, beta=0.0: 1.0 * -1.36 + 0.0 = -1.36; 1.0 * -0.55 + 0.0 = -0.55; 1.0 * 0.86 + 0.0 = 0.86; 1.0 * 1.06 + 0.0 = 1.06. Final output: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Why have gamma and beta if they start as identity? During training the model may learn some dimensions need more spread (gamma > 1) or a non-zero center (beta != 0). Without them, normalization would be too rigid.
summary: Walks through the four-step Layer Norm computation on [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] with real arithmetic and initial gamma=1.0, beta=0.0.

text: The Complete Add & Norm Pipeline for the 'cats' embedding flowing after Attention: Input (cats embedding) [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] goes into Attention → Attention Output [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4] → Add: input + output → After Add [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] → Normalize → After Norm [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] → ready for FFN → To FFN [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. This exact same Add & Norm happens again after FFN. Every Transformer block does this twice - once after Attention, once after FFN.

text: Why It Matters - The Big Picture. Without Add & Norm, values drift to extremes across depth: Layer 1 = 0.7, Layer 10 = 0.24, Layer 50 = 0.004, Layer 96 = ~0. Values drift to extremes and the model stops learning. With Add & Norm, values stay stable across depth: Layer 1 = 0.7, Layer 10 = 0.67, Layer 50 = 0.62, Layer 96 = 0.57. Values stay stable, gradients flow, and training works even at 96 layers deep. In one sentence: Add & Norm is the reason Transformers can be 96 layers deep without losing information. The 'Add' preserves the original values and the 'Norm' keeps them in a stable range for the next layer.
summary: Compares value trajectories with and without Add & Norm across 96 layers and summarises why the sublayer enables deep Transformers.

text: Diagram of the FFN architecture as a neural network. Three columns of neurons: Input (512-dim, cyan) on the left, Hidden (2048-dim, yellow) in the middle, Output (512-dim, orange) on the right. The shown sample is 3 of 512 input neurons (x1, x2, x3), 4 of 2048 hidden neurons (h1, h2, h3, h2048), and 3 of 512 output neurons (y1, y2, y3). Every input neuron connects to every hidden neuron via W1 (drawn as a 512 x 2048 pink matrix box between input and hidden columns). Every hidden neuron connects to every output neuron via W2 (a 2048 x 512 green matrix box between hidden and output columns). GELU is applied at the hidden layer. The visual makes the expand-then-compress shape explicit: data widens from 512 to 2048 and compresses back to 512.
summary: Diagram visualizes the FFN as a fully-connected expand-then-compress network: 512-dim input → W1 → 2048-dim GELU hidden → W2 → 512-dim output.
queries: FFN drawing with W1 W2 matrices

text: Graph of the ReLU activation function over x from -3 to 3. The curve is flat at y = 0 for all negative x (a horizontal line along the x-axis), then bends with a hard kink at the origin and continues linearly as y = x for all positive x. Annotations show the dead region on the left (gradient = 0, neurons stuck) and the hard kink at the origin. Visual demonstrates ReLU's sharp corner and zero output for negatives - the source of the dying-ReLU problem.
summary: ReLU graph shows a flat dead region for x<0, a hard kink at 0, then y=x for x>0 - illustrating the dying-neuron problem.
queries: what does ReLU look like plotted

text: Graph of the GELU activation function over x from -3 to 3. The curve is a smooth S-shape: nearly zero for large negative x, dips slightly below zero around x = -1 (the leak region, where the gradient is alive), passes smoothly through the origin with a continuous derivative, and asymptotically matches y = x for large positive x. Annotations highlight the leak around x = -1 (gradient alive, neurons can recover) and the smooth S through the origin. Contrasts directly with the ReLU graph: no sharp corner, gradient never fully dies, small negatives produce small non-zero outputs.
summary: GELU graph is a smooth S-curve that dips slightly below zero near x=-1 and matches y=x for large positives, keeping gradients alive for negative inputs unlike ReLU.
queries: what does GELU look like plotted

text: This chapter covers the Feed-Forward Network sublayer of a Transformer block. Key points: FFN sits between the two Add & Norm layers and processes each token independently (no cross-token communication). It is two linear layers with an activation in between: FFN(x) = W2 · GELU(W1·x + b1) + b2. It uses an expand-then-compress shape, widening to 4x d_model in the hidden layer (e.g. 512 → 2048 → 512). The activation is GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit), GELU(x) = x · Φ(x), smoother than ReLU and avoids the dying-neuron problem. Worked example traces "cats" through all three steps with real numbers. Roles: attention routes information between tokens, FFN injects stored knowledge into each token. Three knowledge types: implicit (area/breadth), factual recall (India/Delhi), disambiguation (river bank). FFN holds about 2/3 of a block's parameters via W1 and W2; attention holds 1/3 via W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O. Researchers verify FFN-as-memory via ablation studies showing knowledge is localized to specific neurons.
summary: Chapter summary: FFN is two linear layers with a GELU activation in between that expand to 4x d_model, hold most of a block's parameters, and store the model's knowledge (implicit, factual, disambiguation) while attention handles routing.
queries: summary of feed-forward network in transformer
what does FFN do in transformer
two linear layers with GELU summary

text: The Feed-Forward Network (FFN) sits between the two Add & Norm layers inside a Transformer block. The flow is: Input Embeddings → Multi-Head Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm → Output. Unlike attention, the FFN processes each token independently - no communication between words. That independence is what makes FFN different from attention: attention mixes information across tokens, FFN transforms each token's vector on its own.
summary: FFN sits between the two Add & Norm layers in a Transformer block and processes each token independently, unlike attention which mixes tokens.

text: An FFN is just two linear layers stacked with an activation function in between. The full formula is: FFN(x) = W2 · GELU(W1 · x + b1) + b2. Variables: x is the input from Add & Norm. W1, b1 are the first layer weights and bias. GELU is the activation function. W2, b2 are the second layer weights and bias. No attention, no softmax, no multi-head anything - just two matrix multiplies with an activation between them. Same building blocks as a basic neural network layer (multiply by W, add b).
summary: FFN is two linear layers with an activation in between, written FFN(x) = W2 · GELU(W1·x + b1) + b2.

text: FFN uses an expand-then-compress shape. The first layer W1 expands the dimension from d_model to a wider hidden size; the second layer W2 compresses back down. In GPT-2 with d_model = 512, W1 is 512 x 2048 (expanding to 2048-dim hidden), then W2 is 2048 x 512 (compressing back to 512). Every input neuron connects to every hidden neuron via W1, GELU activates each hidden neuron, then every hidden neuron connects to every output neuron via W2. The hidden layer has 4x more neurons than input/output. The 4x ratio comes from the original Attention is All You Need paper and is standard across GPT-2, GPT-3, and most Transformers. Intuition: spread your notes across a big desk (2048), work things out, then summarize on a small card (512).
summary: FFN expands the vector 4x in a hidden layer (e.g. 512 to 2048) then compresses back, a ratio standard since the original Transformer paper.

text: Step 1 of FFN on the word "cats": multiply by W1 and add b1, expanding from 4 dims to 8 dims. Input vector (from the previous Add & Norm) is [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Compute [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] · W1 + b1. Each of the 8 output values is a dot product of the input with one column of W1, plus a bias - same as a standard linear layer. Result (8 dims): [0.42, -1.80, 0.95, 2.10, -0.33, 1.47, -0.88, 0.61]. Real models use 512 dims but the process is identical.
queries: how does W1 expand the vector
worked example of first FFN layer
expand 4 dim to 8 dim FFN example
real numbers for FFN first layer

text: Step 2: apply GELU elementwise. Input [0.42, -1.80, 0.95, 2.10, -0.33, 1.47, -0.88, 0.61] becomes [0.28, -0.04, 0.77, 2.07, -0.12, 1.37, -0.17, 0.44]. Each element passes through GELU independently - large positives barely change, large negatives get squashed toward zero but leak slightly. Step 3: multiply by W2 and add b2, compressing 8 dims back to 4 dims. [0.28, -0.04, ..., 0.44] · W2 + b2 = [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]. Same size in, same size out, but the vector has been transformed through a thinking space 4x wider. This output now feeds into the second Add & Norm.
summary: Worked steps 2 and 3: GELU squashes each of the 8 values, then W2+b2 compresses back to 4 dims [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22].
queries: how W2 compresses back to model dim

text: GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit) is the smoother activation used in modern Transformers instead of ReLU. The real formula: GELU(x) = x · Φ(x), where Φ(x) is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the standard normal distribution: Φ(x) = (1/2) [1 + erf(x / √2)]. Variables: x is the input (raw number from the linear layer). Φ(x) is the probability x is kept, ranging 0 (fully blocked) to 1 (fully passed). erf is the Gauss error function, a smooth S-curve mapping numbers to [-1, 1]. Plain English: GELU multiplies x by the probability x is large under a standard normal bell curve. Worked examples: GELU(2.0) = 2.0 · 0.977 = 1.95 (almost fully passed); GELU(0.5) = 0.5 · 0.691 = 0.35 (69% passed); GELU(0.0) = 0; GELU(-0.5) = -0.5 · 0.309 = -0.15 (31% leaked); GELU(-2.0) = -2.0 · 0.023 = -0.05 (almost fully blocked). Contrast with ReLU: ReLU is a hard cutoff (if x<0 output=0; if x>0 output=x); negatives are dead, gradient = 0 forever. GELU has a smooth S-curve, small negatives leak through, and the gradient never fully dies. This matters during backpropagation: ReLU's gradient is exactly 0 for negative inputs so a dead neuron can never recover; GELU's smooth curve means even slightly negative values get a small gradient and neurons can recover. GPT-2 and GPT-3 both use GELU.
summary: GELU(x) = x · Φ(x) is a smooth activation that lets negative inputs leak through with non-zero gradient, unlike ReLU which kills negatives.
queries: why use GELU instead of ReLU

text: Knowledge vs Routing: attention and FFN play different roles. Attention decides which words to listen to; FFN decides what to do with what it heard. Inside every block attention runs first, then FFN, so FFN never sees the raw input - only attention's context-enriched output. Example 1, implicit knowledge: "The area of this rectangle depends on its length." Note: "breadth" is never mentioned. Attention step: "area"'s Query asks "what determines me?" "length"'s Key scores 0.55 after softmax; "rectangle"'s Key scores 0.30. The weighted Value sum gives "area" a vector meaning "I'm the area of a rectangle and length is involved." Attention found real connections, but cannot add the missing word "breadth". FFN step: W1 row 1203 is a trained "rectangle-area with length" detector. Dot product W1[1203] · x = 3.9. GELU(3.9) = 3.89 → neuron 1203 fires strongly. Meanwhile neuron 502 ("circle-area-pi") got -0.8 → GELU suppresses it. W2 column 1203 shifts the vector toward a region encoding "area = length x breadth." The model now implicitly knows breadth matters even though the word never appeared. During training the FFN saw "area = length x breadth" thousands of times and baked it into W1 row 1203 (detector) and W2 column 1203 (output).
summary: Attention routes context between tokens; FFN injects stored knowledge - example: FFN can pull in "breadth" even though only "area" and "length" appear in the sentence.
queries: why attention cannot add missing words
circle vs rectangle area FFN neuron

text: Example 2, factual recall: "The capital of India is ___". Attention step on the "___" position: its Query asks "what country am I about?" "India"'s Key scores 0.72 after softmax, "capital" scores 0.18. Weighted Value sum = 0.72 · V_India + 0.18 · V_capital + ... The "___" vector encodes the question "I need the capital of India" but attention cannot look up facts - it only averages existing vectors. FFN step: W1 row 847 is a trained "capital-of-India" detector. Dot product W1[847] · x = 4.2. GELU(4.2) = 4.19 → neuron 847 fires. Neuron 200 ("capital-of-France") gets -1.3 → GELU(-1.3) = -0.05, suppressed. Of 2048 neurons, typically only 100-300 fire meaningfully for any input. W2 column 847 was trained to shift toward "Delhi". Output += 4.19 · W2[:,847] writes "Delhi" into the vector. Key insight: W1 rows act as keys ("what pattern to detect") and W2 columns act as values ("what to output"). This is the key-value memory interpretation from interpretability research: the FFN is a lookup table with 2048 entries per block.
summary: Factual recall example: attention assembles the question "capital of India" and FFN looks up "Delhi" via a W1 detector row and matching W2 output column - the key-value memory view.
queries: how many FFN neurons fire per input
where do facts live in transformer

text: Example 3, disambiguation across multiple blocks: "She sat by the river bank." Does "bank" mean money or riverbed? Block 1, attention: "bank"'s Query matches "river"'s Key (adjacent). After softmax, "bank" absorbs ~40% of "river"'s Value, ~20% of "sat"'s Value. "bank" now carries weak river context. Block 1, FFN: row 310 ("river-location") fires weakly at 1.2; row 744 ("financial-institution") also fires weakly at 0.8. GELU passes both; W2 nudges toward both meanings. Ambiguity not yet resolved. Block 5, attention: "bank" attends more strongly to "sat", "by", and "river" because Blocks 1-4 FFNs strengthened nature/location features in those Value vectors. Block 5, FFN: row 310 fires at 3.8 (strong); row 744 fires at -1.1 → GELU(-1.1) = -0.03, effectively dead. W2 column 310 shifts hard toward geography; column 744 contributes nothing. "Bank" now means riverbank - ambiguity resolved. Why multiple blocks: each round attention provides better context because FFN improved vectors, and FFN makes better decisions because attention gathered more context. They bootstrap each other across all 96 blocks. Summary of the three FFN knowledge types: (1) Implicit knowledge - adds concepts not in input (area/breadth). (2) Factual recall - writes a stored fact into the vector (India/Delhi). (3) Disambiguation - suppresses wrong meanings and boosts the right one (bank/river).
summary: Disambiguation example: across multiple blocks attention and FFN bootstrap each other so "bank" gradually resolves to "riverbank" - the third of three FFN knowledge types alongside implicit knowledge and factual recall.

text: Deep questions about FFN. Q: Why can't attention replace FFN? A: Attention computes weighted averages of Value vectors - a linear operation. It mixes information across tokens but never creates new features. A rule like "if river AND bank are both present, output riverbank" requires non-linearity (GELU). Only FFN provides that. Q: Why can't FFN replace attention? A: FFN processes each token in complete isolation. Without attention first injecting "river" into "bank"'s vector, FFN would treat "bank" identically in "river bank" and "investment bank" - same input, same output, no disambiguation. Q: What exactly does "FFN stores knowledge" mean? A: Each W1 row is a pattern detector learned during pre-training. Row 847 learned to fire on "capital-of-India." The corresponding W2 column learned to output a vector that shifts toward "Delhi." This row-column pair is literally one stored fact. GPT-3 has 2048-49152 such detectors per block across 96 blocks. Q: How do researchers prove this isn't just a metaphor? A: Ablation studies - zero out specific FFN neurons and "The capital of India is" stops producing "Delhi" while grammar stays intact. Artificially activate those neurons and "Delhi" appears in unrelated contexts. Knowledge is localized to specific neurons.
summary: Four deep questions: attention can't replace FFN (no non-linearity), FFN can't replace attention (no cross-token mixing), FFN stores knowledge as W1-W2 row-column pairs, and ablation studies prove this empirically.
queries: what does FFN stores knowledge mean
FFN row column pair as fact
why do transformers need both attention and FFN

text: Where parameters live in a Transformer block. Parameters are all learnable numbers - every weight and bias tuned during training. They are the model's memory; more parameters = more capacity. In one Transformer block (~3.15M parameters total) the split is roughly 1/3 attention, 2/3 FFN. Attention (~1.05M params): W_Q, W_K, W_V create Queries, Keys, Values; W_O blends multi-head outputs. These learn how to find relationships between words. FFN (~2.1M params): W1 (512 x 2048) expands to thinking space; W2 (2048 x 512) compresses back. These learn facts and transformations - the actual knowledge. GPT-3 has 96 blocks, so 96 copies = 96 x 3.15M ≈ 302M params per layer-type at d_model=512. GPT-3's actual total is 175 billion because d_model is 12,288 (not 512), making every matrix vastly larger.
summary: Within a Transformer block roughly 2/3 of parameters live in the FFN (W1 and W2) and 1/3 in attention (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O); FFN stores the knowledge, attention stores the routing.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: This chapter shows why the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) inside a Transformer block can handle any number of input words with the same fixed weight matrices. The number of words is the row dimension of the input matrix; W1 (d_model x d_ff) and W2 (d_ff x d_model) only depend on d_model and d_ff, not on sequence length. Stacking all words into one matrix turns N sequential multiplies into one big matrix multiply, which GPUs run in parallel because FFN treats each word independently. In GPT-2 (d_model=512, d_ff=2048), W1 has 512 x 2048 = 1,048,576 weights whether you feed 10 or 100,000 words. This is embarrassingly parallel, unlike Attention which has cross-word dependencies.
summary: FFN weights stay fixed regardless of word count because num_words is just the row dimension; stacking enables GPU parallelism since each word is processed independently.
queries: why does FFN not care about sequence length
how does feed-forward network handle variable length input

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Recap: FFN with just one word. The word "cats" is a 3-dimensional vector [0.5, -0.3, 0.9]. Multiply it by W1 (3x6) to get a hidden vector (1x6). Shapes: 1x3 times 3x6 = 1x6. The weight matrix W1 has 3 x 6 = 18 weights. These 18 weights are the same for every single word that ever goes through this FFN. W1 expands a 3-dim input to a 6-dim hidden representation.
summary: Recap of passing one word through FFN: 1x3 input times 3x6 W1 equals 1x6 hidden, with 18 reusable weights.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: The trick: stack words into a matrix. With 3 words ("I", "love", "cats") we don't run the FFN 3 times. Instead we stack all 3 word vectors into a single 3x3 matrix and multiply once. 3x3 times 3x6 = 3x6. Still the same W1. Still 18 weights. Row 1 of the input only interacts with W to produce row 1 of the output. No mixing between words - that's how matrix multiplication works.
summary: Stacking 3 word vectors into a 3x3 matrix and multiplying by the same 3x6 W1 yields a 3x6 output in one operation, with rows kept independent.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: 6 words: "The cat sat on the mat". 6 words, each a 3-dim vector. Stack them into a 6x3 matrix. 6x3 times 3x6 = 6x6. The W1 matrix is still 3x6 = 18 weights. Doesn't matter that we went from 3 words to 6 - the weight matrix stays the same size. Row 1 ("The") hits W and produces its own output. Row 4 ("on") hits the exact same W and produces its own output. The rows never interact. Each word is independently transformed by the same 18 weights.
summary: Six stacked word vectors form a 6x3 matrix which times 3x6 W1 gives 6x6, demonstrating the weight matrix size is independent of word count.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: 10 words? 1000 words? Same 18 weights. The number of words is just the number of rows in the input matrix. The weight matrix W never changes. Pattern: (num_words x d_model) times W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (num_words x d_ff). Examples: 1 word -> 1x3 times 3x6 = 1x6; 3 words -> 3x3 = 3x6; 6 words -> 6x6; 10 words -> 10x6; 1000 words -> 1000x6. W1 always = 18 weights. The "num_words" dimension is a free variable. It can be anything. W1 only cares about d_model (3 in our toy example, 512 in real transformers). In GPT-2 (d_model=512, d_ff=2048): W1 has 512 x 2048 = 1,048,576 weights. That number stays the same whether you feed it 10 words or 100,000 words.
summary: Generalizes the FFN shape rule: num_words is a free row dimension and W1 depends only on d_model and d_ff, with GPT-2 having ~1M weights regardless of input length.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Scaling back down: W2 has the inverse shape. After GELU activation we compress the hidden representation back to the original dimension. W2 has the flipped shape of W1. For 6 words: Input 6x3 times W1 3x6 = Expanded 6x6 -> GELU 6x6 times W2 6x3 = Output 6x3. For 10 words: Input 10x3 times W1 3x6 = Expanded 10x6 -> GELU 10x6 times W2 6x3 = Output 10x3. General pattern: Layer 1: (words x d_model) times W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (words x d_ff). GELU: (words x d_ff) -> (words x d_ff). Layer 2: (words x d_ff) times W2 (d_ff x d_model) = (words x d_model). Word count flows through as the row dimension. W1 and W2 only depend on d_model and d_ff. In, expand, compress back out - same size going in and coming out, regardless of how many words.
summary: W2 reverses W1's shape (d_ff x d_model) so FFN output matches input dimensionality; the expand-then-compress pattern works for any number of words.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Why this matters: massive GPU parallelism. The reason we stack words into a matrix isn't just math elegance - it's about hardware speed. Without stacking (for loop): for word in words: output = word times W. 1000 words = 1000 sequential matrix multiplies. Each one waits for the previous to finish. The GPU sits mostly idle because each multiply is tiny (1x3 times 3x6). With stacking (one multiply): output = all_words times W. 1000 words = one massive parallel matrix multiply (1000x3 times 3x6). GPU processes all rows simultaneously across thousands of cores. One instruction, all words done. This parallel trick works because FFN treats each word independently. No word's output depends on any other word. That's the fundamental difference from Attention, which creates cross-word dependencies (Q dot K). You can't parallelize Attention the same way because token 3's output depends on tokens 1 and 2. FFN is embarrassingly parallel - the GPU's dream workload. This is also why the KV cache during generation only caches attention K,V - not FFN outputs. FFN has no cross-word state to remember. Run it on the new word, get the answer. Previous words' FFN results are already baked into the K,V cache.
summary: Stacking enables GPU parallelism because FFN has no cross-word dependencies, contrasting with attention and explaining why KV cache stores only attention K and V.

text: The second Add & Norm in a Transformer block stabilizes the FFN's output the same way the first Add & Norm stabilized attention. It adds the FFN input back to the FFN output (residual connection) then applies Layer Normalization. Worked example with 'cats': FFN input [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] plus FFN output [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22] gives [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]; normalizing yields block output [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72]. Add & Norm appears twice because each sub-layer (Attention, FFN) independently distorts values and needs its own residual + norm. This pattern lets Transformers stack 96 blocks deep without signal degrading.
summary: Overview of why a Transformer block has a second Add & Norm after the FFN and the full single-block pipeline.

text: The FFN just transformed each token's representation, but values drift in deep networks so we need to stabilize again. The block flow is: Multi-Head Attention -> Add & Norm (first) -> FFN -> Add & Norm (second, highlighted) -> Block Output. This second Add & Norm works exactly the same way as the first, except its input is the FFN output instead of the Attention output.
summary: Locates the second Add & Norm in the block flow and frames it as a re-application of stabilization after the FFN.

text: The Add - residual around FFN. Same idea as before: keep the original input by adding it back. The input to FFN was the first Add & Norm's output. Continuing with 'cats': FFN input = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Goes through FFN. FFN output = [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]. The Add step: FFN input + FFN output = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] + [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22] = [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]. The residual connection ensures that even if the FFN distorts values, the original signal from Add & Norm #1 survives by being added back.
summary: Walks through the residual add step around the FFN with concrete vectors for the 'cats' token.

text: The Norm - stabilize again. Same Layer Normalization formula: compute mean, subtract, divide by standard deviation, scale by gamma, shift by beta. Normalizing [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]: mu = (-0.85 + -1.28 + 2.00 + 1.28) / 4 = 0.2875. variance = 1.9172, sqrt(variance + epsilon) = 1.3846. Normalize each: (x - mu) / 1.3846. -0.85 -> -0.82; -1.28 -> -1.13; 2.00 -> 1.24; 1.28 -> 0.72. Block output for 'cats': [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72]. With gamma=1.0 and beta=0.0 (initial values). The model will learn optimal gamma and beta during training.
summary: Worked Layer Normalization on the post-residual vector producing the final block output for 'cats'.

Here's 'cats' flowing through one entire Transformer block, start to finish: Input (cats) = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]; through Multi-Head Attention -> Attention Output = [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]; through Add & Norm #1 -> After 1st Add & Norm = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]; through Feed-Forward Network -> FFN Output = [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]; through Add & Norm #2 -> Block Output = [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72]. One block: Attention moves information between tokens, Add & Norm stabilizes, FFN processes each token's knowledge, Add & Norm stabilizes again. This is the heartbeat of every Transformer.

text: Why twice? One per sub-layer. Add & Norm appears twice because each Transformer block has two sub-layers: Attention and FFN. Each sub-layer gets its own Add & Norm because each one can independently cause value drift. Sub-layer 1 (Attention): Input x -> Attention(x) -> Add & Norm #1, stabilizes after attention changes. Sub-layer 2 (FFN): From A&N #1 -> FFN(x) -> Add & Norm #2, stabilizes after FFN changes. The rule: in Transformers, every sub-layer that transforms values gets wrapped with its own residual connection and layer norm. This is what makes it possible to stack 96 blocks deep without the signal degrading. If you only normalized once at the end of the block, the FFN's output could already be in a bad range before it gets stabilized.
summary: Explains the design rule that every value-transforming sub-layer gets its own residual + norm, enabling deep stacking.

An arrow leads down into Block 1, then Block 2, then a vertical ellipsis (...), then Block N. Each block is a horizontal row of four sub-step labels: Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm. After Block N, an arrow points down to the Encoder Output box, labeled as contextual hidden states for each token. A square bracket on the right side spans from Block 1 down to Block N and is labeled 'Nx', indicating the block is repeated N times. Block 1 is shown in green, Block 2 in yellow, and Block N in red to suggest progressive depth.

text: The Transformer block (Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm) is repeated N times in a stack, written as 'Nx' in architecture diagrams. One block only learns simple patterns; depth lets the model build from grammar up to abstract reasoning. Real models: GPT-2 Small uses N=12 blocks with d_model=768, GPT-2 Medium N=24 d=1024, GPT-2 Large N=36 d=1280, GPT-3 N=96 d=12288. Every block has the same structure but its own unique learned weights: W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O for attention, gamma1/beta1 for first Add & Norm, W1,b1 and W2,b2 for FFN, gamma2/beta2 for second Add & Norm. If weights were shared, stacking would just repeat the same transformation. Research shows early blocks (1-4) learn surface patterns and part-of-speech, middle blocks (5-12) learn syntax and pronoun resolution, deeper blocks (13-24) learn semantics and word sense, and deepest blocks (25-96) learn abstract reasoning and world knowledge. Full encoder recipe: Tokenize → Embed → Add positions → N blocks → contextual hidden states, with bidirectional attention.
summary: Overview of why Transformer blocks repeat N times, what weights each block owns, and how depth maps to learned linguistic complexity.

text: We've now seen all 4 steps inside one Transformer block: Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm. But one block can only learn simple patterns. To understand language, the model needs to repeat this entire block many times. Think of it like this: 1 block learns basic word associations ("cat" relates to "sat"); 4 blocks learn grammar structure (subject-verb agreement); 12 blocks learn context and nuance (sarcasm, tone); 96 blocks learn deep reasoning, world knowledge, complex inference. Each repeat of the block refines the token representations further. The output of block 1 becomes the input of block 2, and so on.
summary: One Transformer block only learns simple patterns - stacking more blocks unlocks grammar, nuance, and reasoning.

text: Different models use different numbers of blocks. In the original paper and in practice, this number is called N (hence "Nx" in architecture diagrams). Real sizes: GPT-2 Small uses 12 blocks with d_model=768; GPT-2 Medium uses 24 blocks with d=1024; GPT-2 Large uses 36 blocks with d=1280; GPT-3 uses 96 blocks with d=12288. What this means: In GPT-3, every single token in your prompt passes through 96 identical blocks in sequence. Each block runs the full Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm pipeline. That's 96 rounds of attention, 96 rounds of FFN, and 192 Add & Norm operations per token.

text: Every block has the same structure: Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm. But every learnable weight inside each block is unique - learned separately during training. Multi-Head Attention weights: W_Q creates Queries ("what am I looking for?"), W_K creates Keys ("what do I contain?"), W_V creates Values ("what info do I pass along?"), W_O blends all 8 heads back together. Add & Norm #1 (after Attention) owns gamma1 (learnable scale per dimension, stretches or shrinks normalized values) and beta1 (learnable shift per dimension, moves the center up or down). Feed-Forward Network owns W1, b1 (first layer expands from 512 to 2048 dims - the thinking space) and W2, b2 (second layer compresses from 2048 back to 512 dims). Add & Norm #2 (after FFN) owns gamma2 and beta2 - same roles as the first pair, but separate copies learned independently. Every block has its own copy of ALL these weights: Block 1 has its own W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, W1, W2, gammas, and betas; Block 2 has completely different weights; Block 96 yet another set. If they shared the same weights, stacking 96 blocks would be no better than having one - you'd just repeat the same transformation. Each block's unique weights let it specialize: Block 1 learns different patterns than Block 50.
summary: Full weight inventory of one Transformer block (W_Q/K/V/O, gammas, betas, W1/W2) and why every block keeps its own independent copy.

text: Research on Transformer internals has revealed a clear pattern: early blocks learn simple things, deep blocks learn abstract things. Blocks 1-4 - Surface Patterns: part-of-speech (noun, verb, adjective), basic grammar and word order, simple co-occurrence ("New" near "York"). Blocks 5-12 - Syntax & Structure: subject-verb agreement across distance, clause boundaries and nesting, pronoun resolution ("she" refers to "Alice"). Blocks 13-24 - Semantics & Meaning: word sense disambiguation ("bank" = river vs money), sentiment and tone detection, entity relationships and facts. Blocks 25-96 - Abstract Reasoning: multi-step inference and logic, world knowledge and common sense, task-specific meaning and generation. This is why depth matters - you need many blocks to build from grammar all the way up to meaning and reasoning. It's the same principle as "deep" networks from this chapter, but now each block has both attention (for context) and FFN (for knowledge).
summary: Mapping of Transformer depth to learned linguistic abstraction, from part-of-speech at shallow blocks to reasoning at deep blocks.

text: The complete picture - token to output through N blocks. The full journey of a single token from input to output: Token Embedding + Positional Encoding feeds into Block 1, then Block 2, then ... then Block N, producing the Encoder Output (contextual hidden states for each token). Each block runs the same internal pipeline: Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm. An Nx bracket marks the repeated stack. Real sizes: GPT-2 Small N=12 blocks, 117M parameters; GPT-3 N=96 blocks, 175B parameters. The full recipe of a Transformer encoder: Tokenize → Embed → Add positions → Pass through N blocks (each: Self-Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm) → Rich contextual hidden states. The encoder uses bidirectional attention - every word can see every other word, no masking. This gives the encoder a full understanding of the entire input.
summary: End-to-end token journey through embedding, N repeated blocks, and the bidirectional encoder output, with GPT-2/GPT-3 parameter counts.

text: Residual connections add the input x back to a layer's output, computing F(x) + x. This creates a skip path whose derivative is exactly 1, so the total gradient F'(x) + 1 never collapses to zero even when layer weights produce tiny derivatives. This solves the vanishing gradient problem that would otherwise kill learning in deep stacks like GPT-3's 96 layers. Each transformer block uses two residuals (one around Attention, one around FFN), giving GPT-3 a total of 192 gradient highways and making deep transformers trainable.
summary: Residual connections F(x) + x add a skip path with gradient 1, fixing vanishing gradients and enabling deep transformer training.

why do transformers use skip connections
how do residuals fix vanishing gradients
why two residuals per transformer block
how do skip connections enable deep networks
main idea behind ResNet applied to transformers
what does adding x to F(x) achieve

text: In deep networks, gradients shrink as they travel backward through layers. During backpropagation, each layer multiplies the gradient by its derivative. If that derivative is even slightly below 1.0, the gradient shrinks exponentially. Multiplying through 96 layers: with F'(x) = 0.99, you get 0.99^96 = 0.38 (even near-perfect derivatives lose 62% of the gradient). With F'(x) = 0.9, you get 0.9^96 = 0.0000396 (gradient is effectively zero by layer 1). With F'(x) = 0.5, you get 0.5^96 = 1.3 x 10^-29 (completely gone, no learning possible). GPT-3 has 96 layers. Without a fix, the gradient arriving at layer 1 from layer 96 would be essentially zero. Layer 1 would never learn. The model would be dead on arrival.
summary: Vanishing gradients break deep networks because per-layer derivatives below 1.0 shrink exponentially across 96 layers.

why can't deep networks learn without tricks

what is the effect of derivative less than 1
why do early layers fail to learn
why is layer 1 of GPT-3 hard to train
what does F'(x) = 0.5 do across 96 layers
why deep networks dead on arrival

text: The fix is deceptively simple: add the input directly to the output. The residual connection computes output = F(x) + x. The '+ x' creates a shortcut path that bypasses the entire layer. Without residual (Path A), the gradient must travel through every layer: input x -> weight1 -> activation -> weight2 -> activation -> output, shrinking at each step. With residual (Path B), the same path produces F(x) + x, and a skip connection adds a direct highway around all the layers. The '+ x' adds a direct highway that bypasses every weight and activation.
summary: A residual connection adds the input x directly back to the layer output, creating a shortcut around all the weights.
queries: why add the input to the output
what is the formula for a residual block
what is a shortcut path in neural networks
why is it called a highway
what is the structure of a residual block

text: The math is elegant. Take the derivative of the residual output: d/dx [F(x) + x] = F'(x) + 1. F'(x) is the gradient through the layer weights and can shrink toward 0. The '+ 1' is the gradient through the skip connection and is ALWAYS exactly 1, no matter what. That + 1 is everything. Even if F'(x) approaches 0, the total gradient is at least 1. Without residual: gradient = F'(x); if F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 0.01. With residual: gradient = F'(x) + 1; if F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 1.01. The gradient through the skip connection is always 1, regardless of what the layer weights do.
summary: Differentiating F(x) + x gives F'(x) + 1, so the skip path always contributes a gradient of exactly 1 no matter how small F'(x) gets.
queries: what is the derivative of F(x) + x
what if F'(x) is 0.01 with residual

text: Visualize a 6-layer transformer. Gradients travel backward from Layer 6 to Layer 1 via two paths. Through the weights, the gradient shrinks by 0.7 at each layer: at layer 6 it is 1.00, then 0.70, 0.49, 0.34, 0.24, and 0.17 at layer 1. Through the skip connection, the gradient is always 1.0 at every layer. Through weights: 0.7^5 = 0.17 (lost 83% of the signal). Through skip connections: 1.00 (full signal preserved). The skip path delivers the gradient from layer 6 to layer 1 without any multiplication.
summary: Across a 6-layer stack with per-layer factor 0.7, the weight path drops to 0.17 while the skip path stays at 1.00 the whole way.

how much signal is lost through weights
compare weight path vs skip path gradient

text: Modern transformers are absurdly deep. Without residual connections, training them would be impossible. GPT-2 has 12 layers and 24 residuals. GPT-3 has 96 layers and 192 residuals. GPT-4 (estimated) has 120 layers and 240 residuals. Every transformer block has two sub-layers: Attention and FFN. Each gets its own residual connection. That means GPT-3's 96 blocks have 192 gradient highways - one around every Attention layer and one around every FFN layer. The block flow: Input -> Attention + Residual #1 -> FFN + Residual #2 -> Block Output. Without residual connections, gradients would vanish by layer 3. With them, gradients flow freely through all 96 layers on the skip path, letting even the earliest layers learn from the loss signal. This is what makes deep transformers trainable.
summary: Each transformer block has two residuals (around Attention and FFN), so GPT-3's 96 blocks give 192 gradient highways that make deep training possible.

text: This chapter contrasts post-norm and pre-norm transformer blocks. Post-norm (original 2017 paper) places LayerNorm after the residual Add, so the skip path itself passes through LayerNorm. Pre-norm (modern approach) applies LayerNorm before each sub-layer, leaving the residual add to receive the raw, unnormalized input. A numeric trace of [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] shows post-norm's skip path getting normalized while pre-norm's skip path stays as pure addition. Pre-norm wins because the gradient highway has derivative 1 (cleaner gradient flow) and training is stable with larger learning rates and less warmup. Post-norm was used in the 2017 paper and early BERT; pre-norm is the standard for GPT-2/3/4, LLaMA, Mistral, Mixtral, Claude, and virtually all modern LLMs since ~2019.
summary: Overview comparing post-norm and pre-norm transformer blocks, why pre-norm has cleaner gradients and easier training, and which models use each.

text: Post-Norm (Original 2017 Paper): In the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, LayerNorm comes after the residual Add. Exact order per sub-layer: Input x -> Attention(x) -> Add: x + Attention(x) -> LayerNorm -> FFN -> Add: prev + FFN(prev) -> LayerNorm. Key feature: LayerNorm sits after the residual add. The residual path passes through LayerNorm before reaching the next sub-layer.
summary: Post-Norm places LayerNorm after the residual Add, so the skip path itself passes through LayerNorm between sub-layers.

text: Pre-Norm (Modern Approach): LayerNorm comes before each sub-layer. The residual add gets the raw, unnormalized input - not the normalized version. Order: Input x -> LayerNorm(x) -> Attention(LayerNorm(x)) -> Add: x + Attention(LayerNorm(x)) -> LayerNorm(prev) -> FFN(LayerNorm(prev)) -> Add: prev + FFN(LayerNorm(prev)). Key difference: the Add step receives the raw input x, not the normalized version. LayerNorm only feeds into the sub-layer, not into the skip path.
summary: Pre-Norm applies LayerNorm before each sub-layer and keeps the residual skip path on the raw, unnormalized input.

text: Side-by-Side Comparison. Trace input [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] ('cats' embedding) through the Attention sub-layer. Post-Norm: Input x = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]; Attention(x) = [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]; Add x + Attn = [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]; LayerNorm = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Norm is last; skip path passes through Norm. Pre-Norm: Input x = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]; LayerNorm(x) = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]; Attn(LN(x)) = [0.28, -0.12, 0.18, 0.38]; Add x + Attn = [-0.22, 0.18, 0.88, 0.98]. Norm is first; skip path is clean - just addition. Critical difference: in post-norm the skip path goes through LayerNorm; in pre-norm the skip path is pure addition - no transformations on the highway.

text: Why Pre-Norm Wins. Two key advantages make pre-norm the clear winner for deep models. 1. Cleaner Gradient Flow: In post-norm, the gradient on the skip path must pass through LayerNorm at every block. LayerNorm has its own derivative that modifies the gradient - not as bad as vanishing, but it adds noise and makes optimization harder. In pre-norm, the skip path is pure addition. The gradient flows from the last block to the first block on a clean highway with derivative = 1 at every step. No LayerNorm interference on the residual stream. 2. Training Stability: Post-norm requires very careful learning rate warmup - start with a tiny learning rate and slowly increase it over thousands of steps. Without warmup, gradients in early training are unstable and the model diverges (loss goes to infinity). Pre-norm is more forgiving. It works with larger learning rates from the start. The clean gradient highway stabilizes training even without careful warmup schedules. This makes training faster and less finicky.
summary: Pre-norm wins because its pure-addition skip path gives a derivative-1 gradient highway and tolerates larger learning rates without elaborate warmup, while post-norm needs careful warmup or it diverges.

text: Who Uses What. Post-Norm: 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017); early BERT models; some early translation models. Historical - used mainly in the original paper. Pre-Norm: GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4; LLaMA, LLaMA 2, LLaMA 3; Mistral, Mixtral; Claude; virtually all modern LLMs. The industry standard since ~2019. The Field Converged: the shift happened because pre-norm trains faster, scales better to deep models, and is more robust. When you hear about a modern LLM with 96+ layers, it is almost certainly using pre-norm. The original paper used post-norm, but practice proved pre-norm superior for large-scale training.
summary: Catalogues which models use which scheme: post-norm was historical (original paper, early BERT) while GPT, LLaMA, Mistral, Claude, and almost every modern LLM use pre-norm, the industry standard since ~2019.

text: This chapter contrasts Batch Normalization and Layer Normalization. Batch Norm (Ioffe and Szegedy, 2015) normalizes across the batch dimension - for each feature, it computes mean and variance across all examples in the batch. It works for CNNs because images share structure, have fixed sizes, and provide meaningful per-position statistics, and it sped up CNN training 10-14x. But Batch Norm fails for language because sequences have variable lengths (position 5 may not exist in shorter sentences), inference batch size is often 1 (no batch to compute statistics over), and tokens at the same position across different sentences share nothing semantically. Layer Norm (Ba et al., 2016) instead normalizes across the feature dimension - each token computes mean and variance across its own features, independent of the batch. This is why Transformers use Layer Norm: each token is its own universe, normalization works at any batch size or sequence length, and statistics are semantically meaningful per token.
summary: Compares Batch Norm (normalizes down the batch column, used in CNNs) with Layer Norm (normalizes across one token's features, used in Transformers) and explains why language demands Layer Norm.
queries: What is the difference between batch normalization and layer normalization?
Why do Transformers use layer norm instead of batch norm?

text: Batch Normalization (Ioffe and Szegedy, 2015) normalizes across the batch dimension. Given a batch of examples, for each feature, compute the mean and variance across all examples in the batch. Concrete grid: a batch of 4 examples, each with 3 features. Example 1 = [0.5, 1.2, -0.3]. Example 2 = [0.8, 0.9, 0.1]. Example 3 = [0.3, 1.5, -0.5]. Example 4 = [0.6, 0.6, 0.2]. Batch Norm picks one feature column and computes mean and variance across the 4 examples. For Feature 2: mean = (1.2 + 0.9 + 1.5 + 0.6) / 4 = 1.05. Then it normalizes each example's value for that feature using that mean and variance.
summary: Defines Batch Normalization as normalizing each feature column across all examples in the batch, with a worked 4-example, 3-feature example computing mean 1.05 for Feature 2.

text: Why Batch Norm works for images and CNNs. Three reasons. (1) Shared statistics: images in a batch share similar structure - the feature 'edge detector at position (3,5)' has a meaningful distribution across a batch of cat photos because they all have edges in roughly similar places. (2) Fixed input size: images are always the same dimensions (e.g., 224x224). Position (3,5) means the same thing in every image, so computing statistics across the batch at that position makes sense. (3) Training speedup: BN reduced internal covariate shift (layer inputs changing distribution during training), letting CNNs train 10-14x faster, and it acted as a mild regularizer. Batch Norm revolutionized CNN training in 2015. But language is a fundamentally different domain.
summary: Explains why Batch Norm succeeds for CNNs: shared image structure across batch, fixed 224x224 input size, and 10-14x training speedup via reduced internal covariate shift.

text: Why Batch Norm fails for language. Three fundamental problems. (1) Variable Sequence Lengths: sentences have different lengths. In a batch of 4 sentences - 'I love cats' (3 tokens), 'The cat sat on the mat last week' (8 tokens), 'Hello' (1 token), 'Deep learning is fascinating' (4 tokens) - what is 'position 5' across this batch? Only one sentence even has a position 5. Normalizing across the batch at position 5 is meaningless because there's nothing to compare. (2) Batch Size 1 at Inference: when you chat with an LLM, your prompt is batch size 1. There's no batch to compute statistics over. Batch Norm must fall back to running averages stored from training - a fragile approximation that can drift from the true distribution. (3) Tokens Are Fundamentally Different: normalizing feature 42 across 'the', 'cat', 'sat', and 'love' from different sentences treats them as comparable, but they're completely different words with different roles. Unlike pixels at the same position in different images, tokens at position 3 across different sentences share nothing semantically.
summary: Lists three reasons Batch Norm fails for language: variable sequence lengths make positions non-comparable, batch size 1 at inference forces fragile running averages, and tokens at the same position across sentences share no semantic meaning.

text: Layer Normalization (Ba et al., 2016) normalizes across the feature dimension. For each token independently, compute mean and variance across its own features. Same 4x3 grid as before. Layer Norm picks one example row and computes mean and variance across its 3 features. For Example 2 = [0.8, 0.9, 0.1]: mean = (0.8 + 0.9 + 0.1) / 3 = 0.60. Each token normalizes itself independently. Three properties matter. No batch dependency: each token normalizes using only its own features, works with batch size 1. Any sequence length: token 5 normalizes itself the same way whether the sentence has 5 or 500 tokens. Semantically meaningful: normalizing across a single token's features rescales its representation without mixing different words' statistics.
summary: Defines Layer Normalization as per-token normalization across feature dimensions, with worked example mean 0.60 for [0.8, 0.9, 0.1], highlighting batch independence, length independence, and semantic validity.
