client to load balancer to replica flow

text: This chapter covers replication and high availability for vector databases. Vector search is read-heavy (10-100 reads per write), so production systems run N read replicas behind a load balancer to scale QPS linearly. A leader-follower pattern handles writes: the leader accepts writes and ships them asynchronously to followers, producing replication lag (50 ms steady, 200 ms during bursts, 2 s during heavy spikes/rebuilds). Reads in the lag window see stale results. On leader failure, Raft consensus promotes a follower; async replication risks losing the in-flight write window, while sync replication achieves zero data loss at ~2x write latency. Durability comes from layering WAL on disk, periodic snapshots, and source-of-truth rehydration. Recovery time for 100M vectors: snapshot+WAL replay 10 min - 2 hours, WAL-only ~12 hours, full re-embed from source 1-14 days. RTO budget = snapshot cadence + WAL replay rate.
summary: Full overview of read replicas, leader-follower writes, replication lag, failover semantics, durability layers, and recovery time tradeoffs.
queries: replication and high availability vector database
how do read replicas work for vector search
leader follower replication for ANN index
what happens when leader dies in vector DB
RTO recovery time objective vector database
data loss on failover vector DB
ensure no data loss on leader crash
vector DB cluster recovery from RAM loss

text: Vector search is read-heavy: most production workloads ship 10-100 queries per write. Run several identical copies of the index (read replicas) behind a load balancer and let queries spread out. Each replica holds the full index in RAM and answers independently. Throughput scales linearly with replica count up to the write rate the leader can distribute. QPS capacity ≈ replicas · per-replica QPS. Memory cost ≈ replicas · per-replica RAM. Reads scale cheaply; you pay in memory rather than latency. Every managed vector DB exposes replicas as a knob.
summary: Read replicas are N identical full copies of the index behind a load balancer, scaling read QPS linearly at the cost of memory.
queries: what are read replicas in vector database
how do read replicas scale QPS
load balancer in front of vector replicas
ratio of reads to writes in vector search
memory cost of replicating vector index
do replicas hold full copy of index
load balancing ANN queries across copies
how many replicas do I need for vector search
are vector replicas independent or coordinated

text: Writes go to a single leader (primary). The leader applies the write and ships it asynchronously to followers, who apply it and become eventually consistent. Replication lag is the gap between leader-applied and follower-applied. Typical lag: steady state 50 ms (P50 normal load), moderate spike 200 ms (write burst, healthy pipeline), heavy spike or rebuild 2 s (followers fall behind). Leader accepts at t=0; followers apply at t=50 ms to 2 s. Queries in the lag window see stale results. "I just added a document but search doesn't find it" is almost always lag, not a bug. Either wait out the window, or route the read-after-write to the leader.
summary: Leader-follower writes with async replication produce 50 ms to 2 s lag, causing stale reads inside the lag window.
queries: why do my new docs not show up immediately
read after write consistency vector search
how long until follower catches up
fix stale read in vector DB
route reads to leader for consistency

text: If the leader fails, a follower is promoted to primary via a consensus algorithm (Raft in most modern vector DBs). Failover timeline: t=0 ms leader processes write W_{n+1}; t=10 ms leader crashes - W_{n+1} never replicated; t=50 ms followers notice, Raft election starts; t=500 ms new leader promoted, cluster accepts writes again. Lost window: writes in-flight at t=0 to t=10 ms. Async replication (default) gives fast writes (ack at leader), at-most-50-ms lag, but possible data loss on failover. Sync replication gives slower writes (ack only after one follower applies), zero data loss on failover, but write latency ≈ 2x async. Most vector DBs default to async; pick sync only when a few lost writes in a rare failover would cost more than steady-state latency.
summary: On leader crash, Raft promotes a follower in ~500 ms; async replication risks losing in-flight writes while sync replication trades 2x write latency for zero data loss.
queries: what happens when leader crashes vector DB
data loss on failover vector database
when should I use sync replication
ack on leader vs ack on follower

text: Vector indexes live in RAM for speed. RAM is volatile - if every node restarts at once, durability depends on the persistence model. Three patterns, usually layered: WAL on disk - every write appended to a log before ack, RPO = 0 (synchronous) or 1 batch. Periodic snapshots - dump the index to disk every N minutes, RPO = interval, faster startup. Rehydrate from source - re-read every doc from the app DB and re-embed, RPO = 0 but slow. If every RAM copy dies, the index is only as durable as its WAL + snapshot. The source-of-truth DB is the ultimate recovery path. Many teams never test full cluster loss; the first time it happens they discover snapshot cadence is 24 hours and WAL retention is 1 hour, leaving them stuck re-embedding for days.
summary: Durability comes from layering WAL, periodic snapshots, and source-of-truth rehydration, each with different RPO and recovery characteristics.
queries: how is vector index made durable
WAL write ahead log vector database
RPO recovery point objective vector DB
what if every RAM copy dies
source of truth database for vectors
test full cluster loss vector DB

text: Worst case: entire cluster dies. Recovery time depends on what is cached. For a 100M-vector corpus: snapshot + WAL replay takes 10 min to 2 hours (disk I/O bound, graph pages stream in); WAL-only replay with no snapshot takes ~12 hours (every insert replayed from scratch); re-embed from source DB takes 1 to 14 days (embedding API throughput plus dollars billed per token). RTO budget = snapshot cadence + WAL replay rate. Pick snapshot interval that matches your RTO target. Re-embed is the fallback of last resort, measured in days. Put this math into the runbook before a real outage; the cost of a one-day outage at 10K QPS usually dwarfs the cost of snapshots every 30 minutes.
summary: Recovery from total cluster loss for 100M vectors ranges from 10 minutes (snapshot+WAL) to 14 days (full re-embed); pick snapshot cadence to hit your RTO.
queries: how long to recover 100M vector index
recovery time after total cluster loss
cost of one day vector search outage

text: Hybrid-search flow diagram with a green query icon on the left that splits into two parallel lanes. The upper cyan lane flows to a Vector ANN box emitting a top-N ranked list. The lower yellow lane flows to a BM25 box emitting another top-N ranked list. Both lists arrive at a green merge node on the right labeled RRF (Reciprocal Rank Fusion). Illustrates the parallel-ranker architecture of hybrid search: two independent retrievers run side by side and a score-free rank fusion step merges their outputs into a single final ranking.
summary: Diagram of hybrid-search architecture: query fans out to parallel Vector ANN and BM25 lanes, each producing top-N, merged at an RRF node.
queries: diagram of vector and BM25 in parallel
fan out to vector and lexical diagram

text: Hybrid search recovers the literal-token signal that pure vector embeddings smooth away by running BM25 keyword search and vector ANN in parallel and merging their ranked lists. BM25 scores docs by IDF times saturating term-frequency with Lucene defaults k1=1.2, b=0.75. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60 combines the two ranked lists score-free as sum of 1/(k+rank). Hybrid wins when queries mix exact terms (SKUs, proper nouns, rare tokens like 'tabby') with semantic intent ('cat genetics'); pure vector misses literals, pure BM25 returns off-topic exact matches. Production deployments use weighted RRF score = alpha*RRF_vec + beta*RRF_bm25, default alpha=0.7 beta=0.3 for natural language, flipped for code/catalog. Worth it for mixed queries, multi-lingual, RAG; skip for pure chat or strict <10ms latency.
summary: Hybrid search merges vector ANN with BM25 via RRF to recover exact-token retrieval that pure embeddings lose.
queries: when to use hybrid search recap
hybrid search architecture in one place
BM25 RRF weighted alpha beta summary

text: Semantic embeddings reward meaning - two docs saying the same thing with different words end up close in embedding space. The flip side: docs containing unique identifiers like SKU-A4291, proper nouns like 'Dr. Whiskers', or rare tokens like 'kernel panic 0x7B' get no special treatment - the exact token is smoothed into its neighborhood. Semantic search silently misses lookups where the word itself is the signal. What vectors lose: SKUs and IDs (SKU-A4291, ORD-88213) need exact match; proper nouns (Rajul, Mumbai, AlphaFold) are rare in training; rare terms (Tabby, kernel panic, RoPE) get blurred into neighbors. Vector search excels at meaning, misses literal tokens. Hybrid search combines vectors with keyword search to recover both. The rescue is classic BM25 lexical search running alongside the vector index - merge the two ranked lists and you keep the best of each.
summary: Why pure vector search fails on exact-token lookups like SKUs, proper nouns, and rare technical terms.
queries: why does vector search miss exact terms
why semantic search fails on SKUs
why dense vectors lose rare tokens
why I need keyword search alongside vectors
why pure vector retrieval misses identifiers
embeddings vs keyword search exact match
what is hybrid search and why use it
why we need BM25 plus vectors

text: BM25 is the lexical-search workhorse, shipped in every production search engine since Lucene. For a query of terms t, a document d scores by summing the IDF of each term (rare terms count more) times a saturating term-frequency contribution (more appearances help, with diminishing returns). Formula: BM25(q, d) = sum over t in q of IDF(t) * TF_norm(t, d). IDF(t) = log(N / df(t)) - inverse document frequency. TF_norm(t, d) = tf(t, d) * (k1 + 1) / (tf(t, d) + k1 * length-adjustment). Standard Lucene defaults: k1 ~ 1.2, b ~ 0.75. Worked example on cat corpus, query 'cats domesticated': Doc 1 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores' BM25=4.82 rank 1; Doc 5 'Tigers are striped cats' BM25=2.30 rank 2; Doc 3 'Lions are big cats that live in Africa' BM25=2.10 rank 3; Doc 8 'The dog chased the cat' BM25=1.90 rank 4; Doc 7 'Kittens grow up to be cats' BM25=1.70 rank 5. Doc 1 wins because it contains both query terms and is the shortest match. BM25's length-normalization keeps it fair across docs of different sizes.
summary: BM25 lexical scoring formula combines IDF and saturating term frequency with length normalization.

text: Given a query, fire both retrieval systems simultaneously. The vector index returns its top-N by embedding similarity; the BM25 index returns its top-N by lexical score. Neither list needs to know the other exists at this stage - they each do what they are good at. Merging is a separate step. Vector returns top-N by similarity, BM25 returns top-N by lexical score, then merge them. The two systems can even live on different nodes. The only shared state is the query itself and the final merge point. Architecture: query splits into two parallel lanes - upper lane to Vector ANN producing top-N ranked list, lower lane to BM25 producing top-N ranked list, both feeding into an RRF merge node. This is the parallel-ranker architecture of hybrid search.
summary: Hybrid search runs vector ANN and BM25 in parallel as independent rankers, each producing its own top-N list.
queries: how does hybrid search architecture work
vector ANN and BM25 in parallel
how do you combine vector and keyword search
can vector and BM25 run on separate nodes
fan out to vector and BM25
how to merge two ranked lists

text: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines two ranked lists without needing their raw scores - BM25 and cosine live on different scales. For each doc, compute 1 / (k + rank) on every ranker that returned it, then sum. The constant k dampens the contribution of low-ranking docs; the Lucene-standard value is k = 60 from the 2009 TREC paper. Formula: RRF(d) = sum over r in rankers of 1 / (k + rank_r(d)). RRF on cat corpus example: Doc 1 vec rank 2, BM25 rank 1, calc 1/62 + 1/61 = 0.0326 (winner); Doc 3 vec rank 1, BM25 rank 3, 1/61 + 1/63 = 0.0323; Doc 5 vec rank 4, BM25 rank 2, 1/64 + 1/62 = 0.0317; Doc 7 vec rank 3, BM25 rank 5, 1/63 + 1/65 = 0.0313; Doc 8 vec rank 5, BM25 rank 4, 1/65 + 1/64 = 0.0310. Doc 1 wins because it ranked well on both. RRF rewards doc consensus; docs only one ranker likes fall below the consensus winners.
summary: Reciprocal Rank Fusion merges ranked lists by summing 1/(k+rank) with k=60 to reward documents both rankers agree on.
queries: how to merge ranked lists without raw scores
how to combine different score scales

text: Concrete side-by-side for query 'tabby cat genetics'. Pure vector finds semantically cat-ish docs and misses 'tabby': 1) Lions are big cats (near miss), 2) Tigers are striped cats (near miss), 3) Cats are small carnivores, 4) Kittens grow up to be cats, 5) My cat sat on the mat. No hit on tabby - the actual topic. Pure BM25 finds the exact 'tabby' hit but ranks unrelated fluff with the word in it: 1) Tabby cat coat color genetics, 2) Abyssinian cat tabby pattern, 3) Genetics 101 textbook (unrelated), 4) Random tabby mention in a tweet, 5) Tabby bar and grill menu. Off-topic exact matches rank high. Hybrid (RRF) merges: 1) Tabby cat coat color genetics, 2) Abyssinian cat tabby pattern, 3) Tigers are striped cats, 4) Cats are small carnivores, 5) Kittens grow up to be cats. Tabby + genetics concentrated at the top. Hybrid wins when the query has both exact and semantic intent. Pure vector loses 'tabby', pure BM25 loses 'cat genetics'. If product queries ever include a specific term that must match exactly, hybrid is almost always worth enabling.
summary: Worked comparison showing pure vector misses 'tabby', pure BM25 returns off-topic exact matches, hybrid RRF concentrates relevant results at top.
queries: hybrid search vs vector search example
when does hybrid search beat vector
side by side hybrid vs pure vector
why hybrid wins on mixed queries
comparison hybrid pure vector pure BM25

text: Production deployments often weight the two rankers. Standard form: score(d) = alpha * RRF_vec(d) + beta * RRF_bm25(d). Typical defaults: alpha = 0.7, beta = 0.3 when corpus is mostly natural language; flip to 0.3/0.7 for code or catalog data where literal tokens dominate. Tune via held-out recall on your own data via A/B tests. When hybrid wins: mixed natural-language plus SKU queries, multi-lingual or technical corpora, long-tail identifier lookups, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). When hybrid is not worth it: pure chat or FAQ where vectors are already great, very short queries with no identifiers, strict latency budgets under 10 ms, BM25 index is itself unmaintained. Weighted hybrid adds operational complexity - two indexes, two query paths, one merge. Turn it on only after you measure a specific quality gap vectors alone cannot close.
summary: Weighted RRF tunes alpha/beta per data type and hybrid is only worth its operational complexity for specific workload patterns.
queries: when is hybrid search worth it
when not to use hybrid search

text: Cross-encoder architecture diagram. Row of input token boxes labeled [CLS], Query, Query, [SEP], Doc, Doc, Doc, Doc, [SEP] sits at bottom, connected by arrows up into single wide yellow transformer rectangle spanning all tokens labeled "Transformer (full attention across all tokens)". Green circle labeled Score sits at top connected by arrow from transformer. Illustrates that cross-encoder rerankers run full attention over concatenated query and doc tokens in one forward pass producing one scalar relevance score.
summary: Diagram of cross-encoder: concatenated [CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP] tokens enter one transformer with full attention, outputs single Score.
queries: CLS query SEP doc SEP diagram
full attention across query and doc visualization
how query and doc combine in transformer

text: Rerankers implement two-stage retrieval for vector search. Stage 1 uses fast ANN (HNSW/IVF-PQ) over a billion-vector corpus in ~5 ms to fetch top-100 candidates. Stage 2 runs a slower cross-encoder over those 100 candidates in ~100 ms to re-score and return top-10. Cross-encoders concatenate query and doc tokens ([CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP]) through one transformer forward pass with full attention, outperforming bi-encoder cosine by +10 to +20 MRR@10 because tokens interact directly. Cost scales linearly with candidate count and GPU class (T4: 5 ms/pair, A10: 1 ms, A100: 0.5 ms, H100: 0.3 ms). Production options: Cohere Rerank 3 (managed), BGE-reranker v2 (open weights from BAAI), MS-MARCO cross-encoders (MiniLM/BERT-base baseline), RankGPT/LLM-as-judge (highest quality, 10x latency). Reranker cannot recover docs missing from stage-1 candidate set.
summary: Two-stage retrieval pattern: fast ANN fetches top-100 candidates, slow cross-encoder rescores to top-10 with much better quality.
queries: what is a reranker in vector search
how much latency does reranking add
reranker GPU cost A10 A100 H100
can reranker fix bad stage 1 recall

text: Two-stage retrieval: ANN returns top-100, rerank returns top-10. Accurate retrieval not need be accurate everywhere - only at top. Stage 1 use ANN to quickly pull 100 roughly-relevant candidates from billion-vector index. Stage 2 is slower but smarter model that re-scores those 100 candidates. Total cost: fast over billion, slow over hundred. Win: stage-2 accuracy matters only on k=100, not on N=1B. Stage 1: Corpus N = 1B vectors. ANN latency ~5 ms per query (HNSW). Output top-100 candidates, may include some near-misses. Candidate quality fine at k=100, not at k=10. Comparison table: Stage 1 - retrieval, 5 ms, HNSW / IVF-PQ, 1B -> 100. Stage 2 - rerank, 100 ms, Cross-encoder, 100 -> 10. Final, ~105 ms, Combined, 10. Two-stage pattern standard in search engines, ads systems, RAG pipelines. Only real question is what stage-2 model is.
summary: Two-stage retrieval splits work: stage 1 ANN over 1B vectors in 5ms returns 100 candidates; stage 2 reranker over 100 in 100ms returns final 10.
queries: what does ANN do in two-stage retrieval
why not rerank the whole corpus
why is top-100 easier than top-10
accuracy at top vs accuracy everywhere
why stage 1 must have good recall

text: Cross-encoder: query and doc concatenated into one transformer pass. Cross-encoder takes query and candidate doc, concatenates their tokens into one input stream (often "[CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP]"), runs whole thing through transformer, emits single relevance score. Because query and doc tokens share attention inside stack, model can condition on exactly which part of doc matches which part of query. Architecture: input row of tokens [CLS], Query, Query, [SEP], Doc, Doc, Doc, Doc, [SEP] feeds into single transformer with full attention across all tokens, outputs one Score. Formula: Input = [CLS] query [SEP] doc [SEP]. One transformer pass, one scalar output. Score = P(doc is relevant to query). Architecture dead simple. Expense is running it per candidate: each doc is full forward pass through model.
summary: Cross-encoder concatenates query and doc tokens, runs full attention through one transformer, outputs single relevance score per pair.
queries: CLS SEP token format for reranker
one forward pass per candidate doc
what is P(doc relevant to query)

text: Bi-encoder cosine cannot see query-doc token interactions. Why cross-encoder better than just comparing two vectors? Bi-encoder produces one vector per query and one per doc, then measures cosine. All token-level information collapsed into those two vectors before they meet. Cross-encoder keeps tokens separate and lets full attention decide which interactions matter. Bi-encoder (stage 1): Query -> encoder -> q_vec. Doc -> encoder -> d_vec. Score = cosine(q_vec, d_vec). No token-level interaction. Everything pooled into single vector first. Cross-encoder (stage 2): Concat(query, doc) -> transformer. Full attention across all tokens. Score = one scalar. Query tokens attend to doc tokens and vice versa. Maximally informed score. Typical quality jump: +10 to +20 MRR@10 over bi-encoder alone. MS-MARCO leaderboards: cross-encoders win on almost every benchmark. Price: cannot pre-compute cross-encoder scores way you pre-compute embeddings. Every (query, doc) pair is fresh forward pass.
summary: Bi-encoders pool query and doc into separate vectors before scoring, losing token interactions; cross-encoders run joint attention for +10 to +20 MRR@10 gain.
queries: why must rerank run at query time
full attention across query doc tokens

text: Rerank: score each candidate, sort, return top-10. Once stage 1 supplied 100 candidates, run cross-encoder once per candidate with same query. Sort by score, return top-10 to user. Rerank does not change which candidates exist - only changes their ordering. Bad stage-1 candidate set cannot be fixed at stage 2. Pseudocode: For candidate d in top-100: score(d) = cross_encoder(query, d). Sort candidates by score desc. Return top-10. Example reordering table (Doc, Text, ANN rank, Rerank): Doc 1 "Cats are small domesticated carnivores" ANN=4 Rerank=1. Doc 7 "Kittens grow up to be cats" ANN=2 Rerank=2. Doc 3 "Lions are big cats that live in Africa" ANN=1 Rerank=3. Doc 4 "My cat sat on the mat" ANN=7 Rerank=4. Doc 5 "Tigers are striped cats" ANN=3 Rerank=5. Reranker not psychic - stage 1 has to include right doc in its top-100 for reranker to promote it. Recall at stage 1 still upstream bottleneck.
summary: Reranker runs cross_encoder(query, d) for each of 100 candidates, sorts descending, returns top-10; cannot recover docs missing from stage 1.
queries: reranker promotes doc from rank 4 to rank 1
ANN rank vs rerank rank example
why bad stage 1 cannot be fixed

text: Latency cost: 100 rerank runs about 100 ms on A10 GPU. Cross-encoders are small transformers - BERT-base or BERT-large sized. On modern GPU, one (query, doc) forward pass ~1 ms. 100 candidates = ~100 ms latency added on top of stage 1. Big enough to notice, small enough to justify for quality-critical paths. GPU latency table (GPU, Per-pair, 100 candidates, QPS cap): T4 (older) 5 ms, 500 ms, 2/sec. A10 1 ms, 100 ms, 10/sec. A100 0.5 ms, 50 ms, 20/sec. H100 0.3 ms, 30 ms, 33/sec. Formula: Reranker latency = candidates x per-pair cost. Batch all 100 pairs on one GPU to hit numbers above. Dropping to top-50 halves latency. In RAG, extra 100 ms usually trades against LLM call that already costs 500 ms. Rerankers popular because they fit comfortably in that budget.
summary: Reranking 100 candidates costs ~100 ms on A10 GPU (1 ms per pair); scales linearly with batch size and GPU class from T4 to H100.
queries: A10 vs A100 vs H100 reranker speed

text: Production models: Cohere Rerank, BGE-reranker, MS-MARCO cross-encoders. Three families dominate production. Cohere managed API ships general-purpose rerankers with simple REST call. BGE and similar open-weight models from BAAI run locally on GPU. MS-MARCO cross-encoders are classic open-source baseline and still competitive. Each has different latency, quality, operational stories. Model cards: Cohere Rerank 3 - Managed API, ~400M params, Multi-lingual, context 4K tokens, simple REST. BGE-reranker v2 - Open weights, Base 268M / large 560M, BAAI release, self-host on A10/A100, BGE-reranker-m3 is multilingual. MS-MARCO cross-encoder - Open weights, MiniLM ~33M, BERT-base 110M, Sentence-Transformers, classic baseline, smallest latency. RankGPT / LLM-as-judge - Managed LLM, Listwise LLM-driven, Highest quality, 10x latency, expensive. Strategy: Start with MS-MARCO MiniLM as cheap baseline. Move to BGE-reranker or Cohere when need quality. Reserve LLM-as-judge for offline evals. Swap reranker without touching stage 1. Interface always (query, candidates) -> reordered candidates, makes A/B tests straightforward.
summary: Production reranker families: Cohere Rerank (API), BGE-reranker (open weights, BAAI), MS-MARCO cross-encoders (MiniLM/BERT baseline), RankGPT (LLM-as-judge for offline).

text: Diagram: 3x5 token-level similarity grid for ColBERT max-sim. Rows are query tokens (Cats, Live, Outside); columns are doc tokens (Cats, Are, Small, Carnivores, That). Each cell shows the cosine similarity between that query token and doc token: row 1 [0.94, 0.22, 0.18, 0.35, 0.21] max 0.94; row 2 [0.11, 0.17, 0.15, 0.25, 0.28] max 0.28; row 3 [0.19, 0.14, 0.21, 0.17, 0.26] max 0.26. The max in each row is highlighted in green. The right-most 'Max' column accumulates row maxes; their sum (0.94 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 1.48) is the ColBERT max-sim score for this (query, doc) pair. The visual makes clear that each query token picks its single best doc-token match independently.
summary: Visualization of the 3x5 ColBERT similarity grid - query tokens on rows, doc tokens on columns, row maxes highlighted, summed to score 1.48.
queries: how to draw a ColBERT score table
highlighted row max in similarity matrix
what does the ColBERT scoring matrix look like

text: Multi-vector retrieval (ColBERT-style) keeps one contextual embedding per token instead of pooling each document into a single dense vector. ColBERT (Stanford 2020, Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT) encodes a ~200-token doc as ~200 vectors and a query as ~5 token vectors, then scores via late interaction max-sim: for each query token, take the maximum cosine/dot similarity against any doc token, sum those maxes. This rewards docs that match every query token somewhere, recovering rare-term and long-document fidelity that single-vector cosine averages away. Cost: storage balloons 20-100x (e.g. 1M docs at d=768, 200 tokens/doc => 600 GB float32, ~60 GB with PQ/ColBERTv2 residual compression vs 3 GB single-vector). Production support: Vespa native tensor max-sim, Qdrant native multi-vector since v1.10 (Jul 2024), Elasticsearch nested dense_vector 8.13+, Weaviate preview. Pinecone and pgvector do not support it natively. Use for RAG over long docs and dense technical corpora.
summary: This chapter overview of ColBERT-style multi-vector retrieval: per-token embeddings, max-sim late interaction, storage tradeoffs, and production support.
queries: why per-token embeddings beat pooled embeddings on long docs
how much memory does multi-vector retrieval need
when to use multi-vector retrieval over single vector
RAG over long documents retrieval quality

text: One vector per doc blurs token-level signal. A single dense embedding per document is the workhorse of vector search: compact, fast to compare, great on short text. For long docs (paragraphs, articles, chapters) the single-vector representation loses fidelity because every token is averaged into one 768-dim summary. Rare terms and specific phrasing get blurred into bulk meaning. Example: 'Cats are small domesticated carnivores that originated in the fertile crescent...' encoded into [0.81, 0.12, 0.45, ..., 0.22] (768 dims, one per doc) - every token's contribution is averaged into that one vector. Long doc + single vector = lossy compression. Multi-vector retrieval keeps one vector per token instead. This motivates ColBERT and the whole multi-vector family: keep more information, rank better, pay in storage.
summary: Single-vector embeddings average all tokens into one 768-dim vector, blurring rare terms in long docs - motivation for multi-vector retrieval.
queries: why does single vector embedding lose information on long documents
why average pooling hurts long doc retrieval
how do dense vectors blur rare terms
tradeoff between compactness and fidelity in embeddings
why one vector per document is not enough
what does mean pooling lose in BERT embeddings
single vector blur problem long passages
why rare terms disappear in pooled embeddings

text: ColBERT idea: one vector per token. ColBERT (Contextualized Late Interaction over BERT, Stanford 2020) keeps every token's contextual embedding instead of pooling to a single doc vector. A 200-token document becomes 200 vectors. Queries are also expanded to per-token embeddings (~5 query tokens for 'where do cats live'). Scoring happens at token granularity - the 'late interaction' - rather than after pooling. Single-vector retrieval: 1 vector per doc, cosine similarity, scalar output, fast and compact. Multi-vector ColBERT: ~200 vectors per doc (per token), max-sim aggregation across tokens, more accurate on long text. ColBERT is still a dense-embedding model; only the pooling step is dropped. The pipeline otherwise looks like any other BERT encoder.
summary: ColBERT keeps every BERT token's contextual embedding (~200 per doc, ~5 per query) instead of pooling, enabling token-granularity late interaction.
queries: how many vectors per document in ColBERT
what is late interaction in retrieval
how does ColBERT differ from a bi-encoder
BERT token embeddings retained for retrieval
what makes ColBERT a multi-vector model

text:
Max-sim: for each query token, find the best matching doc token, then sum. Given Q query vectors and D doc vectors, compute pairwise cosine (or dot) similarities - a Q x D matrix. For each query token, take the maximum similarity across all doc tokens. Sum those maxes over the query tokens. This is 'max-sim over late interaction'. It rewards a doc that matches each query token somewhere, even if no single doc token is a perfect match for everything. Formula:

score(q, d) = sum over i in query tokens of  max over j in doc tokens of  q_i · d_j

Sum of per-query-token max similarities against all doc tokens. Max-sim keeps the best signal per query token. A doc that matches 'cats' perfectly but does not mention 'genetics' at all scores lower than one that hits both, even if neither match is perfect.
summary: Max-sim score = sum over query tokens of the maximum similarity to any doc token; rewards docs that match each query token somewhere.
queries: what is the late interaction scoring formula
sum of max similarities over query tokens
why use max not average across doc tokens
Q x D similarity matrix scoring
how does multi-vector retrieval rank documents
max over j of q_i dot d_j
advantages of max aggregation over mean pooling for retrieval

text:
Max-sim walkthrough on the cat corpus. Query 'cats live outside' (3 tokens). Doc 'Cats are small carnivores that' (5 tokens). Compute the 3x5 similarity matrix, highlight the max in each row, sum them.

Grid (rows = query tokens, cols = doc tokens):
              Cats   Are   Small  Carnivores  That    Max
Cats          0.94   0.22  0.18   0.35        0.21    0.94
Live          0.11   0.17  0.15   0.25        0.28    0.28
Outside       0.19   0.14  0.21   0.17        0.26    0.26

Score = 0.94 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 1.48. Strong pick on 'cats' drags the overall score up. No doc token is perfect for 'live' or 'outside', but each query token is still scored against its nearest neighbor. This robustness is what single-vector cosine throws away.
summary: Worked example: query 'cats live outside' vs doc 'Cats are small carnivores that' yields max-sim score 0.94 + 0.28 + 0.26 = 1.48.
queries: how to calculate max-sim score by hand
query doc token similarity grid example
how max picks per row in late interaction
showing max-sim aggregation on cat corpus
how late interaction handles imperfect matches
max per row sum scoring example

text: Storage cost: N x tokens x d bytes (20 to 100x larger). The tradeoff is memory. Single-vector retrieval stores one 768-dim vector per doc. Multi-vector stores one per token. At ~200 tokens/doc that is 200x more vectors. Compression (PQ on the token vectors) and shorter vector widths reduce overhead to 20-50x in practice, but ColBERT-style indexes are always much bigger. Memory at N = 1M, d = 768, 200 tokens/doc: Single-vector float32 = 3 GB (1x baseline). ColBERT raw float32 = 600 GB (~200x larger). ColBERT compressed via PQ = ~60 GB (~20x larger). Multi-vector storage = N · tokens · d bytes, typical 20-100x single-vector footprint. PQ and ColBERTv2's residual compression take it to ~20x at good quality. Multi-vector is a great pick when retrieval quality matters more than cost; for 1B-scale workloads expect to pay for extra nodes.
summary: Multi-vector indexes cost N·tokens·d bytes - 200x raw, ~20x with PQ/ColBERTv2 residual compression - so 1M docs goes from 3 GB single-vector to 60-600 GB.
queries: how much memory does ColBERT use
N times tokens times d storage formula
is ColBERT worth the storage cost
storage tradeoff ColBERT vs single vector

text: Production support: Vespa, Qdrant native multi-vector, Elasticsearch. A few production systems handle multi-vector natively. Vespa: native tensor fields with max-sim as a built-in ranking expression, since v7 in Yahoo production. Qdrant multi-vector collections since v1.10 (July 2024), per-vector payload. Elasticsearch nested dense_vector fields with script-score max-sim, 8.13+. Weaviate multi-vector preview via RAG module experiments since 1.25. Multi-vector is table stakes in most production systems now; Vespa and Qdrant are common production picks. Pinecone and pgvector currently do not support it natively. Worth it for RAG on long documents, search over dense technical corpora, and any case where single-vector retrieval has been quality-limited. Swap is straightforward where supported.
summary: Production multi-vector support: Vespa tensor fields, Qdrant v1.10+, Elasticsearch 8.13 nested dense_vector, Weaviate preview - Pinecone and pgvector lack it.
queries: which vector databases support ColBERT natively
production support for late interaction retrieval
comparing ColBERT support across vector DBs

text: Embedding lifecycle and re-embedding covers what happens when the embedding model behind a production vector index becomes outdated. A 500M-doc index built on OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002 (1536 dims) faces deprecation as text-embedding-3-large (3072 dims) becomes the default. Dimension mismatch forces a new collection. Three migration options: re-embed everything and swap (~$32,500 one-time, requires retained source text), parallel indexes with dual-write and shadow-query (low risk, double storage), or pin the old model (cheap short-term, decays via drift / deprecation). All paths require drift monitoring with a ground-truth eval set tracking recall@k.
summary: Overview of embedding model lifecycle, dimension migration, three re-embedding strategies, and drift monitoring.
queries: how to handle deprecated embedding models
what to do when embedding model is deprecated
should I pin the embedding model version
ground truth eval set for retrieval

text: Two years ago a team shipped a retrieval system with 500M documents indexed via OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002 (d = 1536). Today that model is deprecated and text-embedding-3-large (d = 3072) is the new default. Every vector in the existing index is 'wrong' in the sense that it was produced by an encoder that is no longer state of the art. Timeline of an indexed-years-ago corpus: 2024: 500M docs embedded with ada-002 (d = 1536). 2025: OpenAI upgrades to text-embedding-3-large (d = 3072). 2026: ada-002 announced deprecated. Production system runs on two-year-old encoders. This is the most common silent pain in production vector search: models change and what used to be state-of-the-art embedding decays into yesterday's encoder.
summary: Sets up the problem: a 500M-doc index on ada-002 is now stale because the model was deprecated and replaced by text-embedding-3-large.
queries: why does my vector index become stale
what happens when OpenAI deprecates an embedding model
500 million vectors indexed years ago what to do
encoder upgrade in production vector search
old embedding model still in production
vector DB built on deprecated model
how often do embedding models change
production system on two year old encoder

text: Dimension mismatch is a hard migration. The new model outputs a different vector dimension: ada-002 emits 1536 dims; text-embedding-3-large emits 3072. Vector DBs pin the collection to one dimension at creation time - you cannot mix 1536-dim vectors with 3072-dim vectors in the same index. Every migration path has to deal with this up front. Old index: 1536 dims (ada-002). New index: 3072 dims (text-embedding-3-large). Vector DB collections are dimension-pinned. No mixing 1536-dim and 3072-dim in the same index. Any migration starts with creating a new collection. Matryoshka embeddings partly ease this: truncating a 3072-dim Matryoshka to 1536 is still a sensible embedding, though not from the older model.
summary: Vector DB collections are dimension-pinned, so a different-dim new model forces creation of a new collection.
queries: can I mix different embedding dimensions in one index
vector database dimension pinned at creation
why must I create a new collection to upgrade embeddings
dimension mismatch in pinecone or weaviate
do vector indexes support multiple dimensions
what happens if model output dim changes

text: Option 1: re-embed everything and swap indexes. The conceptually clean option. Keep the source text around (if you still have it), run every doc through the new model, build a fresh index, atomically swap. You pay the full re-embed cost one time. Re-embed cost estimate at 500M docs, 500 tokens each: Tokens: 500M times 500 = 250 billion. Price: $0.00013 per 1K tokens (text-embedding-3-large). Total: ~$32,500 one-time bill. Time: ~1 week at API rate limits, parallelized. Requires retained source text (huge gotcha if you dropped it). Cost scales linearly with N and token budget. Clean cut-over, no two versions to maintain. Re-embedding is often the cheapest option measured over a year. The upfront bill is what blocks it - engineering teams are reluctant to spend five figures on something that looks like cleanup.
summary: First migration option: re-embed the entire corpus through the new model and atomically swap, with a concrete $32,500 / 1-week cost estimate.
queries: how much does it cost to re-embed 500 million documents
atomic index swap pattern for embeddings
OpenAI embedding API pricing for migration
do I need to keep source text for re-embedding
what if I dropped the source text after embedding
cost of re-embedding linear in N
engineering reluctance to spend on cleanup

text: Option 2: parallel indexes during migration, serve from old, populate new. Stand up the new index alongside the old. Dual-write every new or updated doc to both. Backfill the old corpus to the new index in the background. Compare quality on real traffic by shadow-querying. When the new index is caught up and its quality is verified, flip traffic. Then deprecate the old one. Parallel migration timeline: Week 0: create new collection, start dual-write on new docs. Week 1-3: backfill old corpus into new index in background. Week 4: shadow-query both, measure recall delta. Week 5: cutover traffic to new index. Week 6: retire old index. Traffic stays on the old index the whole time. Double storage cost during the overlap window. Quality can be flipped back if the new index underperforms. This is the standard low-risk path. It costs more - you run two indexes for weeks - but regressions never reach production traffic.
summary: Second migration option: parallel indexes with dual-write, background backfill, shadow-query verification, then cutover - low-risk but doubles storage.
queries: how to migrate vector index with zero downtime
shadow query pattern for retrieval migration
how long does parallel migration take
verifying new index quality before cutover

text: Option 3: pin the old model forever (pin and pray). The do-nothing option. Pin the embedding model version, keep using it, never migrate. Works until the provider deprecates the model or drift quietly erodes quality. Many teams end up here because upgrading feels optional; a year later they discover retrieval quality has decayed and they now face a forced migration under deadline pressure. The failure modes of pinning: Provider deprecation - vendor sunsets the old model, forced migration under a deadline. Silent drift - model did not change but the domain did, recall decays over time. New language or domain - content types the old model never saw are served poorly. Missed capability - competitors ship features (longer context, better multilingual) that the pinned model cannot match. Pinning is short-term cheap, long-term expensive. Works fine for 6-12 months, pain compounds after. Drift is invisible unless you monitor it. Pinning is a valid choice as long as you plan the exit. Shipping without a re-embedding story is the failure mode.
summary: Third option: pin the embedding model and never migrate - cheap short-term but invites deprecation, drift, and forced migration later.
queries: should I pin my embedding model version
what is pin and pray strategy for embeddings
risks of never upgrading embedding model
long term cost of pinning embedding model
when does pinning the model break

text: Drift monitoring: periodic ground-truth evals. The cure for silent quality decay is explicit monitoring. Keep a held-out set of (query, relevant-doc) pairs. Every week or month, run the current index against the eval set and record recall@k. Any sudden drop signals either a model change, a content change, or an index regression - each demands a different response. Drift monitoring stack: Ground-truth eval set - 1K to 10K (query, relevant-doc) pairs, refreshed quarterly. Weekly recall@10 run - run the eval, record the curve, alert on regression. Content-change monitor - track the embedding distribution (mean, variance) per ingest batch. Model-change signal - lock embedding model version, surface drift when you bump it. Monitor recall, drift, eval, or discover regression after users notice. Ground-truth sampling is cheap; a quality regression is not. Whatever migration option you pick, drift monitoring is the common prerequisite. Without it, none of the other three can be operated safely.
summary: Drift monitoring with a ground-truth eval set tracking recall@k is the prerequisite for any safe embedding migration path.
queries: how to monitor embedding drift in production
ground truth eval set for retrieval
how to detect retrieval quality regression
what is a query relevant doc pair eval
how large should retrieval eval set be
lock embedding model version to detect drift
silent quality decay in vector search

text: This chapter covers production observability for vector search systems: tracking percentile-based latency (P50/P95/P99/P99.9) instead of mean, periodic recall@k sampling against brute-force ground truth to catch ANN regressions, per-query cost telemetry (L3 cache hit rate, memory pages read, distance computations, CPU cycles), the ANN-Benchmarks QPS-vs-recall methodology for comparing index configs, dashboard design separating alert lines (P99 latency >100ms, recall@10 <0.95) from watch lines (cache hit rate ~0.85, memory usage), and the hidden-metric checklist (per-tenant latency, filtered-query recall, cold-start latency, fan-out tail, write-to-read lag, index build time, tombstone percent).
summary: Production observability stack for vector DBs: tail latencies, recall sampling, per-query cost signals, ANN-Benchmarks, dashboards, and hidden metrics.

text: Mean latency tells you almost nothing in production vector search. Queries are cheap most of the time; the slow tail ruins the user's day. Track percentile-based latency: P50 (median), P95 (worst that most users see), P99 (worst 1%). Alert budgets are written against the tail, not the average. Typical production targets for HNSW: P50 = 10 ms (median query), P95 = 30 ms (95% of queries faster), P99 = 80 ms (99% of queries faster), P99.9 = 200 ms (tail blows up here). Mean masks the tail. Alert on P99, watch P99.9, write the SLA against the tail. The P99 is usually 5-10x the P50. Investments in caching, SIMD, and index tuning show up in the tail, not the mean.
summary: Track P50/P95/P99/P99.9 latency percentiles for HNSW search; mean hides the tail and the tail is what hurts users.

text: ANN indexes are approximate; without active monitoring a recall regression goes unnoticed for months. The cure is periodic evaluation: sample K queries, compute the exact top-k via brute force on the full corpus, compare against what the ANN index returned. Recall@10 is the most common single number to track. Protocol: Step 1 - sample 1,000 queries from production traffic. Step 2 - for each, compute exact top-10 via brute force. Step 3 - compare against ANN top-10 (intersection / 10). Step 4 - average over the 1,000 queries = recall@10. Step 5 - repeat weekly, plot the trend. The sample is cheap compared to the damage of not knowing; even a slow brute-force run at 1K queries is minutes, not hours.
summary: Sample 1K production queries weekly, compute exact top-10 via brute force, compare to ANN top-10, average to track recall@10 trend.

text: Beyond top-level latency, modern vector DBs emit per-query cost signals you watch when latency regressions appear without any code change. Key signals: L3 cache hit rate (fraction of hot graph pages served from CPU cache), memory pages read (how many 4 KB pages touched per query), distance computations (how many candidate comparisons), CPU cycles per query (wall-clock profile of a typical query). A sudden dip in cache hit rate is the most common early warning - it usually means the working set grew or the graph layout changed after a rebuild.
summary: Per-query cost telemetry: cache hit rate, memory pages, distance comps, CPU cycles - cache-hit drop is earliest regression signal.

text: The open-source ANN-Benchmarks (github.com/erikbern/ann-benchmarks) is the standard way to compare index configurations. It plots queries per second (QPS) on the horizontal axis and recall on the vertical. Every point is a parameter combination; the Pareto frontier tells you what tradeoffs are even possible. X-axis: recall (0.90 to 1.00). Y-axis: QPS (log scale, queries per second). Each curve = one index family (HNSW, IVF-PQ, Vamana). Each point = one parameter combination. Winners sit on the Pareto frontier (upper-right). Methodology: same dataset, same hardware, same recall target. Fair comparison forces every system to trade QPS for recall. Run it on your data, not just the public datasets. ANN-Benchmarks shows up in almost every paper, blog, and vendor comparison.
summary: ANN-Benchmarks plots QPS vs recall across parameter combos; Pareto frontier shows tradeoffs across HNSW, IVF-PQ, Vamana.

text: A good production dashboard is the difference between catching a regression in 5 minutes vs a customer email 3 days later. Core panels split into ALERT lines (page on breach) and WATCH lines (track trend). ALERT panels: P99 latency threshold 100 ms (SLA violation if above for 5 minutes), Recall@10 threshold <0.95 (quality regression - investigate model or index). WATCH panels: cache hit rate ~0.85 (degrades before latency shows symptoms), index memory usage 60-70% RAM (growth predicts scale-up or shard). Keep the number of alerts small. Alerts on everything train the team to ignore them - two alerts you trust beats ten you mute.
summary: Dashboard separates ALERT panels (P99 latency, recall@10) from WATCH panels (cache hit rate, memory usage); few trusted alerts beat many ignored ones.

text: Latency and recall are headline metrics; the quiet ones that trip production: per-tenant latency (is one customer degrading the P99 for everyone?), filtered-query recall (does recall tank when a predicate is tight?), cold-start latency (first query after a new replica is up - how long?), fan-out tail (slowest shard on a multi-shard query?), write-to-read lag (time from upsert to queryable?), index build time (duration of the last full rebuild?), tombstone percent (delete pressure on the current graph?). Every production incident has a postmortem that ends in 'we were not capturing X'. Build the checklist before you need it. Rule of thumb: for every new feature (filtering, sharding, replication) add a monitor before you ship - the feature is cheap, the blind spot it creates is not.
summary: Hidden metrics that trip production: per-tenant latency, filtered recall, cold-start, fan-out tail, replication lag, build time, tombstone percent - add a monitor with every feature.

text: This chapter Capacity Planning & Cost Models walks through how to size a production vector search system. Starts with six sizing inputs (N vectors, d dimensions, QPS target, P99 target, filter selectivity, availability). Derives RAM via the formula RAM = N x (vec_bytes + graph_bytes) x cache_factor (~1.2) x frag_factor (~1.3) where vec_bytes = d x 4 for float32 and graph_bytes ~100 for HNSW or ~20 for IVF-PQ. CPU sized as cores = per-query-cost x QPS x headroom; HNSW typical = 30 hops x 10 us = 300 us per query. Worked example: 500M vectors at d=768, 200 QPS, P99 100 ms needs ~1.5 TB raw + 50 GB graph, ~2.4 TB with overhead, 6 r7i.24xlarge nodes (768 GB each), 2x replication = 12 nodes / 3 TB / ~96 cores. Monthly cost order-of-magnitude: Pinecone ~$30K, self-host Qdrant ~$8K, pgvector ~$5K. Decision lens: $ per million vectors per month (storage) and $ per million queries (compute), adjusted for ops capacity and feature needs.
summary: Sizing inputs, RAM and CPU formulas, worked 500M-vector example, vendor cost comparison, and per-million decision ratios.

Pinecone vs Qdrant vs pgvector cost

decision framework for picking a vector database

text: Inputs to size a vector search system. Good capacity planning starts with the inputs the workload gives you: how many vectors, how big each one is, how many queries per second, what latency budget, how selective the filters are, what availability the business needs. Six numbers determine almost everything about the provisioning decision. The six sizing inputs: N vectors (1M - 10B, example 500M), d dimensions (128 - 3072, example 768), QPS target (10 - 10K, example 200), P99 target (30 - 300 ms, example 100 ms), Filter selectivity (0.1% - 100%, example 5%), Availability (99% - 99.99%, example 99.9%). Everything that follows - memory, CPU, node count, cost - is derived from these six numbers plus the index choice.
summary: Six workload inputs (N, d, QPS, P99, selectivity, availability) drive every downstream sizing decision.
queries: what inputs do I need to size a vector database
what numbers matter for capacity planning
how to plan vector index hardware
what is the QPS target for vector search
how many dimensions in a vector
what numbers do I collect before sizing vector index

text: Memory formula: vectors + graph + cache + fragmentation. RAM is usually the gating resource. Total = vector storage + graph overhead + working-set cache headroom + allocator-fragmentation fudge factor. Each multiplier accumulates; undersize any one and P99 latency explodes. Formula: RAM = N x (vec_bytes + graph_bytes) x cache_factor x frag_factor. Cache_factor ~ 1.2 (20% headroom). Frag_factor ~ 1.3 (allocator). vec_bytes = d x 4 for float32, d for int8, m for PQ m-bytes. graph_bytes ~ 100 for HNSW, ~20 for IVF-PQ. Each multiplier matters - undersizing cache or fragmentation blows P99. Plan for 1.5x the naive 'N times vec_bytes'. Every production team learns this the hard way; cache and fragmentation factors are easy to forget and expensive to discover.
summary: RAM formula multiplies N by per-vector + graph bytes then by cache (~1.2) and fragmentation (~1.3) factors.
queries: how to calculate vector index memory
how much memory does PQ use
why is my vector index using more RAM than expected
memory per vector float32 int8 PQ
how to estimate RAM for HNSW
cache headroom for vector working set

text: CPU sizing: per-query ops x QPS x headroom. CPU capacity sizing is simpler: estimate per-query work, multiply by target QPS, add headroom for spikes and replication overhead. For HNSW at typical parameters, each query touches about 30 graph hops averaging 10 microseconds each - 300 microseconds of compute per query. CPU formula for HNSW: Per-query CPU = hops x us/hop. Typical: 30 hops x 10 us = 300 us. Cores = per-query-cost x QPS x headroom. Worked: 300 us x 200 QPS x 2x headroom = 120,000 us = 0.12 core-seconds/sec. Round up to 8-16 cores per replica with write and merge overhead. CPU is rarely the bottleneck when memory is sized correctly. Most production systems have plenty of CPU headroom and end up memory-bound first.
summary: CPU = per-query work (HNSW ~300 us) x QPS x headroom; usually 8-16 cores per replica suffice.
queries: how many CPU cores for HNSW
how many cores per replica vector DB
is CPU the bottleneck for vector search
memory bound vs CPU bound vector DB
core count for 200 QPS vector search
how to size CPU vector search

text: Worked example: 500M vectors x d=768 x 200 QPS. 500M docs, d = 768, HNSW index, 200 QPS, P99 target 100 ms, 99.9% availability. Compute the memory, CPU, and node count explicitly. Sizing walkthrough: Vectors: 500M x 3 KB = 1.5 TB. HNSW graph: 500M x 100 B = 50 GB. Cache + fragmentation (1.2 x 1.3 = 1.56): ~2.4 TB total. Spread across 6 nodes (r7i.24xlarge with 768 GB each). Each node holds ~100M vectors = 400 GB of the working set. Replicate 2x for availability: 12 nodes total, 3 TB RAM. Summary: Memory 3 TB across replicas (1.5 TB primary + replication). Nodes: 6 + 6 (primary shards + 1 replica each). CPU: ~96 cores (16 per node times 6 nodes). This is the shape of every well-sized deployment - start from raw numbers, apply cache and fragmentation factors, decide shard count from single-node ceiling, multiply by replication.
summary: Worked sizing for 500M x d=768 HNSW: ~2.4 TB RAM, 6 r7i.24xlarge nodes, 2x replication = 12 nodes / 3 TB / 96 cores.
queries: 768 GB RAM r7i node vector DB
how to figure out node count vector search
how to plan replication for 99.9% availability

text: Cost comparison: Pinecone pods vs self-host Qdrant vs pgvector. Cost depends entirely on who runs the infrastructure. Pinecone's managed pods include the ops team, at a premium. Self-hosted Qdrant on AWS is cheaper in compute but you run it yourself. pgvector on a big Postgres box is the cheapest, but the operational model has its own caveats. Monthly cost estimates for 500M x d=768 x 200 QPS (illustrative): Pinecone (pod-based) ~$30K - managed, opinionated scaling, no ops team needed. Self-host Qdrant on AWS ~$8K - you run it, ops load but great cost/feature ratio. pgvector on big Postgres ~$5K - SQL transactions, cheapest, ops burden + feature limits. Numbers are order-of-magnitude illustrations, not authoritative. Ratios are durable: Pinecone ~4x-6x self-host, pgvector cheapest but operationally limited. The real decision is ops capacity: a two-engineer team may rationally pay Pinecone's premium to skip the infrastructure grind.
summary: Monthly costs for 500M workload: Pinecone ~$30K, self-host Qdrant ~$8K, pgvector ~$5K - choice depends on ops capacity.
queries: managed vs self-hosted vector DB cost

text: Decision lens: cost per million vectors, cost per million queries. Normalize the cost comparison to two numbers: dollars per million vectors per month (storage cost) and dollars per million queries (compute cost). These ratios stay roughly constant as the workload changes, which makes them the cleanest metric for vendor comparisons and scale-up planning. Cost ratios as the decision lens: $ per million vectors per month - storage side, scales with N - Pinecone ~$60 / Qdrant ~$16 / pgvector ~$10. $ per million queries - compute side, scales with QPS - Pinecone ~$6 / Qdrant ~$1.5 / pgvector ~$1. The right decision framework = per-million cost ratios, adjusted for ops capacity + feature needs. Numbers change over time; the shape of the decision does not. With per-million ratios + ops capacity in hand, the team can reach a defensible vendor decision from first principles rather than marketing copy.
summary: Normalize vendor cost to $/million vectors/month (storage) and $/million queries (compute) for clean comparison.
queries: cost per million queries vector DB
how to compare vector database vendors
storage cost vs compute cost vector DB
ratio that stays constant as workload grows
marketing vs first principles vector DB

text: FAISS is Meta's 2017 open-source C++ ANN library, not a database. It ships every major production index (IVF, PQ, HNSW, IVF-PQ) on CPU and CUDA, exposes Python bindings, and is the benchmark every new algorithm is compared against. It deliberately omits persistence, REST API, filtering, ACID, and replication - those are what vector databases (Milvus, OpenSearch, Vespa, Azure AI Search, Qdrant, Pinecone, pgvector) add on top. The decision rule: FAISS to build a system, a DB to use one.
summary: This chapter frames FAISS as the algorithm library underneath most vector DBs, contrasts library vs database responsibilities, and gives the build-vs-use decision rule.
queries: What did I learn about FAISS?
Should I pick FAISS or a vector DB?
Why FAISS matters for vector search
Recap of FAISS algorithms and limits

text: FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is the library Meta open-sourced in 2017. It is not a database - it is a collection of C++ implementations of the core ANN algorithms: IVF, PQ, HNSW, IVF-PQ. When you use Milvus or OpenSearch or half the commercial systems on the market, FAISS code is somewhere down the stack actually computing distances. Timeline: 2017 Meta open-sources FAISS on GitHub; 2018 Python bindings mature, used across ML teams; 2020+ embedded inside Milvus, OpenSearch, Vespa cores; Today still the ANN reference implementation. FAISS = library, not a database. Gives you algorithms; everything else is on you. A library hands you the index; a database hands you the index plus persistence, filtering, replication, an API, and someone to page.
summary: FAISS is Meta's 2017 open-source C++ ANN library, not a database, that ships the core algorithms (IVF, PQ, HNSW, IVF-PQ) used inside many vector systems.
queries: Is FAISS a database or a library?
What language is FAISS written in?
Difference between a library and a vector database
Why is FAISS called the reference implementation?
Why FAISS is not a full vector database
What FAISS gives you and what it does not

text: FAISS does not pick one algorithm - it ships every major production-worthy index in a single library. You instantiate the one you want. IVF: cluster space with k-means, probe nprobe cells; medium-scale static corpora. PQ: compress each vector to m bytes via per-slot codebooks; memory-bound workloads. HNSW: layered small-world graph, log-N navigation; recall-sensitive, dynamic workloads. IVF-PQ: combine - cluster then PQ-encode residuals; 1B+ static vectors on a node. Backends: CPU (default) plus CUDA/GPU for IVF, PQ, IVF-PQ. GPU gives 10x to 100x throughput on high-QPS indexing. Every ANN and compression algorithm has a FAISS implementation. When a paper claims something beats HNSW, the comparison usually runs against the FAISS HNSW.
summary: FAISS ships IVF, PQ, HNSW, and IVF-PQ in one library on CPU plus GPU backends, making it the benchmark every new ANN algorithm gets compared against.
queries: What is IVF in FAISS used for?
When to use PQ in FAISS?
When to use HNSW in FAISS?
When to use IVF-PQ in FAISS?
How much faster is FAISS on GPU?
Why is FAISS the ANN benchmark?
Comparing FAISS HNSW to other libraries

text: FAISS is written in C++ for speed and exposes Python bindings so data scientists can call it directly from Jupyter or a training pipeline. Typical FAISS usage: import faiss; index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(d=768, M=16); index.add(corpus_vectors)  # N x 768 numpy array; D, I = index.search(query_vectors, k=10). Two-layer architecture: C++ core handles dense kernels, SIMD, CUDA. Python bindings provide numpy-native API, scripting, notebooks. The embed-in-your-pipeline ergonomics are why FAISS shows up inside training loops and evaluation scripts, not just at serving time.
summary: FAISS pairs a C++ SIMD/CUDA core with numpy-native Python bindings, letting you build an HNSW index and search it in four lines from a notebook.
queries: How do I use FAISS from Python?
How to add vectors to a FAISS index?
How to search a FAISS index?
Why is FAISS written in C++?
Use FAISS in a Jupyter notebook
Build HNSW index with FAISS d=768 M=16

text: FAISS gives you indices and distance computations. What it does not give you is every other thing a production system needs. Persistence: no WAL, no durable snapshots out of the box - if process dies, index is gone. REST API: no HTTP server, no auth, no rate limits - you write the serving layer. Filtering: no metadata index, no predicate support - queries on tenant_id must be post-filtered. ACID/replication: no transactions, no leader-follower, no HA - single-node only; crashes lose writes. FAISS provides the algorithm, not the system. Everything above is a vector database's job. The real vector databases (Qdrant, Pinecone, Milvus, pgvector) are all answers to the question 'what do we build on top of FAISS-style algorithms?'
summary: FAISS provides only algorithms; persistence, REST API, filtering, ACID, and replication are missing and are exactly what vector databases add on top.
queries: Does FAISS have a REST API?
What happens to a FAISS index if the process dies?
Why is FAISS not enough for production?
What features do vector databases add over FAISS?

text: When people say a new vector database is 'fast,' the speed is very often FAISS speed. Milvus and OpenSearch embed FAISS directly; Vespa and several commercial offerings reimplement the same algorithms with FAISS as the correctness reference. The database wraps FAISS with persistence, API, and ops. Systems with FAISS (or its algorithms) inside: Milvus - FAISS inside, adds distributed cluster, API, metadata, lifecycle. OpenSearch - FAISS engine, adds Lucene index, REST, auth, hybrid with BM25. Vespa - same algorithms, adds tensor compute, multi-vector, ranking pipelines. Azure AI Search - FAISS/DiskANN variant, adds managed service, filtering, auth. FAISS lives underneath most vector DBs. The DB is the wrapper that turns 'library' into 'product.' Learning FAISS first is the right order - understanding its algorithms transfers directly to every system that embeds them.
summary: Milvus, OpenSearch, Vespa, and Azure AI Search all sit on top of FAISS or its algorithms, so learning FAISS transfers directly to every wrapper system.
queries: Which vector databases use FAISS internally?
Does Azure AI Search use FAISS?
What does Milvus add on top of FAISS?
What does OpenSearch add on top of FAISS?
Why is FAISS underneath so many vector DBs?
What is the relationship between FAISS and Milvus?
Why learn FAISS before vector databases

text: The decision reduces to one line. If you are building a system - researching a new index, embedding ANN in a training loop, or writing a custom service where you control persistence, API, and ops - use FAISS. If you want a system - a running database with an API, persistence, filters, backups, and replication handled - use a database, which wraps these algorithms behind a product. Use FAISS if: you are building a new system or research prototype; you control persistence, API, and ops yourself; you need custom index composition FAISS exposes; you want to embed ANN in a training loop. Use a DB if: you want to use one not write one; you need filters, metadata, HA out of the box; you want a running service with API and auth; operational story is someone else's problem. FAISS to build a system, a DB to use one.
summary: Use FAISS when you are building or researching a custom system; use a vector database when you want filters, HA, API, and ops handled for you.
