text: Delete propagation and versioning: where 90% of in-house RAG breaks. Sync is not just 'add new docs.' Hard parts are propagating deletes (when source removes a doc) and versioning chunks (so retrieval never sees a gap during a re-embed). Both are silent failures - eval sets catch them, but customers just get wrong answers. Three-row versioning timeline: Row 1 t=0 doc-1 v1 indexed, chunks [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1], status=active - queries hit v1, source and index agree. Row 2 t=+1min v2 ingested, v1 deprecated - chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2] active + [c1_v1, c2_v1, c3_v1] deprecated, status=active OR status=deprecated - both versions live during re-embed window, no retrieval gap. Row 3 t=+15min v1 dropped after grace period - chunks [c1_v2, c2_v2, c3_v2], status=active - only v2 active, old chunks tombstoned and removed. Delete propagation: source removes doc-1, webhook fires event 'page.deleted', ingestion writes a tombstone in vector DB, tombstone propagates to all replicas, all chunks for doc-1 removed (Section 17.2 multi-replica pattern). Without delete propagation: deleted docs still served - ghost chunks pointing to content no longer in source. Versioning + grace period: new chunks land status=active, old chunks flip status=deprecated, both queryable during grace period (typically 5-15 min), after grace deprecated chunks dropped. Without versioning: old chunks dropped before new ones land - retrieval gap, queries during re-embed window return empty or stale results. Silent failures invisible to users - eval catches them, customers do not. Build delete propagation + versioning from day one; retro-fitting costs 10x dev time because every downstream component (retrieval, eval, monitoring) already assumes chunks are immutable.
summary: Delete propagation (tombstones) and chunk versioning with a grace period prevent ghost chunks and retrieval gaps - silent failures most in-house RAG misses.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: This chapter: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline. Embedding a whole document fails because embedding models cap at ~8,192 tokens and a single vector for 500 pages carries no specific signal. Production RAG always chunks for three reasons: context limits, retrieval granularity, and signal dilution. The simplest strategy is fixed-size sliding-window chunking: chunks = slide(doc, size=128, overlap=16). This produces uniform chunks but cuts land mid-sentence, breaking cross-boundary retrieval. Overlap of 16 tokens duplicates boundary text into both adjacent chunks, fixing sentence-level cuts at ~12% extra storage, but still fails on long-range pronoun references. Fixed-size is the baseline: use it for short, homogeneous docs or as a measurement reference; move to structural or semantic chunking when docs have headings, are heterogeneous, or cross-chunk facts hurt recall.
summary: Overview of why RAG chunks documents and how fixed-size sliding-window chunking with overlap works, its failure modes (mid-sentence cuts, long-range references), and when to use it as a baseline.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Why not embed the whole document? The intuitive first idea is to take a full 500-page product manual, embed it as one vector, and search. This fails for two hard reasons: the document is too long to fit through the embedding model, and even if it did, one vector for 500 pages would mean nothing specific. Token Budget vs Embedding Model Ceiling: a 500-page product manual is roughly 250,000 tokens. The OpenAI ada-3 embedding model ceiling is 8,192 tokens. Only the first 8,192 fit; the remaining 241,808 tokens are overflow. Even if it fit, a single vector like [0.21, 0.84, 0.12, ...] for 500 pages would carry no specific signal.
summary: Embedding an entire long document fails because it exceeds the 8,192-token embedding model ceiling and a single vector for hundreds of pages dilutes all specific meaning.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Three Reasons Production RAG Always Chunks. Chunking is not a workaround, it is the fundamental design choice. Three forces make it unavoidable. 1) Context Limits: embedding models cap at 8K tokens; LLMs cap at context-window length. A 500-page manual cannot fit in either. 2) Retrieval Granularity: top-K retrieval returns chunks, not whole docs. Smaller chunks = more focused matches. 3) Signal Dilution: averaging 500 pages into one vector buries any specific fact. Smaller chunks keep semantic density. Bottom line: chunking is the highest-leverage decision in RAG. Get the chunk strategy wrong and every downstream stage compounds the error.
summary: Production RAG always chunks documents because of three forces: embedding/LLM context limits, top-K retrieval granularity, and signal dilution from averaging too much text into one vector.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Fixed-Size Chunking: A Sliding Window With Overlap. The simplest possible strategy: slide a fixed token window across the document. Each window becomes one chunk. Adjacent chunks overlap by a few tokens so a sentence cut in half still appears whole somewhere. Example doc-1 (Password Reset Article, ~480 tokens): "To reset your password, visit account.example.com and click forgot password. Enter the email tied to your account. You will receive a reset link valid for 24 hours..." Formula: chunks = slide(doc, size=128, overlap=16). Applied to doc-1 this yields four chunks: Chunk 1 Tokens 0-128, Chunk 2 Tokens 112-240, Chunk 3 Tokens 224-352, Chunk 4 Tokens 336-464. Each adjacent pair shares 16 tokens of overlap.
summary: Fixed-size chunking slides a constant-token window with small overlap across a document, producing four 128-token chunks with 16-token overlap on the example password-reset article.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Fixed-Size Cuts Land Anywhere - Including Mid-Sentence. The token window does not care about grammar. The 128th token is wherever the 128th token is. Zoom into the boundary between chunk 1 and chunk 2 in doc-1. Chunk 1 ends mid-phrase: "...click the reset link in the email within 24 hours to set a new". Chunk 2 starts mid-phrase: "password. The link expires after that and you must request a new one...". The Boundary Cut Breaks Retrieval: the chunk that ends with "set a new" has no idea what the user is setting. The chunk that starts with "password." has no idea what action was being taken. Retrieval cannot match the query "password reset expiry" to either piece alone - both lose the cross-boundary fact.
summary: Fixed-size token windows ignore grammar and cut mid-sentence, splitting the phrase "set a new password" across two chunks so neither chunk can answer a cross-boundary query.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Overlap Helps - But Only Partially. The standard fix is overlap: duplicate the last N tokens of each chunk into the start of the next. A sentence cut at the boundary now appears whole in one of the two adjacent chunks. The cost: storage and redundant compute. Overlap = 0 (Naive): two chunks meet at a hard cut; cuts can lose context entirely; recall drops on cross-chunk facts. Overlap = 16 Tokens: chunks share a 16-token region; the shared text in both adjacent chunks is "set a new password. The link expires". Tradeoffs: overlap = duplicate storage cost; overlap = ~12% more vectors at 128/16; still breaks long-range references (entity earlier in doc, pronoun later). Overlap fixes the easy case (sentence-level cuts) but not the hard case (a definition on page 3 referenced by "it" on page 17).
summary: Adding 16-token overlap duplicates boundary text into both adjacent chunks to recover sentence-level cuts, but costs ~12% more vectors and still misses long-range entity-pronoun references.

chapterTitle: Why Chunk At All + Fixed-Size Baseline
text: Fixed-Size: The Baseline To Beat. Fixed-size chunking is the right default in a narrow band of cases. Outside that band, every other strategy beats it. Use Fixed-Size If: docs are short (< 2K tokens each); content is homogeneous (uniform paragraphs); you need a baseline to measure other strategies against; budget = zero engineering time. Move To Structural / Semantic If: docs have clear structural markers (markdown, HTML headings); content is heterogeneous (one doc = manual + FAQ + changelog); cross-chunk facts hurt recall in your eval set. Fixed-size is the baseline. Every chunking strategy in production is measured against it.
summary: Fixed-size chunking is the right default only for short, homogeneous docs or as a baseline; structural or semantic chunking should replace it once docs have headings, are heterogeneous, or cross-chunk facts hurt recall.

text:
This chapter teaches recursive structural chunking: try the most structural separator first (paragraph break 

), fall back to line break, sentence break (". "), then word break only when the chunk still exceeds the size limit. Fixed-size chunking on doc-7 mixes Browser Compatibility with Account Lockout in chunk 2; recursive structural on the same doc using separators [
##, 

, 
, . , space] with size=128 yields three clean one-topic chunks (~120, ~95, ~115 tokens). When a section like doc-12's 300-token Authentication Header Format exceeds the limit, recursion falls back to paragraph break and emits three sub-chunks of 100, 110, 90 tokens. Recursive structural is the default in LangChain RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter and LlamaIndex SentenceSplitter, covering ~80% of production cases; it preserves structural boundaries cheaply but misses semantic topic boundaries and parent-child relations.
summary: Overview of recursive structural chunking: separator fallback hierarchy, worked doc-7 and doc-12 examples, and why it is the 80% RAG baseline.

separator hierarchy paragraph line sentence word
when to use recursive structural chunking

text:
Recursive structural chunking splits text by structure, not by token count. Fixed-size chunking cuts mid-phrase; recursive structural tries the most structural separator first (a paragraph break) and only falls back to smaller separators when the resulting chunk still exceeds the size limit. The cut lands at a boundary the writer already chose. Separator priority tree: Try Paragraph Break First (separator = "

", one blank line between blocks); Fall Back To Line Break (separator = "
", newline inside a paragraph); Fall Back To Sentence Break (separator = ". ", period followed by space - smallest grammar unit); Last Resort: Word Break (separator = " ", plain space, never cuts mid-word). The Recursive Rule: try the most structural separator first; only fall back if the resulting chunk still exceeds size limit. Separators = ["

", "
", ". ", " "]. The newline character "
" is what the writer typed to end a line. Two newlines mark a paragraph boundary.
summary: Recursive structural chunking tries paragraph break first and falls back to line, sentence, then word breaks only when chunks exceed the size limit.

text: Worked example: doc-7 is a login troubleshooting markdown article of about 380 tokens with three sections - ## Common Login Errors, ## Browser Compatibility, ## Resetting Account Lockout. Apply fixed-size chunking with size=128, overlap=0 and the cuts ignore section structure. Chunk 1 (tokens 0-128): All of Common Login Errors - wrong password, account locked, session expired - one clean topic. Chunk 2 (tokens 128-256): Tail of Browser Compatibility + head of Account Lockout - spans two unrelated topics; a retrieval query for 'Safari cookie SSO' matches this chunk but the answer is half-Safari, half-lockout-admin, neither topic retrievable cleanly. Chunk 3 (tokens 256-380): Tail of Resetting Account Lockout - missing the heading and first sentence, topic context gone. Chunk 2 mixes browser compatibility with account lockout - retrieval cannot answer either cleanly.
summary: Fixed-size chunking on doc-7 produces a chunk 2 that straddles two unrelated topics, making retrieval unable to answer cleanly.

text:
Same doc-7 with recursive structural chunking. Call: chunks = recursive_structural(doc-7, separators=["
## ", "

", "
", ". ", " "], size=128). The chunker sees three top-level headings and uses the heading break first. Each resulting chunk fits under 128 tokens and contains exactly one topic. Chunk 1 (~120 tokens) - Common Login Errors: wrong password, account locked, session expired. Chunk 2 (~95 tokens) - Browser Compatibility: Safari blocks third-party cookies, Chrome strict tracking protection, add auth domain to site exceptions list. Chunk 3 (~115 tokens) - Resetting Account Lockout: open account-admin panel, click reset lockout, audit log captures admin action with timestamp. 3 chunks, each is exactly one topic. A Safari cookie query goes to chunk 2 and nowhere else.
summary: Recursive structural chunking on the same doc produces three clean one-topic-per-chunk results using the heading separator.

text: If a section is too big, recurse into paragraphs. doc-12 (API keys reference) has an 'Authentication Header Format' section that runs 300 tokens - more than double the 128-token target. Step 1: try heading split - section comes out at 300 tokens, limit 128, too big. Step 2: fall back to paragraph break "\n\n" - the section has three paragraphs of 100, 110, and 90 tokens, each fits under 128. Step 3: emit three chunks. Recursion stops as soon as every produced chunk fits. No need to drop to sentence or word splits. If a paragraph were still > 128, recursion would try ". " (sentence) next, then " " (word). The recursion goes deeper only when needed; most chunks stop at the paragraph level.
summary: When a heading-bounded section exceeds the limit, recursion falls back to paragraph splits and stops once every sub-chunk fits.

text: Recursive structural is the 80% baseline. It is the default chunker in every major RAG framework (LangChain RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, LlamaIndex SentenceSplitter) and covers around 80% of production cases out of the box. Pros: preserves sentence and paragraph boundaries; respects markdown / HTML structure for free; zero embedding cost at chunk time (just string ops); implemented by every major RAG framework. Cons: doesn't understand semantic boundaries (a topic spanning four paragraphs gets split); heading-aware but not heading-equivalent (two ## sections on the same theme are split into two chunks); plain-text docs without structural markers fall through to sentence/word splits (closer to fixed-size). This chapter fixes the semantic-boundary gap. This chapter fixes the parent-child gap.
summary: Recursive structural chunking is the framework default that handles ~80% of cases but misses semantic-topic boundaries and parent-child relations.

text: This chapter Semantic Chunking. When a document has no structural markers (no headings, no blank lines), recursive structural chunking degrades to word splits. Semantic chunking instead: (1) splits the doc into sentences, (2) embeds each sentence to get vectors, (3) computes cosine similarity between adjacent sentence vectors, (4) emits a chunk break wherever similarity dips below a threshold like 0.7. On doc-12 (12-sentence API keys reference: 6 creating sentences then 6 revoking sentences) within-cluster similarity stays at 0.85-0.92 and the create-to-revoke shift at boundary 6-7 drops to 0.41 - producing exactly one clean topic break and two chunks that don't mix sub-topics. Cost: 10-50x more expensive than structural chunking because every sentence must be embedded before chunking (~60 ms per 12-sentence doc; ~$13,000 to chunk 10M docs), then chunks are re-embedded for the index. Use semantic chunking when structural markers are absent and retrieval quality matters more than indexing budget.
summary: Semantic chunking detects topic shifts in marker-less prose by splitting wherever adjacent-sentence cosine similarity dips below a threshold, at 10-50x the cost of structural chunking.

text: What if a document has no structural markers? doc-12 is an API keys reference written as pure flowing prose - no headings, no bullet lists, no blank-line separators. Six paragraphs of plain narrative. Recursive structural chunking falls all the way through paragraph break, line break, sentence break, and lands on word splits - essentially fixed-size. doc-12 contains 12 sentences: the first six describe creating an API key (developer settings page, New Key button, human-readable label, audit logs, scope dropdown, optional expiry, Generate button - key shown once). The last six describe revoking a key (same developer settings page, find by label in active keys list, trash icon, revocation takes effect within sixty seconds, in-flight requests return 401, revoked keys remain in audit log for compliance). The topic shift lives in the middle and nothing in the markup tells the splitter where. The structural gap: no headings (no ##, no #), no blank lines (no double newline - recursive splitter sees one long paragraph), topic shift is invisible (creating-to-revoking switch is a semantic boundary with no surface marker; structural splitter cuts mid-topic at the 128-token mark). Need a chunker that reads meaning, not markup.
summary: Flat narrative docs with no headings or blank lines defeat structural chunkers because the topic shift has no surface marker.

text: Step 1: Embed every sentence independently. Split doc-12 into individual sentences using a sentence tokenizer (period-plus-space heuristic, or a real NLP library). Twelve sentences come out. Send each sentence through the embedding model and get back a vector. Now there are 12 vectors capturing the meaning of each sentence. Formula: embed(sentence_i) -> v_i  |  12 sentences -> 12 vectors. Example vectors (8-dim, illustrative): s1 'Creating an API key starts in the developer settings page.' = [0.82, 0.14, 0.46, 0.71, 0.09, 0.55, 0.23, 0.66]; s6 'Click Generate and the key value is shown one time only - copy it now.' = [0.86, 0.13, 0.50, 0.72, 0.12, 0.57, 0.24, 0.70]; s7 'Revoking an API key is done from the same developer settings page.' = [0.21, 0.78, 0.16, 0.34, 0.74, 0.18, 0.69, 0.27]; s12 'Revoked keys remain in the audit log forever for compliance review.' = [0.24, 0.82, 0.13, 0.32, 0.78, 0.17, 0.73, 0.26]. Sentences s1-s6 (creating cluster) have vectors pointing in similar directions. Sentences s7-s12 (revoking cluster) have vectors pointing in a different direction. The 8-dim vectors shown are illustrative; production models use 768 or 1536 dims.
summary: Tokenize the doc into sentences then embed each one independently to get 12 meaning vectors per the doc-12 example.

text: Step 2: Plot cosine similarity between adjacent sentences. For each pair of adjacent sentences, compute cosine similarity between their vectors. Plot it along the doc. Inside a topic cluster similarity stays high (0.85-0.92). At the topic shift between sentence 6 (creating) and sentence 7 (revoking) it drops to 0.41. Set a threshold like 0.7 - any boundary below the threshold is a chunk break. Per-boundary cosine values: 1-2 = 0.91, 2-3 = 0.89, 3-4 = 0.87, 4-5 = 0.90, 5-6 = 0.88, 6-7 = 0.41 (DROP), 7-8 = 0.86, 8-9 = 0.85, 9-10 = 0.91, 10-11 = 0.83, 11-12 = 0.90. Algorithm: For i in 1..11: sim_i = cosine(v_i, v_i+1). If sim_i < 0.7 -> emit chunk break at boundary i. The red dashed threshold line at 0.7 marks the chunk-break cutoff. Only boundary 6-7 dips below 0.7.
summary: Compute adjacent-sentence cosine similarities, plot them, and emit a chunk break wherever similarity dips below 0.7.

text: Step 3: Each cluster of high-similarity sentences becomes one chunk. One boundary fell below the threshold (boundary 6-7 at sim 0.41). That single break splits the doc into exactly 2 chunks. Chunk 1 (creating cluster, sentences 1-6): 'Creating an API key starts in the developer settings page. Click the New Key button and provide a label. The label appears in audit logs. Choose a scope. Optional expiry sets a date. Click Generate and the key value is shown one time only.' Chunk break at boundary 6-7, sim = 0.41. Chunk 2 (revoking cluster, sentences 7-12): 'Revoking an API key is done from the same developer settings page. Find the key by its label. Click the trash icon. Revocation takes effect within sixty seconds. In-flight requests return 401 after propagation. Revoked keys remain in the audit log for compliance.' The win: semantic chunking found the topic shift that structural chunking would miss - no headings, no double newlines, no markers of any kind. Retrieval behavior: a query for 'how do I revoke a key' matches chunk 2 cleanly; a query for 'creating a scoped key' matches chunk 1. Neither chunk mixes the two sub-topics. Structural chunkers would have cut at the 128-token mark (somewhere inside sentence 5 or 6) and emitted a chunk that mixes 'optional expiry' with 'revoking the key'.
summary: The single cosine dip at boundary 6-7 splits doc-12 into a clean creating chunk and revoking chunk where structural chunkers would have cut mid-topic.

text: The cost: embed-before-chunk is 10-50x more expensive. Semantic chunking has to embed every sentence before it can decide where to chunk. That makes it an order of magnitude (or more) slower and costlier than structural chunking, where chunk-time work is just string splitting. Then you still re-embed the final chunks for the index. Structural / Fixed-Size cost: per doc 1 pass of string-splitting, about 0.1 ms. Embedding cost at chunk time is $0 - embedding happens later, once, on the final chunks. Pure CPU string ops. Semantic cost: per doc split into sentences (~0.5 ms) + embed each sentence (~5 ms per sentence x 12 = ~60 ms) + cosine math (~0.1 ms). At $0.10 per million tokens, a 30-doc corpus chunked semantically costs about $0.04. At 10M docs that is about $13,000 just to chunk - then you re-embed the final chunks for the index. Use semantic chunking when retrieval quality matters more than indexing budget, and when structural markers are absent.
summary: Semantic chunking is 10-50x costlier than structural because every sentence must be embedded before chunking, plus the final chunks are re-embedded for the index.

text: Side-by-side comparison diagram: left panel shows chunk-then-embed with three independent embedding passes per chunk producing v1, v2, v3 where each vector sees only its own chunk. Right panel shows late chunking with one whole-doc attention pass over all 384 tokens producing token hidden states (N x hidden_dim), followed by mean-pool at chunk boundaries to emit v_chunk1, v_chunk2, v_chunk3 where every chunk vector sees the whole doc.
summary: Diagram contrasts three separate per-chunk embeddings against a single whole-doc embedding pass that mean-pools into chunk vectors.
queries: chunk-then-embed vs late chunking comparison diagram
side by side late chunking visualization

text: Token-attention sweep diagram over doc-1 password reset: single attention arc spans all 12 token cells (Sarah ... she ...) with mean-pool boundary lines at token positions 128 and 256. Each token has a hidden state h1..h12; mean-pool over h1..h4 = v_chunk1, h5..h8 = v_chunk2, h9..h12 = v_chunk3. Annotation: v_chunk3 pools h9 to h12 and h10 ('she') attended to h1 ('Sarah'), so v_chunk3 still encodes 'Sarah' even though the word lives in chunk 1.
summary: Diagram of single attention arc covering all tokens with pool boundaries at 128 and 256, showing chunk 3's vector inherits the Sarah signal through attention.
queries: token attention sweep diagram late chunking

text: This chapter Late Chunking (Jina 2024): chunk-then-embed strips cross-chunk references like pronouns ('she' in chunk 3 doesn't know it means Sarah from chunk 1). Late chunking reverses the order - run one embedding pass over the whole doc so attention links every token to every other token, then mean-pool token hidden states at chunk boundaries. v_chunk_i = mean-pool(H[chunk_i.start : chunk_i.end]). Result: chunk 3's vector still encodes Sarah, flipping a 0.34 retrieval miss into a 0.81 Top-1 hit on the same query. Same compute as chunk-then-embed. Requires a model that exposes token-level hidden states (jina-embeddings-v2) and a doc that fits one embedding pass (8K-32K). Doesn't work with OpenAI/Cohere APIs that only return pooled vectors. Use on anaphora-heavy corpora (support, legal, narrative); otherwise stick with chunk-then-embed plus hybrid/rerank.
summary: Late chunking embeds the whole doc first then pools by chunk so cross-chunk references survive, boosting recall on anaphora-heavy docs without extra compute.
queries: when does late chunking help RAG
mean pool token hidden states summary

text: Chunk-then-embed loses cross-chunk context. doc-1 password-reset article split into 3 chunks. Chunk 1 (tokens 0-128) names 'Sarah' and sets up reset flow: 'When a user named Sarah forgets her password, the system generates a one-time token. The reset flow begins on the login page when she clicks the Forgot Password link.' Antecedent 'Sarah' lives here. Chunk 2 (tokens 128-256): token hashed, stored in password_reset_tokens table, emailed as single-use link, subject 'Reset Your Password', arrives within 30 seconds. No mention of who requested it. Chunk 3 (tokens 256-384): 'The link expires after 24 hours; she must request a new one if she misses the window. The old token is invalidated immediately on reuse.' Pronoun 'she' has no antecedent in this chunk. When you embed each chunk in isolation, the chunk-3 vector has zero knowledge that 'she' refers to Sarah from chunk 1. Cross-chunk references (pronouns, 'the system', 'this approach', 'that field') bleed context the moment you slice the doc.
summary: Embedding chunks in isolation strips cross-chunk references like pronouns, leaving the chunk-3 vector unable to know 'she' means Sarah.

text: Chunk-then-embed vs embed-then-chunk (late chunking). Baseline (chunk-then-embed): doc-1 sliced into 3 chunks, each chunk independently fed through embedding model, producing v1, v2, v3 - 3 separate passes, each vector sees only its own chunk. Late chunking (embed-then-chunk, Jina 2024): doc-1 (384 tokens) feeds a single embedding pass that runs attention over all 384 tokens, producing token hidden states (N x hidden_dim). Pool boundaries at the chunk cut points mean-pool the token states into v_chunk1, v_chunk2, v_chunk3. One pass plus pool - every chunk vector sees the whole doc. Same compute as chunk-then-embed for the same doc; vastly different vectors. Formulas: Baseline: v_i = embed(chunk_i). Late: H = embed_tokens(doc); v_chunk_i = mean-pool(H[chunk_i.start : chunk_i.end]).
summary: Late chunking reverses the order - embed the whole doc first so attention links all tokens, then mean-pool token hidden states at chunk boundaries.

single embedding pass over whole doc
mean pool token hidden states for chunks
difference between chunk-then-embed and late chunking

text: Late chunking on doc-1: chunk 3's vector now encodes Sarah. Embedding model runs once over all 384 tokens. Attention links every token to every other token. The hidden state for 'she' in chunk 3 has attended to 'Sarah' in chunk 1. Pool boundaries at token 128 and token 256 split the token hidden states into 3 chunks. v_chunk_i = mean-pool(h_start...h_end). v_chunk3 pools h9 to h12 - and h10 ('she') attended to h1 ('Sarah'), so v_chunk3 still encodes 'Sarah' even though the word lives in chunk 1. Trace: H = embed_tokens(doc-1); H.shape = [384, 768]; v_chunk3 = mean(H[256:384]).
summary: Because attention runs across the whole doc before pooling, the 'she' token in chunk 3 carries the Sarah signal into v_chunk3.

text: Query 'Did Sarah get her reset email?' now matches chunk 3 with late chunking. Same query, same 3 chunks, same embedding model - only embedding order changes. Chunk-then-embed (baseline) scores: Chunk 1 (mentions Sarah, forgets password) = 0.78 Top-1; Chunk 2 (token generation, email send) = 0.65; Chunk 3 (link expiry, 'she' re-request) = 0.34. Top-1 is chunk 1, misses the actual answer because chunk 3's 'she' vector has no idea who 'she' is. Late chunking scores: Chunk 1 = 0.72; Chunk 2 = 0.68; Chunk 3 = 0.81 Top-1. Hits the answer because chunk-3 token hidden states attended to 'Sarah' in chunk 1 before pooling. Retrieval scores flip - chunk 3 goes from 0.34 miss to 0.81 hit because its vector now carries the Sarah signal.
summary: Same query and chunks; late chunking flips chunk 3 from a 0.34 miss to a 0.81 Top-1 hit because its vector encodes the cross-chunk Sarah antecedent.

text: Late chunking: pros, cons, when to use. Wins: preserves cross-chunk pronouns and references (anaphora resolution); single embedding pass = same compute as chunk-then-embed for the same doc; significant recall gains on docs with anaphora (he, she, it, this, that, the X); released 2024 by Jina AI for the jina-embeddings-v2 model family. Limits: requires an embedding model that exposes token-level hidden states (most OpenAI/Cohere APIs return only the pooled final vector - you cannot late-chunk with them); requires the whole doc to fit in one embedding pass (8K-32K token ceilings); pooling strategy matters (mean-pool vs CLS vs attention-weighted - jina-embeddings-v2 uses mean-pool). Reach for late chunking when corpus has heavy anaphora (support docs, legal text, narrative articles), docs fit in one embedding pass, and you can run an open-weights model like jina-embeddings-v2 that exposes token-level hidden states. Otherwise keep chunk-then-embed and lean on reranking or hybrid search to recover cross-chunk recall.
summary: Late chunking is free recall on anaphora-heavy docs but only works with open models that expose token hidden states and docs that fit one embedding pass.

text: Tree hierarchy diagram extending the doc-4 refunds tree with a small yellow summary card placed beside each section node. Each summary card contains a one-sentence LLM-generated summary ('Who qualifies and refund window', 'Filing steps and SLAs', 'Late requests and appeals'). The index now stores summary vectors (one per section) instead of leaf vectors. Leaves still appear faintly below as the eventual generation target. Bottom annotation: 'Index stores summary vectors. Retrieval finds a summary. Swap to the full parent section on a hit.'

text: Hierarchical / parent-child chunking resolves the tug-of-war between small chunks (precise retrieval) and large chunks (rich generation context) by chunking the document twice. A tree is built with a root (whole doc), mid-level section nodes (~300 tokens each), and small leaf chunks (~64 tokens each). Only the leaf vectors are indexed; parent texts live in a separate parent-text store keyed by leaf id. Retrieval searches the leaf index and finds the most precise match. Before generation, the system looks up the retrieved leaf's parent_id and swaps in the full parent section, giving the LLM 4-5x more context than the leaf alone. A summary-chunk variation indexes LLM-generated 1-2 sentence section summaries instead of leaves for denser semantic matching. Storage cost is ~2-3x the raw corpus; latency adds one sub-millisecond lookup per hit. LangChain (ParentDocumentRetriever) and LlamaIndex (RecursiveRetriever) ship the pattern out of the box.
summary: Hierarchical chunking indexes small leaves for precise retrieval and swaps in full parent sections for generation, getting both precision and context.
queries: how does parent document retrieval work

framework support for parent child chunking

text: Chunk size is a tug-of-war between two phases of RAG. A retriever finds the best matching chunk; an LLM answers from the chunk text. Small chunks (64 tokens) give high retrieval precision because each chunk is one focused fact, but low generation context because the LLM may need surrounding paragraphs to answer fully. Large chunks (1024 tokens) give low retrieval precision because many topics per chunk make the vector average them, but high generation context because the LLM has the whole section. Pick one and you are forced to compromise. What if you did not have to?
summary: Small chunks retrieve precisely but starve the LLM of context; large chunks give context but blur retrieval - a fundamental tug-of-war.

what chunk size should I use for RAG
why do small chunks help retrieval
why do large chunks help LLM generation
can I have small retrieval and large context

text: Hierarchical chunking: chunk the doc twice and build a tree. The root is the whole doc. Mid-level nodes are sections of around 300 tokens each. Leaves are tiny 64-token chunks that fit one fact apiece. Only the leaf vectors live in the index - they are what we search. Parents live in a separate parent-text store keyed by id, ready to be swapped in at generation time. Doc-4 refunds policy example: 1 root (900 tokens total) + 3 sections (Eligibility, Process, Edge Cases, ~300 tokens each) + 12 leaves (64 tokens each, 4 per section). Index: { leaf_id -> vector }. Parent Store: { leaf_id -> parent_text }.
summary: Build a tree of root + sections + leaves; index only the small leaves but keep parents in a separate store for swap-in at generation time.

text: Step 1: retrieval finds the best leaf. The user asks 'Can I get a refund if I cancel after 30 days?' The query is embedded once. We search the leaf index. Top-3 leaves come back ranked by cosine similarity: L7 wins at 0.89 ('...no refunds after 30 days...'), L3 at 0.74 ('Refund eligibility window: 14 days full, 15-30 days prorated...'), L12 at 0.68 ('Appeal process for denied refund requests...'). L7 wins because its 64-token text directly states the 30-day cutoff - a precise hit on the query.
summary: Embed the query once, search the leaf index, and the top-1 leaf is the precise 64-token snippet that answers the question.
queries: how does retrieval work in hierarchical chunking

text: Step 2: swap the leaf for its parent before the LLM. We retrieved a leaf but we do not send the leaf to the LLM. Each leaf carries the id of its parent section. A parent-lookup pulls the full 300-token Edge Cases section from the parent-text store. That section enters the prompt so the LLM has the eligibility nuance, the appeal path, and the carve-outs - not just one sentence in isolation. Flow: Retrieved Leaf L7 (64 tokens, '...no refunds after 30 days...', parent_id: sec-edge-cases, score 0.89) → parent_store[L7.parent_id] → Parent Section Edge Cases (300 tokens: 14-day full refund window, 15-30 day prorated refund, no refunds after 30 days, manual-review carve-out, appeal path Form A-12, special-case refunds; ~4.7x more context than leaf) → LLM Prompt Context. Retrieval precision comes from the leaf; generation context comes from the parent.
summary: After retrieval, look up the leaf's parent_id and send the full ~300-token parent section to the LLM instead of the leaf.

why send parent not leaf to LLM
why retrieval precision and generation context together

text: Variation: index summary chunks instead of leaves. Same tree, different retrieval target. Run an LLM over each section once at index time to generate a 1-2 sentence summary. Embed and index those summary chunks instead of the leaves. On a hit, swap to the full parent section (or even the whole doc). Doc-4 example: each section node gets a yellow summary card placed beside it ('Who qualifies and refund window' for Eligibility; 'Filing steps and SLAs' for Process; 'Late requests and appeals' for Edge Cases). Index stores 1 summary vector per section. Summaries pack more meaning per token so the vector match can be more semantic. Trade-off: summaries are denser semantically (one vector encodes the gist of a 300-token section) but require an LLM pass at index time to generate them.
summary: Instead of indexing leaves, run an LLM once per section to make a 1-2 sentence summary, index those, and swap to the parent on a hit.
queries: cost of summary chunking index time

text: Hierarchical chunking is a workhorse pattern in production RAG: precise retrieval and rich generation context at the price of storing parent texts. Use it when: docs are long and have natural section structure (manuals, policies, RFCs); queries need surrounding context to answer well (legal, medical, technical docs where one sentence is rarely enough); storage cost for parent texts is acceptable (roughly 2-3x raw doc size). Cost & implementation: Storage = index + parent-text store, roughly 2-3x raw corpus size. Latency = one extra lookup per hit (parent-from-leaf-id), sub-millisecond. Implementation: every major framework has this pattern - LangChain ParentDocumentRetriever, LlamaIndex RecursiveRetriever - working in a few lines.
summary: Use hierarchical when docs have section structure and queries need context; pay ~2-3x storage and one extra lookup; LangChain and LlamaIndex ship it.
queries: when not to use hierarchical chunking

text: This chapter covers Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval technique (September 2024). Chunks lifted out of their source documents often share near-identical surface phrasing (e.g. three different docs ending in 'click Save to confirm'), so their embeddings collide and recall@1 collapses to guessing. The fix is to run a cheap LLM (Claude Haiku) once per chunk at index time to generate a 1-2 sentence context prefix naming the document and section, prepend it to the chunk body, and embed the augmented text. On the email-change query example, recall@1 jumps from 33% to 100% and Chunk A's score rises from 0.71 to 0.93. Anthropic's benchmark shows top-20 failure rate dropping from 5.7% (naive embedding) to 3.7% (+contextual embedding, 35% reduction), 2.9% (+contextual BM25 hybrid, 49% reduction), and 1.9% (+reranking, 67% reduction). Cost is roughly $0.001 per chunk (~$100 for 100K chunks, lower with prompt caching). Skip on small homogeneous corpora; use when many docs share surface phrases and recall is the bottleneck. Composes with hybrid search and rerankers.
summary: Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval prepends LLM-generated context to chunks at index time, fixing recall collapse from duplicate phrasing and cutting failure rate 49% (67% stacked with rerank).

text: Chunks out of context look identical to the embedding model. Three chunks from three completely unrelated docs all end with the exact same sentence: 'click Save to confirm.' Query: 'How do I change my email?' -> q_vec. Chunk A from Doc-2 Email Change Guide: '...verify your new email address, then click Save to confirm.' vec [0.31, 0.42, 0.18, 0.55, 0.27, 0.39, 0.46, 0.22], similarity 0.71. Chunk B from Doc-15 Role Permissions Guide: '...assign the new role to the user, then click Save to confirm.' vec [0.30, 0.41, 0.19, 0.54, 0.29, 0.38, 0.45, 0.23], similarity 0.71. Chunk C from Doc-18 Notifications Settings: '...select the channels you want notifications on, then click Save to confirm.' vec [0.32, 0.40, 0.18, 0.55, 0.28, 0.39, 0.47, 0.21], similarity 0.71. The embedding model sees only the token sequence; with nearly identical tokens the three chunks produce nearly identical vectors. All three vectors are nearly identical because the chunks are nearly identical token sequences. The embedding is indistinguishable on the part of the chunk the query cares about. Recall@1 collapses to guessing - the wrong chunk is returned roughly two times out of three.
summary: Three unrelated chunks ending with the same 'click Save to confirm' phrase produce nearly identical embedding vectors and all tie at 0.71 similarity, collapsing recall@1 to a coin flip.

text: Solution: prepend an LLM-generated context line. Before each chunk is embedded, run a cheap LLM call once at index time to write a short 1-2 sentence context: what doc the chunk is from, what section, and what it is about. Prepend that context line to the chunk text. The augmented chunk - prefix plus original body - is what the embedder sees. Now the vector carries enough signal to disambiguate even when chunks share surface phrasing. Example - Chunk A after augmentation. LLM-generated context (1-2 sentences) - prepended prefix: 'Document: Email Change Guide. Section: Step 4 - Verify New Address. This chunk is about confirming the new email after verification.' Original chunk body (unchanged): '...verify your new email address, then click Save to confirm.' Full augmented chunk = [Prefix] + [Body] -> this is what gets embedded and indexed. The body of the chunk never changes - the LLM generation only writes a short prefix that situates the chunk inside its source. The original tokens are still there for the LLM to read at generation time. Only the index-side representation gets richer.
summary: Contextual retrieval prepends a 1-2 sentence LLM-written context (doc title, section, topic) to each chunk before embedding, enriching only the index-side representation while the body stays unchanged.

text:
The augmentation prompt template (run once per chunk). The template below is the exact prompt fired at index time for every chunk in the corpus. The placeholders get filled in with the document title, the section heading, and the raw chunk text. The model returns the 1-2 sentence context that gets prepended. Template:

Here is a chunk from a documentation page.
<document_title>{document_title}</document_title>
<section_heading>{section_heading}</section_heading>
<chunk>
{chunk}
</chunk>
Write a short (1-2 sentence) context that situates this chunk within the document and section so retrieval models can find it. Output only the context, no preamble.

Model choice: Anthropic recommends Claude Haiku - a cheap model is enough; the task is short and structured. Cost at index time: roughly $0.001 per chunk. For 100K chunks that is about $100 one-time. With prompt caching the document prefix is paid once across all its chunks.
summary: The augmentation prompt feeds document_title, section_heading, and chunk into Claude Haiku to produce a 1-2 sentence context, costing about $0.001 per chunk (~$100 for 100K chunks, less with prompt caching).

text: Before vs. after: recall on the email-change query. Same query, same three chunks, same embedding model. Only difference is whether the chunks were augmented with a context prefix at index time. Row 1: Without augmentation. Chunk A Doc-2 Email Change Guide score 0.71. Chunk B Doc-15 Role Permissions Guide score 0.71. Chunk C Doc-18 Notifications Settings score 0.71. All three tied at 0.71. Top-1 is effectively random. Recall@1 = 33%. Row 2: With augmentation. Chunk A Doc-2 Email Change Guide score 0.93 Top-1 - augmentation explicitly mentions email change, direct hit. Chunk B Doc-15 Role Permissions Guide score 0.42 - augmentation pulls vector toward roles, away from email. Chunk C Doc-18 Notifications Settings score 0.39 - augmentation pulls vector toward notifications, away from email. Chunk A wins at 0.93. Top-1 is stable. Recall@1 = 100%. The augmented vectors are no longer near-duplicates. Chunk A's prefix mentions email change explicitly, pulling its vector toward the query. Chunks B and C drift away because their prefixes are about roles and notifications. The retriever now has signal to separate them.
summary: Before augmentation all three chunks tie at 0.71 and recall@1 is 33%; after augmentation Chunk A jumps to 0.93 while B drops to 0.42 and C to 0.39, lifting recall@1 to 100%.

text: Anthropic's 2024 benchmark: 49% recall improvement. Anthropic published a paper-length blog post in September 2024 measuring contextual retrieval over multiple corpora. Top-20 retrieval failure rate (lower is better): naive embedding only 5.7% (baseline); +contextual embedding 3.7% (35% reduction); +contextual BM25 2.9% (49% reduction); +reranking 1.9% (67% reduction). Contextual embedding alone cuts failures by 35%. Add contextual BM25 hybrid and it is 49%. Stack a reranker on top and the combined stack hits 67% fewer failures than naive embedding retrieval. The 49% headline number is the failure rate reduction from naive embedding (5.7%) to contextual embedding plus contextual BM25 hybrid (2.9%).
summary: Anthropic's September 2024 benchmark shows top-20 failure rates of 5.7% naive, 3.7% +contextual embedding (-35%), 2.9% +contextual BM25 (-49%), 1.9% +reranking (-67%).

text: When contextual retrieval is overkill. Contextual retrieval pays for itself when many docs share surface phrases and recall is a real bottleneck. On a small homogeneous corpus the one-time augmentation cost is hard to justify - the recall problem can be solved more cheaply by hand-tuning chunks or by adding a reranker first. Skip if: corpus is fully homogeneous (one product manual; no risk of cross-doc duplicate phrases); index size is small (under 1K chunks; recall problems can be solved by hand-tuning); index budget is hard (the one-time $100-$1,000 augmentation cost is too high for a prototype). Use if: corpus has many docs with similar surface phrases (FAQ + support + product = lots of click Save to confirm overlap); recall is the bottleneck in your eval metrics; hybrid and reranking are already in place and you want one more lever. Rule of thumb: if recall is not your bottleneck, do not add the augmentation pass. The 49% headline number is a ceiling, not a guarantee - it only shows up when chunks would otherwise collide on surface tokens.
summary: Skip contextual retrieval on small or homogeneous corpora or tight budgets; use it when many docs share surface phrasing, recall is the bottleneck, and hybrid+rerank are already in place.

text: Stacks with hybrid search and rerankers for the full 67%. Contextual retrieval is not a replacement for hybrid search or for rerankers - it composes with them. Augment at index time, then run hybrid retrieval and rerank as usual. Each layer attacks a different failure mode, so the wins stack. Step 1: Contextual augmentation (index time). Run an LLM over each chunk once. Prepend a 1-2 sentence context line that says what doc and section the chunk lives in. Embed and index the augmented chunk. Step 2: Hybrid retrieval (query time). Dense embedding search and BM25 lexical search run in parallel over the augmented chunks. Their top-20 results are merged into a single candidate list. (Hybrid covered in Section 17.5 - merges semantic and lexical recall.) Step 3: Reranker (top-20 to top-3). A cross-encoder scores each query-chunk pair jointly and reorders the candidates. The top 3 go into the LLM prompt. (Rerankers covered in Section 17.6 - cross-encoder scores each query-chunk pair.) Combined: 67% failure-rate reduction over naive (Anthropic 2024). Augmentation lifts dense and BM25 recall in parallel. The reranker fixes ordering on whatever the hybrid stack surfaces. Each component is independently useful, but the full stack is where the 67% number lives.
summary: The full retrieval stack is contextual augmentation at index time, then hybrid dense+BM25 search at query time, then cross-encoder reranker top-20 to top-3 - composing for 67% failure reduction.

What are the three steps of the full retrieval stack?
How to combine contextual retrieval and rerankers

text: This chapter consolidates the six chunking strategies introduced earlier in the section (Fixed-Size, Recursive Structural, Semantic, Late, Hierarchical, Contextual Retrieval) and gives a three-axis decision framework for picking among them: document structure, query type, and indexing budget. It then walks through applying the framework to a customer support corpus, showing that production systems usually mix multiple strategies per doc-type rather than committing to one.
summary: Decision framework for choosing among the six chunking strategies along three axes - structure, queries, budget - applied to a real corpus.
queries: how do I choose a chunking strategy for RAG
which chunking method should I use

best chunking strategy for customer support docs
when to use semantic vs recursive chunking
is one chunking strategy enough for production
mixing chunking strategies per doc type

text: Six Strategies, One Page. Earlier chapters introduced six chunking strategies. Lined up side by side each has its own retrieval quality, embedding cost at chunk time, and implementation difficulty profile. Fixed-Size: sliding window, no structure awareness; quality 1, cost 1, difficulty 1. Recursive Structural: tries paragraph then line then sentence then word; quality 2, cost 1, difficulty 1. Semantic: embed sentences, split where cosine dips; quality 3, cost 2, difficulty 2. Late: one whole-doc attention pass, then pool per chunk; quality 3, cost 2, difficulty 3. Hierarchical: small chunks retrieve, parents go to LLM; quality 3, cost 2, difficulty 2. Contextual Retrieval: LLM-generated context prefixed to each chunk; quality 4, cost 3, difficulty 2. No strategy wins on all three axes. Fixed-Size is cheapest but loses quality; Contextual gives highest retrieval quality but adds index-time LLM cost. The next three sub-steps pick the right one along three axes: doc structure, query type, and budget.
summary: Side-by-side scorecard of all six chunking strategies on quality, cost, and implementation difficulty.
queries: quality cost difficulty of chunking methods
which chunking strategy has highest quality
fixed-size vs recursive vs semantic chunking scorecard
is there a best chunking strategy

text: Axis 1: What structure do your docs have? Structure constrains the chunker more than content does. Markdown / HTML: Recursive Structural (uses heading and paragraph markers natively). PDF (scanned or extracted): Recursive Structural after extraction, or Semantic if extraction quality is bad. Code: Recursive Structural with language-aware separators (function defs, class declarations). Flat-text narrative (no headings, no markdown): Semantic Chunking (the only way to find topic boundaries). Mixed / heterogeneous corpus: apply different strategies per doc-type; don't force one strategy. The single biggest signal is whether the corpus has explicit split points. If yes, Recursive Structural is almost always the right starting baseline. Only flat narrative forces you up the cost ladder to Semantic on day one.
summary: Map each document type (Markdown, PDF, code, flat text, mixed) to a recommended chunking strategy based on whether the docs have explicit split points.
queries: how to chunk code for retrieval
do my docs have explicit split points

text: Axis 2: What kinds of queries will you get? Query shape changes which chunk size and which retrieval pattern wins. Factual queries ("What is X?"): small chunks are fine; Recursive Structural plus Contextual if FAQ-style overlap; Hierarchical optional for context-heavy answers. Relational queries ("How does X relate to Y?"): Hierarchical helps because the LLM needs the whole relationship section; Late Chunking helps when entities are referenced across chunks. Comparative queries ("Compare X and Y"): multi-hop retrieval on top of any chunking, bigger chunks help, Hierarchical preferred. Hierarchical is the safest middle bet: it serves factual queries through its leaves and relational queries through its parents. Comparative queries on top of any chunking still need multi-hop retrieval.
summary: Match query type (factual, relational, comparative) to the chunking and retrieval pattern that best serves it.
queries: how does query type affect chunk size
small chunks vs big chunks for queries
late chunking entity references across chunks
what chunking works for what is X
chunking for how does X relate to Y
compare X and Y query chunking

text: Axis 3: What's your indexing budget? Chunking cost is a one-time index-time spend that scales with corpus size. Three tiers along a cost spine: Lab / Prototype (free) - Recursive Structural only, zero embedding cost at chunk time, iterate fast. Startup (~$100 one-time) - Recursive Structural plus Hierarchical for long-form docs; skip semantic and contextual until you see recall problems in your eval. Enterprise ($100 - $10K one-time) - Recursive Structural baseline plus Hierarchical plus Contextual Retrieval plus Semantic where prose dominates; stack with hybrid plus rerankers; budget for the augmentation cost. Cost climbs with augmentation, not with chunking itself. Recursive Structural is free to run; Contextual Retrieval is the big spike because it fires an LLM call per chunk. Treat the spike as a one-time index cost amortized across every future query.
summary: Three budget tiers (lab, startup, enterprise) with the chunking stack appropriate to each and an explanation that augmentation drives cost.
queries: one-time chunking cost vs query cost
chunking cost scales with corpus size

text: Walkthrough: picking strategies for the customer support corpus. The support corpus splits into three doc-types with three different profiles. The right answer is a mix, not one strategy. Account & Billing: 10 docs, FAQ-style, lots of repeated phrases like "click Save" - use Recursive Structural plus Contextual Retrieval; Contextual disambiguates duplicate phrases across email, role, and notifications docs. Product Features: 10 docs, longer technical pages with sections and code samples - use Recursive Structural plus Hierarchical; sections give clean splits, parent-swap gives the LLM context for technical answers. Troubleshooting: 10 docs, free-form runbooks, narrative paragraphs - use Recursive Structural plus Semantic for longer narratives; Semantic catches topic shifts within a runbook. Production chunking is rarely one strategy: mix per doc-type, measure recall, iterate. The 30-doc support corpus uses three different chunking recipes - Contextual for FAQ-style billing docs, Hierarchical for technical product features, Semantic for free-form troubleshooting runbooks.
summary: Concrete walkthrough applying the three-axis framework to a 30-doc customer support corpus, choosing a different chunking recipe for each doc-type.
queries: mixing chunking per doc type example

Step 2 asks 'Specialized domain?' - Yes leads to domain-tuned BGE or fine-tune. Step 3 asks 'Budget sensitive?' - Yes leads to self-host BGE. Step 4 is the default pick: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Cohere embed-v3. Each 'No' arrow flows down to the next question.

text: Picking an embedding model: the model sets a hard recall ceiling no reranker can fix. Compare candidates on five axes - dimension, multilingual coverage, domain, cost, max context. Dimension scales memory and compute linearly; 1024 is the production sweet spot. Match language coverage to your data - multilingual models waste capacity on English-only corpora. Cost at 100M token ingest ranges from ~$50 self-hosted BGE to $13,000 OpenAI text-embedding-3-large. MTEB is a hint but over-fit risk is real - run your own eval. Use the four-step decision flow: multilingual? specialized domain? budget sensitive? else default to OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Cohere embed-v3.
summary: End-to-end framework for picking an embedding model across recall, five axes, cost, and a decision flow.

text: The embedding model decides the recall ceiling for retrieval. Chunking decides how text is split; the embedding model decides what those chunks mean as vectors. Two systems on the same corpus, with the same chunks and the same vector DB, can have wildly different recall just because one uses a stronger embedding model. The chosen model sets a hard ceiling: no reranker or hybrid trick can recover documents your embedder failed to bring close enough in the first place. Recall@10 on a support corpus (same chunks, same index): OpenAI ada-002 (older) = 0.62, Cohere embed-v3 = 0.83, BGE-Large = 0.81. Recall is 'did the right doc land in the top 10?' Higher is better.
summary: Embedding model sets the hard recall ceiling that no reranker can recover.

text: Five axes that matter when picking an embedding model: Model, Dim (output vector length), Multilingual (trained on multiple languages), Domain (general purpose vs specialized), Cost (price per million input tokens or self-host), Max Context (longest single input). Comparison table: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large - dim 3072, multilingual Yes, general domain, $0.13/M tokens, context 8191. Cohere embed-v3 - dim 1024, multilingual Yes, general, $0.10/M tokens, context 512. BGE-Large - dim 1024, EN + CN, general, free self-host, context 512. SBERT all-mpnet-base - dim 768, English mostly, general, free self-host, context 384. Voyage-2 - dim 1024, multilingual Yes, domain variants available, $0.10/M tokens, context 16000.
summary: Compares five embedding models across dimension, multilingual support, domain, cost, and max context.
queries: what dimensions does OpenAI text-embedding-3-large have

text: Higher dim costs more memory and compute. Every dimension is a 4-byte float. Memory scales linearly with dim. At 1 million vectors (float32): d=384 = 1.5 GB, d=768 = 3 GB, d=1024 = 4 GB, d=3072 = 12 GB. Difference between d=384 and d=3072 is 1.5 GB versus 12 GB - same corpus, 8x the RAM bill and 8x the dot-product cost at query time. Bigger dim helps quality up to a point. Beyond ~1024 the marginal gain shrinks fast - returns diminish while memory and compute keep growing linearly. 1024 is the sweet spot for most production systems.
summary: Memory and dot-product cost scale linearly with embedding dimension; 1024 is the production sweet spot.
queries: how much memory does 1 million vectors take
why is 1024 the sweet spot for embedding dim
does higher dimension always give better quality
why not always use highest dimension

text: Multilingual models underperform on English-only data. A multilingual model spends representation capacity on every language it was trained on. If your corpus is only English, that extra capacity is wasted and the English representation is slightly weaker than a same-size English-only model. Match the model's language coverage to your data. 2x2 matrix: English-only model (e.g. SBERT) + English data = best quality, smallest dim, cheapest. English-only model + multilingual data = will fail on non-English queries. Multilingual model (e.g. Cohere v3) + English data = quality slightly worse, extra capacity wasted. Multilingual model + multilingual data = right tool for the job. If corpus is medical, legal, or code, off-the-shelf general models often underperform a domain-tuned model. Domain adaptation and fine-tuning are covered in the next chapter.
summary: Match model language coverage to data; multilingual capacity is wasted on English-only corpora.
queries: should I use multilingual embedding for English-only data
does multilingual model hurt English quality
what happens English-only model on multilingual data
when is multilingual embedding worth it

text: Cost math at 100M token ingest. Ingesting a 100M-token corpus is a one-time charge but dominates the bill. Same corpus runs from ~$50 (self-hosted GPU for two days) to $13,000 (managed API on largest OpenAI model). OpenAI text-embedding-3-large: 100M / 1M x $0.13 = $13,000. Cohere embed-v3: 100M / 1M x $0.10 = $10,000. Self-hosted BGE on g5.xlarge (2 days): $1.006/hour x 48 hours = ~$50. Self-hosting is dramatically cheaper at scale. Managed APIs win on operational simplicity for small corpora where the per-token rate is rounding error compared to engineering time.
summary: Walks through ingest cost at 100M tokens showing $13k OpenAI vs $10k Cohere vs ~$50 self-hosted BGE.
queries: how much does it cost to embed 100 million tokens
when is self-hosting embeddings worth it
managed API vs self-host embedding cost
is OpenAI embedding cheaper than Cohere

text: MTEB is a hint, not an answer. MTEB is a public benchmark with 50+ retrieval and classification tasks. It is the best public signal for ranking embedding models - and also the trap most teams fall into. The MTEB caveat: MTEB is a public leaderboard. Models can be over-fit to it: trained on data resembling the test sets, tuned for MTEB metrics, or both. The leader on MTEB may underperform on YOUR data. Always run a small eval on your own queries before committing. Four-step decision flow: Step 1 - Need multilingual? Yes = use Cohere v3 / Voyage / OpenAI 3-large. Step 2 - Specialized domain? Yes = domain-tuned BGE or fine-tune (see next chapter). Step 3 - Budget sensitive? Yes = self-host BGE. Step 4 - Default = OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Cohere embed-v3. Follow flow top to bottom. Each 'No' sends you to next question. Run your eval on your own data before locking it in.
summary: MTEB benchmark is over-fit risk; use the four-step decision flow then eval on your own data.

text: Training loss curve showing triplet loss dropping from 0.50 at epoch 0 to 0.22 at epoch 1, 0.10 at epoch 2, and 0.05 at epoch 3 over a fine-tuning run on 50k support-corpus triples. Pink line plot with labeled data points; x-axis epochs, y-axis loss 0.00 to 0.50.

text: Domain Adaptation - Fine-Tuning Embeddings: general-purpose embedders miss domain-specific semantics (medical abbreviations like MI, tPA). Contrastive fine-tuning with triplet loss L_triplet = max(0, d(A,P) - d(A,N) + margin), margin = 0.2 typical, pulls positives closer and pushes negatives away. Training data = (anchor query, positive chunk, negative chunk) triples; hard negatives mined from top-20 retrieval results teach the boundary. Fine-tune when vocabulary is specialized, recall < 80% on a 200-500 pair golden set, and 5k+ labeled pairs exist. Example: 50k support-corpus triples on BGE-large, ~$200 managed cost, lifts Recall@5 from 0.78 to 0.91 and MRR from 0.62 to 0.84 while latency stays at 30 ms. Re-train every 3-6 months for drift.
summary: Overview of fine-tuning embedding models with triplet contrastive learning for domain-specific RAG retrieval.
queries: what is embedding fine-tuning for RAG
when should I fine-tune an embedding model
how does domain adaptation for embeddings work
how to adapt embeddings to medical legal code domain
fine-tune vs off the shelf embedder

text: Off-the-shelf embeddings sometimes miss domain semantics. General-purpose embedding models are trained on web text - they know cat and dog are similar but have barely seen MI (myocardial infarction) or tPA (tissue plasminogen activator). On a medical corpus this shows up as low cosine similarity between an abbreviation and its full term, which wrecks retrieval. Cosine similarity on a medical corpus: 'MI' vs 'Myocardial infarction' - off-the-shelf 0.42, fine-tuned 0.95. 'tPA contraindicated' vs 'Tissue plasminogen activator should not be given' - off-the-shelf 0.51, fine-tuned 0.90. Recall@10 on a held-out medical query set: off-the-shelf embedder 0.62, fine-tuned on medical corpus 0.88 - a 26-point recall lift. That is the value of teaching the embedder your domain.
summary: Off-the-shelf embedders fail on specialized vocabulary, causing huge recall drops on domain corpora.
queries: why do general embedders fail on medical text
why off the shelf embedder bad for legal docs
how much does fine-tuning improve recall

text: Contrastive fine-tuning: pull positives close, push negatives away. We do not retrain the embedder from scratch - we take the existing model and nudge its vector space using triples: an anchor query, a positive document that should match, and a negative document that should not. Each training step pulls anchor closer to positive and farther from negative. Triplet loss formula: L_triplet = max(0, d(A, P) - d(A, N) + margin), where d = distance, A = anchor, P = positive, N = negative, margin = 0.2 typical. The loss is zero only when the negative is at least 'margin' farther from the anchor than the positive. If the negative is too close, the gradient pushes it away. The margin prevents the loss from going to zero just because positives are slightly closer than negatives.
summary: Triplet loss formula and contrastive learning mechanism that warps embedding space to favor true matches.
queries: what is the margin in triplet loss
why use a margin in triplet loss

text: Pair construction decides quality - the training data is the model. Each row is one triple: anchor (real user query), positive (chunk that should match), negative (chunk that should not). Example triples from a support corpus: anchor 'How do I reset my password?' / positive '[doc-1, chunk 1] - To reset your password, visit the account settings page and click Forgot Password' / negative '[doc-3, chunk 1] - Refunds are processed within 5-7 business days of approval'. Anchor 'I can't sign in' / positive '[doc-25] Login Troubleshooting: clear cookies, verify caps lock, and check for typos in your email' / negative '[doc-7] Dashboard tour: the home screen shows your usage metrics and recent activity'. Anchor 'Cancel my subscription' / positive '[doc-15] Account Deletion Flow: go to billing settings and click Cancel Plan to stop renewal' / negative '[doc-2] Email Change: update your contact email from the profile settings page'. Hard negatives teach the most: a hard negative sits NEAR the positive in vector space but is irrelevant - same vocabulary, wrong topic. Mining hard negatives (top-20 retrieval results minus the true positive) teaches the actual semantic boundary. Easy negatives (random docs from unrelated topics) waste compute because the gap is already obvious to the base model.
summary: How to construct anchor/positive/negative triples and why hard-negative mining matters more than random sampling.
queries: how to build training data for embedding fine-tune
examples of training triples for support corpus
mine hard negatives from top-20 retrieval
how to label query positive pairs

text: When is fine-tuning worth the cost? Fine-tuning takes labeled data, compute time, and a recurring re-training budget as your corpus drifts. Fine-tune when: highly specialized vocabulary (medical, legal, code); off-the-shelf recall below 80% on your golden set; you have 5k+ query-positive pairs labeled; budget for periodic re-training as embeddings drift. Skip and use off-the-shelf when: general English domain; common domain (e-commerce, support, news); fewer than 5k labeled pairs available; recall already above 80% on the golden set. The cleanest signal: build a small golden set (200-500 query-positive pairs) and measure recall with the off-the-shelf model. Below 80%, fine-tuning is usually worth it. Above 80%, the marginal gain rarely pays for the recurring training cost.
summary: Decision rubric for when to fine-tune embeddings vs sticking with off-the-shelf based on domain, data, and golden-set recall.
queries: fine-tune vs off the shelf decision

text: Before vs after on the support corpus: fine-tuning on 50k support-ticket triples on BGE-large. Recall and MRR jump, latency does not - fine-tuning changes vector geometry, not the index or query path. Recall@5: before 0.78, after 0.91. MRR: before 0.62, after 0.84. Latency: before 30 ms, after 30 ms. Training loss over 3 epochs (50k triples): epoch 0 loss 0.50, epoch 1 loss 0.22, epoch 2 loss 0.10, epoch 3 loss 0.05. Cost: ~$200 for a 50k-pair fine-tune on BGE-large via a managed service. Quality delta: usually 10-20 points on recall. Re-evaluate every 3-6 months as the corpus drifts and new query patterns appear.
summary: Concrete before/after metrics, loss curve, and cost for a 50k-triple fine-tune on the support corpus.
queries: before after fine-tune recall MRR latency

Hybrid (RRF) bar at 0.86 (green). Caption: Recall@5 On 50 Held-Out Queries. Hybrid RRF is the tallest bar, showing +12 points over dense alone and +18 points over BM25 alone.
summary: Bar chart visualizing recall@5 for BM25 (0.68), dense (0.74), and hybrid RRF (0.86) on a 50-query held-out set.

chart showing hybrid beats single retrievers
BM25 vs dense vs hybrid bars

text: This chapter - Hybrid Retrieval for RAG. Dense embeddings miss exact terms (API keys, error codes) while BM25 misses synonyms ("cancel" vs "Account Deletion"). Hybrid retrieval runs both and fuses rankings. Two fusion strategies: Reciprocal Rank Fusion RRF(d) = sum 1/(k + rank_r(d)) with k=60, parameter-free and robust; and weighted score Score(d) = alpha * bm25 + (1-alpha) * dense after min-max normalization. RRF worked example shows doc-25 wins because both retrievers placed it top-2. Empirically recall@5 jumps from 0.68 (BM25) and 0.74 (dense) to 0.86 (hybrid RRF) on a 50-query held-out set, costing only +30ms. Alpha can be tuned per query type: 0.7 factual, 0.3 conceptual, 0.5 default; a small LLM classifier picks alpha at runtime for 4-8 extra recall points. Decision rule: always start with RRF, add weighted-alpha and classifier only if metrics demand, re-evaluate quarterly.
summary: Hybrid retrieval fuses BM25 and dense rankings via RRF or weighted alpha, lifting recall@5 from ~0.7 to 0.86 with +30ms latency; default to RRF and tune alpha by query type only if needed.
queries: what is hybrid retrieval for RAG
RAG retrieval combining BM25 and embeddings
when to use RRF vs weighted score

text: Dense retrieval misses exact terms; BM25 misses synonyms. Hybrid search ships dense vectors and BM25 side by side and fuses their rankings. Example 1 - Query: "How do I get an API key?" Truth: doc-13 (API Keys). Dense Top-3: [doc-7, doc-12, doc-5] missed doc-13 because the embedding of "API key" sat closer to generic dev-tools docs than the actual API-Keys page. BM25 Top-3: [doc-13, doc-7, doc-12] found it. Example 2 - Query: "Subscription cancel" Truth: doc-15 (Account Deletion). Dense Top-3: [doc-15, doc-19, doc-3] found it. BM25 Top-3: [doc-3, doc-19, doc-25] missed it because the cancel page is titled "Account Deletion" and does not contain the token "cancel". Hybrid keeps both retrievers and fuses rankings - each catches what the other misses.
summary: Shows two concrete queries where dense and BM25 each fail, motivating hybrid retrieval.
queries: why does dense retrieval miss exact terms
why do we need hybrid retrieval
what retriever catches what other misses
dense top-3 vs BM25 top-3 comparison
why one retriever is not enough

text: Two fusion strategies dominate production RAG. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): RRF(d) = sum over retrievers r of: 1 / (k + rank_r(d)), where k = 60 (standard). Robust default. No weights to tune. Each retriever contributes a rank-reciprocal, so doc-1 always outweighs doc-2 regardless of underlying score scales. Weighted Score Fusion: Score(d) = alpha * bm25_score(d) + (1 - alpha) * dense_score(d), where alpha is in [0, 1]. Tunable per query type. Requires score normalization first - BM25 scores are unbounded positive while dense cosine sits in [-1, 1], so min-max scaling each list to [0, 1] is standard prep.
summary: Defines the two main fusion formulas - parameter-free RRF and tunable weighted score - with k=60 and alpha in [0,1].
queries: what is the constant k in RRF
how to combine BM25 and dense scores

text: RRF Worked Example. Query: "I can't sign in". BM25 Top-3: 1. doc-25, 2. doc-7, 3. doc-13. Dense Top-3: 1. doc-12, 2. doc-25, 3. doc-7. Compute RRF(d) with k = 60. doc-25: BM25 term = 1 / (60 + 1) = 0.01639, Dense term = 1 / (60 + 2) = 0.01613, RRF sum = 0.03252. doc-7: BM25 term = 1 / (60 + 2) = 0.01613, Dense term = 1 / (60 + 3) = 0.01587, RRF sum = 0.03200. doc-12: BM25 term = 0 (not in BM25 top-3), Dense term = 1 / (60 + 1) = 0.01639, RRF sum = 0.01639. doc-13: BM25 term = 1 / (60 + 3) = 0.01587, Dense term = 0 (not in dense top-3), RRF sum = 0.01587. Final Ranking: doc-25 (0.03252) > doc-7 (0.03200) > doc-12 (0.01639) > doc-13 (0.01587). doc-25 wins because both retrievers ranked it in their top-2. doc-7 is second because both kept it in top-3. Singletons trail behind.
summary: Walks through RRF math step by step for the query 'I can't sign in', showing doc-25 wins because it appears high in both lists.
queries: what happens when doc not in list RRF
fused ranking from BM25 and dense
doc appearing in both lists fusion

text: Complementary Recall: Hybrid catches what either misses. Recall@5 measured on a 50-query held-out set against the support corpus. BM25 Only: 0.68. Dense Only: 0.74. Hybrid (RRF): 0.86. Hybrid is not just an average of the two retrievers - the failures of BM25 and dense are largely uncorrelated, so fusion picks up the union of their wins. Hybrid is rarely worse than the best single method, often noticeably better. Complementary recall gain here is +12 points over dense alone. Latency Cost: +30 ms for the BM25 lookup on top of the dense search. For most RAG systems that is well below the LLM generation tail, so the recall lift is almost always worth it.
summary: Empirical recall@5 numbers show hybrid (0.86) beats BM25 (0.68) and dense (0.74) due to uncorrelated failures, at +30ms latency cost.
queries: hybrid recall improvement over BM25 dense
recall at 5 BM25 vs dense vs hybrid
how much better is hybrid retrieval
is hybrid worth the latency cost RAG
why hybrid is not just average

text: Tune Alpha By Query Type. One alpha for everything leaves recall on the table. Factual lookups (account IDs, error codes) lean BM25. Conceptual questions lean dense. A small classifier picks the right alpha per query before retrieval. Factual / Lookup: example "What is my account ID?", alpha = 0.7, Lean BM25, why: exact tokens like account IDs, error codes, and SKUs dominate the answer. Conceptual / How-Does-X-Work: example "How does our pricing model work?", alpha = 0.3, Lean Dense, why: synonyms and paraphrase dominate, the answer rarely uses the same words as the query. Mixed / Ambiguous (Default): example "Why is my charge wrong?", alpha = 0.5, Balanced, why: mix of keywords (charge, refund) and intent (something is wrong), hedge with 0.5. A simple LLM-based classifier (one call with a 3-class prompt) can label each query at runtime before retrieval. Cost: 1 extra LLM call (~$0.0002 with a small model). Adds ~150 ms but unlocks 4-8 points of recall on mixed query workloads.
summary: Per-query-type alpha tuning: 0.7 for factual lookups, 0.3 for conceptual, 0.5 default; a small classifier picks alpha at runtime for 4-8 points of recall.
queries: how to tune alpha hybrid search
examples of factual vs conceptual queries

text: Hybrid Defaults For RAG. Hybrid is the production default for RAG. Start simple with RRF and only add complexity if your evaluation metrics demand it. (1) Always start with RRF (no tuning needed). (2) Move to weighted-alpha only if you have query-type labels. (3) Add a classifier when your queries naturally split into factual vs conceptual. (4) Re-evaluate alpha quarterly as your corpus and query mix drift. The cheapest big win in RAG retrieval. If you only do one thing after picking an embedding model, ship hybrid with RRF first - it almost always beats either retriever alone with a tiny latency cost.
summary: Decision rules: start with RRF, upgrade to weighted-alpha + classifier only when metrics demand, re-evaluate quarterly.
queries: when to use RRF vs weighted alpha
should I use a query classifier
first thing to ship after embedding model
do I need to tune alpha

text: The reranker cascade is a 3-stage RAG pipeline: vector retrieval (1M -> top-50, ~30 ms, $0.0001) feeds a cross-encoder reranker (50 -> top-10, ~80 ms, $0.0003) which feeds an LLM (top-3 -> answer, ~800 ms, $0.0120). Each stage shrinks the candidate pool ~5x so the next can afford more compute per item. Total ~910 ms and $0.0124 per query; LLM dominates both latency and cost, so caching the LLM call yields the biggest wins. Top-K retrieved and top-K reranked are the two main tuning knobs; the 50 -> 10 -> 3 default is the typical recall-per-millisecond sweet spot, with diminishing returns from widening.
summary: Wraps up the cascade lesson with stage roles, latency and cost totals, dominant LLM bottleneck, and the 50 -> 10 -> 3 tuning sweet spot.
queries: what to remember about reranker cascade
when to use reranker in RAG

text: Each stage of a RAG pipeline cuts the candidate set and pays for better quality. Rerankers (introduced earlier in section 17.6) re-score a candidate list using a cross-encoder that reads the query and the document together. Here that idea is applied as a 3-stage RAG cascade. The vector retriever is cheap and lossy. The reranker is more expensive but precise. The LLM is the most expensive and works on the smallest set. Each stage filters work for the next one. The 3-stage cascade: vector top-50 (cheap) -> cross-encoder rerank to top-10 (medium cost) -> LLM reads top-3 (expensive) and generates the answer.
summary: Introduces the 3-stage RAG cascade where vector retrieval, cross-encoder reranker, and LLM each filter the candidate set so compute is spent only on the most promising items.
queries: why use a reranker cascade in RAG
what is a 3 stage RAG pipeline
vector search then reranker then LLM
why not use one retriever for RAG
what does each stage of RAG do
why is reranker more expensive than vector search
spend more compute on fewer items

text: Stage 1: vector retrieval (HNSW or IVF-PQ scan) narrows 1M chunks to top-50, approximate but blazing fast, ~30 ms / $0.0001. Stage 2: cross-encoder reranker takes 50 candidates and returns top-10, ~80 ms / $0.0003. It reads query + each candidate jointly, requiring 50 forward passes through a small transformer. Stage 3: LLM reads top-3 chunks stuffed into the prompt and generates the answer, ~800 ms / $0.0120. The candidate pool shrinks roughly 5x at each stage. Each shrink lets the system spend more compute per remaining item - that is the whole game. Vector is cheap and lossy, reranker is medium cost with sharp scores, LLM is expensive and writes the final answer.
summary: Walks through the three cascade stages with concrete candidate counts, latencies, costs, and the role each stage plays.

text: Three-stage RAG cascade funnel diagram. A wide trapezoid at the top labeled Vector Retrieval shows 1M Chunks narrowing to Top-50, ~30 ms / $0.0001, role 'Cheap And Lossy'. A medium trapezoid in the middle labeled Cross-Encoder Reranker shows 50 Candidates narrowing to Top-10, ~80 ms / $0.0003, role 'Medium Cost, Sharp Scores'. A narrow trapezoid at the bottom labeled 'LLM Reads Top-3 + Generates' shows 10 Reranked narrowing to Answer, ~800 ms / $0.0120, role 'Expensive, Final Answer'. Arrows connect each trapezoid to the next, visualizing the 5x shrink and growing per-item compute at each stage.

text: Latency budget for the cascade: 30 + 80 + 800 = ~910 ms end-to-end p50 on a typical RAG deployment. Breakdown: vector retrieval (HNSW, top-50) 30 ms; cross-encoder reranker (50 -> 10) 80 ms; LLM generation (200 tokens, top-3) 800 ms. Total p50 latency: 910 ms. LLM generation dominates the budget. Optimizing reranker speed alone gives only small wins. Caching the LLM call (covered in this chapter) gives the biggest latency win.
summary: Quantifies the per-stage latency budget for the cascade and shows that LLM generation dominates the 910 ms total.

text: Cost per query for the cascade: $0.0001 + $0.0003 + $0.012 = $0.0124. Breakdown: vector retrieval (managed HNSW) $0.0001; cross-encoder reranker (50 candidates) $0.0003; LLM generation (4000 input + 200 output tokens) $0.0120. Total: $0.0124. LLM cost dominates. Reranker is cheap, vector retrieval is essentially free. At 1M queries/day, daily cost = $12,400. Caching can cut this 50-90%.

text: The two cascade tuning knobs are top-K retrieved (stage 1 width) and top-K reranked (stage 2 width). Three operating points on the running corpus: 20 -> 5 gives recall@final 0.82, latency 850 ms, cost $0.011 - narrow, saves a touch of latency and cost but recall slips. 50 -> 10 (default) gives recall 0.89, latency 910 ms, cost $0.012 - the sweet spot, best recall per millisecond. 100 -> 20 gives recall 0.92, latency 990 ms, cost $0.013 - wider, buys +3 points of recall for +80 ms; diminishing returns. Sweet spot is usually 50 -> 10 -> 3. Wider buys diminishing returns; narrower trades recall for latency.
