text: This chapter explains why raw user queries are rarely optimal retrieval queries and previews four transformation strategies. Three failure modes: lexical mismatch (user and doc use different words like sign-in vs log-in), ambiguity (one query has multiple plausible intents like "How do I export?"), and multi-intent (one query asks two questions like cancel + refund). Four fixes: HyDE (21.6) for lexical mismatch, Multi-Query Expansion (21.7) for ambiguity, Query Decomposition (21.8) for multi-intent, and Query Routing (21.8) for picking the right index. Concrete failure: Cosine(user, best_doc) = 0.61, correct doc at rank 7.
summary: Chapter overview - three retrieval failure modes (lexical mismatch, ambiguity, multi-intent) and four query-transformation fixes (HyDE, multi-query, decomposition, routing).

text: User queries are rarely optimal for retrieval. The user types a sentence in their own words, the retriever embeds it and finds nearest chunks, but the user's wording is rarely the wording that lives in the index, so the right doc stays in the corpus while the retriever misses it. Concrete miss: user query "I can't sign in" vs best doc in corpus "Login Troubleshooting". Cosine(user, best_doc) = 0.61. Top-3 misses it. Correct doc lands at rank 7. Three failure shapes ruin naive retrieval and four query-transformation strategies fix them.
summary: Frames why raw user queries fail retrieval and motivates query-transformation strategies with a concrete cosine=0.61 / rank-7 miss.
queries: why do user queries fail retrieval
user query vs doc wording mismatch
why does the right doc rank low
what is query transformation in RAG
I can't sign in vs Login Troubleshooting

text: Lexical mismatch: the user and the doc use different words. Users and docs share an idea but not vocabulary; the retriever sees two strings of tokens that look unrelated and the right chunk falls out of top-k. Examples: user "I can't sign in" vs doc "Login Troubleshooting" (sign in vs log in); user "How do I cancel?" vs doc "Account Deletion Flow" (cancel vs delete); user "My screen is frozen" vs doc "Browser Performance Issues" (screen frozen vs browser performance). Dense embeddings help a bit (sign-in and log-in sit close in vector space) but only partially. HyDE (21.6) and multi-query (21.7) close the gap by rewriting the user query into wording that matches the doc.
summary: Defines lexical mismatch with three concrete user/doc word-pair examples and names HyDE and multi-query as fixes.
queries: what is lexical mismatch in retrieval
user and doc using different words example
sign in vs log in retrieval
screen frozen vs browser performance example
why dense embeddings only partially fix lexical mismatch
how does HyDE fix lexical mismatch

text: Ambiguity: the query has multiple plausible intents. One short question "How do I export?" has three different things the user might want. A naive retriever picks one direction and quietly drops the other two. Three branches from the same user query: "Export data to CSV" -> doc-9 (Export Formats); "Export user list for admin" -> doc-26 (Bulk Operations); "Cancel an export job that failed" -> doc-29 (Export Failures). A naive retriever picks one and misses the other two. Multi-query (21.7) generates paraphrases that cover each branch, and routing (21.8) sends each branch to the index that fits best.
summary: Shows ambiguity failure: one query splits into three plausible intents pointing at different docs, fixed by multi-query and routing.
queries: what is query ambiguity in RAG
How do I export query example
export to CSV vs bulk export vs failed export
Export Formats Bulk Operations Export Failures

text: Multi-intent: one query asks two or more questions. The user packs two asks into one sentence; the retriever embeds the whole thing and lands on docs that cover one ask, missing the other entirely. Example user query: "Cancel my subscription and get a refund". Sub-Intent 1: How to cancel subscription -> doc-15 (Account Deletion Flow). Sub-Intent 2: How to get a refund -> doc-4 (Refunds). Naive retrieval finds docs for one intent and misses the other. Decomposition (21.8) splits the query into two sub-queries, retrieves for each, then combines the results.
summary: Defines multi-intent failure where one sentence packs two asks, fixed by query decomposition into sub-queries.
queries: Cancel my subscription and get a refund example
how to handle two intents in one query

text: Four strategies map to three failures. Each transformation tackles a different failure shape. Strategies: HyDE (21.6) fixes lexical mismatch - ask an LLM to draft a hypothetical answer, then embed the answer instead of the question. Multi-Query Expansion (21.7) fixes ambiguity - generate N paraphrases of the query, retrieve for each, then merge results. Query Decomposition (21.8) fixes multi-intent - split a compound query into independent sub-queries, retrieve for each, then combine. Query Routing (21.8) picks the right index - classify the query and send it to the index, tool, or filter that best matches. Picking the right transformation is itself a routing decision; in production a small classifier or LLM call inspects the query and chooses which strategy (or combination) to run.
summary: Maps the four query-transformation strategies (HyDE, multi-query, decomposition, routing) to the three failure modes they fix.
queries: embed hypothetical answer instead of question

text: Five-box horizontal flow diagram showing the HyDE pipeline: user query, LLM generates hypothetical answer, embed hypothetical answer, vector retrieval, top-K real docs. Boxes are color-coded: orange (User Query), purple (LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer), cyan (Embed Hypothetical Answer), yellow (Vector Retrieval), green (Top-K Real Docs). Arrows connect each stage left to right.
summary: Visual reference for the five-box left-to-right HyDE pipeline diagram with color-coded stages.

text: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) is a RAG retrieval trick: instead of embedding the user's question directly, have an LLM draft a plausible hypothetical answer first, embed THAT, and use it to retrieve real top-K docs. It works because answers share shape and vocabulary with documents while questions do not, so cosine similarity scores higher even if the draft is factually wrong. The pipeline adds one LLM call (~$0.0005, ~200-400ms). HyDE helps on long descriptive, conceptual, or vocabulary-mismatched queries (sign-in vs log-in) and hurts on short factual queries (API key), hallucination-prone topics, latency-critical systems, and when golden-set recall is already above 0.85. A single prompt template instructs the LLM to write one concise paragraph using doc vocabulary and never to refuse with 'I don't know'. Cache by query hash to amortize cost.
summary: Chapter-level overview of HyDE: embed an LLM-drafted hypothetical answer to bridge the question-document vector gap, with concrete tradeoffs, cost, and prompt template.
queries: what does HyDE do in RAG

text: Don't embed the question - embed the hypothetical answer. A user's question and the doc that answers it often live far apart in vector space. They use different words and have different shapes. HyDE flips the order: have an LLM draft a plausible answer first, then embed THAT, then retrieve. Normal RAG: Embed("Why is my dashboard slow?") -> retrieve. HyDE: LLM("Why is my dashboard slow?") = "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions, or network latency." -> Embed that ANSWER -> retrieve. The hypothetical answer is text-shape-closer to actual docs than the question is. Cosine similarity wins.
summary: HyDE inverts RAG by embedding an LLM-drafted hypothetical answer instead of the raw user question because answers sit closer to docs in vector space.
queries: why embed the answer instead of the question
why are questions far from answers in vector space
cosine similarity between question and document
why does embedding the question fail

text: The HyDE flow: Query -> LLM -> Hypothetical Answer -> Embed -> Retrieve. HyDE adds one extra step before retrieval: an LLM call that writes a hypothetical answer. That answer is what gets embedded and sent to the vector index. The user never sees it directly. Five stages in order: (1) User Query, (2) LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer, (3) Embed Hypothetical Answer, (4) Vector Retrieval, (5) Top-K Real Docs. The hypothetical answer is throwaway. Only its embedding goes to the index. The user gets the real top-K docs back, never the LLM's draft.
summary: Walks through the five-stage HyDE pipeline where an extra LLM step produces a throwaway answer whose embedding drives retrieval.
queries: what are the steps of HyDE
how HyDE works step by step
does the user see the hypothetical answer
is the LLM draft shown to the user
where does the embedding happen in HyDE

text: Worked example: 'Why is my dashboard slow?' Three panels: what the user typed, what the LLM drafted, and what the retriever returned. The LLM's draft pulled in vocabulary like 'page load', 'browser extensions', and 'network latency' - exactly the words that appear in the docs that actually help. User Query: "Why is my dashboard slow?" LLM Hypothetical Answer: "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions interfering with rendering, network latency, or background sync jobs consuming resources. Try refreshing, disabling extensions, or checking the network panel." Top-3 Retrieved Docs: doc-22 (Slow Page Load Troubleshooting), doc-23 (500 Errors), doc-27 (Browser Compatibility Issues). Without HyDE, retrieval returned doc-7 (Dashboard Tour) - a generic doc that doesn't help. The question 'why slow' embedded poorly; the answer embedded well.
summary: Concrete walk-through showing how HyDE turns a vague dashboard-slow query into doc-vocabulary that retrieves troubleshooting articles instead of a generic tour.
queries: how HyDE finds the right doc
page load browser extensions network latency

text: Why HyDE works. Three reasons the hypothetical-answer trick beats embedding the raw question. Even if the LLM's draft is factually wrong, the SHAPE of the answer matches the SHAPE of real docs - and that is what cosine similarity scores. (1) Different shapes in vector space: questions look different from answers in vector space. (2) Borrows doc vocabulary: hypothetical answers borrow doc vocabulary even if the LLM doesn't know the actual answer. (3) Robust to lexical mismatch: robust to lexical mismatch because the LLM does the rewriting for you.
summary: Three reasons HyDE works: question and answer shapes differ, drafts inherit doc vocabulary, and lexical mismatch gets absorbed by the LLM rewrite.
queries: shape of answer vs question vector
does HyDE need a correct answer
what if the LLM hallucinates in HyDE
factually wrong hypothetical answer still works
does HyDE need to be right
shape matters more than facts in HyDE

text: When HyDE helps vs when it hurts. HyDE is not free and is not universal. It earns its keep when the user's words are far from the doc's words. It hurts when the query is already short, factual, and embeds well. Helps: long descriptive queries; lexical mismatch (sign-in vs log-in); conceptual / 'why does X happen' questions; queries that don't share vocabulary with docs. Hurts: short factual queries with good embeddings (e.g., 'API key'); queries where the LLM might hallucinate the wrong answer shape; latency-sensitive pipelines (HyDE adds an LLM call: +200-400 ms). If recall on your golden set is already above 0.85, HyDE likely won't help and will add latency.
summary: Decision guide for when HyDE pays off (vague conceptual queries with lexical gap) versus when it hurts (short factual queries, latency-sensitive systems, recall already above 0.85).
queries: should I use HyDE for short queries

text: The HyDE prompt template. The exact prompt you fire at the LLM. The placeholder gets filled with the user's question. The LLM's response is the hypothetical answer that gets embedded. Prompt: "You are a helpful assistant. Write a single concise paragraph that answers the user's question as if you knew the answer. The paragraph will be used to search a documentation index, so include domain terms and likely doc vocabulary. Do not say 'I don't know' - guess plausibly. Question: {query} Hypothetical Answer:" Cost: +1 LLM call per user query (~$0.0005 + ~250ms). Cache the hypothetical answer by query hash to amortize.
summary: Provides the literal HyDE prompt template plus its per-query cost (~$0.0005, ~250ms) and a caching tip keyed on query hash.
queries: what prompt to use for HyDE
do not say I don't know HyDE prompt

Edges colored orange (query -> variants), yellow (variants -> retrieved sets), green (sets -> fused ranking).

text: Three-box horizontal pipeline diagram for multi-query expansion: box 1 'LLM Generates 3-5 Query Variants' (orange), box 2 'Run Vector Retrieval For Each Variant' (yellow), box 3 'Fuse Rankings With RRF' (green). Arrows in orange flow between boxes left to right.

what are the three stages picture

text: Multi-Query Expansion (also called RAG-Fusion) rewrites a user query into 3-5 LLM-generated variants, runs vector retrieval independently for each variant, then fuses the rankings with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) into one final list. Pipeline: LLM generates variants -> parallel retrievals -> RRF fuse. RRF formula: RRF(d) = sum over variants v of 1 / (k + rank_v(d)), k = 60. Step-back prompting is a variant style that asks a broader version of the question. Helps on ambiguous, multi-intent, recall-priority queries. Skip for short factual lookups or latency-critical paths. Adds ~300 ms (1 LLM call) plus N parallel retrievals.
summary: Overview of multi-query expansion: LLM rewrites query into variants, retrieve each, fuse with RRF.
queries: how to expand queries with LLM
reciprocal rank fusion for query variants

text: One query becomes many; fuse the results. If the user's exact wording missed the right doc, one of the variant wordings might catch it. Ask an LLM to rewrite the query into 3-5 variants, retrieve top-K for each, then fuse the rankings into one. RAG-Fusion is the same idea under a different name; this chapter absorbs it.
summary: Core insight: rewrite query into many variants and fuse retrieved rankings to catch docs missed by original wording.
queries: why rewrite a query into variants
what if user wording misses the doc
is RAG-Fusion the same as multi-query
how many query variants to generate

text: Three steps: Generate Variants -> Retrieve Each -> Fuse. The whole pipeline fits in three stages: (1) LLM generates 3-5 query variants, (2) run vector retrieval for each variant, (3) fuse rankings with RRF. Stage 2 runs in parallel - N retrievals fire at once, not in sequence. Latency is dominated by stage 1 (the LLM call), not stage 2.
summary: Three-stage pipeline: LLM rewrites, parallel vector retrievals, RRF fuses; latency dominated by the LLM call.
queries: how many steps in multi-query expansion
where is the latency in multi-query

text: Worked example: 'Cancel my subscription and get a refund' - one query, two intents. A single vector lookup is forced to compromise. With multi-query expansion the LLM splits the wording. Variants generated: Variant 1 'How to cancel my subscription', Variant 2 'How to get a refund after cancellation', Variant 3 'Stop billing immediately'. Top-3 retrieved per variant: Variant 1 -> doc-15, doc-7, doc-3; Variant 2 -> doc-4, doc-15, doc-3; Variant 3 -> doc-5, doc-15, doc-21. Final fused top-3 (after RRF): Rank 1 doc-15 (covers cancellation), Rank 2 doc-4 (covers refund), Rank 3 doc-3 (subscription tiers). Each variant brings a different doc to the top; fusion captures both intents - doc-15 for cancellation and doc-4 for refund.
summary: Worked example showing a two-intent query split into 3 variants, each retrieving 3 docs, fused into a final top-3.
queries: what variants does the LLM produce

text: Fuse with RRF (same formula as the hybrid search chapter). Reciprocal Rank Fusion takes N rankings and produces one. Each doc's score is the sum of 1 / (k + its rank) across every ranking it appears in. The constant k = 60 dampens the influence of any single list so agreement across variants matters more than a top-1 appearance. Formula: RRF(d) = sum over variants v of: 1 / (k + rank_v(d)), where k = 60 (standard). Same fusion math as hybrid search; different inputs - here the rankings come from N variants of the same query rather than from BM25 + dense.
summary: RRF formula RRF(d) = sum over v of 1/(k + rank_v(d)) with k=60 fuses N variant rankings into one.
queries: BM25 plus dense vs multi-query RRF

text: Step-back prompting: add a more general variant. Step-back is a specific variant style - ask a broader, less specific version of the question. The intuition: a doc might not match the user's exact narrow wording, but it almost certainly matches a more general phrasing of the same problem. Example - Original (very specific): 'Why is the user role page failing on Chrome v124 with error E_PERM?'. Step-back (more general): 'Why does the user role page fail?'. The original is too specific - the doc might not mention Chrome v124. The step-back catches more general docs. Reasoning models reduce the need for explicit step-back; include it when your queries are unusually specific. This is one of many possible variant prompts; the main approach covers the common case and step-back is a niche addition.
summary: Step-back prompting generates a broader phrasing of the query so docs not mentioning narrow specifics still match.
queries: how to generalize a query for retrieval

text: When multi-query helps. Multi-query expansion is not a default - it earns its keep on hard, ambiguous, or multi-intent queries. On short factual lookups it just burns budget. Helps: ambiguous queries with multiple plausible intents; complex multi-clause queries; recall is the priority over latency; reranker can absorb the wider candidate set. Skip: short factual lookups ('What is my account ID?'); latency-critical paths (adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals); when HyDE already solves the same failure. Cost: adds ~300 ms (1 LLM call) + N parallel retrievals (cheap). Often pairs well with HyDE for hard cases.
summary: When multi-query expansion helps (ambiguous, multi-clause, recall-priority) versus skip (factual, latency-critical, HyDE covers it); ~300 ms overhead.

text: Diagram: Router decision tree with four leaves. The root node is labeled 'Classify Query Type' (cyan). Four branch lines drop to four leaf boxes laid out left-to-right: Account & Billing -> Account-Billing Index, Top-K = 5 (cyan); Product Features -> Product-Features Index, Top-K = 5 (purple); Troubleshooting -> Troubleshooting Index, Top-K = 8 (yellow); Chitchat / Off-Topic -> Skip Retrieval, Reply Directly (red). The tree visually conveys that classification routes each query to one of three indexes or skips retrieval entirely.
summary: Diagram of a four-leaf router decision tree mapping query types to indexes (or skip-retrieval) with Top-K values.
queries: what does the routing diagram show

text: Query Routing and Decomposition are two query-transformation strategies in RAG. Routing decides WHERE a query goes (which index or tool, or skip retrieval entirely for chitchat). Decomposition decides HOW MANY sub-queries are needed (split complex queries into 2-3 parts, retrieve each, then assemble). Routing implementations: semantic router (embedding-based, ~$0.0001/query, ~10ms, 85-90% accuracy) vs LLM classifier (~$0.0002/query, ~150ms, 92-97%). Decomposition fits comparison, multi-hop, and disjunction query shapes. The 2x2 decision: Route only (default for production), Decompose only (multi-hop heavy), Both (compose), Neither (simple lookups). Combined overhead ~300-450 ms / $0.0006-0.0007 per query.
summary: Chapter overview of query routing (where) and decomposition (how many) as composable RAG query-transformation strategies.
queries: what is query routing in RAG
when to split a query into sub-queries
how to route queries to the right index
should I route or decompose RAG queries
cost of query routing and decomposition

text: Routing decides WHERE; Decomposition decides HOW MANY. Two related query-transformation strategies share this chapter. Routing answers a where question - which index or tool should the query hit. Pick one target. Skip retrieval entirely for off-topic chitchat. Decomposition answers a how-many question - does the query need to be split into smaller sub-queries first. Split complex queries into 2-3 sub-queries; retrieve and assemble. Both rely on an LLM (or a cheaper classifier) to inspect the query and decide. They compose - a router can send a query to a decomposition step, or a decomposed sub-query can itself be routed to a different index.
summary: Introduces routing as a where-question and decomposition as a how-many-question, both LLM-driven and composable.
queries: what does routing decide in RAG
where vs how many query strategies
pick one target index for a query
how many sub-queries to split into
two strategies for query handling RAG

text: Routing sends each query to the right target. On the running support corpus, queries split cleanly into four buckets. A router classifies each query into one of them, then sends it to the matching index - or skips retrieval entirely for chitchat. The four leaves: Account & Billing -> Account-Billing Index, Top-K = 5. Product Features -> Product-Features Index, Top-K = 5. Troubleshooting -> Troubleshooting Index, Top-K = 8. Chitchat / Off-Topic -> Skip Retrieval, Reply Directly. Routing avoids retrieving from indexes that can't help. Skipping retrieval on chitchat saves latency and cost - and prevents the LLM from hallucinating about a doc it doesn't need to read.
summary: Shows a four-leaf router decision tree mapping each query type to a target index and Top-K, including a skip-retrieval branch for chitchat.
queries: four query buckets in RAG router
why route queries to different indexes

text: Two router implementations: Embedding-Based vs LLM Classifier. Semantic Router (Embedding-Based): embed the query, compare to embeddings of route prototypes ('Billing question', 'Troubleshooting question'). Pick highest cosine. Cost ~$0.0001 / query, Latency ~10 ms, Accuracy 85-90%. LLM Classifier: pass the query + route labels to a small LLM ('Classify this query: billing / feature / troubleshoot / chitchat'). Pick the label. Cost ~$0.0002 / query, Latency ~150 ms, Accuracy 92-97%. Embedding-based is ~15x faster and slightly cheaper; LLM classifier handles ambiguous wording better. Start with semantic; switch to LLM if routing accuracy stalls below your bar.
summary: Compares semantic (embedding) router vs LLM classifier across cost, latency, and accuracy with concrete numbers.
queries: how does a semantic router work
when to use LLM classifier for routing
which router implementation to start with

text: Decomposition splits 'Compare X and Y' into two sub-queries. User query: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' LLM splits into sub-queries. Sub-Query 1: 'What's in the Pro plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers) and doc-10 (Team Seat Management). Sub-Query 2: 'What's in the Enterprise plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers), doc-10 (Team Seat Management), and doc-21 (Quota Exceeded - Enterprise-Only Feature). LLM assembly step: union of retrieved docs (doc-3, doc-10, doc-21) is passed to a single LLM call that writes the side-by-side comparison. Without decomposition, a single vector lookup gets pulled toward whichever subject has stronger phrasing and misses the other. Decomposition guarantees both sides of the comparison are retrieved before the LLM has to answer.
summary: Worked example: comparing Pro vs Enterprise plans is split into two sub-queries, retrieved separately, then unioned for one LLM assembly call.
queries: compare Pro and Enterprise plans RAG example
how to decompose a comparison query
union of retrieved docs for comparison
why single vector lookup misses comparisons
Pro plan vs Enterprise plan retrieval

text: When to decompose: Multi-Hop, Comparison, Disjunction. Three query shapes consistently benefit from being split before retrieval. Comparison example: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' Split: Sub-1: What's in the Pro plan? + Sub-2: What's in the Enterprise plan? Multi-Hop example: 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' Split: Sub-1: How to recover email? + Sub-2: How to reset password? Disjunction (Mixed Types) example: 'Why is the dashboard slow and showing 500 errors?' Split: Sub-1: Dashboard slow troubleshooting + Sub-2: 500 error troubleshooting. If the query has 'and' / 'compare' / 'or' between two distinct subjects, decomposition usually helps. Single-subject queries don't benefit and just pay the extra LLM call for nothing.
summary: Lists the three query shapes (comparison, multi-hop, disjunction) that benefit from decomposition with concrete examples and a heuristic.

text: The decision: Route, Decompose, Both, or Neither. Four combinations cover every production case. Route Only: most production RAG systems start here. Cheap, easy, high-impact. Decompose Only: use when queries are consistently complex (multi-hop, comparisons). Adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals. Both: route first to pick the right index, then decompose if the query is complex. They compose - they don't interfere. Neither: default for simple lookup queries. Don't add complexity you don't need. Cost summary: Routing adds +10-150 ms / +$0.0001-0.0002 per query. Decomposition adds +200-300 ms / +$0.0005 per query. Combined: ~+300-450 ms total query-transformation overhead.
summary: 2x2 decision grid for route/decompose/both/neither with cost-and-latency overhead numbers for each combination.
queries: should I use both routing and decomposition
when not to use query transformation
when to start with route only

Segment widths are proportional to token counts on a centered bar inside an 800x130 viewBox. Legend and numeric breakdown table show each slot's token count and percent share of the 8000-token total.

text: Context packing turns retrieved chunks into a final prompt that fits a token budget. The 8000-token window splits into system prompt, retrieved context, user question, reserved completion, and headroom. Chunks can be ordered relevance-first (best at position 1, default for single answers), chronologically (for time-sensitive docs), or deduplicated with MMR (lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked))) to drop near-duplicates. The packed prompt combines a system instruction, citation-marked chunks, the question, and an allowed refusal phrase, fitting within budget.
summary: This chapter covers token budgeting, chunk ordering strategies (relevance-first, chronological, MMR deduplication), and assembling the final packed prompt.

token budget and chunk ordering overview

text: Every prompt has a token budget. An 8000-token context window splits across four jobs: the system prompt that frames the model (200 tokens, 2.5%), the retrieved context with the answer (5000 tokens, 62.5%), the user question (50 tokens, 0.6%), and the reserved completion tokens the model uses to write its answer (1500 tokens, 18.8%). Headroom (1250 tokens, 15.6%) is the slack you keep so a long answer never overflows. Slots: System Prompt 200, Retrieved Context 5000, User Question 50, Reserved Completion 1500, Headroom 1250. Total = 8000.
summary: An 8000-token context window splits across system prompt, retrieved context, user question, reserved completion, and headroom.

text: Pack Top-3 chunks for the password reset query. The running query 'How do I reset my password?' returned three chunks from doc-1 with cosine scores. The scores tell us how to order; the chunks tell us what to pack. [doc-1, chunk 1] Score 0.91: 'To reset your password, go to Settings then Security.' [doc-1, chunk 2] Score 0.84: 'An email with a reset link will be sent to your registered address.' [doc-1, chunk 3] Score 0.77: 'Click the link within 24 hours to set a new password.'
summary: Three retrieved chunks with cosine scores form the candidate set for packing the password reset query.

how to identify chunk by id and score

text: Ordering Option 1: Relevance-First. Sort by retrieval score, highest first. The most-relevant chunk lands at position 1 where the model attends best. Default for single-best-answer queries. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] (Score 0.91), 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] (Score 0.84), 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] (Score 0.77). Most relevant first. Cheap to do - just sort by score. Risk: tail-position chunks lose attention weight.
summary: Relevance-first ordering sorts packed chunks by descending retrieval score so the best chunk lands where the model attends most.
queries: how to order RAG chunks by score
should I sort retrieved chunks by score
where does the model attend best
how to pack chunks in prompt

text: Ordering Option 2: Chronological. Sort by publication date or document position. Useful for time-sensitive docs - changelogs, policy histories, incident timelines - where the natural reading order helps the model follow cause-effect. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] - Document Position 1, 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] - Document Position 2, 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] - Document Position 3. Time-sensitive ordering preserves the natural sequence so the model reads cause then effect.
summary: Chronological ordering packs chunks by date or document position so the model follows the natural cause-effect sequence.
queries: when to order RAG chunks by date
when not to sort by relevance

text: Ordering Option 3: Deduplicate with MMR. Maximal Marginal Relevance keeps high-score chunks while dropping near-duplicates. Same budget, more unique information. Before MMR (5 chunks, redundant): chunk 1, chunk 2, chunk 3, chunk 4 (duplicate - wastes budget, struck through), chunk 5. After MMR (4 chunks, diverse): [doc-1, chunk 1], [doc-1, chunk 2], [doc-1, chunk 3], [doc-9, chunk 1] 'MFA must be re-verified after a reset (different but related).' The MMR formula: MMR = lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked)). Lambda = 0.7 weights relevance over diversity. Lambda = 0.3 weights diversity over relevance. Tune per workload.
summary: MMR drops near-duplicate chunks using a lambda-weighted formula that balances query relevance against similarity to already-picked chunks.
queries: how to balance diversity and relevance
sim chunk query minus sim chunk picked
how to pick diverse chunks for RAG

text:
The final packed prompt: system instruction, retrieved chunks with citation markers, user question, and an allowed refusal phrase. Prompt template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below to answer.
If the docs don't contain the answer, say "I don't have enough information".

Documentation:
[doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, go to Settings then Security.
[doc-1, chunk 2] An email with a reset link will be sent to your address.
[doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link within 24 hours to set a new password.

Question: How do I reset my password?
Answer:

Within budget: ~4,800 / 8,000 tokens (60%).
summary: The final packed prompt combines system instruction, citation-marked chunks, the user question, and a refusal phrase, fitting within ~60% of the 8000-token budget.

text: LLMs attend best to the start and end of a long context window and ignore the middle - the lost-in-the-middle effect (Liu et al. 2023), shown as a U-shaped accuracy curve over chunk position. The same retrieved chunk can produce a right or wrong answer depending solely on its slot. Two cheap fixes reorder packed chunks: front-load (sort by score descending so the best chunk goes to position 1) and sandwich (top-2 at front, next-best at back, weakest in middle). Both fail for truly long multi-fact queries, tail-buried critical facts, or models with flat curves; those need hierarchical summarization, multi-hop retrieval, or harder reranking. Always benchmark on your own model.
summary: This chapter explains the lost-in-the-middle effect and two reordering strategies (front-load and sandwich) that exploit the U-shaped attention curve.

text: Liu et al. (2023) showed that LLMs attend best to the start and end of a long context window, and the middle gets ignored. Plotting 20 packed chunks, answer accuracy is highest when the relevant chunk sits at position 1 or position 20, and lowest in the middle. The curve is U-shaped: accuracy starts near 85% at position 1, dips to about 55% around positions 8-10 (the middle dip), then climbs back toward 82% at position 20.
summary: LLMs read long contexts in a U-shaped way - accuracy peaks at the ends and dips in the middle.

text: Query: "Does the Pro plan include SSO?" The answer-bearing chunk is "SSO is Enterprise-only" (doc-15, chunk 3). With 10 packed chunks, the position of that chunk decides whether the model finds the fact. Row A - Answer at position 5 (middle): 10 packed chunks; the SSO chunk lands at position 5. Model: "I don't see SSO mentioned for the Pro plan." Wrong - the model ignored the middle chunk. Row B - Answer at position 1 (front): same 10 chunks reordered; the SSO chunk lands at position 1. Model: "No, SSO is Enterprise-only." Correct - same chunk, better position.

text: Strategy 1: Front-load the most relevant chunk. Relevance-first ordering means sort chunks by retrieval score descending. The top-scored chunks land in positions 1, 2, 3 - the high-attention region. Tail positions hold the lowest-scored chunks. Example slot scores: Pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), Pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), Pos 3 = 0.84 (Top-3), tailing down to Pos 10 = 0.38. Cheap. Helps single-best-answer queries. Risk: tail context still ignored, but the top-scored chunks land in the attention sweet spot.
summary: Front-load strategy sorts chunks by score descending so best chunks hit the high-attention front positions.
queries: how to fix lost in the middle problem
sort chunks by retrieval score descending
best position for top chunk in context
should top chunk go first in prompt
high attention region in context window
where to put best chunk in RAG prompt
cheap fix for lost in the middle
putting top score chunk at position 1

text: Strategy 2: Sandwich the best chunks at start and end. Put the top-2 chunks at the front and the next-best two at the back. The middle dip on the U-curve becomes the least-relevant region. Same total tokens; reordering is negligible compute. Slot layout: Pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), Pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), Pos 9 = 0.84 (Top-3), Pos 10 = 0.78 (Top-4), with lower-scored chunks (0.66 down to 0.31) filling positions 3-8. More robust for multi-fact queries. Same total tokens. Costs reordering compute (negligible).
summary: Sandwich strategy bookends best chunks at start and end so the U-curve's middle dip holds the least-relevant content.
queries: put best chunks at start and end
fix middle dip in attention curve
where to put second best chunk
how to handle multi-fact queries in RAG
best chunks bookend start and end
place lowest scored chunks in middle

text: When reordering isn't enough: front-load and sandwich help most workloads but not all. Three failure modes need different fixes than position tricks. (1) Truly long multi-fact queries: 30 chunks all needed; even sandwich leaves middle facts ignored. Fix: hierarchical summarization or multi-hop retrieval. (2) Tail-buried critical detail: the killer fact lives at chunk 18; both strategies miss it. Fix: rerank harder, fetch fewer. (3) Model-specific curve shape: some models have flatter curves than others; benchmark on your own model. Lost-in-middle is a model fingerprint, not a universal law. Always benchmark on your own model and workload.
summary: Reordering tricks fail for long multi-fact queries, tail-buried facts, and model-specific curve shapes - each needs a different fix.
queries: when does reordering not fix lost in middle
failure modes of front-load and sandwich

lost in middle as model fingerprint

text: Citations and refusal make RAG answers auditable and safe. Inline [doc-N] markers (or structured JSON citations) let every claim trace back to a retrieved chunk, while an explicit refusal instruction gives the model an authorized exit when context is missing so it stops hallucinating. After generation, a regex parser maps markers back to chunks for footnotes/tooltips, and a 3-step audit converts citations into a faithfulness score from 0 to 1. A production-ready template combines citation rules, quoted-span requirement, and the exact refusal sentence as numbered RULES so the model treats them as non-optional.
summary: Overview of citations, refusal, faithfulness audit, and a combined production prompt for grounded RAG generation.
queries: how to make RAG answers cite sources

text: Without citations, the user cannot verify the answer and a reviewer cannot audit it. Hallucinations slip through silently. With inline [doc-N] markers, every claim is traceable. The same answer with citations becomes an artifact you can review, replay, and trust. Example - No Citations: Query 'Why is my account locked?' Answer: 'Your account may be locked due to too many failed logins or a billing issue.' User cannot verify. Reviewer cannot audit. Hallucination has nowhere to land. With Citations: Same query. Answer: 'Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7].' Every claim traces to a specific chunk. Audit-friendly. Hallucinations exposed.
summary: Citations turn an opaque answer into an auditable artifact - every claim traces to a specific chunk via [doc-N].

text: The citation instruction lives in the system prompt. Use a literal [doc-N] marker the model can emit inline. Specify that N must come from the Documentation block, not be invented. Prompt template: 'You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below to answer the user's question. For every claim you make, cite the source as [doc-N], where N is the doc number from the Documentation section. Do NOT cite a doc you did not use. Documentation: {context} Question: {query} Answer:'
summary: Sample system prompt that instructs the model to emit inline [doc-N] citations sourced from the documentation block.

text: JSON output is machine-parseable and downstream audit pipelines can ingest it without regex. The schema pairs the answer with its citations and a confidence score. Example structured output: { "answer": "Your account locks after 5 failed logins.", "citations": [ { "doc_id": "doc-12", "chunk_id": 3, "quote": "5 failed login attempts" } ], "confidence": 0.92 }. Structured output makes citations machine-parseable. Better for downstream audit pipelines.
summary: Alternative to inline markers: ask the model for a structured JSON object containing answer, citations array, and confidence.
queries: how to get structured citations from LLM
how to format LLM citations as JSON

text: Without an explicit refusal instruction, models tend to invent. With one, the model has an authorized exit for 'I don't know'. The exact wording matters - models follow the most precise instructions in the prompt. Refusal prompt: 'If the documentation does not contain enough information to answer, respond with EXACTLY this sentence and nothing else: "I don't have enough information to answer that." Do not invent facts. Do not guess. Do not cite a doc you didn't use.' Without refusal instruction: Query 'What's the refund window for monthly plans?' (no matching doc). Answer: 'Monthly plans have a 7-day refund window.' Hallucinated - the model guessed because it had no exit. With refusal instruction: Same query, same missing doc. Answer: 'I don't have enough information to answer that.' Safe - the user knows the system was honest about its limit.
summary: An explicit refusal instruction gives the model a sanctioned 'I don't know' exit so it stops fabricating answers when context is missing.
queries: how to make LLM refuse when no answer exists
how to stop LLM from hallucinating answers
I don't have enough information to answer that prompt

why do models hallucinate without refusal
force model to say I don't know
do not cite a doc you didn't use

text: Faithfulness is the share of claims in an answer that are supported by the retrieved chunks. A 3-step audit converts citations into a measurable score. Step 1: Parse Answer Into Atomic Claims - Example: '5 failed logins triggers a lock' / 'Billing issues hold for 24 hours'. Step 2: Locate The Cited Chunk For Each Claim - Follow the [doc-N] marker back to the retrieved chunk text. Step 3: Score: claim_supported = LLMJudge(claim, chunk) - Aggregate per-claim scores into a faithfulness number from 0 to 1. Output is a single number from 0 (none supported) to 1 (every claim has evidence in the retrieved chunks). RAGAS faithfulness is covered in depth in later chapters.
summary: Three-step faithfulness audit: split answer into atomic claims, follow each [doc-N] to its chunk, and use an LLM judge to score support, then aggregate to a 0-1 score.
queries: how to measure faithfulness of RAG answer
what is faithfulness score in RAG

text: After generation, parse [doc-N] markers out of the answer with a regex. Map each marker back to the retrieved chunk and render as footnotes or hover tooltips in the UI. Example model output: 'Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7].' Regex: \[doc-\d+\]. Resolved citation table: marker [doc-12] -> doc-12 (Account Lockouts), chunk 3, quoted span '5 failed login attempts'. Marker [doc-7] -> doc-7 (Billing Holds), chunk 1, quoted span '24-hour hold after a failed charge'. Render as footnotes or inline tooltips in your UI so the user can click a citation and see exactly what the model used to write that sentence.
summary: Post-generation step: regex-extract [doc-N] markers, resolve to source chunk + quoted span, and render as footnotes or tooltips.

text: One combined template does both citations and refusal. Numbered RULES make the contract explicit so the model treats citations and refusal as non-optional. Production prompt: 'You are a helpful support assistant. Use ONLY the documentation below to answer the user's question. RULES: 1. Cite every claim inline as [doc-N], where N is the doc number. 2. Quote 3-7 words from the cited chunk after each [doc-N]. 3. If the docs do not answer the question, respond with EXACTLY: "I don't have enough information to answer that." 4. Do not invent facts. Do not paraphrase what isn't in the docs. Documentation: {context} Question: {query} Answer:' Numbered rules survive long context better than prose instructions. The model attends to short structured constraints more reliably than to a paragraph.
summary: Production-grade combined template uses numbered RULES to enforce inline citations, short quoted spans, an exact refusal sentence, and no invention.
queries: how to write production RAG system prompt

text: SVG diagram: Multi-hop retrieval control-loop flowchart showing User Query box at top flowing into Retrieve Top-K For Current Query box, then into a yellow diamond labeled 'Does Context Answer The Question?'. YES (green arrow) exits right to Generate Answer box. NO with Hops < max_hops (orange arrow) exits left to Reformulate Query box, then loops back up to Retrieve. NO with Hops >= max_hops (red arrow) exits down to Refuse box labeled 'I Don't Have Enough Information'. Colors: purple for query, cyan for retrieve, yellow for decision diamond, green for generate, orange for reformulate, red for refuse.
summary: Visual SVG flowchart of the multi-hop loop: retrieve, sufficiency diamond, reformulate loop-back, refuse on cap.
queries: user query to generate answer flow

text: This chapter covers multi-hop retrieval in RAG: queries that need a chain of facts across multiple documents. Single-hop retrieval fails when one chunk only contains half the answer. The multi-hop control loop retrieves, checks sufficiency, then either generates the answer or reformulates the query and loops again, capped by max_hops (typically 3-5). A sufficiency-check prompt asks the model to respond SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT with a follow-up query. Multi-hop is worth it for compound questions, cross-doc reasoning, and step-dependent flows, but each hop multiplies latency and cost (~3x for 3 hops). Failure modes: infinite loops, divergence from original intent, and overconfident sufficiency judges. Mitigations: hard cap max_hops, anchor original query, separate calibrated judge model.
summary: Overview of multi-hop retrieval: why, how the control loop works, sufficiency prompts, costs, and failure modes.
queries: what is multi-hop retrieval in RAG
when do I need more than one retrieval
how does a multi-hop RAG loop work
should I use multi-hop or single-hop
RAG when one retrieval isn't enough
how many hops should RAG do

text: Some queries need more than one retrieval. Single-hop retrieval works when the answer fits in one chunk. Multi-hop retrieval kicks in when the question depends on a chain of facts that live in different docs. The signal you need it: even a perfect retrieval returns only half of what is needed. Single-Hop Works example: Query 'How do I reset my password?' One retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset). Answer: 'Go to Settings then Security, click Reset Password.' Multi-Hop Needed example: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' One retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset) but the question also needs doc-3 (email change / recovery). One retrieval misses half. Multiple retrievals needed.
summary: Single-hop suffices for self-contained queries; multi-hop is required when answers depend on facts spread across multiple documents.
queries: why is one retrieval not always enough
RAG retrieval misses half the answer
how do I reset password if I forgot email RAG
RAG fetches only half the information
signal that a question needs chained retrievals

text: Two hops on the running query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?'. Hop 1: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?', Retrieved: doc-1 (password reset). Reasoning: Reset requires email access. The user says they forgot email - need email recovery info before answering. Decision: INSUFFICIENT -> Reformulate as: 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'. Hop 2: Query 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?', Retrieved: doc-3 (email change / recovery). Reasoning: Now both pieces are in hand: the reset flow (doc-1) and the email-recovery path (doc-3). Decision: SUFFICIENT. Final answer combines doc-1 + doc-3 with citations. Each hop reformulates the query to fetch the missing piece.
summary: Walkthrough of a 2-hop trace showing how Hop 1 finds doc-1, reformulates, and Hop 2 fetches doc-3 to complete the answer.
queries: doc-1 password reset doc-3 email recovery
how LLM combines docs across hops
how does the model decide INSUFFICIENT

text: The multi-hop control loop. Loop structure: retrieve, check sufficiency, generate or reformulate. A hard cap on max_hops prevents infinite loops. If the cap is hit without sufficiency, the system refuses. Flow: User Query -> Retrieve Top-K For Current Query -> diamond 'Does Context Answer The Question?'. YES branch -> Generate Answer. NO branch with Hops < max_hops -> Reformulate Query -> loop back to Retrieve Top-K. NO branch with Hops >= max_hops -> Refuse: 'I Don't Have Enough Information'. max_hops typically 3-5. Each hop adds 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call latency. Tune based on workload and budget.
summary: Control-loop diagram for multi-hop retrieval with sufficiency check, reformulation loop-back, and refusal on max_hops.
queries: refuse when not enough information RAG
what happens if multi-hop hits max hops

text:
Asking the model: do you have enough? The sufficiency check is a small structured task. The model decides whether the retrieved text answers the question. SUFFICIENT means 'I can answer now'; INSUFFICIENT means 'fetch this follow-up'. Prompt Template - Sufficiency Check:

You are an assistant deciding whether you have enough information.

Question: {query}
Retrieved so far:
{context}

Respond with EXACTLY one of:
- SUFFICIENT (you can answer the question from the retrieved text)
- INSUFFICIENT: <one short follow-up query to retrieve more>

Do not answer the question yet.

Example Trace: Hop 1: 'INSUFFICIENT: How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'. Hop 2: 'SUFFICIENT'.
summary: Exact prompt template that asks the LLM to output SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT plus a follow-up query, with an example trace.
queries: how to ask LLM if it has enough info
prompt to decide whether to keep retrieving
how does the model know to stop retrieving

text: When to reach for multi-hop. Multi-hop is not free. Each hop multiplies latency and cost. Use it only when the query genuinely needs chained facts, and always with a hard cap on hops. Worth It: compound questions (password reset if you forgot your email), cross-doc reasoning (compare Pro vs Enterprise plans), step-dependent flows (cancel subscription then refund). Not Worth It: single-doc lookups, simple FAQ-style queries, cost outweighs benefit when one hop already answers. Cost Multiplier: each hop = 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call; 3 hops = ~3x latency, ~3x cost vs single-hop; budget per query becomes a hard constraint. Mitigations: cap max_hops at 3, cache reformulated queries, use a smaller model for the sufficiency check.
summary: When multi-hop is worth it versus not, the cost multiplier per hop, and mitigations like caps, caching, and smaller judge models.
queries: FAQ queries multi-hop not worth it
cost of each hop retrieval plus LLM

text: Failure modes: infinite loops and divergence. Three known failure modes show up in production multi-hop systems. Each has a known mitigation that costs either an extra check or stricter caps. Infinite Loop: model never says SUFFICIENT. Each hop reformulates without making progress. Mitigation: hard cap on max_hops + log every reformulation. Divergence: each reformulation drifts farther from the original intent. 'How do I reset my password?' becomes 'How do I configure SSO?' by hop 3. Mitigation: keep the ORIGINAL query in the reformulation prompt as an anchor. Stuck Sufficiency Judge: model always says SUFFICIENT after hop 1 even when context is incomplete (overconfident). Mitigation: separate judge model, calibrate via a golden dataset.
summary: Three multi-hop failure modes (infinite loop, divergence, stuck overconfident judge) and their mitigations.
queries: how to prevent infinite multi-hop loops

text: Self-RAG token-emission timeline diagram: horizontal line with six markers showing the model alternating between special control tokens and answer text across the generation sequence. Markers in order: <retrieve> (model gates retrieval), Reformulate (tokens 2-3 produce search query), doc-1 In (retriever returns top doc), <isrel> (model judges doc-1 relevant), Answer (tokens 6-20 generate answer text), <issup> (model judges answer supported). Generation order left to right.
summary: Diagram showing the left-to-right order of Self-RAG control tokens interleaved with answer generation.
queries: what does Self-RAG timeline look like

text: Retrieve / no-retrieve decision diamond diagram: User Query box at top flows into yellow diamond labeled "Need external knowledge?". YES branch on right exits to green box "<retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>". NO branch on left exits to red box "<no-retrieve> -> Answer". Below: "Trained via instruction tuning + RL. Reduces unnecessary retrievals ~40% (Asai et al. 2023)".
summary: Diagram of the Self-RAG gate diamond routing queries to either retrieve-plus-critique or direct-answer.

text: Self-RAG (Asai et al. 2023) trains the LLM to decide per-query whether to retrieve, then self-critique what comes back. Model emits four special control tokens inline: <retrieve>, <no-retrieve>, <isrel> (judges each retrieved doc's relevance), and <issup> (judges whether final answer is supported by kept docs). Runtime intercepts tokens and acts. Cuts unnecessary retrievals ~40%. Wins on mixed-knowledge corpora, latency-sensitive workloads, adaptive depth. Limits: needs a fine-tuned model, larger vocab, classifier calibration is the bottleneck.
summary: Self-RAG teaches the LLM to gate retrieval and self-critique results using four special control tokens.
queries: special tokens RAG retrieve isrel issup

text: Should the model retrieve, or just answer? Always-retrieve wastes tokens and latency. Never-retrieve risks hallucination. Self-RAG decides per-query. Example queries: "What is 2 + 2?" -> <no-retrieve> (factoid already known from pre-training). "How do I reset my password?" -> <retrieve> (custom-corpus knowledge model cannot answer without retrieval). "Summarize what we just discussed." -> <no-retrieve> (context already in conversation; no external lookup needed).
summary: Self-RAG gates retrieval per-query so the model only fetches when external knowledge is genuinely needed.

text: Self-RAG extends model vocabulary with four control tokens emitted inline during generation; runtime intercepts each. <retrieve>: retrieve a document now. <no-retrieve>: do not retrieve, answer from model's own knowledge. <isrel>: is this retrieved doc relevant? Emitted after each retrieved doc. <issup>: is the answer supported by the retrieved doc? Emitted with the answer.
summary: Four special tokens (<retrieve>, <no-retrieve>, <isrel>, <issup>) extend the model vocabulary to control retrieval and grade results.

text: Token emission timeline for query "How do I reset my password?": <retrieve> (model gates retrieval) -> Reformulate (tokens 2-3 produce a search query) -> doc-1 In (retriever returns top doc) -> <isrel> (model judges doc-1 relevant) -> Answer (tokens 6-20 generate answer text) -> <issup> (model judges answer supported). Each marker is a decision point the runtime watches. Generation order left to right.
summary: Walk through a real query showing the sequence of control tokens and answer text Self-RAG emits.

text: Retrieve / no-retrieve gate sits at top of every generation. User query enters decision diamond "Need external knowledge?" YES branch -> <retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>. NO branch -> <no-retrieve> -> Answer. Trained via instruction tuning + RL on retrieval decisions. Reduces unnecessary retrievals ~40% in original Asai et al. 2023 paper.
summary: The gate diamond routes queries to retrieval-plus-critique or direct-answer paths, trained with instruction tuning plus RL.

text: Self-critique re-scores every retrieved doc. After retrieval, model emits <isrel> per doc to grade relevance. Per-doc critique example: doc-1 (Password Reset) RELEVANT -> Keep; doc-9 (Login Troubleshooting) PARTIALLY RELEVANT -> Keep but de-weight; doc-22 (Rate Limits) IRRELEVANT -> Drop. After answer, model emits <issup> final verdict: FULLY-SUPPORTED | PARTIALLY-SUPPORTED | NO-SUPPORT. Labels how grounded the answer is in the kept docs.
summary: After retrieval Self-RAG grades each doc with <isrel> and grades the final answer's groundedness with <issup>.

text: Self-RAG wins: mixed-knowledge corpora (some queries need retrieval, some don't); latency-sensitive workloads (skips retrieval for ~40% of queries); adaptive depth (decides how many docs to fetch). Limits: requires a model trained for Self-RAG (not plug-and-play); token vocabulary increases serialization cost; calibration of the special-token classifier is the bottleneck. Pick Self-RAG when fine-tuning is on the table and the workload mixes retrieval-needing and already-knew-it queries.
summary: Self-RAG wins on adaptive mixed-knowledge workloads but requires fine-tuning and classifier calibration.

text: CRAG three-branch decision tree: an evaluator score routes retrieved docs to a CORRECT path (use directly), an AMBIGUOUS path (combine internal + web), or an INCORRECT path (web-search fallback), all converging into knowledge refinement before generation.
summary: Diagram showing the CRAG evaluator routing docs into three branches that converge into knowledge refinement and generation.
queries: Visual showing CORRECT AMBIGUOUS INCORRECT branches

text: CRAG (Corrective RAG, Yan et al. 2024) inserts a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation. Each retrieved doc is scored and classified as CORRECT (use directly), AMBIGUOUS (combine internal docs with external web search), or INCORRECT (fall back to web search). All branches converge into a knowledge refinement step that decomposes docs into 100-150 token strips, drops off-topic strips, and packs only on-topic ones before generation. Threshold guidance: below 0.5 = INCORRECT, above 0.8 = CORRECT, between = AMBIGUOUS. Demonstrated on query 'What is our cancellation policy for monthly plans?' where doc-7 scored 0.92 (CORRECT), doc-22 scored 0.31 (INCORRECT), doc-15 scored 0.62 (AMBIGUOUS).
summary: CRAG adds an evaluator that routes retrieved docs into CORRECT, AMBIGUOUS, or INCORRECT branches and refines surviving docs into strips before generation.
queries: When does CRAG fall back to web search
How to handle bad retrieval in RAG
How to detect irrelevant retrieved docs
What threshold makes a doc CORRECT in CRAG

text: CRAG (Yan et al. 2024) adds a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation. Each doc is scored and classified: CORRECT (use it), AMBIGUOUS (combine with external sources), or INCORRECT (fall back to web). Running query: 'What is our cancellation policy for monthly plans?' Scored docs: doc-7 (Refunds + Cancellation) score 0.92 verdict CORRECT - snippet 'Customers can cancel monthly plans any time before the next billing cycle.' doc-22 (API Rate Limits) score 0.31 verdict INCORRECT - snippet 'Free tier allows 100 requests per minute; Pro tier 1000 per minute.' doc-15 (Subscription Tiers) score 0.62 verdict AMBIGUOUS - snippet 'Monthly and annual plans differ in pricing and feature access.' The evaluator can be a small classifier model or an LLM judge. Threshold below 0.5 = INCORRECT, above 0.8 = CORRECT, between = AMBIGUOUS.
summary: The CRAG retrieval evaluator scores each retrieved doc and labels it CORRECT, AMBIGUOUS, or INCORRECT using thresholds 0.5 and 0.8.

text: CORRECT branch: highest-confidence path. Doc passes the evaluator; CRAG routes it through knowledge refinement and then to the LLM with citations. Flow: Retrieved Doc -> Knowledge Refinement -> Generate Answer With Citations. Same as baseline RAG once the docs pass the evaluator.
summary: On the CORRECT branch, passing docs go through knowledge refinement and into generation just like baseline RAG.
queries: What happens on the CRAG CORRECT branch
How does CRAG use docs that pass the evaluator
Is CRAG CORRECT branch same as baseline RAG
Flow after CORRECT verdict in CRAG
When evaluator gives high score what happens

text: INCORRECT branch: doc fails the evaluator. CRAG rewrites the query for web search, calls an external search API, and uses the web results in place of the internal doc. Flow: Failed Doc -> Rewrite Query -> External Web Search -> Use Web Results -> Generate Answer. Web fallback costs latency (300-800 ms) and requires a search API. Use when refusal is unacceptable. Trade-off versus admitting 'I don't know'.
summary: On the INCORRECT branch, CRAG rewrites the query and falls back to web search instead of using the failed internal doc.
queries: What does CRAG do when a doc is INCORRECT
Latency cost of web fallback in CRAG
When evaluator score is too low what happens
Query rewriting for web search in CRAG
Why use web search instead of refusal

text: AMBIGUOUS branch: doc partially answers. CRAG keeps it AND also fetches external web results, then merges both into the generation prompt. Flow: Partial Doc + Web Results -> Merge Into Context -> Generate Answer. The hedge bet: highest cost path, lowest miss rate. Use when refusal is unacceptable but confidence in internal docs is low.
summary: On the AMBIGUOUS branch, CRAG hedges by combining the partial internal doc with web results before generating.
queries: What does CRAG do on AMBIGUOUS verdict
CRAG combine internal docs and web
When CRAG merges internal and external sources
Why combine docs and web search

text: CRAG knowledge refinement splits each retrieved doc into 100-150 token strips and scores each strip individually. Off-topic strips are dropped; on-topic strips are packed. Doc-7 (Refunds) starts at 800 tokens; after refinement, only 3 of 6 strips are kept (~50% reduction). Strip breakdown: Strip 1 Refund Eligibility KEEP. Strip 2 Annual Plans Prorating KEEP. Strip 3 Refund Processing Time KEEP. Strip 4 Tax Handling DROP. Strip 5 Legal Disclaimers DROP. Strip 6 Chargeback Policy DROP. Final: 3 kept strips, ~50% of original tokens, all on-topic. Packed into the prompt for generation.
summary: Knowledge refinement splits each doc into 100-150 token strips, drops off-topic ones, and keeps only the on-topic strips for the prompt.
queries: How many strips are kept after CRAG refinement

text: The full CRAG decision tree: Query + Retrieved Docs feed into an Evaluator Score diamond, which branches three ways - CORRECT -> Use Docs (green), AMBIGUOUS -> Combine (yellow), INCORRECT -> Web (red). All three branches converge into a Knowledge Refinement (Strips) step, which feeds Generate Answer. One evaluator. Three branches. All converge into knowledge refinement and generation (Yan et al. 2024).
summary: Full CRAG pipeline diagram: one evaluator routes to three branches that all converge into knowledge refinement and final generation.
queries: From evaluator to generation in CRAG
Where does knowledge refinement sit in CRAG

text: Same 15-node citation graph re-colored into three communities (Civil Rights, Contract Law, IP Law) for global-query summarization. Civil Rights community contains 6 nodes (top cluster); Contract Law contains 5 nodes (middle cluster); IP Law contains 4 nodes (bottom cluster). Each community is colored differently to show the partition produced by Leiden or Louvain.
summary: Visual of the 15-node graph partitioned into three colored communities used for global-query map-reduce.
queries: Civil Rights Contract Law IP Law clusters

text: GraphRAG (Microsoft 2024) is a RAG variant that builds a knowledge graph from documents at index time and retrieves subgraphs or community summaries at query time. An LLM extracts entities and relations from each document (~$0.02 per doc), builds a graph (here: 15 legal cases as nodes, 27 citation edges), and runs community detection (Leiden or Louvain) to cluster densely-connected nodes. Each community gets an LLM-generated summary. Local queries retrieve a small subgraph (e.g., 5-node precedent chain); global queries (themes, comparisons) read the community summaries via map-reduce. Worth it for rich entity-relationship corpora (legal, biomedical, code) with frequent global queries; not worth it for self-contained FAQ docs or schema churn. Mitigations: cache extractions, run community detection nightly, hybrid GraphRAG + naive RAG.
summary: End-to-end overview of GraphRAG: offline entity/relation extraction, citation graph, subgraph retrieval, community detection, and when it pays off.
queries: how to retrieve subgraph for query

text: The customer-support corpus is mostly self-contained how-to pages with sparse relationships, so a GraphRAG graph over it would be thin and demonstrate nothing. This chapter switches to a 15-node legal-citation network because the customer-support corpus lacks rich entity-relationship structure. Customer Support Corpus - Won't Fit: 30 docs, mostly self-contained how-to pages, relationships sparse, graph would have few edges. Legal Citation Network - Fits: 15 case decisions where each cites 2-5 prior cases, rich entities (Plaintiff, Defendant, Court, Statute) and a real graph topology. The customer-support corpus returns in the next chapter; this chapter only uses the legal-citation secondary corpus.
summary: Why this chapter swaps the customer-support corpus for a 15-node legal-citation graph: GraphRAG needs rich entity-relationship structure.

text: Offline extraction: each case runs through an LLM extraction prompt once at index time. The LLM emits entities (people, organizations, statutes) and relations (sued, decided-in, applies, holding). Cost is ~$0.02 per doc. Example for 'Smith v Jones (2018)'. Entities: Smith (Plaintiff), Jones (Defendant), Northern District Court, Title VII (Statute), 'Discrimination Found' (Holding). Relations: Smith -- sued --> Jones; Smith v Jones -- decided-in --> Northern District Court; Smith v Jones -- applies --> Title VII; Smith v Jones -- holding --> Discrimination Found.
summary: How an LLM extracts entities and relations from each document at index time, with a Smith v Jones worked example.

text: The 15-Node Citation Graph: built once offline. Each node is a case; each directed edge is a citation from the citing case to the cited case. 15 cases, 27 citation edges. Built once offline; updated incrementally as new cases arrive. Nodes include Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State, Miller v EEOC, Apex v Beta, Cosmos v Stellar, Delta v Epsilon, Falcon v GoTech, Hera v Olympus, Aurora v Beacon, Cipher v Decode, Eagle v Falcon IP, Sigma v Tau.
summary: Describes the 15-node, 27-edge directed citation graph used as the running GraphRAG corpus.

nodes and edges in legal graph

text: Query Time: Retrieve A Subgraph. Query: 'What is the precedent chain for workplace discrimination cases?' The retriever finds a 5-node subgraph (Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State) and sends only those nodes + their edges to the LLM. Entity-aware retrieval; the graph topology is the signal. 5 nodes highlighted in orange. The LLM reads only the highlighted subgraph - not the whole 15-node corpus.
summary: Subgraph retrieval at query time: pick a connected component of relevant nodes and pass only those to the LLM.
queries: how many nodes per GraphRAG query
what goes into the LLM prompt GraphRAG

text: Communities: Cluster The Graph For Global Queries. Community detection (Leiden or Louvain) groups densely-connected nodes into clusters. Each community gets an LLM-generated summary at index time. Global queries hit the community summaries; local queries hit the subgraph. Three communities: Civil Rights (6 nodes) - cases established protected-class precedents under Title VII and the Equal Protection Clause. Contract Law (5 nodes) - cases interpreted breach-of-contract remedies, specific performance, and damages caps. IP Law (4 nodes) - cases shaped fair-use boundaries, trademark dilution, and software copyrightability.
summary: Community detection clusters densely-connected nodes; each cluster gets an LLM summary used for global queries.
queries: what is community detection in GraphRAG
how does GraphRAG handle global queries
how to summarize a graph community

text: Global Queries Use Communities, Local Queries Use Subgraphs. Two query patterns, two routing paths. Local queries need a specific subgraph; global queries need an overview that no single subgraph contains. Local Query example: 'How did the court rule in Smith v Jones?' Plan: Retrieve the Smith v Jones node + 1-hop neighbors (5 nodes total). Output: Direct answer from the subgraph. Global Query example: 'What are the main themes across this corpus?' Plan: Retrieve all 3 community summaries; map-reduce summarize them. Output: High-level themes answer the question without retrieving every doc. Global queries are GraphRAG's killer feature - naive RAG cannot answer them without retrieving everything; GraphRAG answers from the community summaries.
summary: Local queries route to subgraphs, global queries route to community summaries via map-reduce; global queries are GraphRAG's edge over naive RAG.
queries: local query vs global query GraphRAG
what is a global query in RAG
how does naive RAG fail on global queries
specific question vs overview question RAG
Smith v Jones local query example
why naive RAG cannot answer themes

text: When To Reach For GraphRAG. GraphRAG is expensive to build and maintain. Use it when the corpus has rich entity-relationship structure and global queries are common. Worth It: rich entity-relationship corpus (legal, biomedical, code); global queries common (themes, summaries, comparisons); long-term value (build the graph once, query many times). Not Worth It: mostly self-contained docs (FAQ, support knowledge base); only local/factoid queries; schema churn (entities change weekly). Cost: per-doc LLM extraction at index time (~$0.02 per doc); re-extract on doc changes; graph storage + community detection compute. Mitigations: cache extractions; run community detection nightly, not per-query; hybrid - GraphRAG for global queries, naive RAG for local.
summary: Decision framework: when GraphRAG pays off, when it doesn't, cost drivers, and mitigations.

text: Tool-call loop flowchart for agentic RAG. Boxes and arrows show: User Query at top -> Model -> decision diamond labeled 'Need A Tool?'. YES path on the right leads to a tool_call(...) box, whose tool_response is fed back via a curved path returning to the Model node. NO path on the left leads to Final Answer. Footer reads max_iterations = 10, hard cap prevents runaway loops.
summary: Diagram of the agentic tool-call loop: the model alternates between deciding it needs a tool, calling it, and feeding the response back until it emits a final answer or hits max_iterations.

text: This chapter introduces tool-augmented (agentic) RAG, where retrieval is one tool among many - SQL, calculator, web search, internal APIs - that an LLM picks between via function calling. The runtime intercepts tool_call tokens, executes them, and feeds tool_response back into the model in a loop that terminates on a final-answer marker or max_iterations. A worked four-turn trace shows vector_search + sql_query + calculator combined to compare Pro/Enterprise plans and price 25 seats. Loop control uses layered defenses: hard caps (max_iterations ~5-10, wall-clock budget), soft signals (repeated identical tool calls, error-rate thresholds), cost tracking, and mitigations like caching tool responses. Orchestration is picked last - DIY while loops give full control; LangGraph-style state machines trade lock-in for checkpointing, retries, and human-in-the-loop.
summary: Overview of agentic RAG: the function-calling loop, multi-tool worked example, termination defenses, and DIY-vs-framework orchestration trade-off.

text: Tool-augmented (agentic) RAG treats retrieval as one of several tools the LLM can invoke. SQL queries, calculators, web search, and internal APIs sit alongside vector search. The model picks the right tool per query. Tools available: Vector Search (semantic retrieval over embedded chunks - the RAG built so far), SQL Query (structured lookups over a relational store like pricing tables or user accounts), Calculator (precise arithmetic since models are bad at math but calculators are not), Web Search (fresh or out-of-corpus info, used as fallback or for time-sensitive queries). The LLM decides which tool to call - this is the function-calling pattern. Each tool returns text the model can read.
summary: Agentic RAG treats retrieval as one tool among many (SQL, calculator, web search) that the LLM picks between via function calling.
queries: what tools can an LLM agent use
how does an LLM pick a tool

text:
Function-calling: how the model picks a tool. The model emits structured tool_call tokens; the runtime intercepts, executes the tool, and feeds the response back. The loop continues until the model emits a final answer or hits max_iterations. Function-calling trace example:

User: Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.

Model: I need the pricing table. Calling tool.
  tool_call: sql_query(table="plans", filter="name in ('Pro','Enterprise')")
  tool_response: [{"plan":"Pro","price":29,"users":10}, {"plan":"Enterprise","price":199,"users":"unlimited"}]

Model: I also need feature comparison docs. Calling tool.
  tool_call: vector_search(query="Pro vs Enterprise features", k=3)
  tool_response: [doc-15 chunk 1, doc-15 chunk 2, doc-19 chunk 1]

Model: Now I can answer. Generating response with citations.
summary: Shows the function-calling trace where a model emits tool_call tokens, the runtime executes them, and responses are fed back until a final answer.
queries: what does a tool_call token look like
how model knows to call sql

text: The tool-call loop. The control loop runs until the model declares done or max_iterations is reached. A loopback path lets the model chain multiple tool calls. Flow: User Query -> Model -> decision diamond 'Need A Tool?'. YES branch -> tool_call(...) -> tool_response fed back -> returns to Model. NO branch -> Final Answer. Hard cap shown: max_iterations = 10 prevents runaway loops.
summary: Explains the tool-call control loop: model decides whether a tool is needed, executes it, feeds the response back, and exits with a final answer or when max_iterations hits.
queries: how does the tool call loop work

text:
Worked multi-tool trace. Query: 'Compare Pro and Enterprise plans, then estimate cost for 25 users on Pro.' Four turns, three tools, one final answer.

Turn 1: vector_search("Pro Enterprise comparison") -> 3 chunks (doc-15 + doc-19).
Turn 2: sql_query(SELECT price FROM plans WHERE name='Pro') -> $29 per seat.
Turn 3: calculator(25 x 29) -> 725.
Turn 4: Final Answer -> Pro is $29/seat. 25 users => $725/month. Pro includes features X, Y, Z [doc-15]; Enterprise adds SSO + audit logs [doc-19].
summary: Walks through a concrete four-turn agentic trace that combines vector search, SQL, and a calculator to answer a pricing-comparison question.
queries: vector search then sql then calculator

text:
Loop control: termination criteria. Termination is a layered defense. Hard caps stop runaway loops; soft signals catch loops that are technically progressing but diverging; cost tracking catches expensive flows; mitigations cut the rate.

Hard Caps: max_iterations (typically 5-10); total tool calls per query (rate-limit by tool); wall-clock budget (timeout in seconds).

Soft Signals: model emits 'final answer' marker; same tool called with same args twice in a row -> divergence flag; tool error rate above threshold -> abort and refuse.

Cost Tracking: each tool call has a fixed cost; sum across iterations; alert if over per-query budget; bill chargebacks to feature owners.

Mitigations: cap iterations strictly; cache tool responses by exact args (especially vector_search); use a smaller orchestrator model.
summary: Lists the layered defenses (hard caps, soft signals, cost tracking, mitigations) that keep agentic loops from running away or getting expensive.

text:
Orchestration: frameworks like LangGraph. Pick orchestration last. The patterns above work without any framework. Frameworks are a productivity choice, not a correctness one.

DIY (custom while loop): you implement the function-calling parse, tool dispatch, max_iterations check, response merge yourself. Full control. More code.

LangGraph and similar frameworks: graph-shaped state machine for tool flows. Nodes are tool calls, edges are transitions. Built-in checkpointing, retries, human-in-the-loop. Less code. Framework lock-in.

Pick a framework when it saves more time than it costs. Patterns above are framework-agnostic.
summary: Compares DIY while-loop orchestration to frameworks like LangGraph and advises picking a framework only when it saves more time than it costs.

text: Long-context vs RAG compares three strategies for a 200-page (~120k token) product manual: stuff the whole document into the prompt (long-context-only), chunk and retrieve top-5 (RAG-only), or retrieve top-30 and pack 50k tokens with reordering (Hybrid). Long-context-only costs $0.36/query, takes 5-8 seconds, and suffers lost-in-the-middle. RAG-only is ~$0.008/query and ~1.5s but risks missing the relevant chunk in top-5. Hybrid sits in the middle on cost ($0.15) and latency (~3s) but wins on accuracy (92% golden recall) because more candidates lower miss rate. Hybrid is the production default; RAG-only fits high-volume cost-sensitive workloads; long-context-only fits single-doc, low-volume, no-retrieval-infra cases.
summary: Chapter-level summary of the three strategies (RAG-only, Hybrid, Long-Context-Only) with their cost, latency, accuracy tradeoffs for a 200-page product manual.

text: Long-Context Only: pack ALL 120k tokens of the manual into the prompt. The LLM reads everything and answers. Flow: User Query -> Pack ALL 120k Tokens -> LLM Reads -> Answer. Cost: at $3 per 1M input tokens, this is $0.36 per query. Latency: 120k tokens to read produces 5-8 seconds first-token latency. Simple. Expensive. Slow. Subject to lost-in-the-middle for facts buried mid-document.

text: RAG Only: chunk the 200-page manual into 240 chunks of 512 tokens. At query time, embed the query, retrieve top-5 (2,560 tokens), pack into prompt. Flow: Chunk Manual Into 240 Chunks -> Retrieve Top-5 -> Pack ~2,560 Tokens -> LLM Reads -> Answer. Cost: ~$0.008 per query - about 45x cheaper than long-context-only. Latency: 50ms retrieval + 1-2s generation = ~1.5s. Risk: if the relevant chunk is not in top-5, the answer is wrong.
summary: RAG-only strategy chunks the manual into 240 chunks, retrieves top-5 (2,560 tokens): ~$0.008/query, ~1.5s, miss risk if relevant chunk excluded.

text: Hybrid: retrieve top-30 chunks (15,000 tokens), optionally rerank, then pack 50k tokens into the prompt. Reorder using lost-in-middle strategies (front-load or sandwich). Flow: Retrieve Top-30 -> Optional Rerank -> Pack 50k Tokens -> Front-Load Or Sandwich -> LLM -> Answer. Cost: ~$0.15 per query (5x cheaper than long-context-only). Latency: 80ms retrieval + 3s generation = ~3s. Accuracy: highest. More candidates means lower miss rate. The pragmatic middle - most production systems land here.
summary: Hybrid strategy retrieves top-30, reranks, packs 50k tokens with front-load/sandwich ordering: $0.15/query, ~3s, highest accuracy.

Each group has three bars: RAG-Only (green), Hybrid (yellow), Long-Context-Only (red). Values: Cost - RAG-Only $0.008, Hybrid $0.150, Long-Context-Only $0.360 (max $0.40). Latency - RAG-Only 1.5s, Hybrid 3.0s, Long-Context-Only 6.0s (max 7.0s). Accuracy - RAG-Only 78%, Hybrid 92%, Long-Context-Only 84% (max 100%). Hybrid wins accuracy; RAG-only wins cost and latency. text: Decision guide for when each strategy wins. RAG Only: cost-critical at scale (~$0.008/query), latency-critical (~1.5s p50), large multi-doc corpus, citations required (chunk-level provenance). Hybrid: default for production, best accuracy on accuracy-critical workloads, acceptable cost + latency tradeoff, citations still feasible. Long-Context Only: single long document corpus, low query volume (cost not pressing), citations not required (or post-extracted), no retrieval infrastructure available. Hybrid is the production default. Long-context-only is a niche. RAG-only is for high-volume cost-sensitive systems.
summary: Decision framework listing the workload shapes where RAG-only, Hybrid, and Long-Context-Only each are the right choice.
queries: When to choose RAG-only vs hybrid vs long-context
