text: Triangle diagram with three vertex boxes: top vertex 'Retrieval - Did We Find The Right Docs?' (cyan), lower-left vertex 'Generation - Did The Model Use Them Faithfully?' (purple), lower-right vertex 'End-To-End - Did The Final Answer Help The User?' (orange). Edges connect the three vertices forming an equilateral-style triangle on a viewBox of 540x360. Each vertex is a rounded rectangle (220x72) with the layer name and a one-line caption describing the question that layer answers.
summary: Triangle visual showing the three RAG eval layers as vertices with one-line descriptions.

text: The RAG evaluation triangle splits quality measurement into three layers: retrieval (did we find the right docs?), generation (did the model use them faithfully?), and end-to-end (did the final answer help the user?). Each layer has its own metrics: retrieval uses Recall@k, MRR, Precision@k, and nDCG@k; generation uses Faithfulness and Answer Relevancy; end-to-end uses Correctness (binary vs gold) and Helpfulness (1-5 Likert). Three layers exist so failures locate to a specific root cause - a wrong answer can come from bad retrieval, bad generation, or product/prompt mismatch. BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR are deprecated for RAG because they only measure n-gram overlap and miss faithfulness; use RAGAS and LLM-as-judge instead.
summary: Overview of the three-layer RAG evaluation framework and why word-overlap metrics fail.
queries: how do you evaluate a RAG system
what is the RAG evaluation triangle
why split RAG evaluation into layers
why not use BLEU for RAG

text: Three Layers Of RAG Evaluation. A bad final answer can come from bad retrieval OR bad generation OR a product-side mismatch. We need a metric for each layer so a failure traces to one place. The three layers form a triangle: Retrieval (did we find the right docs?), Generation (did the model use them faithfully?), and End-To-End (did the final answer help the user?).
summary: Introduces the three layers of RAG evaluation as a triangle so failures can be localized.
queries: why three layers for RAG evaluation
what is the RAG evaluation triangle
why separate retrieval and generation metrics
what does the RAG triangle measure
why do we need multiple RAG metrics

text:
Layer 1: Retrieval Metrics. Retrieval metrics measure whether the search step found the right docs. Four metrics dominate the literature:

Recall@k = relevant_retrieved / total_relevant - what fraction of all the relevant docs were retrieved.

MRR = (1/N) * sum( 1 / rank_of_first_relevant ) - mean reciprocal rank of the first relevant doc per query.

Precision@k = relevant_retrieved / k - what fraction of the top-k retrieved are actually relevant.

nDCG@k = DCG@k / IDCG@k - normalised discounted cumulative gain, rank-weighted relevance.

Example: top-3 returned for 'How do I reset my password?': doc-1 (relevant), doc-23 (not), doc-7 (relevant) -> Precision@3 = 2 / 3 = 0.667.
summary: Four retrieval metrics with formulas: Recall@k, MRR, Precision@k, nDCG@k, plus a worked Precision@3 example.
queries: what is MRR mean reciprocal rank
difference between recall and precision in retrieval

text:
Layer 2: Generation Metrics. Generation metrics measure whether the model used the retrieved context to produce a grounded answer. Faithfulness is the headline metric, paired with Answer Relevancy.

Faithfulness asks: Are the claims in the answer supported by the retrieved context? Example: Answer claims '24-hour expiry'; retrieved doc-1 says 'the link expires after 24 hours' -> claim supported -> score 1.0.

Answer Relevancy asks: Does the answer actually address the question? Example: Question 'How do I reset my password?' Answer 'Click forgot password and check your email' -> relevancy 0.9.
summary: Generation layer measures Faithfulness (claims grounded in context) and Answer Relevancy (answer addresses the question).
queries: is the answer grounded in context
did the model use retrieved docs
does the answer address the question

text:
Layer 3: End-To-End Metrics. End-to-end metrics measure whether the user actually got what they needed. Correctness compares to a gold answer; helpfulness asks the user on a 1-5 Likert scale.

Correctness (binary): Did the final answer match the gold answer? Example: Gold 'Click forgot password and check email.' Generated 'Click forgot password link and check your registered email.' -> 1.

Helpfulness (1-5 Likert): How useful was the answer to the user? Example: User rated this answer 4/5: 'Solved it but had to scroll'.
summary: End-to-end layer measures Correctness (binary vs gold answer) and Helpfulness (1-5 Likert user rating).
queries: what is correctness metric in RAG
how to measure overall RAG quality
did the answer help the user

text:
Why Three Layers? Failures Trace To A Layer. With three layers, a wrong final answer locates to a specific root cause. Walk the multi-hop query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' through three failure scenarios.

Scenario A - Retrieval Fault: Top-k returns doc-4 (refunds) for a password-reset query; doc-1 never surfaces. Recall@10 drops from 0.95 to 0.30. Root cause: retrieval. Fix in the retriever.

Scenario B - Generation Fault: Retrieval returns doc-1 + doc-7 correctly. The model ignores them and hallucinates a phone-support step. Faithfulness drops to 0.50. Root cause: generation. Fix the prompt / model / refusal logic.

Scenario C - Product Fault: Retrieval and generation both score above 0.9. Yet user reports 'this did not help'. Helpfulness drops to 2/5. Root cause: product / prompting / answer style. Fix online.
summary: Three failure scenarios show how a wrong answer traces to retrieval, generation, or product as root cause.
queries: why does my RAG answer fail
tracing RAG errors to a layer

text: Deprecated For RAG: BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR. These are word-overlap metrics: they count how many n-grams in the generated answer also appear in a reference answer. They were designed for machine translation and summarisation, where wording proximity matters. They do not measure faithfulness or groundedness. A perfectly faithful answer written in different words scores 0; a hallucinated answer that recycles reference words scores high. Use RAGAS and LLM-as-judge instead.
summary: BLEU/ROUGE/METEOR are deprecated for RAG because n-gram overlap misses faithfulness; use RAGAS or LLM-as-judge.
queries: why not use BLEU for RAG
what metrics not to use for RAG

text: Judge calibration before-and-after line chart. The x-axis is the judge score percentile (low to high); the y-axis is agreement with human labels. A green dashed horizontal line marks the 0.85 target. Before curve (red): roughly flat around 55-60% agreement across all judge-score percentiles, with a long divergence tail. After curve (green): rises from 0.78 at low percentile up to 0.92 at high percentile, crossing above the 0.85 target. Labels: 'Before: ~60%' under the red curve, 'After: ~92%' above the green curve. The after curve was produced by refining the rubric and applying position-bias and verbosity-bias mitigations, showing how calibration narrows the gap to human judgement.
summary: Diagram of the judge calibration line chart: red 'before' curve at ~60% agreement vs green 'after' curve reaching ~92%, crossing the 0.85 human-agreement target after rubric refinement and bias mitigations.

text: This chapter covers LLM-as-judge: using one LLM to evaluate another LLM's outputs. It contrasts human annotation (slow, expensive, high quality), heuristics like BLEU/ROUGE (fast, free, low quality), and LLM-as-judge (fast, medium cost, near-human when calibrated, agreement 0.85+). It walks through the judge prompt artifact (framing, 1-5 criteria, JSON output), the rubric design that anchors integer scores to behaviours for Faithfulness, three biases (position bias - first slot wins; verbosity bias - longer answers preferred even when wrong; self-preference bias - judges favor their own model family), each with mitigations (randomize/swap order, weight conciseness or length-normalise, cross-family judging). Closes with calibration: spot-check 50-100 human-labeled cases until agreement with human rises from ~60% baseline to 0.85+ target (after curve hits ~92%). Use a stronger or cross-family judge model.
summary: Overview of LLM-as-judge for RAG eval: trade-offs vs human/heuristics, judge prompt, 1-5 rubric, three biases with mitigations, and human-spot-check calibration to 0.85+ agreement.

text: Why use an LLM to judge an LLM? Human annotation is the gold standard but does not scale. Word-overlap heuristics like BLEU and ROUGE are fast but miss faithfulness. LLM-as-judge sits between the two: fast, medium cost, and near-human quality on well-rubric'd tasks. Three methods compared: Human Annotation - speed Very Slow, cost Expensive, quality High, 1-2 minutes per example, costs scale with team size. Heuristic (BLEU / ROUGE) - speed Instant, cost Free, quality Low, n-gram overlap that misses faithfulness and groundedness entirely. LLM-As-Judge - speed Fast, cost Medium, quality Near-Human, seconds per example; agreement with human reaches 0.85+ when calibrated.
summary: Compares three eval methods - human annotation, BLEU/ROUGE heuristics, and LLM-as-judge - on speed, cost, and quality to justify LLM-as-judge as the practical middle path.

text: The judge prompt is a text artifact, not a code block. It frames the LLM as an evaluator, names three criteria with a 1-5 scale, and asks for a JSON response. Variable placeholders are filled in at eval time. Prompt text: 'You are an expert evaluator. You will be given a question, the retrieved context, and the candidate answer. Score the answer on three criteria, each 1 (terrible) to 5 (excellent), with one short justification per criterion. Faithfulness: are all claims in the answer supported by the retrieved context? Answer Relevancy: does the answer address the question directly? Helpfulness: would this answer actually help the user reach their goal? Question: {question} Retrieved Context: {retrieved_context} Candidate Answer: {generated_answer} Return JSON: { "faithfulness": <1-5>, "answer_relevancy": <1-5>, "helpfulness": <1-5>, "justification": "<one short paragraph>" }'
summary: Shows the actual judge prompt artifact - role framing, three criteria (faithfulness, answer relevancy, helpfulness) on a 1-5 scale, variable placeholders, and required JSON output schema.
queries: What does a judge prompt look like?
How to write a judge prompt?
What criteria does the judge score?
What scale does the judge use?
Why ask the judge for JSON output?
How to frame an LLM as an evaluator
How does the judge see question context and answer

text: Rubric design: 1-5 per criterion. The rubric is the actual calibration tool. Prose framing in the prompt sets tone; the rubric anchors every integer score to a concrete behaviour. Faithfulness rubric: Score 5 - All Claims Supported, None Missing: every factual statement in the answer is verifiable in the retrieved context. Score 4 - Mostly Supported, Minor Unsupported Detail: core claims grounded; one peripheral detail not in context. Score 3 - About Half Supported: a roughly even mix of supported and ungrounded statements. Score 2 - Mostly Unsupported; Some Grounded: most claims are made up; one or two are anchored in the context. Score 1 - Hallucinated; Nothing In Context Supports It: answer is a confident hallucination with no grounding at all. Rubric design beats prompt prose. The rubric is what makes scores reproducible.
summary: Defines a 5-point Faithfulness rubric where each integer score is anchored to a concrete behaviour, showing why the rubric (not the prose) is what produces reproducible judge scores.
queries: How to design a judge rubric?
What does a 1-5 faithfulness rubric look like?
How to anchor integer scores to behaviours
Why is the rubric more important than the prompt prose?
What does a score of 5 mean in faithfulness?
What does a score of 1 mean in faithfulness?
How to make judge scores reproducible
How to define each score level

text: Bias 1: Position Bias. In pairwise judging, the order of the two candidates affects the verdict even when their content is identical. The first slot wins more often. Order 1 (A, B) - judge picks position 1 (A): same two answers, same judge model, the first slot wins. Order 2 (B, A) - judge picks position 1 (B): swap the order, the judge's pick swaps too. Identical content. Mitigation: Randomize Or Swap. Run both orders for every pair and average; or randomize position per query before passing to the judge.
summary: Explains position bias in pairwise LLM judging - the first slot wins regardless of content - and mitigates by swapping/averaging both orderings or randomizing position.
queries: What is position bias in LLM-as-judge?
Why does the first answer win in pairwise judging?
Does order matter in LLM judging?
Swap order trick for LLM judge
Why swap A and B in judge prompt

text: Bias 2: Verbosity Bias. Judges (especially weaker models) prefer longer answers, mistaking length for thoroughness. The longer answer can be wrong and still score higher than a tight correct one. Answer A - 50 Words, Accurate: 'Go to Settings, click Forgot Password, check your email, use the link within 24 hours.' All claims supported. No padding. Answer B - 200 Words, One Hallucination: 'To begin the secure password recovery process, navigate to your account dashboard... You may also call our 24/7 support line.' (Note: phone support claim is unsupported.) Longer feels thorough. Judges (especially weaker ones) score it higher. Mitigation: Weight Conciseness Or Length-Normalise. Add an explicit conciseness criterion to the rubric, or normalise by answer length before averaging scores.
summary: Demonstrates verbosity bias - judges prefer longer answers even when they hallucinate - using a 50-word accurate vs 200-word hallucinated example, and mitigates by adding conciseness criteria or length normalisation.
queries: What is verbosity bias in LLM judges?
Why do LLM judges prefer longer answers?
Do longer answers win in LLM eval?

text: Bias 3: Self-Preference Bias. Judges score outputs from their own model family higher than human-aligned scores would. The bigger the model, the bigger this effect typically gets. Examples: Judge = GPT-4o, Candidate = GPT-4o -> +12% vs Human (same family). Judge = GPT-4o, Candidate = Claude 3.5 -> -3% vs Human (different family). Judge = Claude 3.5, Candidate = Claude 3.5 -> +9% vs Human (same family). Mitigation: Third-Party / Cross-Family Judging. Pick a judge from a different model family than the candidates. Or average across multiple judge families to wash out single-family bias.
summary: Shows self-preference bias - judges score their own model family +9 to +12% above human-aligned scores - and mitigates with cross-family judging or averaging across families.
queries: What is self-preference bias in LLM judges?
Do LLM judges prefer their own model family?
How much does self-preference bias inflate scores?
Bias when judge and candidate are same model
Why not use the same model to judge itself

text: Calibrate: Spot-Check 50-100 Cases. A judge is a hypothesis. Before trusting its scores in production, verify agreement against 50-100 human-labeled cases. Refine the rubric and mitigations until correlation lands above 0.85. Calibration chart: x-axis is Judge Score Percentile (Low To High); y-axis is Agreement With Human. A horizontal target line is drawn at 0.85. Before curve (red) sits flat around 0.55-0.60 across all percentiles - labeled 'Before: ~60%'. After curve (green) rises from 0.78 at low percentile to 0.92 at high percentile - labeled 'After: ~92%'. The after curve crosses above the 0.85 target line. Judge Model Selection: use a stronger judge model than the candidate (e.g., judge with GPT-4 a candidate from a 7B model). Or use the same model with documented caveats. Cross-family judging is the strongest setup.
summary: Calibrate the judge against 50-100 human-labeled cases until agreement crosses the 0.85 target - the before-curve sits at ~60% agreement, the after-curve (rubric refined plus bias mitigations) rises to ~92%.

text: RAGAS (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment) is a 2023 reference-free metric suite for RAG that uses an LLM-judge to compute four core scores: Faithfulness and Answer Relevancy for the generation side, Context Precision and Context Recall for the retrieval side. Each metric maps to a plain-English question - are the claims supported, does it answer the question, are top results relevant, did we retrieve everything. A worked password-reset example gives Faithfulness 0.8, Answer Relevancy 0.913, Context Precision 0.667, Context Recall 1.0, producing a per-query report card averaging ~0.845. BLEU and ROUGE are explicitly NOT RAGAS - they measure n-gram overlap against a reference, not faithfulness.
summary: Chapter-level overview of RAGAS, its four reference-free metrics, worked-example scores, and why BLEU/ROUGE are inadequate.
queries: LLM as judge for RAG eval
faithfulness answer relevancy context precision recall
why not use BLEU for RAG

text: RAGAS (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment) is a 2023 metric suite that uses an LLM under the hood to compute eval scores without needing a reference answer. It has four core metrics, two for retrieval and two for generation. Faithfulness asks: are the claims supported? (purple). Answer Relevancy asks: does it answer the question? (orange). Context Precision asks: are top results actually relevant? (green). Context Recall asks: did we retrieve everything? (red).
summary: Introduces RAGAS as a reference-free LLM-based eval suite with four metrics split across retrieval and generation.
queries: does RAGAS need ground truth answers

text: Faithfulness asks whether every claim in the generated answer can be verified in the retrieved context. The LLM-judge extracts atomic claims and labels each as supported or not. Formula: Faithfulness = | verifiable_claims | / | total_claims_in_answer |. Worked example - Password Reset Query. Retrieved context (doc-1): 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security. Click Forgot Password. An email is sent to your registered address. The link expires after 24 hours.' Generated answer: 'Go to Settings > Security, click Forgot Password, check your email, and use the link within 24 hours. You can also call support.' Claims: (1) Go to Settings > Security - Supported. (2) Click Forgot Password - Supported. (3) Check your email - Supported. (4) Use the link within 24 hours - Supported. (5) You can also call support - Not Supported. Faithfulness = 4 / 5 = 0.8.
summary: Defines Faithfulness, its formula, and walks through a 5-claim password-reset example yielding 0.8.

text: Answer Relevancy is a round-trip check: the LLM-judge reads the generated answer and writes n new questions that the answer would best answer. Each generated question is embedded and compared with cosine similarity to the original query. The average is the relevancy score. Formula: Answer Relevancy = (1/n) * sum( cosine( embed(q), embed(q_i_generated) ) ). Worked example with n = 3. Original query: 'How do I reset my password?' Q1: 'What are the steps to reset my password?' cos 0.94. Q2: 'How can I change my password if I forgot it?' cos 0.91. Q3: 'How do I update my account password?' cos 0.89. Answer Relevancy = (0.94 + 0.91 + 0.89) / 3 = 0.913.
summary: Explains Answer Relevancy as a round-trip cosine check between original query and LLM-regenerated questions, scoring 0.913 in the example.
queries: cosine similarity original query generated query
how to measure if answer addresses question
did the answer drift off topic metric

text: Context Precision asks what fraction of the top-k retrieved chunks were actually relevant. RAGAS adds a rank-aware weighting in the full version, but the simplified ratio is taught here. Formula: Context Precision = relevant_chunks_at_top_k / k. Worked example - Top-3 for password reset query. Rank 1: doc-1 chunk-1 - Password Reset Intro - Relevant. Rank 2: doc-23 chunk-2 - 500 Error Troubleshooting - Not Relevant. Rank 3: doc-1 chunk-2 - Password Reset Email Step - Relevant. Context Precision = 2 / 3 = 0.667. Naming note: DeepEval / TruLens call this ContextualRelevancyScore - same idea under a different framework name. Older RAGAS shipped a separate 'Context Relevancy' metric; it was deprecated in favor of Context Precision plus Context Recall.
summary: Defines Context Precision as relevant-at-k over k, with a top-3 example yielding 0.667 and a naming note about DeepEval/TruLens equivalents.

text: Context Recall asks what fraction of all the relevant chunks in the corpus were retrieved. It is the dual of precision: precision says 'are top results clean?', recall says 'did we miss anything?'. Formula: Context Recall = relevant_chunks_retrieved / all_relevant_chunks_in_corpus. Worked example - the corpus has 2 chunks relevant to 'How do I reset my password?': doc-1 chunk-1 and doc-1 chunk-2. Both are retrieved, so Context Recall = 2 / 2 = 1.0. Counter-example: if retrieval had returned only doc-1 chunk-1 and missed doc-1 chunk-2, then Context Recall = 1 / 2 = 0.5.
summary: Defines Context Recall as the dual of precision, with a 1.0 example and a 0.5 counter-example when one relevant chunk is missed.
queries: all relevant chunks in corpus metric

text: Putting it together: a per-query report card. One query, four scores. The retrieval pair (precision + recall) covers the search step; the generation pair (faithfulness + answer relevancy) covers the model. Scores from the password-reset example: Faithfulness 0.80, Answer Relevancy 0.91, Context Precision 0.67, Context Recall 1.00. Average = 0.845 - the headline score per query. How RAGAS computes the scores: an LLM-judge extracts atomic claims for faithfulness, generates round-trip questions for relevancy, and labels each retrieved chunk as relevant or not for precision and recall.
summary: Aggregates the four RAGAS metrics into a per-query report card with bar chart and a 0.845 headline average.
queries: how RAGAS LLM judge computes scores
atomic claim plus round trip plus labeling
search step vs model step metrics

text: Not RAGAS: BLEU and ROUGE. BLEU and ROUGE compute n-gram overlap between the generated answer and a reference answer. They were designed for machine translation and summarisation, where wording proximity matters. They do not measure faithfulness or groundedness. A perfectly faithful answer in different wording scores low; a hallucinated answer that recycles reference words scores high. Use RAGAS or LLM-as-judge instead.
summary: Explains why BLEU and ROUGE n-gram metrics are unsuitable for RAG and recommends RAGAS or LLM-as-judge.
queries: why not use BLEU for RAG

The upper lane shows the LLM generating question-answer pairs grounded in a corpus document; the lower lane shows a human reviewer accepting, editing, or rejecting each pair. Illustrates how a small hand-built seed scales into a 500-2000 reviewed set without losing human oversight. LLM lane segments: Corpus Doc -> LLM Generates Q-A Pair -> Pair Candidate. Bridge arrow down to Human lane segments: Pair Candidate -> Reviewer Accept/Edit/Reject -> Golden Record.

text: This chapter Golden Datasets explains how to build and maintain the human-verified ground-truth set every other RAG eval metric depends on. Covers the structure of a golden record (question, expected answer, expected docs, refusal flag), starting with a 30-100 hand-written seed across query types, the five edge cases that must be covered (multi-hop, empty-context, ambiguous, refusal-required, time-sensitive), the regression set that captures every production bug as a permanent test, LLM-bootstrapped generation with mandatory human review to scale to 500-2000 records, and a monthly review cadence with coverage, freshness, and pass-rate health metrics.
summary: Overview of building, scaling, and maintaining golden datasets for RAG evaluation.

text: Golden Datasets are the ground truth every other eval metric depends on. RAGAS, LLM-as-judge, and recall@k all need human-verified labels. The golden dataset is the held-out, human-verified set of records run against every eval. Without it you have no signal; with a flaky one you have noise. One golden record has four fields: Question ('How do I reset my password?'), Expected Answer ('Click Forgot Password and check your registered email.'), Expected Docs ([doc-1]), Refusal (No). Multiply by 30-2000 records and you have a defensible eval set.
summary: A golden record is a tuple of question, expected answer, expected docs, and refusal flag - the ground truth for every other RAG metric.

text: Start with 30-100 hand-written examples. The goal of the first batch is breadth across query types, not volume. Three curation steps: (1) List query types from logs or product spec - mine a week of production queries (or the product spec) for the categories you must support. (2) Write 5-10 examples per type by hand - cover normal cases plus edge cases. (3) Write expected answer + expected docs per example - each tuple becomes one golden record. Week 1 tally by query type: Single-Doc Lookups 20, Multi-Hop 15, Aggregation 10, Refusal-Required 10, Empty-Context 10. Total Week 1 = 65.
summary: Curate the first 30-100 records by hand across query types from logs, aiming for breadth not volume.

text: Five edge cases that break naive RAG and must be covered in the first hand-written batch: (1) Multi-Hop - answer requires combining 2+ docs - example: 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' (2) Empty-Context - no doc in the corpus answers this, must refuse - example: 'What is your stock price?' (3) Ambiguous - two docs conflict, newest-wins or refusal - example: 'What is the refund window?' (doc-A: 14 days; doc-B: 30 days). (4) Refusal-Required - out-of-scope or unsafe query - example: 'Give me another customer's email.' (5) Time-Sensitive - answer depends on today's date - example: 'Is feature X live yet?' Skip any and you ship blind to a class of failures.
summary: Five edge cases - multi-hop, empty-context, ambiguous, refusal-required, time-sensitive - must be covered or you ship blind.
queries: five edge cases for golden dataset
what test cases must I cover

text: Regression Set: every production bug becomes a golden case. Anything that failed in production once must never silently fail again. The regression set is the self-healing memory of your eval suite. Four-step workflow: (1) Production user reports a bad answer. (2) Engineer reproduces; captures query, docs, answer, expected answer. (3) Add tuple to the regression-golden set. (4) Every future eval includes it; failure cannot silently re-emerge. Example regression tuples: 'Reset password forgot email' -> multi-hop missed, bot suggested support phone; 'Cancel and refund' -> refused incorrectly, user blocked from valid request; 'Pro plan SSO' -> hallucinated SSO inclusion in Pro tier.
summary: Every production failure is captured into the regression set so the same bug cannot ship twice.
queries: what is a regression set in RAG eval
how to handle production bugs in RAG
workflow for adding failures to golden set
capture production bug to test case
how to track production RAG failures

text: LLM-Bootstrapped pipeline: generate then human-review. To scale past the hand-written seed, generate question-answer pairs with an LLM grounded in corpus docs, then human-review each pair. Acceptance rate is 60-80% for clear docs. A 30-100 seed grows to 500-2000 reviewed records this way. Two-lane pipeline diagram: LLM Lane (Generate) flows Corpus Doc -> LLM Generates Q-A Pair -> Pair Candidate. Bridge arrow down. Human Lane (Review) flows Pair Candidate -> Reviewer Accept/Edit/Reject -> Golden Record. Caution: never deploy without human review - the LLM can encode its own bias, generating good test cases for itself while failing on adjacent phrasings. Acceptance rate matters; so does reviewer diversity.
summary: Two-lane pipeline where an LLM generates candidate Q-A pairs grounded in corpus docs and a human reviewer accepts, edits, or rejects each, scaling 30-100 to 500-2000 records.
queries: synthetic eval data with human review

text: Monthly review cadence: archive obsolete, add new. Like code, golden sets rot. Monthly review keeps the set defensible: spot-check 20 cases, archive anything tied to deleted docs or changed product behavior, add new cases captured from the past month's production incidents. Each month: spot-check 20 random cases, archive obsolete to /archived/ (audit-kept), add new from production. Quarterly audit. Re-run regression to confirm green. Three health metrics track set quality: Coverage = query types covered / total query types; Freshness = median age of golden cases in months; Pass-Rate = % of golden set currently green on production pipeline.
summary: Monthly cadence spot-checks 20 cases, archives obsolete, adds new; coverage, freshness, and pass-rate measure set health.

text: Shadow-eval pipeline diagram. A user query splits into an old pipeline whose answer is served and a new pipeline whose answer is logged but not served. Both answers feed into an LLM-judge that scores them, producing an aggregate comparison without exposing users to the candidate pipeline.
summary: Diagram of the shadow-eval architecture: one query, two pipelines (served vs logged), one LLM-judge comparison.

The cycle returns to online detection, illustrating the offline-online feedback loop that production-grade RAG eval relies on.

text: Online eval and A/B testing complement offline eval to catch the production regressions that the golden set cannot anticipate. Implicit signals (dwell time, copy-paste, follow-up rephrase, thumbs) become reliable when 3+ are combined; explicit feedback (ratings, free-text) gives highest per-sample signal but lowest volume and demands PII redaction under GDPR/CCPA. Shadow eval runs a candidate pipeline alongside production with no user risk, using an LLM-judge to compare. Once shadow looks good, A/B testing splits users 50/50, scores ~200 queries/day/bucket with the judge rubric, and waits 7-14 days for p<0.05 with guardrails on faithfulness and refusal-rate for auto-rollback. The loop closes: online detects, offline reproduces and fixes via RAGAS plus judge, shadow plus A/B verify, and every regression becomes a permanent golden-set entry.
summary: Chapter-level overview of online evaluation, implicit and explicit signals, shadow eval, A/B testing, and the offline-online feedback loop.
queries: overview of online evaluation for RAG
summary of shadow eval and A/B testing
complete online eval system for RAG
how to evaluate RAG in production end to end
implicit explicit shadow A/B feedback loop

text: Offline eval gates merges before deploy; online eval detects regressions that production users actually hit but the golden set cannot anticipate. You need both. Offline eval is controlled and reproducible across runs, allows fast iteration on the same golden set every time, but does not reflect the production query distribution and misses rare-but-bad cases users hit. Online eval uses the real query distribution from real users and catches edge cases offline never sees, but the signal is noisy and needs aggregation over many sessions, and storing user feedback raises privacy concerns. Use both: offline gates merges before deploy, online catches regressions after deploy and feeds them back into the golden regression set.
summary: Compares offline and online evaluation, showing why production RAG systems need both - offline for reproducible merge-gating, online to catch real user regressions.
queries: why do I need online eval if I already have offline eval

text: Implicit signals are behavioral traces from session telemetry. Each is individually noisy; combining three or more makes a reliable quality regression detector. Thumbs up/down is explicit-light - most users do not click. Dwell time: long dwell means the user read carefully; very short dwell likely means irrelevant. Copy-paste rate: the user copied the answer, meaning useful enough to reuse. Follow-up rephrase: user re-asks the same intent with different words, meaning the previous answer failed. Combine 3+ signals and calibrate against explicit feedback. Any single signal is too noisy alone; a composite quality score over 3+ implicit signals tracks user satisfaction reliably.
summary: Lists four implicit user behavior signals (thumbs, dwell, copy-paste, rephrase) and explains why combining 3+ signals beats any one alone.
queries: what are implicit signals in RAG evaluation
how do users tell you the answer was bad without rating it
thumbs up thumbs down capture rate

text: Explicit feedback (a rating widget or a free-text 'what went wrong?' prompt) is the highest signal per sample and the lowest volume. Use it for diagnosis, not for primary quality measurement alone. Rating Widget: post-answer thumbs, star rating, or Did-This-Help Yes/No - capture rate is 2-8% of sessions. Free-Text Feedback: 'Tell us what went wrong' - higher signal, much lower volume; single best source of qualitative failure modes. Privacy: store feedback with conversation context, but redact PII (names, emails, account numbers) before retention. Follow regional privacy law (GDPR, CCPA). Document the retention period.
summary: Covers explicit feedback widgets (ratings and free-text) with capture rates and privacy/PII redaction requirements under GDPR and CCPA.

text: Shadow eval runs a new candidate pipeline alongside the production one without serving its answers. The judge compares both on real production traffic, giving an apples-to-apples signal at zero user risk. A user query splits into the old pipeline (served to user) and the new pipeline (logged, not served); both feed into an LLM-judge rubric that compares them. Example shadow-eval week 1 result: judge prefers new in 62% of cases, ties in 23%, regresses in 15%. Production traffic provides the comparison; users never see the candidate answer. This is the signal that graduates the new pipeline to A/B testing.
summary: Explains shadow evaluation - running a candidate pipeline in parallel with production for judge-based comparison without any user risk.
queries: what is shadow evaluation for RAG
judge compares old and new pipeline on production traffic
when to graduate shadow to A/B

text: Once shadow looks good, ship A/B. Split production users 50/50; sample answers from each bucket; score with the same judge rubric. Mean rubric score per bucket plus a significance test decides which wins. Steps: (1) Bucket users 50/50 (control/treatment). (2) Sample 200 queries per day per bucket. (3) Run LLM-judge rubric on every sampled (query, answer). (4) Compute mean rubric score per bucket and check significance. Statistical significance: at N=200/day per bucket and effect size 0.1 on a 1-5 scale, expect 7-14 days to reach p < 0.05. Do not peek; pre-register the analysis window. Guardrails and auto-rollback: monitor faithfulness drop and refusal-rate spike at high frequency. Auto-rollback when any guardrail breaches threshold; do not wait for the analysis window.
summary: Walks through 4-step A/B testing with judge rubric scoring, statistical significance timing, and guardrail-based auto-rollback.
queries: how to A/B test a RAG pipeline

do not peek pre register analysis window

text: Production-grade RAG eval is a feedback cycle. Online detects regressions; offline reproduces and fixes them; shadow plus A/B verify the fix; the cycle loops. The offline tools (RAGAS, judge, golden) and online tools (implicit, explicit, shadow, A/B) are two halves of one system. Cycle nodes: (1) Online finds a regression - thumbs-down spike on a query type. (2) Capture to golden regression set - tie the failing case into the regression-set chapter. (3) Offline reproduces and iterates fix - RAGAS plus judge re-score the offending case until green. (4) Shadow plus A/B verify the fix in production - promote only after both confirm no regression. Offline gives reproducibility; online gives reality. The loop turns every production regression into a future regression-set entry, so the same bug cannot ship twice.
summary: Frames offline and online eval as a feedback loop: online detects, offline fixes, shadow plus A/B verify, and every regression becomes a permanent golden-set entry.

text: Semantic cache decision flow diagram. Incoming query 'Reset my password?' -> embed query (1024-dim vector) -> cosine search in cache store comparing vs all cached queries -> diamond decision 'Score >= 0.93?' threshold -> YES branch returns cached answer (cost $0, no LLM call) -> NO branch runs full RAG and stores answer (cost full stack).

text: Horizontal bar chart comparing per-query cost across four cache configurations: no cache $0.0173 (0% saved); prompt cache only at 80% hit rate $0.0044 (74% saved); semantic cache only at 30% hit rate $0.0121 (30% saved); both stacked $0.003 (83% saved). Stacked configuration is the production target and delivers the lowest cost.
summary: Bar chart of per-query cost across four cache configurations showing stacked prompt+semantic cache delivers the lowest cost at $0.003 (83% saved).

text: Caching in RAG: prompt cache stores the static system+retrieved-doc prefix at a 90% discount with byte-exact match (works for stable prefixes, fails when docs change per query). Semantic cache stores (query, answer) pairs and serves hits when cosine similarity to a cached query is at or above a threshold like 0.93. Operations: LRU+TTL eviction, doc-ID tagged invalidation, high thresholds and held-out monitoring to control false hits. Stacked together: 83% per-query cost savings vs no cache. Production RAG systems that skip caching leave 50%+ of cost on the table.
summary: Prompt cache discounts a stable prefix and semantic cache reuses similar prior answers; stacked they cut RAG per-query cost by ~83%.
queries: summary of prompt and semantic cache

text: Why cache in RAG. Every production RAG query burns four cost lines: embedding ($0.0001), retrieval ($0.0002), LLM input for 4k tokens ($0.012), LLM output for 500 tokens ($0.005). Total per query = $0.0173. At 1k QPS over a day this stacks to ~$1,500/day. Caching cuts the bill 50-95% on repeated traffic.
summary: Production RAG queries cost ~$0.0173 each across four cost lines and scale to $1,500/day at 1k QPS, motivating caching.

do I need caching in RAG

text: Prompt cache: cache the static prefix. Provider-side prompt caching (Anthropic-style) keeps the system prompt and retrieved-context prefix on the inference server. Subsequent calls reuse it at a 90% discount as long as the prefix bytes match exactly. TTL ~5 minutes. The artifact has two regions: cached prefix (system prompt + retrieved docs) at 90% discount, and fresh suffix (user question + assistant) at full price. Cost comparison: full price no cache, 4000 input tokens at $3/1M = $0.012; with prompt cache, 3800 cached at $0.30/1M + 200 fresh at $3/1M = $0.00174 = 85% saved.
summary: Prompt cache stores the static system+retrieved-doc prefix on the inference server at a 90% discount with byte-exact match, saving ~85% on input tokens.
queries: how long does prompt cache last

text: When prompt cache applies. Prompt cache only wins when many requests share a long prefix. Cache HITS: support agent looping on a stable doc set; multi-turn conversation with same retrieved docs; RAG pipeline answering many queries with a stable system prompt. Cache MISSES: docs change per query (no shared prefix); query-time chunking (different chunks every time); cold-start every conversation.
summary: Prompt cache hits when many requests share a long stable prefix and misses when retrieved context or chunks change per query.
queries: does prompt cache work for changing docs
why is my prompt cache not hitting
when not to use prompt cache

text: Semantic cache: cache by query similarity. A semantic cache stores past (query, answer) pairs and serves a hit when an incoming query is similar enough to one already cached. Similarity is cosine on the query embedding; threshold is the safety knob (e.g. 0.93). Flow: incoming query -> embed query to 1024-dim vector -> cosine search in cache store vs all cached queries -> if score >= 0.93 return cached answer ($0 cost, no LLM call) -> else run full RAG, store answer (full cost). Example cosine scores vs cached query 'How do I reset my password?': 'How can I reset my password?' score 0.97 HIT; 'Why is my dashboard slow?' score 0.34 MISS; 'How do I change my password?' score 0.88 BORDERLINE.
summary: Semantic cache embeds incoming queries and serves cached answers when cosine similarity to a cached query crosses the threshold, skipping the LLM call.

text: Semantic cache operations: eviction, invalidation, false-hit risk. Eviction: LRU + TTL. Cache fills up; evict oldest unused entries; TTL forces refresh of any answer older than e.g. 24 hours. Invalidation: when the corpus updates (a doc changes), every cached answer that touched that doc is stale. Strategy: tag cache entries with doc-IDs and invalidate on doc update. False-hit risk: two queries with cosine 0.95 can still want different answers. Example: 'Cancel my subscription' vs 'Cancel my trial' - similar embedding, very different policy. Tune threshold high (0.95+) for safety; monitor false-hit rate on a held-out set.
summary: Semantic cache needs LRU+TTL eviction, doc-ID tagged invalidation on corpus updates, and high thresholds plus held-out monitoring to limit false hits.
queries: cancel subscription vs cancel trial cache
tag cache entries with doc id

text: Combined impact: prompt cache + semantic cache compound. Per-query cost for the same RAG pipeline under four cache configurations: no cache $0.0173 (0% saved); prompt cache only at 80% hit rate $0.0044 (74% saved); semantic cache only at 30% hit rate $0.0121 (30% saved); both stacked $0.003 (83% saved). Stacked $0.003/query = 83% savings, compounds prompt cache with semantic cache. Apply both. Tune thresholds. Monitor false-hit rate. Most production RAG systems leave 50%+ of cost on the table by skipping caching.
summary: Stacking prompt cache (80% hit) with semantic cache (30% hit) drops per-query cost from $0.0173 to $0.003 - 83% savings.

text: A production RAG query bill is the sum of five cost lines, walked in order of typical magnitude. LLM output tokens: ~$15 per 1M tokens, about $0.0075 per query (500 output tokens, Claude Sonnet typical). LLM input tokens: ~$3 per 1M tokens, about $0.012 per query (4000 input tokens for system + retrieved + question). Reranker: ~$0.001 per query (cross-encoder, optional). Vector search: ~$0.0002 per query (operator cost, managed service). Embedding: ~$0.0001 per query (1024-dim, modern provider). Total per query = $0.0208. The biggest cost driver - LLM tokens - dominates and is visible first.

text: Five real levers to cut RAG cost, in order of compounding return. Smaller embedding: 1024 -> 384 dim with Matryoshka, -60% storage, no quality drop, small impact (embedding <1% of bill). Fewer chunks retrieved: top-20 -> top-5 to the LLM, -75% input tokens, ~44% of total bill. Smaller LLM: Sonnet -> Haiku, ~10x cheaper per token, ~80% quality on most RAG tasks, ~80% of LLM bill. Prompt cache: 80% prefix hit rate, prefix tokens at 90% discount, ~74% on cached prefix tokens. Semantic cache: 30% hit rate on hot queries, skip LLM entirely on hits, ~30% on affected queries. Apply in order: cache first (free quality), reduce chunks (free quality), then downsize LLM (quality tradeoff).
summary: Five levers cut RAG cost: smaller embedding, fewer chunks, smaller LLM, prompt cache, semantic cache - apply caches first since they are free quality.

text: Worked example stacking all five levers in sequence as a downward waterfall. Start - full price $0.0208. Apply prompt cache (80% hit): $0.0061, -71% on input tokens. Apply semantic cache (30% hit): $0.0043, -30% blended. Reduce top-k from 20 to 5: $0.0028, -35% remaining input. Switch LLM to Haiku: $0.0012, -57% on LLM bill. Final per-query cost $0.0012 = 94% reduction. At 1k QPS this saves about $1.69M per day versus no levers applied.
summary: Stacking five cost levers in sequence drops per-query cost from $0.0208 to $0.0012 (94% reduction, $1.69M/day saved at 1k QPS).

text: This chapter teaches why and how to instrument production RAG with traces. Without per-stage instrumentation, four failure modes are undiagnosable: mystery slowness, silent quality drops, cost blowups, and hallucination hunts. A canonical RAG trace has one root span and five children (embed query, vector search, rerank, pack prompt, generate), each carrying attributes like model versions, doc-IDs, scores, token counts, and per-stage latency. Tool options include LangSmith, Helicone, OpenTelemetry, Arize Phoenix, and native provider tooling. Privacy demands hashing raw query text and logging doc-IDs not chunk text to satisfy GDPR/HIPAA/SOC2. A production dashboard surfaces P50/P99 latency, faithfulness, cost-per-query, the slowest trace, and signal-level facts like model version.
summary: Production RAG observability: span trees per query, per-stage attributes, tool landscape, privacy rules, and dashboard design.

text: Why trace RAG in production: a RAG system is opaque without traces. Four failure modes recur and each needs per-stage instrumentation to diagnose. (1) Mystery Slowness: user reports the system is slow; without per-stage latency you cannot tell whether retrieval or the LLM is the bottleneck. (2) Silent Quality Drop: faithfulness drops 5% week-over-week; no doc-IDs logged means you cannot see which docs were retrieved. (3) Cost Blowup: bill doubles; no per-query token count means you cannot tell which queries pulled in too much context. (4) Hallucination Hunt: user flags a bad answer; no model-version logged so you cannot reproduce the failure.
summary: Four production failure modes that prove RAG must be traced per stage: mystery slowness, silent quality drop, cost blowup, hallucination hunt.

text: The canonical RAG trace span tree: a single user query produces one root span (Query, total 180ms) and five child spans rendered with widths proportional to latency. Embed Query 12ms (model = bge-large-v1.5, dim = 1024). Vector Search 28ms (top-K = 20, index = hnsw, metric = cosine). Rerank 45ms (model = bge-reranker-v2, input = 20, output = 5). Pack Prompt 3ms (chunks packed = 5, total tokens = 4128). Generate 92ms (model = Sonnet 4.7, out tokens = 480, finish = stop). Rerank dominates wall time after Generate; optimization targets reducing input top-k or picking a faster reranker.
summary: Canonical RAG span tree with five child spans whose widths are proportional to per-stage latency, exposing the bottleneck visually.

text: Per-stage attributes to log on every RAG trace. Embed: query_text (hashed), embedding_model_version, dim, latency_ms. Vector Search: top_k_requested, index_name, distance_metric, retrieved_doc_ids, retrieval_scores, latency_ms. Rerank: reranker_model_version, input_count, output_count, reranker_scores, latency_ms. Pack: chunk_count_packed, total_prompt_tokens, citation_count, latency_ms. Generate: llm_model_version, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, finish_reason, latency_ms. Variable identifiers stay lowercase per code convention; column headers stay title-case. This is the minimum every production RAG trace MUST emit.
summary: Minimum attribute set every span must carry across embed, vector search, rerank, pack, and generate stages.

text: Tools that capture RAG traces in 2025. LangSmith: LangChain's official tracer; strong for LangChain users; tight integration with LangChain stack. Helicone: provider-agnostic; logs every LLM call with cost / latency dashboards; lightweight setup. OpenTelemetry: open standard; vendor-neutral spans; export to Datadog / Grafana / Jaeger / custom. Arize Phoenix: open-source; built for LLM eval + trace; strong dashboard for retrieval quality. Native Provider Tooling: Anthropic Console, OpenAI Dashboard; limited but free; first-party cost tracking. Choices span framework-native to vendor-neutral.
summary: Five tool options for capturing RAG traces, from framework-native LangSmith to vendor-neutral OpenTelemetry.
queries: framework native vs vendor neutral tracing

text: Privacy: what NOT to log in plain text. Regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2) and contracts may forbid storing user query content. Use one-way hashes for queries; log doc-IDs not chunk text; redact PII upstream. Log freely: doc-IDs, retrieval scores, latency per stage, model version, token counts, embedding-model version, query length, anonymized user ID, response status code. Do NOT log in plain text: raw query text (hash it), raw retrieved chunks (log only IDs), PII inside the query, user account IDs (use opaque session IDs), API keys, prompt template contents that include secrets.
summary: Privacy rules for RAG telemetry: hash queries, log doc-IDs not chunks, redact PII, and never log secrets or account IDs in plain text.
queries: what not to log in RAG

text: A production RAG trace dashboard surfaces four headline tiles plus the most recent slow trace and three signal-level facts. Tiles: P50 Latency 180ms, P99 Latency 620ms, Faithfulness 0.91, Cost Per Query $0.012. Slowest trace last hour: 620ms with the span tree thumbnail showing each stage's contribution. Footer facts: Slowest Stage = Rerank (45ms P50), Top Retrieved Doc = Doc-7 (Password Reset), Model Version = Sonnet 4.7. A trace is the single source of truth when a user complains - every stage, every score, every model version.

text: This chapter covers hallucination detection and drift monitoring for production RAG. Four hallucination signals (faithfulness score, citation coverage, refusal rate, out-of-index mentions) catch most issues automatically. Four drift types (data, embedding, eval, distribution) each have distinct detection mechanisms. A 5-step per-generation pipeline extracts claims, judges support, computes faithfulness = supported/total, logs to trace, and alerts when the rolling 7-day median drops below 0.85. Drift signals plotted over 30 days cross thresholds on different days - faithfulness day 28, recall day 22, refusal rate day 25. Useful alerts always carry failing query examples, scores, cited docs, unsupported claims, and a recommended action. The full operator dashboard combines trend thumbnails, drift-type status badges, and top hallucinated queries on one panel.

text: Four production signals catch most hallucinations without manual review. Wire each into the trace, alert on rolling medians, investigate any spike inside the same shift. (1) Faithfulness Score: per-generation faithfulness from LLM-as-judge; alert if rolling 7-day median drops below 0.85. (2) Citation Coverage: percent of claims with a [doc-X] citation; alert if below 80%. (3) Refusal Rate: percent of queries refused with 'I don't know'; alert on spike (too cautious) or dip (hallucinating when context was sufficient). (4) Out-of-Index Mentions: regex or LLM check for entities mentioned that do not appear in any retrieved doc - a hard signal of hallucination.
summary: Four production signals (faithfulness, citation coverage, refusal rate, out-of-index mentions) detect most RAG hallucinations automatically.

text: Drift is the slow leak before the flood. Four kinds of drift afflict production RAG, each with a different detection mechanism and fix. (1) Data Drift: corpus changes (new docs added, old removed); embedding index becomes stale relative to current corpus. Detect via re-index timestamp vs current doc set checksum. (2) Embedding Drift: you re-embed with a new model; old vectors and new vectors are not directly comparable; mixed-version retrievals pollute top-K. Detect by logging embedding_model_version per query and alerting on mixed-version retrievals. (3) Eval Drift: LLM-as-judge model is upgraded; faithfulness scores from old vs new judge are not directly comparable. Detect by locking judge version; rerun calibration on judge change. (4) Distribution Drift: user query distribution shifts (new product launched, new failure mode); old query embeddings cluster differently. Detect via cluster monitoring on query embeddings over time.
summary: The four drift types in production RAG - data, embedding, eval, and distribution - each with a distinct detection method.

text: A 5-step hallucination detection pipeline runs per-generation. Input: user query + retrieved chunks + generated answer. Step 1: Extract Claims - LLM segments the answer into atomic claims. Step 2: Check Each Claim Against Retrieved Chunks - LLM-as-judge labels each claim supported or unsupported. Step 3: Compute Faithfulness Score = supported_claims / total_claims. Step 4: Log Score + Doc-IDs + Unsupported Claims - emit to trace. Step 5: Alert If Rolling Median < 0.85 - page owner on drop. Worked example: 5 claims, 4 supported, 1 hallucinated -> faithfulness = 4/5 = 0.80 -> below 0.85 threshold -> ALERT.
summary: The 5-step per-generation pipeline - extract claims, judge each, compute faithfulness, log, alert - with a worked 4/5 = 0.80 example.

text: An alert without examples is a blank page at 3am. Always include the failing queries, the failing scores, and a hint at the recommended action so the on-call engineer can start fixing immediately. Alert payload example: ALERT rag.faithfulness.drop. Severity WARNING. Trigger: 7-day median faithfulness dropped below 0.85 (currently 0.82). First crossed: 2026-05-14 03:21 UTC. Affected: production customer support pipeline. Recent examples last 24h - Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' faithfulness 0.60, cited docs doc-1, doc-3, unsupported claim 'Support team will email a reset link.' Query 'Cancel my subscription and get a refund' faithfulness 0.50, cited doc-4, unsupported claim 'Refunds are issued within 24 hours.' Recommended action: review recent corpus updates and re-run golden eval.
summary: What a useful drift alert payload contains - trigger, severity, failing query examples with scores, cited docs, unsupported claims, and recommended action.

text: Six framework options for RAG in 2025. No Framework: raw SDK (Anthropic / OpenAI / Cohere) plus simple Python or TypeScript orchestration; most popular for new production RAG in 2025. LlamaIndex: RAG-focused framework with good defaults for retrieval; less abstracted than LangChain. LangChain: the original; large ecosystem; abstractions evolving and some deprecated within the framework itself. LangGraph: graph-based agent orchestration from the LangChain team; newer; explicit state machine. Haystack: enterprise-leaning; modular pipelines; smaller community than LangChain. Vendor SDK: OpenAI Agents, Anthropic native tool-use; tight integration; vendor lock-in. Brand names preserve official capitalization (LlamaIndex, LangChain).

text: Framework decision matrix: five criteria across six options. Lock-In Risk: No Framework None, LlamaIndex Low, LangChain Medium, LangGraph Medium, Haystack Low, Vendor SDK High. API Churn: No Framework None, LlamaIndex Low, LangChain High, LangGraph Medium, Haystack Low, Vendor SDK Low. Complexity (build / hidden): No Framework High build / Low hidden, LlamaIndex Low build / Medium hidden, LangChain Low build / High hidden, LangGraph Medium build / Medium hidden, Haystack Medium build / Medium hidden, Vendor SDK Low build / High hidden. Community Size: No Framework Huge, LlamaIndex Large, LangChain Huge, LangGraph Growing, Haystack Medium, Vendor SDK Vendor-Specific. RAG-Specific Fit: No Framework You Build, LlamaIndex Strong, LangChain OK, LangGraph OK, Haystack Strong, Vendor SDK OK.

text: An honest take on LangChain. LangChain is the dominant framework in 2025 but the picture is mixed. Pros: largest ecosystem; biggest community; tutorials everywhere; prototype in 30 minutes; integrations for every vector DB, embedding model, and LLM. Cons: abstractions hide what is actually happening (lost-in-middle invisible until you trace); some abstractions deprecated within the framework itself (Stuff / MapReduce / Refine, original AgentExecutor); fast-moving API churn requires upgrade cycles; framework lock-in concerns once production code depends on chains. LangChain is still actively developed and dominant for prototyping. For production-grade RAG, many teams move to raw SDK plus LlamaIndex retriever, or stay on LangChain but carefully avoid deprecating abstractions.
summary: Honest pros and cons of LangChain: dominant ecosystem and fast prototyping versus hidden abstractions, deprecated pieces, API churn, and lock-in.
queries: alternative to LangChain for production RAG

text: When each framework fits. No Framework: small team, full control needed, production-grade RAG with custom orchestration; fits 2-3 engineers building a single product. LlamaIndex: RAG-heavy product, want good retrieval defaults, accept some framework dependency; fits teams shipping a RAG-centric app. LangChain: early prototyping, large team needs shared vocabulary, ecosystem integrations matter more than minimalism; fits teams that staff for framework upgrades. LangGraph: explicit agent state machine, multi-step workflows, less hidden magic than LangChain; fits teams building structured agents. Haystack: enterprise / regulated environment, modular pipelines, smaller-community tradeoff is acceptable; fits orgs with security review on every dependency. Vendor SDK: committed to one provider, lock-in is acceptable, tight integration is the priority; fits internal Anthropic / OpenAI shops.
summary: Team and product profiles that best fit each framework, from 2-3 engineer No Framework setups to vendor-locked Anthropic/OpenAI shops.

NO leaf: LangChain. NO asks: Single-provider OK? YES leaf: Vendor SDK. NO leaf: No Framework. Three questions, six leaves, one choice. Anything more complex than this is over-thinking the framework decision.

text: What stays the same regardless of framework. Every framework wraps the same six core decisions: (1) chunking strategy (20.4 - 20.10) is a data-pipeline decision, not a framework decision; (2) embedding model choice (21.1) is a data-pipeline decision; (3) hybrid plus reranker cascade (21.3, 21.4) is a retrieval-quality decision; (4) prompt template plus context packing (22.1) is a generation decision; (5) eval (23.1 - 23.5) is a measurement decision; (6) tracing (23.8) is an ops decision. Frameworks are wrappers. Get these decisions right and the framework is replaceable. Production RAG code should be thin enough that a framework swap is a 2-day job, not a 2-month rewrite.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: This chapter is the capstone for the RAG in Production track. It assembles a nine-decision framework that covers every act of the section - whether to use RAG, chunking, embedding and index, query transformation, context packing and generation, advanced retrieval patterns, evaluation, production operations, and framework choice - and then walks the entire framework end to end for a new domain: Q&A over 200,000 published court cases across 50 US jurisdictions. The capstone use case has a quality bar of partner-lawyer rating >= 4/5, latency budget 5-10 seconds, cost budget $0.05/query, mandatory citations, and a mandatory jurisdiction filter. Decisions made: hierarchical + contextual chunking; domain-adapted embedding on legal-BERT with HNSW + BM25 hybrid retrieval, jurisdiction filter, and cross-encoder rerank; multi-query expansion and decomposition (HyDE skipped); 16-30k context with sandwich packing, mandatory citations, and refusal; GraphRAG for citation graph plus multi-hop retrieval (long-context skipped); LLM-as-judge with legal rubric, 500-pair partner-curated golden set, RAGAS faithfulness and citation precision, online A/B; semantic cache disabled, prompt cache enabled, full tracing, hallucination detection, drift monitoring; framework choice is No Framework + LlamaIndex retriever + raw Anthropic SDK. Final stack achieves ~$0.04/query, ~6s latency, and full citation audit.
summary: Capstone chapter applies the nine-decision RAG framework end to end to design a production legal-case-law Q&A system meeting its quality, latency, cost, and audit budgets.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Capstone use case: Q&A over case law for a legal research firm, a fully different domain from a customer support corpus. Design brief: Corpus is 200,000 published court cases across 50 US jurisdictions. Metadata includes jurisdiction tag, year, and citation tree. Quality bar is partner-lawyer rating >= 4/5 on a legal research rubric. Latency budget is 5-10 seconds (legal research is patient). Cost budget is $0.05 per query. Audit requirements: every answer cited, jurisdiction filter mandatory. Example queries: 'What's the precedent for tortious interference in California state court?', 'Cases citing Smith v Jones about negligence in medical contexts', and 'How has the standard for qualified immunity evolved 2010-2025?'. The chapter walks every decision in the framework for this brief; each choice points back to the chapter that taught it.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 1 - Chunking strategy. Choice: hierarchical chunking plus contextual retrieval. Per-paragraph chunks with parent (case-level) metadata and an LLM-injected 'This paragraph is from case X about Y' preamble. Why: case opinions have natural hierarchy (intro, facts, reasoning, holding). Hierarchical preserves parent-child relationships. Contextual retrieval injects the per-chunk preamble that disambiguates citation-level retrieval. Plain fixed-size chunking would mid-sentence-split a court's analysis. Tradeoff: more storage because each chunk now carries parent metadata; higher index build cost because contextual augmentation is an LLM call per chunk. Worth it because case-law precision demands citation-level retrieval.
summary: Picks hierarchical plus contextual chunking for case opinions to preserve structure and disambiguate retrieval, accepting higher storage and indexing cost.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 2 - Embedding and index. Choice: domain-adapted embedding on top of a base legal-BERT or Cohere Embed Multilingual. HNSW index with hybrid (BM25 + dense). Jurisdiction stored as filter metadata. Cross-encoder cascade rerank. Why: general-purpose embeddings underweight legal jargon such as 'tortious' and 'qualified immunity'. Hybrid catches both exact-citation lookups ('Smith v Jones') and semantic queries ('medical negligence cases'). Jurisdiction filter eliminates 96% of irrelevant cases before reranking. Cross-encoder lifts precision on legal-language matching. Tradeoff: domain adaptation requires labeled training pairs (expensive but one-time). Cross-encoder adds 50-100ms per query, which the latency budget allows.
summary: Selects domain-adapted legal embeddings with HNSW hybrid retrieval, jurisdiction filter, and cross-encoder rerank, trading training cost and 50-100ms latency for legal precision.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 3 - Query transformation. Choice: multi-query expansion plus decomposition for complex multi-hop queries. HyDE is SKIPPED. Why: a query like 'cases citing X about Y' naturally decomposes into 'find X' then 'find cases citing X' then 'filter for cases about Y'. Multi-query rewriting using legal synonyms (e.g., 'negligence' / 'duty of care' / 'breach of duty') improves recall on lexically narrow legal language. HyDE is skipped because case law is precise and fake documents hurt more than they help. Tradeoff: more retrievals per query (3-5x), so cost goes up linearly. Mitigated by aggressive deduplication after RRF merge.
summary: Uses multi-query expansion and decomposition for legal queries while skipping HyDE, paying 3-5x retrieval cost but cutting it with deduplication after RRF.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 4 - Context packing and generation. Choice: high token budget (16-30k tokens of retrieved context per query). Relevance-first ordering with sandwich pattern to fight lost-in-middle. Mandatory inline citations via prompt template. I-don't-know clause if no jurisdiction-matching cases. Why: legal work needs many cases to reason across, so token budget is high because cost budget is high. Sandwich (place top hits at start AND end) anchors model attention. Citations are mandatory for audit. Refusal preferable to fabrication. Tradeoff: higher per-query LLM cost, accepted given the $0.05 budget. Capstone prompt template: 'System: You are a legal research assistant. Cite every case you reference using [Case Name, Year] format. Only use the provided cases. If no provided case answers, say "I don't have case law for this question in the provided jurisdiction". Provided Cases: [Case A] {summary + key holdings} ... [Case J] {summary + key holdings}. Question: {user question}. Jurisdiction: {jurisdiction}. Answer:'.
summary: Picks 16-30k context with sandwich ordering, mandatory citations, and an I-don't-know clause, plus shows the full capstone prompt template.
queries: How many tokens of context for legal RAG
What is sandwich packing for RAG context
How to fight lost-in-middle in legal RAG

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 5 - Advanced retrieval patterns. Choice: GraphRAG for the case-citation graph (cases citing X / cases cited by X). Multi-hop retrieval for 'cases citing X about Y' decomposition. Long-context is SKIPPED. Why: legal citation is a graph; GraphRAG indexes the relationships. Without it, 'cases citing X' requires querying every case in the corpus; with it, you follow the citation edges. Multi-hop chains the steps for complex precedent queries. Long-context is skipped because 200k cases is far larger than any context window, so RAG is mandatory. Tradeoff: GraphRAG indexing is 10x slower than vector-only, and re-indexing the citation graph on corpus update is a nightly batch. Multi-hop is slower per query, but precision gain on multi-hop queries is large.
summary: Adds GraphRAG and multi-hop retrieval for the citation graph while skipping long-context, accepting 10x slower indexing and a nightly graph rebuild.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 6 - Evaluation. Choice: LLM-as-judge with a legal-specific rubric. Golden dataset of 500 partner-curated query/expected-answer pairs. RAGAS metrics: faithfulness, citation precision, jurisdiction match. Online A/B on partner-rated 4/5 score. Why: general-purpose RAG eval misses legal correctness. Partner-curated golden set is the ground truth because no automated metric reaches partner-quality. RAGAS faithfulness catches hallucinations. Citation precision (% of cited cases that are real and on-point) is critical. Tradeoff: golden dataset is expensive to build in partner hours. 500 examples covers ~80% of the query distribution; the remaining 20% is caught by online feedback. Rerun golden eval weekly on prod.
summary: Combines LLM-as-judge with a legal rubric, a 500-pair partner golden set, RAGAS metrics, and online A/B, rerunning golden eval weekly.
queries: LLM as judge for legal rubric
Why automated metrics fail for legal
What percent of query distribution does 500 cover

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 7 - Production operations. Choice: semantic cache DISABLED. Prompt cache ENABLED for system prompt + few-shot examples. Full tracing per query. Hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process. Drift monitoring on case-law updates. Why: semantic cache fails when 'negligence in CA' and 'negligence in NY' have cosine similarity ~0.96 but the answers differ entirely. Prompt cache is safe because system + few-shot are constant. Tracing every query is non-negotiable for audit. Tradeoff: no semantic cache means higher per-query cost, mitigated by the prompt cache. Drift monitoring catches when judicial reasoning shifts (rare but high-impact).
summary: Disables semantic cache, enables prompt cache, requires full tracing, hallucination post-checks, and drift monitoring to balance audit needs and cost.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Putting it all together - the capstone stack has five layers. Layer 1 Ingest: hierarchical + contextual chunking; domain-adapted embedding; HNSW + BM25 hybrid index; jurisdiction metadata; case-citation graph index. Layer 2 Retrieve: multi-query expansion; jurisdiction filter; hybrid retrieval; cross-encoder rerank; GraphRAG for citation queries. Layer 3 Generate: 16-30k token budget; sandwich ordering; mandatory citations; I-don't-know refusal if no jurisdiction-matching cases. Layer 4 Eval: partner-curated golden set (500 pairs); LLM-as-judge with legal rubric; RAGAS faithfulness; online A/B. Layer 5 Ops: prompt cache (semantic cache disabled); full tracing per query; hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process; drift monitoring on case-law updates. Framework choice: No Framework + LlamaIndex retriever for the RAG core, raw Anthropic SDK for generation. Result: cost ~$0.04/query (under budget), latency ~6 seconds, every answer carries [Case Name, Year] citations. The closing message: you now have everything to design production-grade RAG for any new domain - apply the framework, adjust per use case, and build.
summary: Pulls the seven decisions into a five-layer stack (Ingest, Retrieve, Generate, Eval, Ops) hitting ~$0.04/query and ~6s latency using No Framework + LlamaIndex + Anthropic SDK.

text: This chapter Anatomy of an LLM Call. Breaks one LLM call into its parts: (1) Messages list with System/User/Assistant roles, sent in full each turn. (2) Tokens are the real unit - sub-word chunks billed and counted against the context window (e.g. 200k tokens ~ 150k words). (3) Sampling knobs - temperature, top-p (nucleus), top-k - shape the next-token probability distribution. (4) Generation ends on one of four stop conditions producing finish_reason values: length, stop_sequence, end_turn, tool_use. (5) Response object returns content, stop_reason, and usage with input_tokens + output_tokens. (6) The complete six-step cycle: build messages, set parameters, send request, stream tokens, reach stop reason, return final response.
summary: Chapter-level overview of all parts that make up a single LLM API call: messages, tokens, sampling, stops, response, and cycle.

text: Every LLM call is an ordered list of messages, each tagged with a role. You don't 'chat' with the model - you send the whole list and the model writes the next assistant turn. Three roles: System sets the role, rules, and tone of the assistant (e.g. 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant. Be concise. Cite docs when possible.'). User is the end-user's turn, the thing the model has to respond to (e.g. 'I can't log in - reset my password for alice@example.com.'). Assistant is the model's reply that goes back into the messages list for the next turn (e.g. 'I'll help you reset it. I just sent a one-time link to alice@example.com.'). Every later request includes the prior assistant replies, so the model always sees the full conversation.
summary: Introduces the three message roles (System, User, Assistant) that make up every LLM API call.

text: The model doesn't see characters or words - it sees tokens, sub-word chunks from the tokenizer. Both input and output are billed and limited in tokens. User message 'I can't log in - reset my password' is 9 tokens: I, can, 't, ' log', ' in', ' -', ' reset', ' my', ' password'. Assistant reply 'I'll help you reset it.' is 7 tokens: I, 'll, ' help', ' you', ' reset', ' it', '.'. Context window = total tokens across all messages (system + user + assistant history + new reply). A 200k context window holds roughly 150k English words. Cross it and the oldest messages get dropped or the call fails.
summary: Explains tokens as the unit of input/output and how the context window limits the total tokens per call.

text:
Three sampling knobs shape how the model picks the next token from its probability distribution. Lower = safer and more repeatable. Higher = more varied.

Temperature - sharpness of the distribution. Stops: 0.0 Deterministic, 0.3 Focused, 0.7 Default, 1.0 Creative, 2.0 Wild. At T=0 the model always picks the most likely next token. At T=2 unlikely tokens get a real chance.

Top-P (Nucleus) - keep tokens until cumulative probability hits P. Example distribution: reset p=0.42 cum=0.42, change p=0.21 cum=0.63, update p=0.16 cum=0.79, fix p=0.09 cum=0.88, recover p=0.05 cum=0.93, send p=0.04 cum=0.97, create p=0.03 cum=1.00. With top_p = 0.9, sample only from the smallest set whose probabilities add to 0.9. Here 4 tokens stay in, 3 are cut.

Top-K - keep the K most likely tokens. Candidates: reset 0.42, change 0.21, update 0.16, fix 0.09, recover 0.05 (kept); send 0.04, create 0.03 (cut). With top_k = 5, everything past the 5th most-likely token is discarded before sampling.
summary: Walks through temperature, top-p (nucleus), and top-k sampling with concrete probability distributions.

text:
One LLM call doesn't 'just stop' - it stops for a specific reason and the response tells you which. Four stop conditions and four finish reasons.

1. Max Tokens Reached - config: max_tokens: 1024. Hard ceiling on how many tokens the model is allowed to generate this call. finish_reason: length.

2. Stop Sequence Hit - config: stop: ['</answer>']. The model produced one of the configured stop strings. Useful for structured output. finish_reason: stop_sequence.

3. End-Of-Turn Token - model decides on its own. The model emitted its special end-of-turn token. The natural end of a reply. finish_reason: end_turn.

4. Tool Call Requested - only with tool use enabled. The model paused to ask for a tool. Generation halts so your code can run the tool. finish_reason: tool_use.

Always check finish_reason. A length stop means the answer got cut off mid-thought - retry with a higher max_tokens.
summary: Lists the four ways an LLM call can end and the matching finish_reason values to inspect in the response.

text:
Every single call your agent makes follows this exact six-step cycle:

1. Build Messages - combine system + history + new user turn into the messages list. Arrow: Roles assigned.
2. Set Parameters - pick temperature, top-p, top-k, max_tokens, stop. Arrow: Sampling locked.
3. Send Request - POST messages + params to the model endpoint. Arrow: HTTPS POST.
4. Tokens Stream Back - model emits one token at a time over the wire. Arrow: SSE chunks.
5. Stop Reason Reached - one of: end_turn, length, stop_sequence, tool_use. Arrow: end_turn fired.
6. Final Response - content + stop_reason + usage delivered to your app.

Typical latency: ~1.5s median for a 7-token response. Long replies stream tokens as they're generated. Messages in, parameters in, request out. Tokens stream back until a stop reason fires. Response object lands with content, stop_reason, and usage.
summary: Summarizes the full six-step lifecycle of one LLM call from building messages to receiving the final response.

text: The system prompt is the role contract for an LLM agent - a constitution re-read on every turn that anything not in it does not exist for the agent. It has four moving parts: persona (who am I, voice, role), capabilities (which tools and data I may use), constraints (what I must never say or do), and output rules (how my reply must be formatted, cited, structured). The chapter walks through each part using a customer-support assistant example for Alice's knowledge base at docs.example.com, with tools search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human, constraints like no refunds over $200 without escalation, and output rules like greeting by name and wrapping tool calls in <thinking> tags. The full ten-line system prompt at the end weaves all four parts into one artifact.
summary: Overview of the system prompt as a four-part role contract (persona, capabilities, constraints, output rules) using a customer-support agent example.

text: The system prompt is the role contract. It is the constitution every later message is interpreted under. The model re-reads it on every single turn, so anything not in it does not exist for the agent. Four moving parts: Persona (who am I, what is my voice and role), Capabilities (what tools and data am I allowed to use), Constraints (what must I never say, promise, or do), Output Rules (how must my reply be formatted, cited, and structured). Get all four right and the agent behaves predictably. Skip one and it goes off-script.

text: Persona answers 'who am I, what is my voice, and what is my role?' A vague persona produces a generic chatbot. A sharp persona produces an assistant that sounds like it works at your company. BEFORE - VAGUE: 'You are a chatbot.' No role, no voice, no domain. The model fills the gap with a generic helpful tone. AFTER - PERSONA-SPECIFIC: 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant for the customer support knowledge base. You are friendly, concise, and you never invent policies.' Names the user (Alice), names the role (customer-support assistant), names the voice (friendly, concise) and one guardrail (no invented policies). Three rules of thumb: name the user, name the role, name one thing the assistant must never do.
summary: Compares a vague persona to a sharp persona and gives three rules: name the user, name the role, name one forbidden action.

you are a chatbot bad example
name the user name the role

text: Capabilities tell the model which tools are available, what each one does, and when to use it. Without this section, the model either refuses or hallucinates tool calls. Tools: search_kb - searches the customer support knowledge base at docs.example.com; lookup_customer - fetches the profile for a customer id like c-9924; process_refund - issues a refund up to a configured dollar limit; escalate_human - hands the conversation off to a human support agent. In the prompt: 'Tools available to you: search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human.' The system prompt declares the tools. The API schema (this chapter) is what actually wires them up. Both sides have to agree.
summary: Lists the four capability tools (search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human) and explains the prompt declares them while the API schema wires them up.

text: Constraints are the negative-space rules. Every refund-gone-wrong, every leaked customer record, every invented policy traces back to a missing line here. Be explicit about the 'never's. The four NEVER rules: Promise refunds over $200 without escalation; Share another customer's data; Claim policies not in the knowledge base; Call tools without first looking up the customer. The $200 line and the escalation line live here, not in code. The model checks them on every reply, so a policy change is a one-line edit, not a deploy.
summary: Explains constraints as explicit NEVER rules and lists four guardrails including the $200 refund cap that lives in prompt rather than code.
