text: Prompt: summarize_ticket. A Prompt is a parameterized template the server publishes. The host surfaces these as slash-commands or quick actions for the user. When the user picks one, the host collects the arguments, fills the template, and sends the resulting message to the model. MCP Prompt (Shape): { "name": "summarize_ticket", "description": "Summarize a support ticket conversation.", "arguments": [ { "name": "ticket_id", "required": true }, { "name": "max_words", "required": false } ] }. The arguments array lists each parameter the template needs, with a required flag. ticket_id is required, max_words is optional. The host can either prompt the user for missing values or fill defaults before invoking.
summary: Prompts are user-invoked parameterized templates shown as slash-commands, with named required/optional arguments.

text: Action vs Data vs Template - the decision rule. When you design an MCP server, every capability falls into exactly one bucket. Ask what the model or host needs to DO, READ, or REUSE. Need to DO something (mutate state, call API)? → TOOL. Need to READ something (fact, doc, file)? → RESOURCE. Need a reusable prompt template the user can invoke? → PROMPT. Decision rule: tool if side-effecting; resource if read-only data; prompt if template the user picks. Putting a read-only doc behind a tool wastes a model turn. Putting an action behind a resource hides it from the model entirely. Putting a one-shot question behind a prompt hides it from end users. The split is the contract.
summary: Decision framework for picking the right MCP primitive based on whether the need is action, data, or template - plus failure modes of miscategorizing.

text: This chapter covers building an MCP server. Server is a sequence of declarations plus a start: define identity (name, version), register tools (name, description, inputSchema, handler), register resources (URI pattern + handler), register prompts (named template with arguments + handler), then start listening on transport. Handlers run server-side, hidden from the model. Lifecycle has four states - REGISTERED, LISTENING, ACTIVE, CLOSED - traversed via connect(), initialize, and shutdown(). Test server in isolation first: unit-test handlers, verify list catalog, test transport handshake, drive with a mock host before connecting to a real LLM.

text: An MCP server is mostly a sequence of declarations followed by a start. You give it a name and version, then register tools, resources, and prompts, then call start. The server itself is short - the handlers do the real work. Five phases: 1) Define Identity (name, version). 2) Declare Tools (callable actions with schemas). 3) Declare Resources (URI patterns the host can read). 4) Declare Prompts (parameterized templates users can pick). 5) Start (begin listening on transport). Each declaration registers what the server offers; start opens the connection.

})

text: From the moment the server boots until it shuts down, it moves through four states. Most of the lifetime is spent in ACTIVE, where it handles list and call requests from the host. States: REGISTERED (definitions declared) -> connect() -> LISTENING (transport open) -> initialize -> ACTIVE (handling list/call requests) -> shutdown() -> CLOSED (shutdown). Most requests are handled in ACTIVE: list, call, read, prompt-get.

text: An MCP server is just a process with handlers and a transport. You can - and should - test every piece in isolation before plugging it into a real host with a real model. Four testing strategies: 1) Test Handler Functions As Plain Unit Tests - call them directly with sample inputs and assert the returned value. 2) Test List Responses - assert all declared tools, resources, and prompts appear in the catalog. 3) Test Transport Connection - spin the server up over stdio or HTTP and assert the initialize handshake completes. 4) Use A Mock Host Before Connecting To A Real LLM - drive list and call from a scripted host to catch contract bugs without paying for tokens. Production hygiene: test the server in isolation, then wire it to the host.
summary: MCP servers should be tested via four strategies: unit-test handlers, verify list responses, test transport, and use a mock host before real LLM.

text: MCP security is layered. Server code is untrusted by default, so the host enforces a trust boundary the MCP transport is the only legal crossing of. A sandboxed OS process isolates the server with transport-only communication, walled-off filesystem, and network off by default. Capability scope narrows allowed tools, gates resources by URI prefix, and offers a global read-only flag. HTTP servers add OAuth so the host proves which user authorized each call via an access token checked against scope. Consent prompts block side-effecting calls until the user approves, and an audit log records timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and decision. Together: sandbox keeps the server from reaching outside; scope keeps it from doing more than allowed; OAuth proves the user granted access; consent and audit keep the user in control.
summary: MCP security stacks sandbox isolation, capability scope, OAuth proof of authorization, and consent plus audit to keep untrusted server code safe and user-controlled.

text: An MCP server is third-party code (open source, vendor-supplied, or written by a teammate) and the host never assumes it is safe. The host holds the user data, the model, and every mutation worth caring about. The server holds the tool implementation. The boundary between them is what makes the architecture safe. The host owns user data and the model; the server runs third-party tool code; the protocol crossing the boundary is the only thing the host has to police. The trust boundary diagram shows a trusted outer host ring (user data, model, allowed mutations) surrounding an untrusted inner server ring (third-party tool code) with three protocol crossings: List (server to host), Call (host to server), and Result (server to host). The MCP transport is the only legal crossing.
summary: MCP servers are untrusted third-party code, so the host keeps data and model inside a trust boundary that only the MCP transport crosses.

text: Sandbox: process isolation. The host launches the server in its own OS process. The host can spawn it, kill it, and shape what it sees, but never shares memory with it. Whatever capability the server wants, it has to ask for over the transport. Three sandbox rules: (1) Transport Only: the server communicates with the host only over the MCP transport, with no back channel. (2) Filesystem Walled Off: no filesystem access except the directories the host explicitly grants. (3) Network Off By Default: no outbound network unless the host opts in for this server. The sandbox is the OS-level fence: even if the tool code is buggy or hostile, it cannot reach beyond the resources the host hands it.
summary: The host runs the MCP server in a separate sandboxed OS process so it has no memory, filesystem, or network access except what the host grants over the transport.

text: Capability scope per host. Even when a server declares ten tools and a hundred resources, the host can narrow what this particular agent is allowed to touch. Scope is a subset of what the server advertises, configured per host. Three scope dimensions: (1) ALLOWED TOOLS - Subset Of Declared Tools: the host picks which tools each agent may call (example: deny `escalate_human` for junior agents). (2) ALLOWED RESOURCES - Prefix-Matched URIs: resource access is gated by URI prefix patterns (example: allow `kb://articles/*` but not `kb://internal/*`). (3) ALLOWED MUTATION - Read-Only Mode Flag: a flag forces every tool call to be non-mutating, regardless of declaration (example: read-only mode for evaluation runs). Capability scope is allow/deny lists at three levels: tool names, resource URI prefixes, and a global read-only flag.
summary: Hosts narrow what an agent can do on a server through allow/deny lists on tool names, resource URI prefixes, and a global read-only mutation flag.

text: OAuth: who granted what. When the server lives over HTTP, the host has to prove the user authorized it. OAuth is the standard answer: the user grants scopes once, the host exchanges that grant for an Access Token, and that token rides along on every server request. Four-step flow (Grant -> Token -> Request -> Check): (1) User Authorizes, (2) Host Gets Access Token, (3) Token Passes To Server, (4) Server Checks Scope. The Access Token is the proof the server demands on every call. Stdio servers skip OAuth because they run under local trust. HTTP servers require it so the host can prove which user authorized the call.
summary: HTTP MCP servers use a four-step OAuth flow where the user grants scopes, the host gets an access token, and the server checks scope on every request.

text: Consent + audit: user sees everything. Sandbox, scope, and OAuth handle the machine side; consent and audit handle the human side. The host asks the user before any side-effecting call and writes every call to a log the user can review. Consent prompts block the call until the user clicks Approve or Deny. Example prompt: 'About to call process_refund / For invoice INV-9924 / Reason: "Customer Requested" / Allow? [Approve] [Deny]'. Required for side-effecting tools. Audit log records timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and consent decision. Example rows: 14:02:11 lookup_customer { id: "C-104" } OK alice@co Auto; 14:02:34 process_refund { invoice: "INV-9924" } OK alice@co Approved; 14:03:02 send_email { to: "cust@x" } OK alice@co Approved; 14:05:50 delete_account { id: "C-104" } Blocked alice@co Denied. The audit log makes every action traceable to a user, a tool, and a decision.
summary: Consent prompts block side-effecting calls until the user approves, and an audit log records every call with timestamp, tool, input, result, user, and decision.

text: This chapter contrasts MCP and A2A. MCP lets an agent borrow a single tool; A2A lets an agent delegate an entire task to a peer agent that runs its own model, memory, and tools. A2A discovery happens via an agent.json card containing name, description, skills, endpoint, and auth. A real delegation flows in five steps: classify, look up a skilled agent, send context, work, return. Long tasks stream intermediate updates (lookup, processing, approval, final) so the caller does not poll. Decision rule: function -> MCP tool, doc -> MCP resource, sub-problem needing a whole agent -> A2A delegation. A2A is heavier than MCP and reserved for collaboration, not function calls.
summary: Chapter overview of A2A: peer-agent delegation, agent.json discovery, five-step delegation flow, streaming updates, and decision rule versus MCP.

text: Agent To Tool, Agent To Agent. MCP handles one direction: an agent reaches out to a tool and gets a value back. A2A handles the other: an agent hands an entire problem to a different agent and lets that agent run its own loop. Same wire idea, very different scope. With MCP, the agent stays in charge and just borrows a tool. With A2A, the agent hands the wheel to a peer that runs its own model, memory, and tools to finish the job.

text: Long Tasks Stream Progress. A delegated task is not a single function call. The receiving agent may need minutes, human approval, or several tool calls of its own. A2A solves this by letting the receiving agent stream intermediate updates back instead of going silent until the final result is ready. Streaming Updates From Billing Agent: Looked Up Customer..., Processing Refund..., Refund > $200, Need Approval, Final Result Returned. Streaming lets triage show the user progress in real time and react if an intermediate update needs human approval before the long task finishes. Triage stays informed without polling.
summary: Explains that A2A delegated tasks stream intermediate progress updates back to the caller so triage can surface progress and react to approvals without polling.

text: Two Protocols, Two Roles. MCP and A2A live side by side, not in competition. The decision is about what you need from the other side: a function, a document, or an entire collaborator. Decision rows: Need To Call A Function? -> MCP Tool. Need To Read A Doc? -> MCP Resource. Need An Entire Other Agent To Handle A Sub-Problem? -> A2A Delegation. A2A is heavier than MCP. Use it when the delegated work needs the receiving agent's own loop, memory, and tools - not just a single function call.

text: Workflow vs Agent: a workflow is a fixed DAG you wire in advance; an agent is an open loop where the model picks each next step. Pick workflow when steps are knowable, outputs predictable, and cost/latency must be bounded - example: Classify -> Route -> Handle for tickets. Pick agent when you can't pre-draw the graph and the model must decide tools and stopping point - example: summarize all customer issues. Most production systems are hybrid: outer deterministic DAG (Classify, Reply, Log) with one inner agent step (Handle). Failure shape differs: workflow fails loud with bounded cost; agent soft-fails with unbounded iterations. Decision rule: workflow when you can; agent when you must.
summary: Workflows are fixed DAGs with bounded cost; agents are open loops with variable cost; production systems combine them with agents used only where needed.

text: Two shapes of control: a workflow is a fixed DAG where you wire the steps and the model follows the wires. An agent is an open loop where the model picks the next step every iteration. Agent side: a central Decide node with arrows out to Tool 1, Tool 2, Tool 3, Tool 4 and a self-loop, different runs take different paths. Workflow is a fixed graph you draw. Agent is an open loop where the model picks the next move. Choose the shape that matches how much variance the task actually has.

text: Workflow: known inputs, known steps. Workflow is the right shape when you already know what to do. The model is a smart cell inside a bigger pipeline you wrote, not the conductor. Pick this when the task is repeatable and the steps are knowable in advance. Pick Workflow When: You can list the steps in advance. Each step's output type is predictable. You need to guarantee certain steps execute. The cost / latency must be bounded. Example: when a ticket comes in: Classify -> Route -> Handle. Always those 3 steps. The model still calls tools and writes text. But you, not the model, decide which step runs in which order. The output of each step is predictable enough to wire to the next.
summary: Pick a workflow when steps are knowable in advance, outputs are typed, and cost/latency must be bounded.

text: Agent: variable steps, variable tools. Agent is the right shape when you can't pre-draw the graph. The model is the conductor: it decides which tool to call, in what order, and when to stop. You pay for that flexibility with cost and unpredictability. Pick Agent When: You don't know the steps in advance. The model needs to decide which tools to call and in what order. The task could take 2 calls or 20. You're willing to pay for flexibility. Example: research-style task: 'Summarize all customer issues from the past week.' Agent decides which docs to read, which to skip, and when it has enough. One ticket might need 2 tool calls. The next might need 20. You can't draw that graph in advance because the shape of the task only becomes visible after the model starts digging.
summary: Pick an agent when steps are unknown ahead of time and the model must dynamically choose tools and stopping point.

text: Most production systems are hybrid. Real systems are rarely pure. The common pattern is a workflow with one agent step inside. You keep the outer skeleton deterministic and let the model loose only inside the one node that actually needs creativity. Hybrid shape: outer DAG, inner agent loop. Example four step pipeline: Classify (deterministic) -> Handle (agent step, marked with ?) -> Reply (deterministic) -> Log (deterministic). The Classify step is deterministic. The Handle step is an agent. Reply and Log are deterministic again. This is the hybrid shape almost every production agent ships with. You get the safety and predictability of a workflow around the parts you can fully spec, and the freedom of an agent only where you actually need it.
summary: Most production systems wrap one agent step inside an outer deterministic workflow to get safety plus flexibility where needed.

text: Predictable cost vs variable cost. The biggest practical difference between workflow and agent is the shape of failure. Workflow failures are loud and bounded. Agent failures are quiet and unbounded - the agent keeps trying and the bill keeps climbing. Workflow: bounded and predictable. Cost: fixed per run. Latency: bounded by slowest step. Failure: hard crash, loud error. Agent: variable and soft-failing. Cost: variable, depends on iterations. Latency: variable, 2 calls or 20. Failure: soft-fail, gets stuck instead of crashing. Decision rule: workflow when you can; agent when you must. The cost of an extra workflow node is one more tool call. The cost of an extra agent iteration is an unbounded number of tool calls. That asymmetry is why teams default to workflow and reach for an agent only on the steps that genuinely need it.
summary: Workflows fail loud with bounded cost; agents fail soft with unbounded cost, so default to workflow and use agents only where needed.

text: Workflow primitives are the three composable building blocks of every production LLM workflow: chaining (sequential steps where step A's structured output becomes step B's input), routing (a classifier picks one of N branches), and parallelization (fan out N concurrent workers, then merge). Chaining is sequence, routing is a fork, parallelization is fan-out plus merge. Real pipelines stack all three: chain into a classifier, route to a branch, parallelize heavy retrieval, then chain the merged result into a final answer. A support agent workflow shows the pattern: ticket arrives, intent classifier routes to billing / troubleshooting / escalation, billing chains lookup_customer then lookup_subscription then respond, troubleshooting parallelizes search_kb across three hypotheses, escalation calls escalate_human directly. The classifier is itself an LLM call with few-shot examples. Latency for parallel workers equals max(worker time), not the sum.
summary: Overview of three workflow primitives - chaining, routing, parallelization - and how production workflows stack them.

text: Workflows are built from three composable primitives. Chaining wires step A's output into step B. Routing classifies the input and picks one branch. Parallelization fans out N workers and merges their results. Every production workflow stacks these three. Three cards summarize them visually: CHAIN shows A -> B -> C as sequential nodes (chaining: sequential steps); ROUTE shows a classifier picking one branch (routing: one of N branches); PARALLEL shows fan-out plus merge (parallelization: concurrent workers). These are the lego bricks. Chaining is a sequence. Routing is a fork. Parallelization is a fan-out plus merge. Real workflows stack them in layers.

text: Chaining: step A's output is step B's input. The structured output of one LLM call becomes the structured input of the next. The glue is JSON. Step A returns a typed object, your code reads it, feeds the next step. Example chain: Ticket -> Classifier (LLM call) -> Handler (LLM call). Concrete data flow: Step A output `{ "category": "billing", "confidence": 0.92 }`; Step B input `{ "category": "billing", "ticket": "Can't bill my card." }`. Because each step returns a typed object (the JSON schema from this chapter), the next step can read fields like ticket.category by name. Chaining is normal function composition with LLM calls as the functions.
summary: Explains chaining as JSON-typed step outputs feeding the next step's input, like function composition.

text: Routing classifies the input and picks one branch. The classifier is itself an LLM call that returns a typed label. Your code then dispatches to the matching handler. Only the chosen branch runs. Example: an Intent Classifier on a support ticket picks one of four branches: Billing, Product, Troubleshooting, or Chitchat. The classifier is an LLM call with few-shot examples (see section 24.3). One classification call replaces a hand-written if/else tree. The model labels the input, your code dispatches the matching branch handler. Adding a new branch is one new few-shot example plus one new handler.
summary: Routing uses an LLM classifier to pick one branch; replaces hand-written if/else with few-shot examples.

text: Parallelization runs N independent calls concurrently and aggregates the results. The total latency is the time of the slowest worker, not the sum. This is the cheapest way to cut wait time on independent sub-tasks. Example: fan-out to 3 workers then aggregate. Worker 1 = 180ms, Worker 2 = 140ms, Worker 3 = 210ms. Formula: Latency = max(worker time), not sum. If three retrieval calls each take 150-210ms, running them in series costs ~540ms; running them concurrently costs ~210ms, the slowest one. The aggregator then merges all results into one structured object.
summary: Parallelization fans out concurrent workers and merges results; total latency equals max worker time.
queries: what is parallelization in LLM workflows
fan out concurrent workers merge results

text: Production workflows compose all three primitives. A typical pipeline: chain into a classifier, route to one of several branches, parallelize the heavy retrieval inside one branch, then chain the merged result into a final answer step. Composed topology: Ticket -> Classifier -> [Billing | Troubleshoot | Chitchat]; the Troubleshoot branch fans out to search_kb, search_docs, search_tickets; those three merge into Merge; Merge chains into Answer. Every primitive does one thing well. The real power comes from stacking them. You combine sequence, branch, and fan-out the same way you combine if, switch, and map in normal code.
summary: Real workflows stack chain, route, and parallel primitives, analogous to combining if, switch, and map.

text: The agent loop is a three-beat cycle - REASON, ACT, OBSERVE - that repeats until termination. Each iteration is a state machine moving through WAITING, REASONING, ACTING, OBSERVING and eventually landing in DONE, FAILED, or ESCALATED. A cheap termination check runs after every OBSERVE, branching to DONE (final answer), ESCALATE (max iterations), or ABORT (budget out), otherwise looping back to REASON. Each iteration costs roughly 1 LLM call plus 1 tool call, and context grows every loop because the full history is replayed. The T2 trace shows the agent discovering step ordering on its own across 4 iterations. Use a loop only when a later tool's input depends on an earlier tool's output - otherwise prefer a single call or fixed workflow.
summary: Summarizes the agent loop chapter - the three-beat cycle, state machine, termination check, costs, traced example, and when to use a loop.

text: The agent loop has exactly three beats: REASON (the model thinks), ACT (the runtime emits a tool_use), OBSERVE (the runtime returns a tool_result). After OBSERVE, the model reasons again with the new observation glued onto the history, then the cycle repeats. Every agent system is some shape of this REASON-ACT-OBSERVE cycle. REASON reads the entire history so far - the model's next decision is always conditioned on every previous tool call and result, not just the original prompt.
summary: Introduces the three-beat REASON-ACT-OBSERVE cycle that defines every agent loop.

text: Zoom in on a single iteration and the loop becomes a state machine. Four primary states: WAITING (idle, awaiting input), REASONING (model call in flight), ACTING (tool call in flight), OBSERVING (got tool result). Transitions: User Input drives WAITING to REASONING, LLM Response drives REASONING to ACTING, Tool Result drives ACTING to OBSERVING, then OBSERVING loops back to REASONING for the next iteration. Three terminal states end the run: DONE (final answer emitted), FAILED (unrecoverable error like tool throw or schema break), ESCALATED (budget or max iterations hit). Primary states loop. Terminal states end the run. Every iteration checks whether it should cross the bottom boundary.
summary: Reframes the loop as a state machine with four primary states and three terminal states.

text: Every iteration the runtime asks: should we keep looping? Without this guardrail an agent can spin forever on a tool that keeps almost-working. The check happens after OBSERVE and before the next REASON. Three outcomes from a diamond decision: Final Answer Emitted? Yes -> DONE. Max Iterations Exceeded? Yes -> ESCALATE. Budget Exceeded? Yes -> ABORT. Otherwise No -> next iteration. The termination check is cheap (no model call) and happens on every iteration. It is the difference between a system you can run in production and a runaway model that bills your card flat.
summary: Describes the per-iteration termination check that decides whether to keep looping or exit to DONE, ESCALATE, or ABORT.

text: One iteration is one full lap around the cycle. Per-beat cost: REASON - 1 LLM call, roughly $0.02 for a 5k-token reasoning context plus 200-token response. ACT - 0 model cost (tool_use is emitted in REASON's response), 1 tool execution cost (varies). OBSERVE - 0 model cost, result appended to history. TOTAL - roughly 1 LLM call + 1 tool call per iteration. Rule of thumb: cost per iteration = 1 LLM call + 1 tool call. Scales linearly with iteration count. Reasoning context grows every iteration because the full history is replayed. By iteration 8 you might be paying for 40k tokens of context per call. Long agent runs get expensive fast even when each individual call looks small.
summary: Breaks down the cost of one agent iteration and explains how context growth makes long runs expensive.

text: Ticket T2: 'I forgot my password and my email also changed.' Two problems, order matters. Iteration 1: REASON - need customer info before anything else. ACT - lookup_customer(email="ada@example.com"). OBSERVE - got customer_id c-9924, plan=Pro, email verified=false. Iteration 2: REASON - email outdated, update first before reset lands in the wrong inbox. ACT - change_email(customer_id="c-9924", new_email="ada+new@example.com"). OBSERVE - email updated, verification pending. Iteration 3: REASON - now reset the password. ACT - reset_password(customer_id="c-9924"). OBSERVE - reset email sent to ada+new@example.com. Iteration 4: REASON - done, summarize for the user. ACT - emit final answer, no tool call. OBSERVE - TERMINATE, loop exits. Outcome: 4 iterations, 3 tool calls, 1 final answer. Loop terminates cleanly because the model emitted a final response instead of a tool call. You never told the agent 'update email before reset'. It worked that out in iteration 2 from the observation in iteration 1. That step ordering is the value of the loop: the model adapts to what it just learned.
summary: Traces a four-iteration ticket where the agent discovers step ordering by reasoning over intermediate observations.

text: Not every task needs a loop. A single tool call is cheaper, faster, and easier to debug. Save the loop for tasks where one call genuinely cannot finish the job. Single call: one tool, one response, done. Example: 'What is the weather in Tokyo?' -> get_weather('Tokyo') -> return the result. No adaptation needed. Loop: multiple iterations, model adapts based on intermediate results. Example: ticket T2 above, where iteration 1's observation changes what iteration 2 decides to do. Decision rule: if the second tool's input depends on the first tool's output, you need a loop. Otherwise stay with a single call or a fixed workflow. That dependency is the whole reason the loop exists. The first tool's output reshapes what the model knows, and the model must re-reason before picking the second tool. No loop, no adaptation, no agent.
summary: Gives the decision rule for when a task needs a loop versus a single tool call or fixed workflow.

text: This chapter ReAct Pattern. ReAct = Reasoning + Acting. Model writes Thought, Action, Observation as visible structured output each iteration of the agent loop instead of hiding reasoning internally. Thought explains why next action is right. Action is exactly one tool call matching JSON schema. Observation is the runtime-supplied tool result (model does not invent). Loop repeats until model emits an Answer instead of an Action. Worked example: Ticket T2 password reset with changed email runs 4 cycles, 3 tool calls (lookup_customer, change_email, reset_password), then final answer. Trade-off: each Thought costs tokens and slows response - modern tool_use APIs already give structured calls without forcing written reasoning. Use ReAct when you need to audit reasoning, debug failures, fine-tune smaller models on traces, or build user trust.
summary: ReAct forces the agent to write Thought, Action, Observation each loop so reasoning is visible for audit, debug, fine-tuning, and trust.

text: Make The Reasoning Visible. ReAct stands for Reasoning + Acting. Instead of hiding the model's reasoning inside its head and only emitting a tool call, ReAct asks the model to write the thought out loud, then the action, then the observation, in that order, every iteration. The loop is the same; what changes is that each block is now visible structured output. Three labeled blocks: Thought = what the model is thinking about the next step. Action = which tool to call and with which arguments. Observation = what the tool returned, fed back into the next thought. Key idea: Thought is INSIDE the model's response now, not hidden. Same three beats as the agent loop. The Thought becomes a written block in the response, so evaluators, debuggers, and downstream tools can read it directly.
summary: ReAct = Reasoning + Acting: model writes Thought, Action, Observation as visible blocks each loop iteration instead of hiding reasoning internally.

text: Thought: Why The Next Action. The Thought block answers one question: why is the next action the right next action? It names the plan in plain language before any tool fires. A good Thought references the user's request, the state so far, and the order of remaining steps. Example: 'Thought: The user asked to reset their password but mentioned the email changed. I should first lookup_customer with the OLD email, then change_email to the NEW one, then reset_password.' Why it matters: the Thought makes the plan visible for evals and debugging. When a reset later goes wrong, you can open the trace and read the model's plan in English. You learn whether the failure was bad reasoning (wrong plan) or bad execution (right plan, wrong tool argument). That split is impossible without a written Thought.
summary: The Thought block is a plain-language plan justifying the next action, enabling separation of reasoning failures from execution failures.
queries: What goes in a ReAct Thought block

text: Action: The Concrete Step. After the Thought, the model emits exactly one Action. It is a tool name plus an argument object that matches the tool's JSON schema. No second tool, no batching - one call, one step. Example: 'Action: lookup_customer({"email": "alice@example.com"})'. Rule: one Action per iteration. Why only one? Because the next Thought must be free to react to whatever this Action returned. Batching two tool calls into one Action makes the model commit to a second step before it has seen the first result, which is exactly the trap the loop is supposed to avoid.
summary: Each ReAct iteration emits exactly one Action - a tool name plus JSON-schema-matching args - so the next Thought can react to the actual result.
queries: What is an Action block in ReAct
Can ReAct batch multiple tool calls
Why only one Action per iteration
Why not batch tool calls in agent loop

Avoiding premature commitment in agent loops

text: Observation: What Came Back. The Observation block holds the result the runtime received from the tool. The runtime writes this in - the model does not invent it. The Observation is appended to the conversation so the next Thought has the new ground truth to reason over. Example: 'Observation: { customer_id: "c-9924", tier: "pro", primary_email: "alice@example.com" }'. The model reads this and decides the next Thought. Notice the structure - keys like customer_id, tier, primary_email. The model is reading a JSON-shaped result, not a free-text paragraph. Keeping the Observation strictly structured is what lets the next Thought pull out specific fields like c-9924 instead of paraphrasing them.
summary: The Observation is the runtime-supplied, JSON-structured tool result appended to the conversation as ground truth for the next Thought.
queries: What is an Observation in ReAct

text: ReAct Trace: Ticket T2. Full four-cycle ReAct trace. Cycle 1: Thought - Customer wants reset, mentioned old email. Look them up first. Action - lookup_customer({"email": "alice@example.com"}). Observation - { customer_id: "c-9924", tier: "pro", primary_email: "alice@example.com" }. Cycle 2: Thought - Email on record is outdated. Update it before the reset link goes to the wrong inbox. Action - change_email({"customer_id": "c-9924", "new_email": "alice+new@example.com"}). Observation - { ok: true, primary_email: "alice+new@example.com", verification: "pending" }. Cycle 3: Thought - Email is fixed. Trigger the password reset now so the link lands at the new address. Action - reset_password({"customer_id": "c-9924"}). Observation - { ok: true, reset_link_sent_to: "alice+new@example.com" }. Cycle 4: Thought - All steps done. Write the user-facing reply and stop the loop. Answer - 'I updated your email to alice+new@example.com and sent a fresh reset link there. Click it within 30 minutes to set a new password.' Outcome: 4 cycles, 3 tool calls. Cycle 4 ends with an Answer instead of an Action, so the loop stops. A human reviewer can audit every decision, and a downstream evaluator can score the Thoughts independently from whether the final answer was right.
summary: Worked four-cycle ReAct trace for a password reset ticket: lookup, change email, reset password, then final answer terminates the loop.

text: When To Force ReAct. ReAct is not free. Each Thought costs tokens and slows the response. Modern tool-use APIs already produce structured tool_use blocks without forcing the reasoning to be written out. Plain tool-use: model emits tool_use blocks, reasoning stays in the model's head, invisible to user / observability. ReAct: same loop, but model required to emit Thought + Action explicitly, reasoning written out as text. When to use ReAct: when you need to audit reasoning; when you need to debug failures; when you train a smaller model on the traces; when you need to build trust with the user. Once the trace is written down, it becomes a teaching signal. You can fine-tune a smaller model on big-model traces, you can show the Thoughts in a UI to make the agent feel transparent, and you can write evals that score the plan separately from the final answer.
summary: ReAct adds token cost and latency - force it only when you need audit, debug, distillation, or user trust; otherwise plain tool_use is enough.

text: Decide up front or step by step. ReAct chooses the next move iteration by iteration. Plan-Execute does the opposite: the model writes the full plan tree up front, then a simple executor walks the leaves. Same tools, same model, very different control shape. ReAct card: reactive chain S1 -> S2 -> S3 -> S4 with question-mark arrows; model picks next action each turn; no plan until step N+1 is chosen. Plan-Execute card: a Root Plan node fans out to four leaves L1, L2, L3, L4; whole plan drawn before any leaf runs; deliberate, plan tree is visible up front. Use ReAct when the next step truly depends on what just happened. Use Plan-Execute when you can name all the steps up front and just want them ordered, parallelized, or audited.
summary: ReAct decides iteratively; Plan-Execute writes the whole plan tree up front then walks it.

text: What a plan looks like. A plan is a tree. The root names the overall goal. Each leaf names a concrete tool call with the data it needs. Plan tree for ticket T4 - Resolve T4: Cancel + Refund (Root Goal) with four leaves: 1. lookup_customer (to get customer_id), 2. lookup_subscription (to get invoice_id), 3. cancel (subscription), 4. process_refund (business_rule Error -> Escalate, marked red). Tree drawn before any tool fires. Key idea: every leaf names a tool and the data it will need. The plan is structured output, not free text. The model emits four leaf objects, each with a tool name, an argument template, and a note about which earlier leaf supplies each argument. The fourth leaf flags a known business_rule snag the planner anticipates.

text: Walk the leaves. Once the plan is set, a thin executor walks the leaves in order. Each leaf calls its tool, captures the result, and feeds the result into the next leaf's arguments. The model only re-enters the loop when something the plan did not anticipate happens. Executor walking leaves 1-4 + adapted leaf 5: 1. lookup_customer -> customer_id: c-9924 (Ok). 2. lookup_subscription -> invoice_id: inv-7741 (Ok). 3. cancel -> status: cancelled (Ok). 4. process_refund -> Error: business_rule (Err). Executor adapts and adds leaf 5. escalate_human, reason: business_rule (Done). When plans break: executor catches the business_rule error and appends step 5 escalate_human. The executor did not throw the entire plan away. Leaves 1, 2, and 3 already succeeded - their results are kept. Only the failed leaf triggers a replan, and the replan appends one new leaf instead of rebuilding the tree from scratch.
summary: Executor walks plan leaves passing results forward; on failure it appends a corrective leaf rather than discarding the whole plan.

text: Reflection: did that answer land? Reflection sits on top of any loop. After the model writes a draft, the same model (or a separate critic call) grades it. If the grade is low, the model revises. It is a cheap, model-only feedback loop that catches confidently wrong answers before they ship. Three steps: 1. Model writes draft answer - first pass, free text or structured output, may be confidently wrong. 2. Model (or critic) scores the draft 0-10 with reasoning - grade covers accuracy, format, completeness, tone; reasoning spells out the misses. 3. If score < 7, model revises and re-evaluates - revision uses the critique as a diff list; re-score decides whether to stop or loop again. Safety valve: one revision cap typical, more triggers escalation. Critic prompt sketch: `Critic: Grade the draft 0-10 on accuracy, format, completeness. Return JSON: { "score": int, "reasoning": str, "fix_list": [str] }. If Score < 7, the orchestrator runs the model again with the fix_list pasted into the prompt. Second draft is re-scored. Two failed revisions in a row escalate_human.` Cost is one extra model call per draft, plus another full call if a revision is needed. Win is catching bugs the original pass missed: math errors, missing fields, tone mismatches. Bound revisions so reflection cannot spiral into an infinite revise loop.
summary: Reflection adds a draft-score-revise loop with a score threshold of 7, a one-revision cap, and a critic that returns JSON with score, reasoning, and fix_list.

text: Pick the right loop pattern. Four shapes, one decision matrix. Plot the task on two axes: task complexity (simple vs complex) and need for auditability (low audit vs high audit). Cells: Simple Task + Low Audit -> Plain Tool-Use (cheap single-call flow). Simple Task + High Audit -> ReAct (simple task, visible reasoning). Complex Task + Low Audit -> Plan-Execute (complex plan, light audit). Complex Task + High Audit -> Plan-Execute + Reflection (complex plan, heavy audit, self-check). Task complexity grows top to bottom. Decision shortcut: single tool, trusted output -> Plain Tool-Use; few steps, need visible reasoning -> ReAct; many steps, order known -> Plan-Execute; high-stakes answer, must self-check -> Plan-Execute + Reflection. Loops are not a ladder where bigger is always better. Each extra structure costs tokens, latency, and review surface. Pick the smallest shape that handles the task and graduate only when the task forces you to.
summary: 2x2 matrix on complexity x auditability picks among Plain Tool-Use, ReAct, Plan-Execute, and Plan-Execute + Reflection.

text: This chapter covers loop termination. An agent loop has exactly four stop conditions: Success (model emits text without tool_use, API stop_reason becomes end_turn), Max-Iters (a hard counter cap of 10-20 force-terminates runaway loops), Budget (token/dollar cap, e.g., $0.50/run, safer than iteration count because per-step cost can vary 10x), and Explicit Stop (a tool returns halt:true or the user cancels). Success is the only one that does not need escalation. Every non-success termination must trigger the fail-safe pattern: emit a user-visible message saying a human will follow up, then call escalate_human with the partial transcript so the human can resume with full reasoning context. Production rule: no silent failures.
summary: Four loop stop conditions (success, max-iters, budget, explicit stop) and a mandatory fail-safe escalation with partial transcript for any non-success case.
queries: all ways an agent loop can end
how to end an agent loop safely

text: An agent loop must stop. There are exactly four reasons it does: Success (model emits final answer, no more tool calls), Max-Iters (hit the cap, e.g., 10), Budget (token/cost exhausted), and Explicit Stop (a tool returns a 'halt' signal, or the user cancels). Three of the four are non-success cases (out of room, out of money, or told to halt). Every production agent must detect each one and respond differently. Knowing which condition fired tells you what to do next.
summary: Four stop conditions end every agent loop: success, max-iters, budget, and explicit stop.
queries: how does an agent loop terminate
what makes an agent stop looping
how to know when agent finishes

text: Success is the subtlest stop condition. The model never says 'I'm done' out loud. It just stops asking for tools. The runtime watches the most recent response and detects the absence of any tool_use block. Iteration N-1: model emits tool_use plus text - still working, runtime runs the tool, feeds result back, loops again. Iteration N: model emits ONLY text (no tool_use). That's success - the model decided no more tools are needed. The stop reason from the API is 'end_turn' instead of 'tool_use'. The final text is the answer to the user. This is the only stop condition that does not require an escalation message; everything else is partial completion and the user must be told what happened.
summary: Success is detected when the model emits text without any tool_use block, signaled by API stop_reason end_turn.
queries: how does agent detect successful completion
how do you know the agent finished
API stop reason after agent loop

text: The max-iter cap (also called max-iters) is the dumbest safety net but the most important. A counter ticks up every loop pass. When it hits the cap, the runtime force-terminates the loop without asking the model. Production rule: 10-20 typical max-iter cap. Higher = pay-to-think; lower = miss answers. The cap protects against runaway loops where the model can't make progress and keeps re-trying the same tool. Without a cap a single buggy ticket can bill thousands of dollars in an hour.
summary: A hard iteration cap of 10-20 force-terminates runaway loops without consulting the model.
queries: how to prevent runaway agent loops
how many iterations should an agent run

text: Iteration count is a proxy for cost; the real number is dollars. A budget cap measures the total tokens (or dollars) spent across all iterations and stops the loop when the wallet is empty. Sample budget: $0.50 per agent run. At 30% ($0.15) iter 1-2 consumed; at 60% ($0.30) iter 3-4 consumed; at 100% budget exhausted - STOP at $0.50. Budget is the SAFER cap than iterations because real cost can vary 10x per iteration. One iteration that triggers a thinking burst can spend 10k tokens; the next might spend 500. Iteration count doesn't see that. A dollar budget does.
summary: A token/dollar budget cap is safer than iteration count because per-iteration cost can vary 10x.

text: Some tools are terminal by design. An escalate_human tool hands the conversation to a human and there is nothing left for the agent to do. The tool result includes a halt flag, and the runtime respects it. Tool result shape: { "tool": "escalate_human", "result": { "ticket_id": "T4", "human_assigned": true }, "halt": true }. The loop respects this signal and terminates cleanly. Similar pattern: a user cancellation injects a halt event. Explicit stops are the cleanest non-success case: the model doesn't get confused, the runtime doesn't guess, and there is a clear handoff record in the transcript.
summary: Terminal tools (e.g., escalate_human) return a halt:true flag that the runtime respects to cleanly stop the loop.
queries: how to design a halting tool

text: Any non-success termination (max-iters, budget, or explicit halt that wasn't a successful resolution) must trigger the same fail-safe pattern. Step 1: Loop terminates on any non-success stop. Step 2: Agent emits a final message to the user - 'I wasn't able to fully resolve this. A human agent will follow up.' Step 3: Call escalate_human with partial transcript - hands off the full chain of reasoning so the human can resume. Production rule: No silent failures. Every non-success termination produces a user-visible message AND an escalate_human call with the full partial transcript. Silent failures are how agent bugs hide in production for weeks. The transcript is the load-bearing artifact: it carries the agent's reasoning, every tool call, every result, so the human picking up the ticket has full context. No re-litigating from scratch.
summary: Every non-success termination must emit a user message and call escalate_human with the partial transcript - no silent failures.
queries: what to do when agent gives up
how to escalate agent to human
what happens when budget runs out

text: This chapter introduces the agent memory taxonomy: one root (Agent Memory), two branches (Short-Term and Long-Term), and four leaves (Working, Episodic, Semantic, Procedural). Short-term working memory lives inside the active context window and is discarded at session end. Long-term memory persists across sessions in external stores and splits by content type: episodic holds past events with timestamps, semantic holds key-value or vector facts, procedural holds cached skills and recipes. A ticket T2 example shows all four layers filled in for Alice's password reset case. Each layer earns its place by solving a distinct problem with different retrieval patterns, update rules, and lifespans.

text: Two Memory Layers. An agent has memory in two very different places. Short-term memory lives inside the active context window of the current conversation. Long-term memory lives outside that window and persists across sessions. SHORT-TERM / Working Memory: lives in the active context window. The model reads and writes it every loop turn. Discarded when the session ends. LONG-TERM / Persistent Memory: lives outside the context window in an external store. Persists across sessions and conversations. Gets loaded back in on demand. Short = this conversation. Long = everything before.
summary: Introduces the two top-level agent memory layers: short-term working memory inside the context window versus long-term persistent memory outside it.

text: Long-Term Splits Three Ways. Long-term memory is not one thing. It separates by what kind of information it holds. Three sub-types - episodic, semantic, procedural - each answer a different question. Episodic: Past Events (Timestamps + Content). Example: Past Ticket #4521: Failed Password Reset Escalated For MFA Bug. Semantic: Facts (Key-Value Or Vector). Example: Alice = Pro Tier, Signed Up 2024-08, Prefers Email. Procedural: Skills (Recipes / Cached Workflows). Example: Refund > $200 -> Escalate To Human. Shape: events (when), facts (what), skills (how).

text: Each Layer Solves A Different Problem. Why have four layers and not one? Because each holds a different shape of information, with different retrieval patterns, different update rules, and different lifespans. Working: Holds the current task state across loop iterations. Episodic: Gives the agent recall of specific past events. Semantic: Provides stable customer facts without re-asking. Procedural: Caches learned routines so the agent does not re-derive them every time. Each layer earns its place.

text: Working memory is the in-context scratchpad an agent reads on REASON and updates on OBSERVE every loop turn. It is a structured object with five fields - customer_context, current_goal, completed_steps, next_step, constraints - that anchors the running task. Across iterations of ticket T2 the scratchpad fills in customer info, appends completed tool calls, and ends with next_step null to signal task completion. When the task ends the scratchpad is discarded, so anything worth keeping must be promoted to long-term memory first. Compared to long-term memory (external vector DB or Postgres, millions of facts, persistent), working memory is in-context, a few hundred tokens, model-written, ephemeral. Agents need both, with promotion as the bridge.
summary: Chapter overview: scratchpad shape, per-iteration updates, discard-at-end lifecycle, and contrast with long-term memory.

text: Working memory is the in-context scratchpad - a note pad the model keeps. The agent reads it on every REASON step and updates it after every OBSERVE step. It lives inside the prompt context window itself, not in any external store. The loop around the note pad has four steps: 1. REASON: the model reads the scratchpad to decide the next tool. 2. ACT: the tool runs. 3. OBSERVE: the result comes back and the scratchpad is updated. 4. LOOP: back to step 1 with the updated note pad. The scratchpad is the agent's short-term reasoning trace. Lose it, and the agent has to re-derive what it was doing from scratch.
summary: Working memory is an in-context scratchpad read on REASON and updated on OBSERVE in every loop iteration.

text:
What goes in the scratchpad. The scratchpad is not free-form text. It is a structured object with stable field names so the model can read and write specific slots. Five fields anchor the running task. Working Memory (Shape): {
  "customer_context": "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro",
  "current_goal": "Reset password (email also changed)",
  "completed_steps": ["lookup_customer", "change_email"],
  "next_step": "reset_password",
  "constraints": ["refund cap $200"]
}. customer_context answers who, current_goal answers what, completed_steps answers what so far, next_step answers what next, and constraints keep the agent within policy. Same five fields appear in 28.6 observability traces.

text: Updated every iteration. Watch the scratchpad evolve across four loop turns of ticket T2 (Alice: password reset + email change). Each row is the state right after the iteration's OBSERVE step. Iteration 1: customer_context: "" (not yet looked up), completed_steps: [], next_step: "lookup_customer". Iteration 2: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer"], next_step: "change_email". Iteration 3: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer", "change_email"], next_step: "reset_password". Iteration 4: customer_context: "alice@example.com, c-9924, Pro", completed_steps: ["lookup_customer", "change_email", "reset_password"], next_step: null (task complete). By iteration 4, next_step is null and the task is done. The scratchpad terminated cleanly, which is how loop termination (26.6) detects success.

text: Working memory dies when the task ends. When the loop terminates, the scratchpad is discarded. Anything worth keeping must be promoted to long-term memory BEFORE that discard. Otherwise it is gone. Timeline: Task Start (Created), Iter N (Updated), Promote (To Long-Term), Task End (Discarded). A promotion arrow points up from the timeline to the Long-Term Store before discard. "Resolved by email reset link" is a working memory entry. After promotion to episodic memory, the next session can recall it. Skip the promote, and the next session asks Alice the same question from scratch.

text: Working vs Long-Term. Side-by-side: what is different between the in-context scratchpad and the external long-term store. Different lifespan, different capacity, different writer. Lives In: Working = Prompt Context Window, Long-Term = External Store (Vector DB / Postgres). Written By: Working = Model On Each Loop Turn, Long-Term = Explicit Promotion Logic. Capacity: Working = A Few Hundred Tokens, Long-Term = Millions Of Facts / Events. Lifespan: Working = Discarded When Task Ends, Long-Term = Persistent Across Sessions. Working memory is fast, cheap, and ephemeral. Long-term memory is slower, more expensive, but persistent. Agents need both - and the bridge between them is the promote step.
summary: Working memory lives in context (hundreds of tokens, model-written, ephemeral); long-term lives in external stores (millions of facts, promotion-written, persistent).

text: This chapter covers episodic memory for agents: time-stamped past events stored as vectors, retrieved via ANN search before reasoning. Walkthrough uses customer Alice with four past episodes (failed password reset with MFA bug, slow dashboard, invoice request, current password+email reset T2). Events embedded into vector DB (Section 15.7 IVF / 15.8 HNSW), recall pipeline retrieves top-3 episodes and injects them into system prompt (same pattern as ChatGPT Memory). Each entry is structured JSON with id, customer_id, timestamp, summary, tools_used, outcome, embedding (1024 floats). Pruning policy required to prevent unbounded growth: drop clean old episodes, keep escalations, summarize duplicates, honor compliance deletion.

Episodes answer 'what happened, when?'. They are NOT the customer's stable facts (semantic memory) and NOT how-to recipes (procedural memory).

text: Episodic memory lives in a vector database. Pipeline has two stages. Stage one (write): each event becomes an embedding (1024 floats) and is stored in the vector DB. Stage two (read): a search query like 'What past issues?' is embedded with the SAME encoder, then an ANN index returns the most similar past events as top-k results. Vector storage substrate is covered in Section 15.7 (IVF) and 15.8 (HNSW); episodic memory sits as a layer above it. Pattern: write once, search by similarity from then on.
summary: Events and queries become embeddings via the same encoder; an ANN index returns top-k similar past events from a vector DB.

text: Recall happens BEFORE reasoning. When a new conversation starts, the agent retrieves relevant past episodes before reasoning about the current ticket. Five steps: (1) User message arrives - T2: 'I forgot my password and my email changed.' (2) Build recall query - 'current ticket summary + customer_id c-9924'. (3) Retrieve top-3 episodes - ANN search returns 3 most similar past events. (4) Inject into system prompt - episodes added to prompt as a context block. (5) Agent reasons with past context - past MFA-bug episode informs current approach. ChatGPT Memory works the same way: the host fetches relevant facts on each new conversation and injects them. The model does not 'remember' - the host system gives it the appearance of memory through retrieval-and-inject.
summary: Five-step recall pipeline runs before reasoning: retrieve top-3 episodes via ANN and inject them into the system prompt as context.

text: Memory has to forget. Unbounded growth wrecks retrieval quality. Pruning policy covers four cases. (1) Age > 12 months AND outcome = resolved cleanly -> Drop. (2) Age > 12 months AND outcome = escalated -> Keep (rare patterns valuable). (3) Aggregated similar events -> Summarize into one parent and drop children. (4) Manual customer request -> Delete on demand (compliance). Compliance-driven deletion is non-negotiable. A customer can request their entire episodic log be wiped, and the agent must honor it within whatever window the regulation requires.
summary: Pruning policy with four rules prevents unbounded memory growth: drop clean old events, keep escalations, summarize duplicates, honor compliance deletion.

text: This chapter Semantic Memory - Learned Facts. Covers: (1) semantic memory holds stable 'currently true' facts, distinct from time-stamped episodic events; (2) the customer profile card shape with typed fields like customer_id, tier, preferred_contact, billing_currency, and a nested preferences bag; (3) two storage substrates - key-value/Postgres for typed exact-match fields and vector store for free-text similarity facts, used together in production; (4) profile growth from Day 1 to Day 30 to Day 90 via confirmed observations only, never single-message guesses; (5) the 6-months test for what counts as a fact: stable preferences, identity, constraints go in; transient mood, one-time requests, and guesses stay out.
summary: Chapter overview: semantic memory stores long-lived facts in structured plus vector stores, growing via confirmed observations and gated by the 6-months test.

text: Semantic memory holds stable facts about a customer. Different from episodic memory: an episode is 'this happened on this date'; a fact is 'this is currently true'. Episodic fact (time-stamped event): On 2026-03-04, Alice opened a ticket about a password reset. Semantic fact (stable state): Alice prefers email contact. Same person, different memory layer. Episodic answers 'what happened?', semantic answers 'what is true?'. Both are needed; they live in different stores with different update rules.
summary: Semantic memory stores stable current-truth facts about a user, distinct from time-stamped episodic events.

summary: Semantic memory is shaped as a customer profile card with typed fields plus a nested preferences bag.

text: Key-Value Or Vector. Two storage substrates serve different fact shapes. Most production agents use BOTH: structured for the spine, vector for the long-tail freetext. Structured (Key-Value / Postgres): use when fields are typed, predictable, queried by exact match. Example: customer_id -> profile lookup. Vector (Embedding + Similarity): use when facts are free-text, queried by meaning. Example: 'User has a dairy allergy' embedded for retrieval. The spine (typed profile) is small and exact. The long-tail (free-form preferences and constraints) is large and fuzzy. Use the right tool for each shape.
summary: Semantic memory uses key-value storage for typed exact-match fields plus vector storage for free-text similarity retrieval.

text: How The Profile Fills Up. Semantic memory grows over time. Alice's profile at signup has 4 fields. By day 30 it has 5. By day 90 it has nested preferences. Growth comes from confirmed observations. Day 1 (Signup): customer_id: c-9924, tier: Pro, signed_up: 2024-08, primary_email: alice@example.com. Day 30: (all Day-1 fields) + preferred_contact: email, learned from 'please email me at...'. Day 90: (all Day-30 fields) + preferences.skip_onboarding_emails: true, learned from user opt-out click. Profile fills only on CONFIRMED observations. A guess from one ambiguous message does not get written. A repeated preference observed across sessions does.
summary: The profile grows incrementally from 4 to 5 to nested fields as the agent confirms observations across days.
queries: Day 1 vs Day 30 vs Day 90 profile
when to write fact to memory
agent learning user preferences over time

text: What Counts As A Fact? Rule of thumb: if it would still be true in 6 months, write it to semantic memory. Otherwise leave it in the working scratchpad or skip it entirely. Write to semantic: stable preferences (always email), confirmed identity (Alice is account owner), constraints (dairy allergy). Do not write: transient mood ('frustrated today'), one-time requests ('send a copy this once'), guesses (uncertain inferences). 6-Months Test: would knowing this still help six months from now? If yes, write it. If no, it belongs in working memory or the chat history and should expire naturally.
summary: The 6-months test decides what to write: stable preferences, identity, constraints go in; transient moods, one-offs, and guesses stay out.
queries: what counts as a fact for semantic memory
rule of thumb for fact storage

text: Procedural memory stores skills (how-to recipes) separately from facts. The customer-support agent maintains a small library of named recipes, each with a trigger embedding, an ordered step list, success_rate, and uses count. New tickets are embedded and matched via ANN search against trigger embeddings; the top-1 match becomes a scaffold that the agent adapts to the ticket. No match means fall back to first-principles reasoning, which can be mined into a future recipe. Versus baking instructions into the system prompt, procedural memory is retrieved on demand (lower token cost), externally stored, and updatable from outcomes (success_rate drives rewrites). Use prompting for universal rules and procedural memory for task-specific recipes.
summary: Chapter shows procedural memory as a retrievable, updatable library of how-to recipes that complements semantic facts and static system prompts.
queries: what is procedural memory in LLM agents

text: Procedural memory encodes skills (HOW to do something), not facts about the world. Contrast: a semantic fact says 'refund cap is $200' (WHAT is true). A procedural skill says 'When a refund > $200 is requested, look up customer tier first. Enterprise customers self-approve up to $500. Otherwise call escalate_human with urgency=Medium' (WHAT TO DO). The agent uses both: the fact gives the $200 cap, the procedure says what to do when the cap is exceeded.
summary: Procedural memory stores how-to recipes (skills) while semantic memory stores what-is-true facts, and agents use them together.

text: The recipe library is a small set of named workflows the customer-support agent reuses. Each recipe has a trigger condition and an ordered step list. Four example recipes: 1) Handle Refund Request - trigger: Customer asks for refund, 3 steps. 2) Resolve Password Issue - trigger: Login / password error, 4 steps. 3) Escalate Billing Dispute - trigger: Disputed invoice or charge, 2 steps. 4) Resolve Sync Failure - trigger: Data out-of-sync across devices, 5 steps. Each used to be a prompt-engineering exercise; once it stabilized, it got moved into procedural memory.
summary: A recipe library is a set of named, trigger-and-step workflows that an agent reuses for common tasks.

text: Recipe retrieval pipeline: a new ticket gets embedded; ANN search runs against the recipe library's trigger_embedding field; the top-1 match is surfaced (or 'no match' if similarity is below threshold). Five stages: New Ticket (free text) -> Embed (1024 floats) -> ANN Search (vs library) -> Top-1 (or no match) -> Use Recipe (scaffolding). The agent uses the retrieved recipe as a scaffold then adapts to specifics. If no recipe matches, agent falls back to first-principles reasoning, and that run becomes a candidate to mine into a new recipe.
summary: Agent retrieves a relevant recipe by embedding the ticket and running ANN search against stored trigger embeddings, falling back to reasoning when no match exists.

text:
A recipe is a structured record. Example shape:
{
  "id": "recipe-refund",
  "name": "Handle refund request",
  "trigger_embedding": "[1024 floats]",
  "steps": [
    "lookup_customer to get customer_id and tier",
    "if tier == Enterprise: auto-approve up to $500; else $200",
    "lookup_subscription for invoice_id",
    "process_refund(invoice_id, reason); if business_rule error: escalate_human"
  ],
  "success_rate": 0.92,
  "uses": 187
}
Trigger embedding is for retrieval; steps are for execution; success_rate and uses drive analytics and self-improvement. success_rate = 0.92 means 92% of runs produced a clean outcome. uses = 187 means the agent has run the recipe 187 times. A falling success_rate triggers a rewrite review.
summary: A recipe record stores an id, name, trigger embedding, ordered steps, success_rate, and uses count - enabling retrieval, execution, and self-improvement.

text: Prompting vs procedural memory comparison: 1) Where it lives - Prompting: inside the system prompt. Procedural: external store, retrieved on demand. 2) Token cost - Prompting: every conversation pays the prompt tokens. Procedural: only retrieved when relevant. 3) Can learn? - Prompting: static, cannot update from outcomes. Procedural: updatable, success_rate drives rewrite. 4) Best for - Prompting: universal rules (tone, format). Procedural: task-specific recipes. Prompting stays great for universal rules - tone, format, safety constraints. Procedural memory wins for task-specific recipes that change with real-world outcomes. Use both deliberately.
summary: Prompting is best for static universal rules while procedural memory is best for retrievable, learnable task-specific recipes - and serious agents use both.

text: Long conversations exceed the context window (8k tokens fills by turn 30 at 250 tokens/turn). Solutions: rolling summary compresses oldest half at 50% capacity; hierarchical summary stacks batch summaries plus a meta-summary for week-long chats; recency/relevance/hybrid choose which turns stay raw; production agents use fixed thresholds (25/50/75/90 percent). Final assembly stacks system prompt, memory block, summary, recent turns, retrieved turns, working memory, and current user message.

text: Long conversations are not free. With an 8k token context window and ~250 tokens per turn, the window fills by turn 30. Past that, something has to give. The agent must compress old turns, drop them, or both. The rest of this chapter is about HOW to manage that ceiling.

text: Rolling-summary technique: when the window hits 50% capacity, summarize the oldest half into a 2-paragraph block. The summary replaces the raw turns. Capacity drops to 25%. Repeat every 50%. The model itself usually generates the summary (a 2-paragraph LLM call per compression cycle). The summary lives in the conversation history at the spot where the raw turns used to be.
summary: Explains rolling summary: at 50% capacity, an LLM summarizes the oldest half into a 2-paragraph block, dropping usage back to 25%.
queries: how to keep long conversation in context

text: For week-long conversations, flat rolling summary degrades. The hierarchical approach organizes raw turns into batches (leaves), per-batch summaries (mid), and a meta-summary of the whole conversation (top). Hierarchical wins for week-long conversations because the meta-summary stays small while you can still zoom into specific batches when needed.
summary: Hierarchical summarization stacks raw turn batches, per-batch summaries, and a meta-summary for week-long conversations.
queries: why hierarchical beats rolling for long chats

text: After summarization, what stays in the prompt? Three strategies with different tradeoffs between continuity and topical fit. Recency keeps the most-recent N turns raw and drops a summary of older turns, good for continuity-heavy conversations. Relevance keeps turns most similar to the current query and drops a summary of less-relevant turns, good for long-range topical recall. Hybrid keeps top-K recent plus top-K relevant and drops a summary of everything else, the best default in practice. Hybrid wins because most conversations have BOTH continuity needs (the user said 'this' earlier) AND topical recall needs (the user asked a similar thing 80 turns ago).
summary: Compares recency, relevance, and hybrid strategies for choosing which turns stay raw in the prompt after summarization.
queries: how to choose which turns to keep
what is hybrid retrieval chat memory

most recent turns vs most relevant turns

text: Production agents trigger compression at fixed thresholds, not on demand. Four bands cover the production rule: at 25% do nothing (plenty of room); at 50% compress half (summarize oldest 25%); at 75% aggressive (summarize older half plus start hybrid retrieval); at 90% panic (drop everything except summary plus last 5 turns plus top retrieved). Threshold-based compression is production-friendly because the cost is predictable. On-demand compression can hide behind a single user turn that suddenly explodes the window.
summary: Lays out four production thresholds (25/50/75/90 percent) that trigger progressively more aggressive context compression.

text: Memory plus context-engineering decide what the model sees on EVERY turn. The assembly order: 1. System Prompt - static plus recipe from procedural memory. 2. Memory Block - retrieved episodes plus profile card from semantic. 3. Conversation Summary - rolling or hierarchical summary of old turns. 4. Recent Turns - last N raw turns (recency). 5. Retrieved Turns - top-K relevant turns from older history (hybrid). 6. Working Memory - current scratchpad state. 7. Current User Message - what the user just said. Every layer comes from somewhere. Memory chapters supply the sources; the prompt-assembly stack joins them into a single prompt; summarization keeps it all under the token budget.
summary: Lists the seven-layer prompt assembly order that joins memory sources, summaries, recent turns, and the user message into one prompt under budget.

text: This chapter (Why Multi-Agent?) explains when to split one agent into many. A single agent loaded with retrieve, classify, refund, troubleshoot, and escalate hits a performance ceiling because each role pulls the system prompt differently. Three motivations for going multi-agent: specialization (one agent per role with tighter prompt and 1-2 tools instead of 8), parallelism (independent sub-tasks like lookup_customer, lookup_subscription, search_kb run concurrently so wall-clock = max latency 1.6s instead of sum 4.2s, ~3x faster), and planner/worker separation (planner decomposes, workers execute single steps, neither crosses layers). Anti-patterns where single agent wins: single-step tasks, context-continuity tasks, latency-critical loops with 100ms SLA, and small dev teams. Decision rule: multi-agent IF specialization OR parallelism OR planner/worker fits; otherwise single agent.
summary: Overview of when multi-agent beats single agent: specialization, parallelism, planner/worker, plus anti-patterns.

text: Why one agent sometimes isn't enough. A single agent loaded with all customer-support roles - retrieve, classify, refund, troubleshoot, escalate - has a crowded system prompt. Each role pulls the prompt in a different direction. A performance ceiling appears. Each new role added to the system prompt costs precision on every other role. The ceiling is real: at some point, splitting roles into separate agents beats stuffing them into one.
summary: One agent given five different jobs hits a precision ceiling because roles compete inside one system prompt.

text: Specialization: one agent per role. Replace the crowded single agent with 3 focused agents. Each has a tighter system prompt, a smaller tool set, a clearer goal. Each does its job better than a generalist would. Specialists: (1) Billing Specialist - tools lookup_subscription, process_refund - prompt 'Handle refunds, invoices, tier changes'. (2) Troubleshooting Specialist - tools search_kb, lookup_customer - prompt 'Diagnose errors, outages, sync failures'. (3) Triage Router - tools lookup_customer - prompt 'Classify each ticket and route to specialist'. Each specialist has 1-2 tools, not the full 8-tool inventory. Smaller surface = fewer wrong-tool errors. The triage router picks which specialist a ticket goes to.
summary: Three specialist agents with tighter prompts and 1-2 tools each replace one generalist; a triage router routes tickets.

text: Run independent tasks at the same time. Some sub-tasks have no data dependencies. Run them on separate agents concurrently. Wall-clock cost is max(sub-task latency), not the sum. Dispatcher fans out to Agent A (lookup_customer, 1.2s), Agent B (lookup_subscription, 1.4s), Agent C (search_kb, 1.6s). Sequential = 1.2 + 1.4 + 1.6 = 4.2s. Parallel = max(1.2, 1.4, 1.6) = 1.6s. Almost 3x faster. The win compounds as you add more independent sub-tasks.
summary: Independent agents run in parallel so wall-clock = slowest leg (1.6s) not sum (4.2s), ~3x speedup.
queries: how does parallel agent execution work
wall clock time for parallel agents
max latency vs sum latency agents
how to know if sub-tasks can run in parallel

text: Planner vs Worker. Some agents are good at decomposing tasks; others are good at executing single steps. Separating these roles produces a 2-layer setup where each agent only does its own job. Planner Agent at top decomposes the task. Worker 1 does Lookup Customer; Worker 2 does Process Refund. The planner does NOT execute steps. The workers do NOT decompose. Each agent stays on its layer. This separation makes both layers more reliable than one mixed agent.
summary: Two-layer architecture: planner decomposes tasks, workers execute single steps, neither crosses layers.
queries: planner doesn't execute, worker doesn't decompose
lookup customer worker process refund worker example

text: Don't multi-agent a small problem. Multi-agent costs something: more LLM calls, more state to track, more places for failure. Four anti-patterns where one agent is right: (1) Single-Step Tasks - just call the tool; adding a second agent doubles latency for zero gain. (2) Context Continuity Tasks - if the same context carries through every step, hand-off loses information. (3) Latency-Critical Loops - each agent adds 1-2 seconds of reasoning; a 100ms SLA loop cannot afford this. (4) Small Dev Teams - multi-agent failures (this chapter) are hard to debug; a 2-person team will drown. Decision rule: Multi-agent IF specialization OR parallelism OR planner/worker fits the workload. Otherwise single agent.
summary: Four anti-patterns where single agent wins, plus the decision rule: multi-agent only if specialization, parallelism, or planner/worker fits.
queries: decision rule single vs multi agent
when is one agent better than many
is multi-agent overkill for small problem

text: This chapter covers the orchestrator-worker pattern: a hub-and-spoke topology with a central orchestrator that plans, dispatches, and aggregates while N workers each handle one isolated sub-task. The orchestrator runs three phases (Plan, Dispatch, Aggregate) as separate LLM calls. Workers run their own reason-act-observe loop within their tool set and return structured results; they never talk to each other. A concrete Ticket T3 trace shows 3 parallel workers (search_kb x2 + lookup_customer) cutting latency to max(worker) + plan + aggregate. Three aggregation patterns - concatenation, voting, synthesis - each fit different output types, with synthesis being the production default.
summary: Chapter-level summary of the orchestrator-worker multi-agent pattern, its phases, worker isolation, parallel latency, and aggregation choices.

text: The orchestrator-worker pattern is the most common production multi-agent shape. A central orchestrator plans and aggregates; workers each handle one sub-task. Hub and spoke topology: workers only talk to the orchestrator, never to each other. The hub-and-spoke shape means the orchestrator owns coordination cost; workers stay simple. No edges between workers means no peer-to-peer chatter and no shared-state corruption.
summary: Introduces orchestrator-worker as a hub-and-spoke multi-agent topology where a central planner coordinates isolated workers.

text: The orchestrator runs three phases per task. Each phase is a separate LLM call with a distinct system prompt - the same agent loops the model through three different mindsets. PLAN: Break the incoming task into 3-5 sub-tasks each workable in isolation. DISPATCH: Send each sub-task to its assigned worker agent. AGGREGATE: When workers return, merge results into a single coherent answer. The 3 phases give the orchestrator 3 separate minds: one to plan, one to dispatch, one to aggregate. Each is simpler than a single do-everything prompt.
summary: Breaks the orchestrator's job into three distinct LLM phases: plan, dispatch, aggregate.

text: Each worker takes one sub-task description, runs its own agent loop within its tool set, and returns a structured result. Workers do NOT talk to each other - only to the orchestrator. Example: input sub-task 'Lookup customer tier for c-9924' -> Worker Agent runs internal loop Reason -> Act -> Observe within its tool set only -> returns structured result { tier: 'Pro', usage: 'heavy' }. If Worker A's result feeds Worker B's input, the orchestrator does that chaining. This is what makes parallel dispatch safe.

text: Ticket T3 = 'Dashboard slow + 500 errors'. The orchestrator plans 3 parallel sub-tasks, dispatches them to workers, then aggregates. STEP 1 Orchestrator: Receives T3. Plans 3 sub-tasks: A) search_kb for 'Dashboard Slow'; B) search_kb for '500 Errors'; C) lookup_customer for usage tier. STEP 2 Worker A: Returns top-3 KB articles on dashboard performance. STEP 3 Worker B: Returns top-3 KB articles on 500 errors. STEP 4 Worker C: Returns Alice's tier (Pro) + usage stats (heavy dashboard user). STEP 5 Orchestrator: Aggregates - composes a single answer combining KB hits + customer context. Recommends dashboard optimization and escalates 500 errors to engineering. Workers A, B, and C ran in parallel - steps 2-4 happened roughly at the same time. Total latency = max(worker time) + plan + aggregate, not sum.
summary: Walks through Ticket T3 step-by-step to show how orchestrator-worker handles a multi-symptom support ticket in parallel.

text: Aggregation - merging worker outputs into a single answer - has three common patterns. CONCATENATION: Stitch all worker outputs together in order. Simple but verbose. Best for quick prototypes; outputs already well-sectioned. VOTING: Workers each propose; orchestrator picks majority. Good for classification. Best for categorical outputs and hallucination reduction. SYNTHESIS: Orchestrator reads all worker outputs and writes a single coherent answer. Most common, most expensive. Best as production default for natural-language answers. Synthesis is the production default for natural-language answers. Voting wins when workers are doing classification. Concatenation is fine for structured outputs that need no rephrasing.
summary: Compares three aggregation patterns - concatenation, voting, synthesis - and when to pick each.
